subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}\vec a,\vec b{/tex} and {tex}\vec c{/tex} be three vectors such that {tex}\vec a + \vec b + \vec c = 0{/tex} and {tex}\left| {\vec a} \right| = 3,\left| {\vec b} \right| = 5,\left| {\vec c} \right| = 7{/tex} find the angle between {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}\vec a,\vec b{/tex} and {tex}\vec c{/tex} be three vectors such that {tex}\vec a + \vec b + \vec c = 0{/tex} and {tex}\left| {\vec a} \right| = 3,\left| {\vec b} \right| = 5,\left| {\vec c} \right| = 7{/tex} find the angle between {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex}
explanation: {tex}\vec a + \vec b + \vec c = 0{/tex} {tex}\vec a + \vec b = - \vec c{/tex} {tex}\left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right).\left( {-\vec c} \right) = - \vec c.\left( { - \vec c} \right){/tex} {tex}\left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right).\left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right) = \vec c.\vec c{/tex} {tex}{\left| {\vec a} \right|^2} + 2ab + \left| {\vec b} \right| = {\left| {\vec c} \right|^2}{/tex} {tex}\vec a.\vec b = \frac{{|\vec c|^2 - |\vec a|^2 - |\vec b|^2}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\vec a.\vec b = \frac{{49 - 9 - 25}}{2} = \frac{{15}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\cos \theta = \frac{{\vec a.\vec b}}{{\left| {\vec a} \right|\left| {\vec b} \right|}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\theta = 60{/tex}
solution: {tex}\vec a + \vec b + \vec c = 0{/tex} {tex}\vec a + \vec b = - \vec c{/tex} {tex}\left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right).\left( {-\vec c} \right) = - \vec c.\left( { - \vec c} \right){/tex} {tex}\left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right).\left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right) = \vec c.\vec c{/tex} {tex}{\left| {\vec a} \right|^2} + 2ab + \left| {\vec b} \right| = {\left| {\vec c} \right|^2}{/tex} {tex}\vec a.\vec b = \frac{{|\vec c|^2 - |\vec a|^2 - |\vec b|^2}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\vec a.\vec b = \frac{{49 - 9 - 25}}{2} = \frac{{15}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\cos \theta = \frac{{\vec a.\vec b}}{{\left| {\vec a} \right|\left| {\vec b} \right|}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\theta = 60{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\vec a + \vec b + \vec c = 0{/tex}

{tex}\vec a + \vec b = - \vec c{/tex}

{tex}\left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right).\left( {-\vec c} \right) = - \vec c.\left( { - \vec c} \right){/tex}

{tex}\left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right).\left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right) = \vec c.\vec c{/tex}

{tex}{\left| {\vec a} \right|^2} + 2ab + \left| {\vec b} \right| = {\left| {\vec c} \right|^2}{/tex}

{tex}\vec a.\vec b = \frac{{|\vec c|^2 - |\vec a|^2 - |\vec b|^2}}{2}{/tex}

{tex}\vec a.\vec b = \frac{{49 - 9 - 25}}{2} = \frac{{15}}{2}{/tex}

{tex}\cos \theta = \frac{{\vec a.\vec b}}{{\left| {\vec a} \right|\left| {\vec b} \right|}}{/tex}

{tex} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}

{tex}\theta = 60{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}\vec{a} {/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} are non-collinear vectors, find the value of x for which vectors {tex}\vec{\alpha}=(x-2) \vec{a}+\vec{b} {/tex} and {tex}\vec{\beta}=(3+2 x) \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}{/tex} are collinear.
question_text: If {tex}\vec{a} {/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} are non-collinear vectors, find the value of x for which vectors {tex}\vec{\alpha}=(x-2) \vec{a}+\vec{b} {/tex} and {tex}\vec{\beta}=(3+2 x) \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}{/tex} are collinear.
explanation: Since vectors {tex}\vec{\alpha}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\beta}{/tex} are collinear. Thus, there exist scalar {tex}\lambda{/tex} such that {tex}\vec{\alpha}=\lambda \vec{\beta}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow (x-2) \vec{a}+\vec{b}=\lambda\{(3+2 x) \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}\}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \{x-2-\lambda(3+2 x)\} \vec{a}+(1+2 \lambda) \vec{b}=\overrightarrow{0}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x-2-\lambda(3+2 x) = 0{/tex} and {tex}1+2 \lambda=0{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} are non-collinear] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - 2 - {tex}\lambda{/tex} (3 + 2x) = 0 and {tex}\lambda=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x-2+\frac{1}{2}(3+2 x)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 4 x+1=0 \Rightarrow x=-\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
solution: Since vectors {tex}\vec{\alpha}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\beta}{/tex} are collinear. Thus, there exist scalar {tex}\lambda{/tex} such that {tex}\vec{\alpha}=\lambda \vec{\beta}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow (x-2) \vec{a}+\vec{b}=\lambda\{(3+2 x) \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}\}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \{x-2-\lambda(3+2 x)\} \vec{a}+(1+2 \lambda) \vec{b}=\overrightarrow{0}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x-2-\lambda(3+2 x) = 0{/tex} and {tex}1+2 \lambda=0{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} are non-collinear] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - 2 - {tex}\lambda{/tex} (3 + 2x) = 0 and {tex}\lambda=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x-2+\frac{1}{2}(3+2 x)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 4 x+1=0 \Rightarrow x=-\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
Since vectors {tex}\vec{\alpha}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\beta}{/tex} are collinear. Thus, there exist scalar {tex}\lambda{/tex} such that
{tex}\vec{\alpha}=\lambda \vec{\beta}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow (x-2) \vec{a}+\vec{b}=\lambda\{(3+2 x) \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}\}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \{x-2-\lambda(3+2 x)\} \vec{a}+(1+2 \lambda) \vec{b}=\overrightarrow{0}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x-2-\lambda(3+2 x) = 0{/tex} and {tex}1+2 \lambda=0{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} are non-collinear]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - 2 - {tex}\lambda{/tex}(3 + 2x) = 0 and {tex}\lambda=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x-2+\frac{1}{2}(3+2 x)=0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow 4 x+1=0 \Rightarrow x=-\frac{1}{4}{/tex}

question_html: Show that area of the parallelogram whose diagonals are represented by {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} is given by {tex}\frac{1}{2}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|{/tex}. Also find the area of the parallelogram whose diagonals are {tex}2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex}.
question_text: Show that area of the parallelogram whose diagonals are represented by {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} is given by {tex}\frac{1}{2}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|{/tex} . Also find the area of the parallelogram whose diagonals are {tex}2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} .
explanation: Let ABCD be a parallelogram such the {tex}\vec{A B}=\vec{p}, \vec{A D}=\vec{q} \Rightarrow \vec{B C}=\vec{q}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{A C}=\vec{A B}+\vec{B C}=\vec{p}+\vec{q}=\vec{a}{/tex} .....(i) [By triangle law of vectors] And {tex}\vec{B D}=\vec{B A}+\vec{A D}=-\vec{p}+\vec{q}=\vec{b}{/tex} .....(ii) Adding (i) and (ii), we get {tex}\bar{a}+\bar{b}=2 \bar{q}{/tex} Subtracting (ii) from Eq. (i), we get {tex}\vec{a}-\vec{b}=2 \vec{p}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{p}=\frac{1}{2}(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex} Now, {tex}\vec{p} \times \vec{q}=\frac{1}{4}(\vec{a}-\vec{b}) \times(\vec{a}+\vec{b}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4}(\bar{a} \times \bar{a}+\bar{a} \times \bar{b}-\vec{b} \times \bar{a}-\bar{b} \times \bar{b}){/tex} So, the area of a parallelogram ABCD = {tex}|\vec{p} \times \vec{q}|=\frac{1}{2}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|{/tex} Now, area of a parallelogram, whose diagonals are {tex}2 i-j+k{/tex} and {tex}i+3 j-\dot{k}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2}|(2 i-j+k) \times(i+3 j-k)|{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {\hat{i}} & {\hat{j}} & {\hat{k}} \\ {2} & {-1} & {1} \\ {1} & {3} & {-1} \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2}|i(1-3)-j(-2-1)+k(6+1)|{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2}|-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}|=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{4+9+49}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{62} \text { sq. units }{/tex} .
solution: Let ABCD be a parallelogram such the {tex}\vec{A B}=\vec{p}, \vec{A D}=\vec{q} \Rightarrow \vec{B C}=\vec{q}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{A C}=\vec{A B}+\vec{B C}=\vec{p}+\vec{q}=\vec{a}{/tex} .....(i) [By triangle law of vectors] And {tex}\vec{B D}=\vec{B A}+\vec{A D}=-\vec{p}+\vec{q}=\vec{b}{/tex} .....(ii) Adding (i) and (ii), we get {tex}\bar{a}+\bar{b}=2 \bar{q}{/tex} Subtracting (ii) from Eq. (i), we get {tex}\vec{a}-\vec{b}=2 \vec{p}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{p}=\frac{1}{2}(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex} Now, {tex}\vec{p} \times \vec{q}=\frac{1}{4}(\vec{a}-\vec{b}) \times(\vec{a}+\vec{b}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4}(\bar{a} \times \bar{a}+\bar{a} \times \bar{b}-\vec{b} \times \bar{a}-\bar{b} \times \bar{b}){/tex} So, the area of a parallelogram ABCD = {tex}|\vec{p} \times \vec{q}|=\frac{1}{2}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|{/tex} Now, area of a parallelogram, whose diagonals are {tex}2 i-j+k{/tex} and {tex}i+3 j-\dot{k}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2}|(2 i-j+k) \times(i+3 j-k)|{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {\hat{i}} & {\hat{j}} & {\hat{k}} \\ {2} & {-1} & {1} \\ {1} & {3} & {-1} \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2}|i(1-3)-j(-2-1)+k(6+1)|{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2}|-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}|=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{4+9+49}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{62} \text { sq. units }{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let ABCD be a parallelogram such the 

{tex}\vec{A B}=\vec{p}, \vec{A D}=\vec{q} \Rightarrow \vec{B C}=\vec{q}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{A C}=\vec{A B}+\vec{B C}=\vec{p}+\vec{q}=\vec{a}{/tex} .....(i) [By triangle law of vectors]
And {tex}\vec{B D}=\vec{B A}+\vec{A D}=-\vec{p}+\vec{q}=\vec{b}{/tex}  .....(ii)
Adding (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}\bar{a}+\bar{b}=2 \bar{q}{/tex}
Subtracting (ii) from Eq. (i), we get
{tex}\vec{a}-\vec{b}=2 \vec{p}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{p}=\frac{1}{2}(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex}
Now, {tex}\vec{p} \times \vec{q}=\frac{1}{4}(\vec{a}-\vec{b}) \times(\vec{a}+\vec{b}){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{4}(\bar{a} \times \bar{a}+\bar{a} \times \bar{b}-\vec{b} \times \bar{a}-\bar{b} \times \bar{b}){/tex}
So, the area of a parallelogram ABCD = {tex}|\vec{p} \times \vec{q}|=\frac{1}{2}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|{/tex}
Now, area of a parallelogram, whose diagonals are {tex}2 i-j+k{/tex} and {tex}i+3 j-\dot{k}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}|(2 i-j+k) \times(i+3 j-k)|{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {\hat{i}} & {\hat{j}} & {\hat{k}} \\ {2} & {-1} & {1} \\ {1} & {3} & {-1} \end{array}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}|i(1-3)-j(-2-1)+k(6+1)|{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}|-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}|=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{4+9+49}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{62} \text { sq. units }{/tex}.

question_html: During a cricket match, the position of the bowler, the wicket keeper and the leg slip fielder are in a line given by {tex}\vec{B}=2 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}{/tex}, {tex}\vec{{W}}=6 \hat{{i}}+12 \hat{{j}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{{F}}=12 \hat{{i}}+18 \hat{{j}}{/tex} respectively. Calculate the ratio in which the wicketkeeper divides the line segment joining the bowler and the leg slip fielder.
question_text: During a cricket match, the position of the bowler, the wicket keeper and the leg slip fielder are in a line given by {tex}\vec{B}=2 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}{/tex} , {tex}\vec{{W}}=6 \hat{{i}}+12 \hat{{j}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{{F}}=12 \hat{{i}}+18 \hat{{j}}{/tex} respectively. Calculate the ratio in which the wicketkeeper divides the line segment joining the bowler and the leg slip fielder.
explanation: Let the wicket keeper divides the line segment in ratio {tex}k: 1{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{W}=\frac{k \vec{F}+1 . \vec{B}}{k+1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 6 \hat{i}+12 \hat{j}=\left(\frac{12 k+2}{k+1}\right) \hat{i}+\left(\frac{18 k+8}{k+1}\right) \hat{j}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow k=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} Hence, the required ratio is {tex}2: 3{/tex}
solution: Let the wicket keeper divides the line segment in ratio {tex}k: 1{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{W}=\frac{k \vec{F}+1 . \vec{B}}{k+1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 6 \hat{i}+12 \hat{j}=\left(\frac{12 k+2}{k+1}\right) \hat{i}+\left(\frac{18 k+8}{k+1}\right) \hat{j}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow k=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} Hence, the required ratio is {tex}2: 3{/tex}
solution_html:
Let the wicket keeper divides the line segment in ratio {tex}k: 1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{W}=\frac{k \vec{F}+1 . \vec{B}}{k+1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 6 \hat{i}+12 \hat{j}=\left(\frac{12 k+2}{k+1}\right) \hat{i}+\left(\frac{18 k+8}{k+1}\right) \hat{j}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow k=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}

Hence, the required ratio is {tex}2: 3{/tex}

question_html: Find a vector of magnitude 21 units in the direction opposite to that of {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}{/tex} where {tex}A{/tex} and {tex}B{/tex} are the points {tex}A(2,1,3){/tex} and {tex}B(8,-1,0){/tex} respectively.
question_text: Find a vector of magnitude 21 units in the direction opposite to that of {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}{/tex} where {tex}A{/tex} and {tex}B{/tex} are the points {tex}A(2,1,3){/tex} and {tex}B(8,-1,0){/tex} respectively.
explanation: {tex}\overrightarrow{B A}=-6 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} Required unit vector of magnitude 21 {tex}=21 \times\left(\frac{-6 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{36+4+9}}\right){/tex} {tex}=3(-6 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} or {tex}-18 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+9 \hat{k}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\overrightarrow{B A}=-6 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} Required unit vector of magnitude 21 {tex}=21 \times\left(\frac{-6 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{36+4+9}}\right){/tex} {tex}=3(-6 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} or {tex}-18 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+9 \hat{k}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\overrightarrow{B A}=-6 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex}
Required unit vector of magnitude 21
{tex}=21 \times\left(\frac{-6 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{36+4+9}}\right){/tex}

question_html: Two friends while flying kites from different locations, find the strings of their kites crossing each other. The strings can be represented by vectors {tex}\vec{a}=3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex}. Determine the angle formed between the kite strings. Assume there is no slack in the strings.
question_text: Two friends while flying kites from different locations, find the strings of their kites crossing each other. The strings can be represented by vectors {tex}\vec{a}=3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} . Determine the angle formed between the kite strings. Assume there is no slack in the strings.
explanation: Let the required angle between the kite strings be {tex}\theta{/tex} . {tex} \text { Then, } \cos \theta=\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{(3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})(2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{9+1+4} \sqrt{4+4+16}}=\frac{12}{\sqrt{336}}=\frac{3}{\sqrt{21}} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{12}{\sqrt{336}}\right) \text { or } \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{\sqrt{21}}\right) {/tex}
solution: Let the required angle between the kite strings be {tex}\theta{/tex} . {tex} \text { Then, } \cos \theta=\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{(3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})(2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{9+1+4} \sqrt{4+4+16}}=\frac{12}{\sqrt{336}}=\frac{3}{\sqrt{21}} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{12}{\sqrt{336}}\right) \text { or } \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{\sqrt{21}}\right) {/tex}
solution_html:
Let the required angle between the kite strings be {tex}\theta{/tex}.
{tex} \text { Then, } \cos \theta=\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{(3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})(2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{9+1+4} \sqrt{4+4+16}}=\frac{12}{\sqrt{336}}=\frac{3}{\sqrt{21}} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{12}{\sqrt{336}}\right) \text { or } \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{\sqrt{21}}\right) {/tex}

question_text: The diagonals of a parallelogram are given by {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{{b}}=\hat{{i}}+3 \hat{{j}}-\hat{{k}}{/tex} . Find the area of the parallelogram.
explanation: {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & -1 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 & -1 \end{array}\right|=-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex} Area of parallelogram {tex}=\frac{1}{2}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{(-2)^{2}+3^{2}+7^{2}}=\frac{\sqrt{62}}{2}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & -1 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 & -1 \end{array}\right|=-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex} Area of parallelogram {tex}=\frac{1}{2}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{(-2)^{2}+3^{2}+7^{2}}=\frac{\sqrt{62}}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & -1 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 & -1 \end{array}\right|=-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex}
Area of parallelogram {tex}=\frac{1}{2}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{(-2)^{2}+3^{2}+7^{2}}=\frac{\sqrt{62}}{2}{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} are unit vectors inclined with each other at an angle {tex}\theta{/tex}, then prove that {tex}\frac{1}{2}|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|=\sin \frac{\theta}{2}{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} are unit vectors inclined with each other at an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} , then prove that {tex}\frac{1}{2}|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|=\sin \frac{\theta}{2}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}|\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|=1{/tex} {tex}|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|^{2} =|\vec{a}|^{2}+|\vec{b}|^{2}-2 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} {tex}=1+1-2|\vec{a}||\vec{b}| \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}=2-2 \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}=2\left(2 \sin ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}\right)=4 \sin ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sin \frac{\theta}{2}=\frac{1}{2}|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|{/tex}
solution: {tex}|\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|=1{/tex} {tex}|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|^{2} =|\vec{a}|^{2}+|\vec{b}|^{2}-2 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} {tex}=1+1-2|\vec{a}||\vec{b}| \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}=2-2 \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}=2\left(2 \sin ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}\right)=4 \sin ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sin \frac{\theta}{2}=\frac{1}{2}|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}|\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|=1{/tex}
{tex}|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|^{2} =|\vec{a}|^{2}+|\vec{b}|^{2}-2 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex}
{tex}=1+1-2|\vec{a}||\vec{b}| \cos \theta{/tex}
{tex}=2-2 \cos \theta{/tex}
{tex}=2\left(2 \sin ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}\right)=4 \sin ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sin \frac{\theta}{2}=\frac{1}{2}|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|{/tex}

question_text: A man needs to hang two lanterns on a straight wire whose end points have coordinates {tex}{A}(4,1,-2){/tex} and {tex}{B}(6,2,-3){/tex} . Find the coordinates of the points where he hangs the lanterns such that these points trisect the wire AB.
explanation: Let {tex}P{/tex} and {tex}Q{/tex} trisect the wire {tex}A B{/tex} . {tex}P{/tex} divides {tex}A B{/tex} in the ratio 1:2 then, coordinate of point {tex}P=\left(\frac{14}{3}, \frac{4}{3},-\frac{7}{3}\right){/tex} {tex}Q{/tex} divides {tex}A B{/tex} in the ratio 2:1 then, coordinate of point {tex}Q=\left(\frac{16}{3}, \frac{5}{3},-\frac{8}{3}\right){/tex}
solution: Let {tex}P{/tex} and {tex}Q{/tex} trisect the wire {tex}A B{/tex} . {tex}P{/tex} divides {tex}A B{/tex} in the ratio 1:2 then, coordinate of point {tex}P=\left(\frac{14}{3}, \frac{4}{3},-\frac{7}{3}\right){/tex} {tex}Q{/tex} divides {tex}A B{/tex} in the ratio 2:1 then, coordinate of point {tex}Q=\left(\frac{16}{3}, \frac{5}{3},-\frac{8}{3}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}P{/tex} and {tex}Q{/tex} trisect the wire {tex}A B{/tex}.
{tex}P{/tex} divides {tex}A B{/tex} in the ratio 1:2 then, coordinate of point {tex}P=\left(\frac{14}{3}, \frac{4}{3},-\frac{7}{3}\right){/tex}
{tex}Q{/tex} divides {tex}A B{/tex} in the ratio 2:1 then, coordinate of point {tex}Q=\left(\frac{16}{3}, \frac{5}{3},-\frac{8}{3}\right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Let {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}{/tex} be three vectors such that {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\vec{a} \times \vec{c}, \vec{a} \neq 0{/tex}. Show that {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{c}{/tex}.
question_text: Let {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}{/tex} be three vectors such that {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\vec{a} \times \vec{c}, \vec{a} \neq 0{/tex} . Show that {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{c}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c} \Rightarrow \vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b}-\vec{c})=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} either {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{c}{/tex} or {tex}\vec{a} \perp(\vec{b}-\vec{c}){/tex} , since {tex}\vec{a} \neq 0{/tex} Also, {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\vec{a} \times \vec{c} \Rightarrow \vec{a} \times(\vec{b}-\vec{c})=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} either {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{c}{/tex} or {tex}\vec{a} \|(\vec{b}-\vec{c}){/tex} , since {tex}\vec{a} \neq 0{/tex} Since vectors {tex}\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{a}}{/tex} and {tex}(\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{b}}-\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{c}}){/tex} cannot be {tex}\|{/tex} and {tex}\perp{/tex} simultaneously Hence {tex}\vec{\boldsymbol{b}}=\vec{\boldsymbol{c}}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c} \Rightarrow \vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b}-\vec{c})=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} either {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{c}{/tex} or {tex}\vec{a} \perp(\vec{b}-\vec{c}){/tex} , since {tex}\vec{a} \neq 0{/tex} Also, {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\vec{a} \times \vec{c} \Rightarrow \vec{a} \times(\vec{b}-\vec{c})=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} either {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{c}{/tex} or {tex}\vec{a} \|(\vec{b}-\vec{c}){/tex} , since {tex}\vec{a} \neq 0{/tex} Since vectors {tex}\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{a}}{/tex} and {tex}(\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{b}}-\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{c}}){/tex} cannot be {tex}\|{/tex} and {tex}\perp{/tex} simultaneously Hence {tex}\vec{\boldsymbol{b}}=\vec{\boldsymbol{c}}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c} \Rightarrow \vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b}-\vec{c})=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} either {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{c}{/tex} or {tex}\vec{a} \perp(\vec{b}-\vec{c}){/tex}, since {tex}\vec{a} \neq 0{/tex}
Also, {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\vec{a} \times \vec{c} \Rightarrow \vec{a} \times(\vec{b}-\vec{c})=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} either {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{c}{/tex} or {tex}\vec{a} \|(\vec{b}-\vec{c}){/tex}, since {tex}\vec{a} \neq 0{/tex}
Since vectors {tex}\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{a}}{/tex} and {tex}(\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{b}}-\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{c}}){/tex} cannot be {tex}\|{/tex} and {tex}\perp{/tex} simultaneously
Hence {tex}\vec{\boldsymbol{b}}=\vec{\boldsymbol{c}}{/tex}

question_html: Find a vector of magnitude 5 which is perpendicular to both the vectors {tex}3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex}.
question_text: Find a vector of magnitude 5 which is perpendicular to both the vectors {tex}3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex} .
explanation: Let {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}=3 \hat{{\imath}}-2 \hat{{\jmath}}+\hat{{k}}, \overrightarrow{{b}}=4 \hat{{\imath}}+3 \hat{{\jmath}}-2 \hat{{k}}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{{\imath}} & \hat{{\jmath}} & \hat{{k}} \\ 3 & -2 & 1 \\ 4 & 3 & -2\end{array}\right|=\hat{{\imath}}+10 \hat{{\jmath}}+17 \hat{{k}}{/tex} {tex}|\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}|=\sqrt{{1}^{{2}}+{1 0}^{{2}}+{1 7}^{{2}}}=\sqrt{{3 9 0}}{/tex} Unit vector {tex}\widehat{{n}}=\frac{\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}}{|\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}|}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{{3 9 0}}}(\hat{{i}}+10 \hat{{j}}+17 \hat{{k}}){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required vector {tex}=\frac{5}{\sqrt{390}}(\hat{{i}}+{1 0} \hat{{j}}+{1 7} \hat{{k}}){/tex}
solution: Let {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}=3 \hat{{\imath}}-2 \hat{{\jmath}}+\hat{{k}}, \overrightarrow{{b}}=4 \hat{{\imath}}+3 \hat{{\jmath}}-2 \hat{{k}}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{{\imath}} & \hat{{\jmath}} & \hat{{k}} \\ 3 & -2 & 1 \\ 4 & 3 & -2\end{array}\right|=\hat{{\imath}}+10 \hat{{\jmath}}+17 \hat{{k}}{/tex} {tex}|\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}|=\sqrt{{1}^{{2}}+{1 0}^{{2}}+{1 7}^{{2}}}=\sqrt{{3 9 0}}{/tex} Unit vector {tex}\widehat{{n}}=\frac{\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}}{|\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}|}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{{3 9 0}}}(\hat{{i}}+10 \hat{{j}}+17 \hat{{k}}){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required vector {tex}=\frac{5}{\sqrt{390}}(\hat{{i}}+{1 0} \hat{{j}}+{1 7} \hat{{k}}){/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}=3 \hat{{\imath}}-2 \hat{{\jmath}}+\hat{{k}}, \overrightarrow{{b}}=4 \hat{{\imath}}+3 \hat{{\jmath}}-2 \hat{{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{{\imath}} & \hat{{\jmath}} & \hat{{k}} \\ 3 & -2 & 1 \\ 4 & 3 & -2\end{array}\right|=\hat{{\imath}}+10 \hat{{\jmath}}+17 \hat{{k}}{/tex}
{tex}|\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}|=\sqrt{{1}^{{2}}+{1 0}^{{2}}+{1 7}^{{2}}}=\sqrt{{3 9 0}}{/tex}
Unit vector {tex}\widehat{{n}}=\frac{\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}}{|\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}|}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{{3 9 0}}}(\hat{{i}}+10 \hat{{j}}+17 \hat{{k}}){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required vector {tex}=\frac{5}{\sqrt{390}}(\hat{{i}}+{1 0} \hat{{j}}+{1 7} \hat{{k}}){/tex}

question_html: If {tex}\hat{a}, \hat{b}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{c}{/tex} are unit vectors such that {tex}\hat{a} \cdot \hat{b}=\hat{a} \cdot \hat{c}=0{/tex} and the angle between {tex}\hat{b}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{c}{/tex} is {tex}\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}, then prove that {tex}\hat{a}= \pm 2(\hat{b} \times \hat{c}){/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}\hat{a}, \hat{b}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{c}{/tex} are unit vectors such that {tex}\hat{a} \cdot \hat{b}=\hat{a} \cdot \hat{c}=0{/tex} and the angle between {tex}\hat{b}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{c}{/tex} is {tex}\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} , then prove that {tex}\hat{a}= \pm 2(\hat{b} \times \hat{c}){/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\hat{a} \cdot \hat{b}=0 \Rightarrow \hat{a} \perp \hat{b}, \hat{a} \cdot \hat{c}=0 \Rightarrow \hat{a} \perp \hat{c}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \hat{a}{/tex} is perpendicular to both {tex}\hat{b}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{c} \Rightarrow \hat{a} \|(\hat{b} \times \hat{c}){/tex} Let {tex}\hat{a}=\lambda(\hat{b} \times \hat{c}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\hat{a}|=|\lambda||(\hat{b} \times \hat{c})| \Rightarrow|\hat{a}|=|\lambda||\hat{b}||\hat{c}| \sin \frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\lambda|=2 \Rightarrow \lambda= \pm 2{/tex} {tex}\therefore \hat{a}= \pm 2(\hat{b} \times \hat{c}){/tex}
solution: {tex}\hat{a} \cdot \hat{b}=0 \Rightarrow \hat{a} \perp \hat{b}, \hat{a} \cdot \hat{c}=0 \Rightarrow \hat{a} \perp \hat{c}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \hat{a}{/tex} is perpendicular to both {tex}\hat{b}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{c} \Rightarrow \hat{a} \|(\hat{b} \times \hat{c}){/tex} Let {tex}\hat{a}=\lambda(\hat{b} \times \hat{c}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\hat{a}|=|\lambda||(\hat{b} \times \hat{c})| \Rightarrow|\hat{a}|=|\lambda||\hat{b}||\hat{c}| \sin \frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\lambda|=2 \Rightarrow \lambda= \pm 2{/tex} {tex}\therefore \hat{a}= \pm 2(\hat{b} \times \hat{c}){/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\hat{a} \cdot \hat{b}=0 \Rightarrow \hat{a} \perp \hat{b}, \hat{a} \cdot \hat{c}=0 \Rightarrow \hat{a} \perp \hat{c}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \hat{a}{/tex} is perpendicular to both {tex}\hat{b}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{c} \Rightarrow \hat{a} \|(\hat{b} \times \hat{c}){/tex}
Let {tex}\hat{a}=\lambda(\hat{b} \times \hat{c}){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|\hat{a}|=|\lambda||(\hat{b} \times \hat{c})| \Rightarrow|\hat{a}|=|\lambda||\hat{b}||\hat{c}| \sin \frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|\lambda|=2 \Rightarrow \lambda= \pm 2{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \hat{a}= \pm 2(\hat{b} \times \hat{c}){/tex}

question_html: {tex}\vec{{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{{b}}{/tex} are two co-initial vectors forming the adjacent sides of a parallelogram such that {tex}|\vec{a}|=10,|\vec{b}|=2{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=12{/tex}. Find the area of the parallelogram.
question_text: {tex}\vec{{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{{b}}{/tex} are two co-initial vectors forming the adjacent sides of a parallelogram such that {tex}|\vec{a}|=10,|\vec{b}|=2{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=12{/tex} . Find the area of the parallelogram.
explanation: Let {tex}\theta{/tex} is the angle between {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} . {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=12 \Rightarrow|\vec{a}||\vec{b}| \cos \theta=12{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(10)(2) \cos \theta=12 \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{3}{5}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \sin \theta=\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)^{2}}=\frac{4}{5}{/tex} Now, area of parallelogram {tex}=|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=|\vec{a}||\vec{b}| \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}=(10)(2)\left(\frac{4}{5}\right)=16{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} area of parallelogram {tex}=16{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}\theta{/tex} is the angle between {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} . {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=12 \Rightarrow|\vec{a}||\vec{b}| \cos \theta=12{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(10)(2) \cos \theta=12 \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{3}{5}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \sin \theta=\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)^{2}}=\frac{4}{5}{/tex} Now, area of parallelogram {tex}=|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=|\vec{a}||\vec{b}| \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}=(10)(2)\left(\frac{4}{5}\right)=16{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} area of parallelogram {tex}=16{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\theta{/tex} is the angle between {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex}.
{tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=12 \Rightarrow|\vec{a}||\vec{b}| \cos \theta=12{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(10)(2) \cos \theta=12 \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{3}{5}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \sin \theta=\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)^{2}}=\frac{4}{5}{/tex}
Now, area of parallelogram {tex}=|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=|\vec{a}||\vec{b}| \sin \theta{/tex}
{tex}=(10)(2)\left(\frac{4}{5}\right)=16{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} area of parallelogram {tex}=16{/tex}

question_text: The scalar product of the vector {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} with a unit vector along sum of vectors {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}=\lambda \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} is equal to 1. Find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} .
explanation: Let {tex}\vec{d}=\vec{b}+\vec{c}=(2+\lambda) \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\hat{{d}}=\frac{(2+\lambda) \hat{{i}}-6 \hat{{j}}+2 \hat{{k}}}{\sqrt{(2+\lambda)^{2}+40}}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}} \cdot \hat{{d}}=(\hat{{i}}-\hat{{j}}+2 \hat{{k}}) \cdot \frac{(2+\lambda) \hat{{i}}-6 \hat{{j}}+2 \hat{{k}}}{\sqrt{(2+\lambda)^{2}+40}}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(2+\lambda)+6+4=\sqrt{(2+\lambda)^{2}+40} \Rightarrow \lambda=-5{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} are two non-zero vectors such that {tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \perp \vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}(2 \vec{a}+\vec{b}) \perp \vec{b}{/tex}, then prove that {tex}|\vec{b}|=\sqrt{2}|\vec{a}|{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} are two non-zero vectors such that {tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \perp \vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}(2 \vec{a}+\vec{b}) \perp \vec{b}{/tex} , then prove that {tex}|\vec{b}|=\sqrt{2}|\vec{a}|{/tex} .
explanation: {tex} (\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \cdot \vec{a}=0 \Rightarrow|\vec{a}|^2+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{a}=0 {/tex} ...(1) {tex} (2 \vec{a}+\vec{b}) \cdot \vec{b}=0 \Rightarrow 2 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+|\vec{b}|^2=0 {/tex} ...(2) {tex} 2\left(-|\vec{a}|^2\right)+|\vec{b}|^2{/tex} = 0 {Using (1) and (2)} {tex} |\vec{b}|^2=2|\vec{a}|^2 \Rightarrow|\vec{b}|=\sqrt{2}|\vec{a}|{/tex}
solution: {tex} (\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \cdot \vec{a}=0 \Rightarrow|\vec{a}|^2+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{a}=0 {/tex} ...(1) {tex} (2 \vec{a}+\vec{b}) \cdot \vec{b}=0 \Rightarrow 2 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+|\vec{b}|^2=0 {/tex} ...(2) {tex} 2\left(-|\vec{a}|^2\right)+|\vec{b}|^2{/tex} = 0 {Using (1) and (2)} {tex} |\vec{b}|^2=2|\vec{a}|^2 \Rightarrow|\vec{b}|=\sqrt{2}|\vec{a}|{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} (\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \cdot \vec{a}=0 \Rightarrow|\vec{a}|^2+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{a}=0 {/tex} ...(1)
{tex} (2 \vec{a}+\vec{b}) \cdot \vec{b}=0 \Rightarrow 2 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+|\vec{b}|^2=0 {/tex} ...(2)
{tex} 2\left(-|\vec{a}|^2\right)+|\vec{b}|^2{/tex} = 0 {Using (1) and (2)}
{tex} |\vec{b}|^2=2|\vec{a}|^2 \Rightarrow|\vec{b}|=\sqrt{2}|\vec{a}|{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: In the given figure, ABCD is a parallelogram. If {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}=2 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{D B}=3 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex}, then find {tex}\overrightarrow{A D}{/tex} and hence find the area of parallelogram ABCD.
question_text: In the given figure, ABCD is a parallelogram. If {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}=2 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{D B}=3 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} , then find {tex}\overrightarrow{A D}{/tex} and hence find the area of parallelogram ABCD.
explanation: {tex} \overrightarrow{A D}+\overrightarrow{D B}=\overrightarrow{A B} {/tex} {tex} \overrightarrow{A D}=(2 \widehat{\imath}-4 \widehat{\jmath}+5 \widehat{k})-(3 \widehat{\imath}-6 \widehat{\jmath}+2 \widehat{k}) {/tex} {tex} =-\widehat{\imath}+2 \widehat{\jmath}+3 \widehat{k} {/tex} {tex} \overrightarrow{A D} \times \overrightarrow{A B}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\imath} & \widehat{\jmath} & \widehat{k} \\ -1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & -4 & 5 \end{array}\right|=22 \widehat{\imath}+11 \widehat{\jmath} {/tex} {tex} \text { Area }=|\overrightarrow{A D} \times \overrightarrow{A B}|=|22 \widehat{\imath}+11 \widehat{\jmath}| {/tex} {tex} =\sqrt{605} \text { or } 11 \sqrt{5}{/tex}
solution: {tex} \overrightarrow{A D}+\overrightarrow{D B}=\overrightarrow{A B} {/tex} {tex} \overrightarrow{A D}=(2 \widehat{\imath}-4 \widehat{\jmath}+5 \widehat{k})-(3 \widehat{\imath}-6 \widehat{\jmath}+2 \widehat{k}) {/tex} {tex} =-\widehat{\imath}+2 \widehat{\jmath}+3 \widehat{k} {/tex} {tex} \overrightarrow{A D} \times \overrightarrow{A B}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\imath} & \widehat{\jmath} & \widehat{k} \\ -1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & -4 & 5 \end{array}\right|=22 \widehat{\imath}+11 \widehat{\jmath} {/tex} {tex} \text { Area }=|\overrightarrow{A D} \times \overrightarrow{A B}|=|22 \widehat{\imath}+11 \widehat{\jmath}| {/tex} {tex} =\sqrt{605} \text { or } 11 \sqrt{5}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \overrightarrow{A D}+\overrightarrow{D B}=\overrightarrow{A B} {/tex}
{tex} \overrightarrow{A D}=(2 \widehat{\imath}-4 \widehat{\jmath}+5 \widehat{k})-(3 \widehat{\imath}-6 \widehat{\jmath}+2 \widehat{k}) {/tex}
{tex} =-\widehat{\imath}+2 \widehat{\jmath}+3 \widehat{k} {/tex}
{tex} \overrightarrow{A D} \times \overrightarrow{A B}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\imath} & \widehat{\jmath} & \widehat{k} \\ -1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & -4 & 5 \end{array}\right|=22 \widehat{\imath}+11 \widehat{\jmath} {/tex}
{tex} \text { Area }=|\overrightarrow{A D} \times \overrightarrow{A B}|=|22 \widehat{\imath}+11 \widehat{\jmath}| {/tex}
{tex} =\sqrt{605} \text { or } 11 \sqrt{5}{/tex}

This follows from the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, which states that the dot product of two vectors is less than or equal to the product of their magnitudes. Equality holds only when the vectors are parallel.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: The unit vector perpendicular to both vectors {tex}\hat{i}+\hat{k} \text { and } \hat{i}-\hat{k}{/tex} is:
question_text: The unit vector perpendicular to both vectors {tex}\hat{i}+\hat{k} \text { and } \hat{i}-\hat{k}{/tex} is:

options.explanation: To find a unit vector perpendicular to both {tex}\hat{i}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{i}-\hat{k}{/tex} , we use the cross product: {tex} (\hat{i}+\hat{k}) \times(\hat{i}-\hat{k})=\hat{j}(-1-1)=-2 \hat{j}{/tex} The unit vector is {tex} \frac{-2 \hat{j}}{2}=-\hat{j}{/tex} Thus, the correct answer is {tex}\hat{j}{/tex} .

explanation: To find a unit vector perpendicular to both {tex}\hat{i}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{i}-\hat{k}{/tex} , we use the cross product: {tex} (\hat{i}+\hat{k}) \times(\hat{i}-\hat{k})=\hat{j}(-1-1)=-2 \hat{j}{/tex} The unit vector is {tex} \frac{-2 \hat{j}}{2}=-\hat{j}{/tex} Thus, the correct answer is {tex}\hat{j}{/tex} .

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If vectors {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{~b}}{/tex} and {tex}2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}{/tex} are unit vectors, then find the angle between {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}{/tex}.
question_text: If vectors {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{~b}}{/tex} and {tex}2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}{/tex} are unit vectors, then find the angle between {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex} |\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|=|2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}|=1 {/tex} {tex} (2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b})^2=|2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}|^2 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 4|\vec{a}|^2+12 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+9|\vec{b}|^2=1 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos \theta=-1{/tex} , where {tex}\theta{/tex} is angle between {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} Hence, {tex}\theta=\pi{/tex}
solution: {tex} |\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|=|2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}|=1 {/tex} {tex} (2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b})^2=|2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}|^2 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 4|\vec{a}|^2+12 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+9|\vec{b}|^2=1 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos \theta=-1{/tex} , where {tex}\theta{/tex} is angle between {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} Hence, {tex}\theta=\pi{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} |\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|=|2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}|=1 {/tex}
{tex} (2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b})^2=|2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}|^2 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 4|\vec{a}|^2+12 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+9|\vec{b}|^2=1 {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \cos \theta=-1{/tex}, where {tex}\theta{/tex} is angle between {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\theta=\pi{/tex}

question_html: The position vectors of vertices of {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC are A {tex}(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex}, {tex}B(\hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}) \text { and } C(3 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}){/tex}. Find all the angles of {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC.
question_text: The position vectors of vertices of {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC are A {tex}(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} , {tex}B(\hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}) \text { and } C(3 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}){/tex} . Find all the angles of {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC.
explanation: {tex} \cos A=\frac{\overrightarrow{A B} \cdot \overrightarrow{A C}}{|\overrightarrow{A B}||\overrightarrow{A C}|}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{(-\hat{\imath}-2 \hat{\jmath}-6 \hat{k}) \cdot(\hat{\imath}-3 \hat{\jmath}-5 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{41} \sqrt{35}}=\frac{35}{\sqrt{41} \sqrt{35}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\sqrt{35}}{\sqrt{41}} {/tex} {tex} A=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{35}}{\sqrt{41}}\right) {/tex} {tex} \cos B=\frac{\overrightarrow{B A} \cdot \overrightarrow{B C}}{|\overrightarrow{B A}||\overrightarrow{B C}|}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{(\hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}+6 \hat{k}) \cdot(2 \hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}+\hat{k})}{\sqrt{41} \sqrt{6}}{/tex} {tex}x = {-b \pm \sqrt{b^2-4ac} \over 2a}{/tex} {tex} B=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{6}}{\sqrt{41}}\right) {/tex} {tex} \cos C=\frac{\overrightarrow{C B} \cdot \overrightarrow{C A}}{|\overrightarrow{C B}||\overrightarrow{C A}|}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{(-2 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}-\hat{k}) \cdot(-\hat{\imath}+3 \hat{\jmath}+5 \hat{k})}{|\overrightarrow{C B}||\overrightarrow{C A}|}=0 {/tex} {tex} \cos C=0 \Rightarrow C=\frac{\pi}{2} {/tex}
solution: {tex} \cos A=\frac{\overrightarrow{A B} \cdot \overrightarrow{A C}}{|\overrightarrow{A B}||\overrightarrow{A C}|}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{(-\hat{\imath}-2 \hat{\jmath}-6 \hat{k}) \cdot(\hat{\imath}-3 \hat{\jmath}-5 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{41} \sqrt{35}}=\frac{35}{\sqrt{41} \sqrt{35}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\sqrt{35}}{\sqrt{41}} {/tex} {tex} A=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{35}}{\sqrt{41}}\right) {/tex} {tex} \cos B=\frac{\overrightarrow{B A} \cdot \overrightarrow{B C}}{|\overrightarrow{B A}||\overrightarrow{B C}|}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{(\hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}+6 \hat{k}) \cdot(2 \hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}+\hat{k})}{\sqrt{41} \sqrt{6}}{/tex} {tex}x = {-b \pm \sqrt{b^2-4ac} \over 2a}{/tex} {tex} B=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{6}}{\sqrt{41}}\right) {/tex} {tex} \cos C=\frac{\overrightarrow{C B} \cdot \overrightarrow{C A}}{|\overrightarrow{C B}||\overrightarrow{C A}|}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{(-2 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}-\hat{k}) \cdot(-\hat{\imath}+3 \hat{\jmath}+5 \hat{k})}{|\overrightarrow{C B}||\overrightarrow{C A}|}=0 {/tex} {tex} \cos C=0 \Rightarrow C=\frac{\pi}{2} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \cos A=\frac{\overrightarrow{A B} \cdot \overrightarrow{A C}}{|\overrightarrow{A B}||\overrightarrow{A C}|}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{(-\hat{\imath}-2 \hat{\jmath}-6 \hat{k}) \cdot(\hat{\imath}-3 \hat{\jmath}-5 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{41} \sqrt{35}}=\frac{35}{\sqrt{41} \sqrt{35}}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{\sqrt{35}}{\sqrt{41}} {/tex}
{tex} A=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{35}}{\sqrt{41}}\right) {/tex}
{tex} \cos B=\frac{\overrightarrow{B A} \cdot \overrightarrow{B C}}{|\overrightarrow{B A}||\overrightarrow{B C}|}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{(\hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}+6 \hat{k}) \cdot(2 \hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}+\hat{k})}{\sqrt{41} \sqrt{6}}{/tex}{tex}x = {-b \pm \sqrt{b^2-4ac} \over 2a}{/tex}
{tex} B=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{6}}{\sqrt{41}}\right) {/tex}
{tex} \cos C=\frac{\overrightarrow{C B} \cdot \overrightarrow{C A}}{|\overrightarrow{C B}||\overrightarrow{C A}|}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{(-2 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}-\hat{k}) \cdot(-\hat{\imath}+3 \hat{\jmath}+5 \hat{k})}{|\overrightarrow{C B}||\overrightarrow{C A}|}=0 {/tex}
{tex} \cos C=0 \Rightarrow C=\frac{\pi}{2} {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Given {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}, \vec{b}=3 \hat{i}-\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex}. Find a vector {tex}\vec{d}{/tex} which is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c} \cdot \vec{d}=3{/tex}.
question_text: Given {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}, \vec{b}=3 \hat{i}-\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex} . Find a vector {tex}\vec{d}{/tex} which is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c} \cdot \vec{d}=3{/tex} .
explanation: Since {tex}\overrightarrow{{d}} \perp \overrightarrow{{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{{d}} \perp \overrightarrow{{b}} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{{d}}=\lambda(\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}){/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{d}}=\lambda(\hat{{i}}+5 \hat{{j}}+3 \hat{{k}}){/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{c}} \cdot \overrightarrow{{d}}=3 \Rightarrow 2 \lambda+5 \lambda-6 \lambda=3 \Rightarrow \lambda=3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{{d}}=3 \hat{{i}}+{1 5} \hat{{j}}+{9} \hat{{k}}{/tex}
solution: Since {tex}\overrightarrow{{d}} \perp \overrightarrow{{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{{d}} \perp \overrightarrow{{b}} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{{d}}=\lambda(\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}){/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{d}}=\lambda(\hat{{i}}+5 \hat{{j}}+3 \hat{{k}}){/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{c}} \cdot \overrightarrow{{d}}=3 \Rightarrow 2 \lambda+5 \lambda-6 \lambda=3 \Rightarrow \lambda=3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{{d}}=3 \hat{{i}}+{1 5} \hat{{j}}+{9} \hat{{k}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Since {tex}\overrightarrow{{d}} \perp \overrightarrow{{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{{d}} \perp \overrightarrow{{b}} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{{d}}=\lambda(\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}){/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{{d}}=\lambda(\hat{{i}}+5 \hat{{j}}+3 \hat{{k}}){/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{{c}} \cdot \overrightarrow{{d}}=3 \Rightarrow 2 \lambda+5 \lambda-6 \lambda=3 \Rightarrow \lambda=3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{{d}}=3 \hat{{i}}+{1 5} \hat{{j}}+{9} \hat{{k}}{/tex}

question_html: Find all vectors of magnitude {tex}8 \sqrt{14}{/tex} units that are perpendicular to the vectors {tex}2 \hat{i}-\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex}.
question_text: Find all vectors of magnitude {tex}8 \sqrt{14}{/tex} units that are perpendicular to the vectors {tex}2 \hat{i}-\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} .
explanation: Vector parallel to the required vector is {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}i & j & k \\ 2 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 2 & 3\end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=2 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k},|2 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}|{/tex} {tex}=2 \sqrt{14}{/tex} The required vectors are {tex}= \pm 8 \sqrt{14}\left(\frac{-2 \hat{{i}}-6 \hat{{j}}+4 \hat{{k}}}{2 \sqrt{14}}\right){/tex} {tex}= \pm(8 \hat{{i}}+24 \hat{{j}}-16 \hat{{k}}){/tex}
solution: Vector parallel to the required vector is {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}i & j & k \\ 2 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 2 & 3\end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=2 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k},|2 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}|{/tex} {tex}=2 \sqrt{14}{/tex} The required vectors are {tex}= \pm 8 \sqrt{14}\left(\frac{-2 \hat{{i}}-6 \hat{{j}}+4 \hat{{k}}}{2 \sqrt{14}}\right){/tex} {tex}= \pm(8 \hat{{i}}+24 \hat{{j}}-16 \hat{{k}}){/tex}
solution_html:
Vector parallel to the required vector is {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}i & j & k \\ 2 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 2 & 3\end{array}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=2 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k},|2 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}|{/tex}{tex}=2 \sqrt{14}{/tex}
The required vectors are {tex}= \pm 8 \sqrt{14}\left(\frac{-2 \hat{{i}}-6 \hat{{j}}+4 \hat{{k}}}{2 \sqrt{14}}\right){/tex}
{tex}= \pm(8 \hat{{i}}+24 \hat{{j}}-16 \hat{{k}}){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}, \overrightarrow{{b}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{{c}}{/tex} are three mutually perpendicular unit vectors. If {tex}\theta{/tex} is the angle between {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}{/tex} and ({tex}2 \overrightarrow{{a}}+3 \overrightarrow{{~b}}+6 \overrightarrow{{c}}{/tex}), find the value of {tex}\cos \theta{/tex}.
question_text: {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}, \overrightarrow{{b}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{{c}}{/tex} are three mutually perpendicular unit vectors. If {tex}\theta{/tex} is the angle between {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}{/tex} and ( {tex}2 \overrightarrow{{a}}+3 \overrightarrow{{~b}}+6 \overrightarrow{{c}}{/tex} ), find the value of {tex}\cos \theta{/tex} .
explanation: {tex} \text { Given }|\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|={/tex} {tex}|\vec{c}|=1 \text { and } \vec{a} .\vec{b}={/tex} {tex}\vec{b} .\vec{c}=\vec{c} .\vec{a}=0 {/tex} {tex} \text { Now, }|2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}+6 \vec{c}|^2{/tex} {tex}=4|\vec{a}|^2+9|\vec{b}|^2+36|\vec{c}|^2=49 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow|2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}+6 \vec{c}|=7 {/tex} {tex} \cos \theta=\frac{\vec{a} \cdot(2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}+6 \vec{c})}{|\vec{a}||2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}+6 \vec{c}|}=\frac{2|\vec{a}|^2}{|\vec{a}||2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}+6 \vec{c}|} {/tex} {tex} \therefore \cos \theta=\frac{2}{7} {/tex}
solution: {tex} \text { Given }|\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|={/tex} {tex}|\vec{c}|=1 \text { and } \vec{a} .\vec{b}={/tex} {tex}\vec{b} .\vec{c}=\vec{c} .\vec{a}=0 {/tex} {tex} \text { Now, }|2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}+6 \vec{c}|^2{/tex} {tex}=4|\vec{a}|^2+9|\vec{b}|^2+36|\vec{c}|^2=49 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow|2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}+6 \vec{c}|=7 {/tex} {tex} \cos \theta=\frac{\vec{a} \cdot(2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}+6 \vec{c})}{|\vec{a}||2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}+6 \vec{c}|}=\frac{2|\vec{a}|^2}{|\vec{a}||2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}+6 \vec{c}|} {/tex} {tex} \therefore \cos \theta=\frac{2}{7} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \text { Given }|\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|={/tex}{tex}|\vec{c}|=1 \text { and } \vec{a} .\vec{b}={/tex}{tex}\vec{b} .\vec{c}=\vec{c} .\vec{a}=0 {/tex}
{tex} \text { Now, }|2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}+6 \vec{c}|^2{/tex} {tex}=4|\vec{a}|^2+9|\vec{b}|^2+36|\vec{c}|^2=49 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow|2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}+6 \vec{c}|=7 {/tex}
{tex} \cos \theta=\frac{\vec{a} \cdot(2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}+6 \vec{c})}{|\vec{a}||2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}+6 \vec{c}|}=\frac{2|\vec{a}|^2}{|\vec{a}||2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}+6 \vec{c}|} {/tex}
{tex} \therefore \cos \theta=\frac{2}{7} {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Let {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{{b}}{/tex} be two non-zero vectors.
Prove that {tex}|\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}| \leq|\overrightarrow{{a}}||\overrightarrow{{b}}|{/tex}.
State the condition under which equality holds, i.e., {tex}|\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}|=|\overrightarrow{{a}}||\overrightarrow{{b}}|{/tex}.
question_text: Let {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{{b}}{/tex} be two non-zero vectors. Prove that {tex}|\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}| \leq|\overrightarrow{{a}}||\overrightarrow{{b}}|{/tex} . State the condition under which equality holds, i.e., {tex}|\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}|=|\overrightarrow{{a}}||\overrightarrow{{b}}|{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}|\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}|=\left.\right|^{\overrightarrow{{a}}}| | \overrightarrow{{b}}| | \sin \theta \mid{/tex} {tex}\text { As, } 0 \leq|\sin \theta| \leq 1{/tex} {tex}\left.\Rightarrow\right|^{\overrightarrow{{a}}}| | \overrightarrow{{b}}| | \sin \theta\left|\leq\left.\right|^{\overrightarrow{{a}}}\right||\overrightarrow{{b}}|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}| \leq\left.\right|^{\overrightarrow{{a}}}| | \overrightarrow{{b}} \mid{/tex} For equality, {tex}\sin \theta=1 \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow \vec{a}{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} .
solution: {tex}|\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}|=\left.\right|^{\overrightarrow{{a}}}| | \overrightarrow{{b}}| | \sin \theta \mid{/tex} {tex}\text { As, } 0 \leq|\sin \theta| \leq 1{/tex} {tex}\left.\Rightarrow\right|^{\overrightarrow{{a}}}| | \overrightarrow{{b}}| | \sin \theta\left|\leq\left.\right|^{\overrightarrow{{a}}}\right||\overrightarrow{{b}}|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}| \leq\left.\right|^{\overrightarrow{{a}}}| | \overrightarrow{{b}} \mid{/tex} For equality, {tex}\sin \theta=1 \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow \vec{a}{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} .
solution_html:
{tex}|\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}|=\left.\right|^{\overrightarrow{{a}}}| | \overrightarrow{{b}}| | \sin \theta \mid{/tex}
{tex}\text { As, } 0 \leq|\sin \theta| \leq 1{/tex}
{tex}\left.\Rightarrow\right|^{\overrightarrow{{a}}}| | \overrightarrow{{b}}| | \sin \theta\left|\leq\left.\right|^{\overrightarrow{{a}}}\right||\overrightarrow{{b}}|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}| \leq\left.\right|^{\overrightarrow{{a}}}| | \overrightarrow{{b}} \mid{/tex}
For equality, {tex}\sin \theta=1 \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow \vec{a}{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\vec{b}{/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the position vector of point C which divides the line segment joining points {tex}A{/tex} and {tex}B{/tex} having position vectors {tex}\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}-\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} respectively in the ratio {tex}4: 1{/tex} externally. Further, find {tex}|\overrightarrow{{AB}}|:|\overrightarrow{{BC}}|{/tex}.
question_text: Find the position vector of point C which divides the line segment joining points {tex}A{/tex} and {tex}B{/tex} having position vectors {tex}\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}-\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} respectively in the ratio {tex}4: 1{/tex} externally. Further, find {tex}|\overrightarrow{{AB}}|:|\overrightarrow{{BC}}|{/tex} .
explanation: {tex} \text { Position vector of } C=\vec{r}=\frac{4 \vec{b}-\vec{a}}{3} {/tex} {tex} \text { i.e. } \vec{r}=\frac{1}{3}(-5 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}) {/tex} {tex} \text { Now, } \overrightarrow{A B}=-2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k} \Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{A B}|=3 {/tex} {tex} \overrightarrow{B C}=-\frac{1}{3}(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}) \Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{B C}|=1 {/tex} {tex} |\overrightarrow{A B}|:|\overrightarrow{B C}|=3: 1 {/tex}
solution: {tex} \text { Position vector of } C=\vec{r}=\frac{4 \vec{b}-\vec{a}}{3} {/tex} {tex} \text { i.e. } \vec{r}=\frac{1}{3}(-5 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}) {/tex} {tex} \text { Now, } \overrightarrow{A B}=-2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k} \Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{A B}|=3 {/tex} {tex} \overrightarrow{B C}=-\frac{1}{3}(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}) \Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{B C}|=1 {/tex} {tex} |\overrightarrow{A B}|:|\overrightarrow{B C}|=3: 1 {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \text { Position vector of } C=\vec{r}=\frac{4 \vec{b}-\vec{a}}{3} {/tex}
{tex} \text { i.e. } \vec{r}=\frac{1}{3}(-5 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}) {/tex}
{tex} \text { Now, } \overrightarrow{A B}=-2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k} \Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{A B}|=3 {/tex}
{tex} \overrightarrow{B C}=-\frac{1}{3}(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}) \Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{B C}|=1 {/tex}
{tex} |\overrightarrow{A B}|:|\overrightarrow{B C}|=3: 1 {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
An instructor at the astronomical centre shows three among the brightest stars in a particular constellation. Assume that the telescope is located at {tex}{O}(0,0,0){/tex} and the three stars have their locations at the points {tex}{D}, {A}{/tex} and V having position vectors {tex}2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}, 7 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}-3 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+11 \hat{k}{/tex} respectively.

Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. How far is the star V from star A? (1)
  2. Find a unit vector in the direction of {tex}\overrightarrow{{DA}}{/tex}. (1)
    1. Find the measure of {tex}\angle {VDA}{/tex}. (2)

      OR

    2. What is the projection of vector {tex}\overrightarrow{{DV}}{/tex} on vector {tex}\overrightarrow{{DA}}{/tex} ? (2)
question_text: An instructor at the astronomical centre shows three among the brightest stars in a particular constellation. Assume that the telescope is located at {tex}{O}(0,0,0){/tex} and the three stars have their locations at the points {tex}{D}, {A}{/tex} and V having position vectors {tex}2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}, 7 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}-3 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+11 \hat{k}{/tex} respectively. Based on the above information, answer the following questions: How far is the star V from star A? (1) Find a unit vector in the direction of {tex}\overrightarrow{{DA}}{/tex} . (1) Find the measure of {tex}\angle {VDA}{/tex} . (2) OR What is the projection of vector {tex}\overrightarrow{{DV}}{/tex} on vector {tex}\overrightarrow{{DA}}{/tex} ? (2)
explanation: {tex}\overrightarrow{A V}={/tex} Position Vector of V - Position Vector of A {tex}=-3 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+11 \hat{k}-7 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}=-10 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} Thus, {tex}|\overrightarrow{A V}|=\sqrt{100+4+9}=\sqrt{113}{/tex} units {tex}\overrightarrow{D A}={/tex} Position Vector of {tex}{A}\ -{/tex} Position Vector of D {tex}=7 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}-2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}=5 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} Unit vector in the direction of {tex}\overrightarrow{D A}=\frac{5 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}}{3 \sqrt{5}}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{D V}=-5 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\angle V D A=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\overrightarrow{D V} \cdot \overrightarrow{D A}}{|\overrightarrow{D V}||\overrightarrow{D A}|}\right)=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{11 \sqrt{2}}{90}\right){/tex} OR {tex}\overrightarrow{D V}=-5 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex} Projection of {tex}\overrightarrow{D V}{/tex} on {tex}\overrightarrow{D A}=\left(\frac{\overrightarrow{D V} \cdot \overrightarrow{D A}}{|\overrightarrow{D A}|}\right)=\frac{11 \sqrt{5}}{15}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\overrightarrow{A V}={/tex} Position Vector of V - Position Vector of A {tex}=-3 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+11 \hat{k}-7 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}=-10 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} Thus, {tex}|\overrightarrow{A V}|=\sqrt{100+4+9}=\sqrt{113}{/tex} units {tex}\overrightarrow{D A}={/tex} Position Vector of {tex}{A}\ -{/tex} Position Vector of D {tex}=7 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}-2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}=5 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} Unit vector in the direction of {tex}\overrightarrow{D A}=\frac{5 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}}{3 \sqrt{5}}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{D V}=-5 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\angle V D A=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\overrightarrow{D V} \cdot \overrightarrow{D A}}{|\overrightarrow{D V}||\overrightarrow{D A}|}\right)=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{11 \sqrt{2}}{90}\right){/tex} OR {tex}\overrightarrow{D V}=-5 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex} Projection of {tex}\overrightarrow{D V}{/tex} on {tex}\overrightarrow{D A}=\left(\frac{\overrightarrow{D V} \cdot \overrightarrow{D A}}{|\overrightarrow{D A}|}\right)=\frac{11 \sqrt{5}}{15}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}\overrightarrow{A V}={/tex} Position Vector of V - Position Vector of A
    {tex}=-3 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+11 \hat{k}-7 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}=-10 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex}
    Thus, {tex}|\overrightarrow{A V}|=\sqrt{100+4+9}=\sqrt{113}{/tex} units
  2. {tex}\overrightarrow{D A}={/tex} Position Vector of {tex}{A}\ -{/tex} Position Vector of D
    {tex}=7 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}-2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}=5 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex}
    Unit vector in the direction of {tex}\overrightarrow{D A}=\frac{5 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}}{3 \sqrt{5}}{/tex}
    1. {tex}\overrightarrow{D V}=-5 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex}
      {tex}\angle V D A=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\overrightarrow{D V} \cdot \overrightarrow{D A}}{|\overrightarrow{D V}||\overrightarrow{D A}|}\right)=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{11 \sqrt{2}}{90}\right){/tex}

      OR

    2. {tex}\overrightarrow{D V}=-5 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex}
      Projection of {tex}\overrightarrow{D V}{/tex} on {tex}\overrightarrow{D A}=\left(\frac{\overrightarrow{D V} \cdot \overrightarrow{D A}}{|\overrightarrow{D A}|}\right)=\frac{11 \sqrt{5}}{15}{/tex}

question_html: Show that the vectors {tex}3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}, 2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-8 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}4 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-7 \hat{k}{/tex} form the vertices of a right triangle.
question_text: Show that the vectors {tex}3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}, 2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-8 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}4 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-7 \hat{k}{/tex} form the vertices of a right triangle.
explanation: Let {tex}{A}(3 \hat{{i}}+\hat{{j}}-2 \hat{{k}}), {B}(2 \hat{{i}}-\hat{{j}}-8 \hat{{k}}){/tex} , and {tex}{C}(4 \hat{{i}}-2 \hat{{j}}-7 \hat{{k}}){/tex} , then {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}=-\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k}, {/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{B C}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}, {/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{C A}=-\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}+\overrightarrow{B C}+\overrightarrow{C A}={/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{CA}}=-2-3+5=0 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{{BC}} \perp \overrightarrow{{CA}}, {/tex} {tex}\therefore {ABC}{/tex} is a right triangle.
solution: Let {tex}{A}(3 \hat{{i}}+\hat{{j}}-2 \hat{{k}}), {B}(2 \hat{{i}}-\hat{{j}}-8 \hat{{k}}){/tex} , and {tex}{C}(4 \hat{{i}}-2 \hat{{j}}-7 \hat{{k}}){/tex} , then {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}=-\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k}, {/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{B C}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}, {/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{C A}=-\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}+\overrightarrow{B C}+\overrightarrow{C A}={/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{CA}}=-2-3+5=0 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{{BC}} \perp \overrightarrow{{CA}}, {/tex} {tex}\therefore {ABC}{/tex} is a right triangle.
solution_html: Let {tex}{A}(3 \hat{{i}}+\hat{{j}}-2 \hat{{k}}), {B}(2 \hat{{i}}-\hat{{j}}-8 \hat{{k}}){/tex}, and {tex}{C}(4 \hat{{i}}-2 \hat{{j}}-7 \hat{{k}}){/tex}, then {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}=-\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k}, {/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{B C}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}, {/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{C A}=-\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}+\overrightarrow{B C}+\overrightarrow{C A}={/tex}{tex}\overrightarrow{{CA}}=-2-3+5=0 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{{BC}} \perp \overrightarrow{{CA}}, {/tex} {tex}\therefore {ABC}{/tex} is a right triangle.

question_html: Find a vector of magnitude 4 units perpendicular to each of the vectors {tex}2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} and hence verify your answer.
question_text: Find a vector of magnitude 4 units perpendicular to each of the vectors {tex}2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} and hence verify your answer.
explanation: Let {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}=2 \hat{{i}}-\hat{{j}}+\hat{{k}}, \overrightarrow{{b}}=\hat{{i}}+\hat{{j}}-\hat{{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{{c}}{/tex} be the vector perpendicular to both {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}} \& \overrightarrow{{b}}{/tex} then, {tex}\overrightarrow{{c}}=\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}=\left\|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{{i}} & \hat{{j}} & \hat{{k}} \\ 2 & -1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & -1\end{array}\right\|=3 \hat{{j}}+3 \hat{{k}}{/tex} Let {tex}\overrightarrow{{d}}{/tex} the vector perpendicular to both the vectors {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}} \& \overrightarrow{{b}}{/tex} and having magnitude 4, {tex}\overrightarrow{{d}}=4 \hat{{c}}=2 \sqrt{2} \hat{{j}}+2 \sqrt{2} \hat{{k}}({/tex} or {tex}-2 \sqrt{2} \hat{{j}}-2 \sqrt{2} \hat{{k}}){/tex} Verification: {tex}\|\overrightarrow{{d}}\|=\sqrt{{8 + 8}}={4}, \overrightarrow{{d}} \cdot \overrightarrow{{a}}=\overrightarrow{{d}} \cdot \overrightarrow{{b}}={0} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{{d}} \perp \overrightarrow{{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{{d}} \perp \overrightarrow{{b}}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}=2 \hat{{i}}-\hat{{j}}+\hat{{k}}, \overrightarrow{{b}}=\hat{{i}}+\hat{{j}}-\hat{{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{{c}}{/tex} be the vector perpendicular to both {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}} \& \overrightarrow{{b}}{/tex} then, {tex}\overrightarrow{{c}}=\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}=\left\|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{{i}} & \hat{{j}} & \hat{{k}} \\ 2 & -1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & -1\end{array}\right\|=3 \hat{{j}}+3 \hat{{k}}{/tex} Let {tex}\overrightarrow{{d}}{/tex} the vector perpendicular to both the vectors {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}} \& \overrightarrow{{b}}{/tex} and having magnitude 4, {tex}\overrightarrow{{d}}=4 \hat{{c}}=2 \sqrt{2} \hat{{j}}+2 \sqrt{2} \hat{{k}}({/tex} or {tex}-2 \sqrt{2} \hat{{j}}-2 \sqrt{2} \hat{{k}}){/tex} Verification: {tex}\|\overrightarrow{{d}}\|=\sqrt{{8 + 8}}={4}, \overrightarrow{{d}} \cdot \overrightarrow{{a}}=\overrightarrow{{d}} \cdot \overrightarrow{{b}}={0} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{{d}} \perp \overrightarrow{{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{{d}} \perp \overrightarrow{{b}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}=2 \hat{{i}}-\hat{{j}}+\hat{{k}}, \overrightarrow{{b}}=\hat{{i}}+\hat{{j}}-\hat{{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{{c}}{/tex} be the vector perpendicular to both {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}} \& \overrightarrow{{b}}{/tex}
then, {tex}\overrightarrow{{c}}=\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}=\left\|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{{i}} & \hat{{j}} & \hat{{k}} \\ 2 & -1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & -1\end{array}\right\|=3 \hat{{j}}+3 \hat{{k}}{/tex}
Let {tex}\overrightarrow{{d}}{/tex} the vector perpendicular to both the vectors {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}} \& \overrightarrow{{b}}{/tex} and having magnitude 4, {tex}\overrightarrow{{d}}=4 \hat{{c}}=2 \sqrt{2} \hat{{j}}+2 \sqrt{2} \hat{{k}}({/tex} or {tex}-2 \sqrt{2} \hat{{j}}-2 \sqrt{2} \hat{{k}}){/tex}
Verification: {tex}\|\overrightarrow{{d}}\|=\sqrt{{8 + 8}}={4}, \overrightarrow{{d}} \cdot \overrightarrow{{a}}=\overrightarrow{{d}} \cdot \overrightarrow{{b}}={0} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{{d}} \perp \overrightarrow{{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{{d}} \perp \overrightarrow{{b}}{/tex}

question_html: Find the projection of vector ( {tex}\vec{b}+\vec{c}{/tex} ) on vector {tex}\vec{a}{/tex}, where {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex}, {tex}\overrightarrow{{b}}=\hat{{i}}+3 \hat{{j}}+\hat{{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{{c}}=\hat{{i}}+\hat{{k}}{/tex}.
question_text: Find the projection of vector ( {tex}\vec{b}+\vec{c}{/tex} ) on vector {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} , where {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} , {tex}\overrightarrow{{b}}=\hat{{i}}+3 \hat{{j}}+\hat{{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{{c}}=\hat{{i}}+\hat{{k}}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\vec{b}+\vec{c}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} Projection of {tex}(\vec{b}+\vec{c}){/tex} on {tex}\vec{a}=\frac{(\vec{b}+\vec{c}) \cdot \vec{a}}{\|\vec{a}\|}=\frac{4+6+2}{\sqrt{4+4+1}}{/tex} {tex}=4{/tex}
solution: {tex}\vec{b}+\vec{c}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} Projection of {tex}(\vec{b}+\vec{c}){/tex} on {tex}\vec{a}=\frac{(\vec{b}+\vec{c}) \cdot \vec{a}}{\|\vec{a}\|}=\frac{4+6+2}{\sqrt{4+4+1}}{/tex} {tex}=4{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\vec{b}+\vec{c}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex}
Projection of {tex}(\vec{b}+\vec{c}){/tex} on {tex}\vec{a}=\frac{(\vec{b}+\vec{c}) \cdot \vec{a}}{\|\vec{a}\|}=\frac{4+6+2}{\sqrt{4+4+1}}{/tex}
{tex}=4{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If the vectors {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}{/tex} represent the three sides of a triangle, then show that {tex}\vec{{a}} \times \vec{{b}}=\vec{{b}} \times \vec{{c}}=\vec{{c}} \times \vec{{a}}{/tex}.
question_text: If the vectors {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}{/tex} represent the three sides of a triangle, then show that {tex}\vec{{a}} \times \vec{{b}}=\vec{{b}} \times \vec{{c}}=\vec{{c}} \times \vec{{a}}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex} \vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=\vec{0} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \vec{a} \times(\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c})=\vec{0} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \vec{a} \times \vec{a}+\vec{a} \times \vec{b}+\vec{a} \times \vec{c}=\vec{0} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \vec{a} \times \vec{b}=-\vec{a} \times \vec{c},{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\vec{c} \times \vec{a} {/tex} Similarly, {tex}\vec{{b}} \times \vec{{c}}=\vec{{c}} \times \vec{{a}}{/tex} {tex} \therefore \vec{{a}} \times \vec{{b}}=\vec{{b}} \times \vec{{c}}=\vec{{c}} \times \vec{{a}}{/tex}
solution: {tex} \vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=\vec{0} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \vec{a} \times(\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c})=\vec{0} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \vec{a} \times \vec{a}+\vec{a} \times \vec{b}+\vec{a} \times \vec{c}=\vec{0} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \vec{a} \times \vec{b}=-\vec{a} \times \vec{c},{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\vec{c} \times \vec{a} {/tex} Similarly, {tex}\vec{{b}} \times \vec{{c}}=\vec{{c}} \times \vec{{a}}{/tex} {tex} \therefore \vec{{a}} \times \vec{{b}}=\vec{{b}} \times \vec{{c}}=\vec{{c}} \times \vec{{a}}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=\vec{0} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \vec{a} \times(\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c})=\vec{0} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \vec{a} \times \vec{a}+\vec{a} \times \vec{b}+\vec{a} \times \vec{c}=\vec{0} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \vec{a} \times \vec{b}=-\vec{a} \times \vec{c},{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\vec{c} \times \vec{a} {/tex}
Similarly, {tex}\vec{{b}} \times \vec{{c}}=\vec{{c}} \times \vec{{a}}{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \vec{{a}} \times \vec{{b}}=\vec{{b}} \times \vec{{c}}=\vec{{c}} \times \vec{{a}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}, \overrightarrow{{b}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{{c}}{/tex} are three unit vectors such that {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}+\overrightarrow{{b}}-\overrightarrow{{c}}=\overrightarrow{0}{/tex}, find the angle between vectors {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{{c}}{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}, \overrightarrow{{b}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{{c}}{/tex} are three unit vectors such that {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}+\overrightarrow{{b}}-\overrightarrow{{c}}=\overrightarrow{0}{/tex} , find the angle between vectors {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{{c}}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex} \text { Given }|\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|=|\vec{c}|=1 {/tex} {tex} \text { Now } \vec{a}-\vec{c}=-\vec{b} {/tex} {tex} (\vec{a}-\vec{c}) .(\vec{a}-\vec{c})=(-\vec{b}) .(-\vec{b}) {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow|\vec{a}|^2+|\vec{c}|^2-2 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}=|\vec{b}|^2 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 1+1-2|\vec{a}||\vec{c}| \cos \theta=1 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 2-2(1)(1) \cos \theta=1 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{3} {/tex}
solution: {tex} \text { Given }|\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|=|\vec{c}|=1 {/tex} {tex} \text { Now } \vec{a}-\vec{c}=-\vec{b} {/tex} {tex} (\vec{a}-\vec{c}) .(\vec{a}-\vec{c})=(-\vec{b}) .(-\vec{b}) {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow|\vec{a}|^2+|\vec{c}|^2-2 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}=|\vec{b}|^2 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 1+1-2|\vec{a}||\vec{c}| \cos \theta=1 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 2-2(1)(1) \cos \theta=1 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{3} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \text { Given }|\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|=|\vec{c}|=1 {/tex}
{tex} \text { Now } \vec{a}-\vec{c}=-\vec{b} {/tex}
{tex} (\vec{a}-\vec{c}) .(\vec{a}-\vec{c})=(-\vec{b}) .(-\vec{b}) {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow|\vec{a}|^2+|\vec{c}|^2-2 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}=|\vec{b}|^2 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 1+1-2|\vec{a}||\vec{c}| \cos \theta=1 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 2-2(1)(1) \cos \theta=1 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{3} {/tex}

question_html: If {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } = \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} then find a unit vector perpendicular to both {tex}\vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { b }{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } = \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} then find a unit vector perpendicular to both {tex}\vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { b }{/tex} .
explanation: According to the question, {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } = \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} Let the required unit vector be {tex}\vec { r } = x \hat { i } + y \hat { j } + z \hat { k },{/tex} then, {tex}\sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + z ^ { 2 } } = 1{/tex} squaring on both sides, we get {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + z ^ { 2 } = 1{/tex} ...(i) Now, {tex}\vec { a } + \vec { b } = ( \hat { i } + \hat { j} + \hat { k } ) + (\hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { b } = ( \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } ) - ( \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= - \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k }{/tex} Since, {tex}\vec { r }{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { b },{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \vec { r } \cdot ( \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) = 0 \text { and }{/tex} {tex} \vec { r } \cdot ( \vec { a } - \vec { b } ) = 0{/tex} {tex}( x \hat { i } + y \hat { j } + z \hat { k } ) \cdot ( 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 x + 3 y + 4 z = 0{/tex} , ...(ii) {tex}( x \hat { i } +y \hat { j } + z \hat { k } ) \cdot ( - \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow -y - 2z = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y = - 2z{/tex} On putting the value of y in Eq. (ii), we get {tex}2x + 3 (-2z) + 4z =0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = z On substituting the value of x and y in Eq. (i), we get {tex}z ^ { 2 } + 4 z ^ { 2 } + z ^ { 2 } = 1 \Rightarrow 6 z ^ { 2 } = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad z = \pm \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 6 } }{/tex} then, {tex}x = \pm \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 6 } }{/tex} and {tex}y = \mp \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 6 } }{/tex} Hence, the required vectors are {tex}\frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 6 } } \hat { i } - \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 6 } } \hat { j } + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 6 } } \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\frac { - 1 } { \sqrt { 6 } } \hat { i } + \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 6 } } \hat { j } - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 6 } } \hat { k }.{/tex}
solution: According to the question, {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } = \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} Let the required unit vector be {tex}\vec { r } = x \hat { i } + y \hat { j } + z \hat { k },{/tex} then, {tex}\sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + z ^ { 2 } } = 1{/tex} squaring on both sides, we get {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + z ^ { 2 } = 1{/tex} ...(i) Now, {tex}\vec { a } + \vec { b } = ( \hat { i } + \hat { j} + \hat { k } ) + (\hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { b } = ( \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } ) - ( \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= - \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k }{/tex} Since, {tex}\vec { r }{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { b },{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \vec { r } \cdot ( \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) = 0 \text { and }{/tex} {tex} \vec { r } \cdot ( \vec { a } - \vec { b } ) = 0{/tex} {tex}( x \hat { i } + y \hat { j } + z \hat { k } ) \cdot ( 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 x + 3 y + 4 z = 0{/tex} , ...(ii) {tex}( x \hat { i } +y \hat { j } + z \hat { k } ) \cdot ( - \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow -y - 2z = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y = - 2z{/tex} On putting the value of y in Eq. (ii), we get {tex}2x + 3 (-2z) + 4z =0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = z On substituting the value of x and y in Eq. (i), we get {tex}z ^ { 2 } + 4 z ^ { 2 } + z ^ { 2 } = 1 \Rightarrow 6 z ^ { 2 } = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad z = \pm \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 6 } }{/tex} then, {tex}x = \pm \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 6 } }{/tex} and {tex}y = \mp \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 6 } }{/tex} Hence, the required vectors are {tex}\frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 6 } } \hat { i } - \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 6 } } \hat { j } + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 6 } } \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\frac { - 1 } { \sqrt { 6 } } \hat { i } + \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 6 } } \hat { j } - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 6 } } \hat { k }.{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question,
{tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } = \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex}
Let the required unit vector be
{tex}\vec { r } = x \hat { i } + y \hat { j } + z \hat { k },{/tex}
then, {tex}\sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + z ^ { 2 } } = 1{/tex}
squaring on both sides, we get 
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + z ^ { 2 } = 1{/tex} ...(i)
Now, {tex}\vec { a } + \vec { b } = ( \hat { i } + \hat { j} + \hat { k } ) + (\hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ){/tex}
{tex}\vec { a } - \vec { b } = ( \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } ) - ( \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ){/tex}{tex}= - \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k }{/tex}
Since, {tex}\vec { r }{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { b },{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \vec { r } \cdot ( \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) = 0 \text { and }{/tex}{tex} \vec { r } \cdot ( \vec { a } - \vec { b } ) = 0{/tex}
{tex}( x \hat { i } + y \hat { j } + z \hat { k } ) \cdot ( 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } ) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 x + 3 y + 4 z = 0{/tex}, ...(ii)
{tex}( x \hat { i } +y \hat { j } + z \hat { k } ) \cdot ( - \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } ) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow -y - 2z = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow y = - 2z{/tex} 
On putting the value of y in Eq. (ii), we get
{tex}2x + 3 (-2z) + 4z  =0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = z
On substituting the value of x and y in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}z ^ { 2 } + 4 z ^ { 2 } + z ^ { 2 } = 1 \Rightarrow 6 z ^ { 2 } = 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad z = \pm \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 6 } }{/tex}
then, {tex}x = \pm \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 6 } }{/tex} and {tex}y = \mp \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 6 } }{/tex}
Hence, the required vectors are
{tex}\frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 6 } } \hat { i } - \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 6 } } \hat { j } + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 6 } } \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\frac { - 1 } { \sqrt { 6 } } \hat { i } + \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 6 } } \hat { j } - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 6 } } \hat { k }.{/tex}

question_html: If vectors {tex} \vec { a }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b }{/tex} are such that {tex} | \vec { a } | = 3,{/tex} {tex} | \vec { b } | = \frac 2 3{/tex} and {tex} \vec { a } \times \vec { b }{/tex} is a unit vector, then write the angle between {tex} \vec { a }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b }.{/tex}
question_text: If vectors {tex} \vec { a }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b }{/tex} are such that {tex} | \vec { a } | = 3,{/tex} {tex} | \vec { b } | = \frac 2 3{/tex} and {tex} \vec { a } \times \vec { b }{/tex} is a unit vector, then write the angle between {tex} \vec { a }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b }.{/tex}
explanation: We have to find the angle between {tex} \vec { a }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b }.{/tex} Given, {tex} | \vec { a } | = 3{/tex} and {tex} | \vec { b } | = 2 / 3{/tex} Let {tex} \theta{/tex} be the angle between {tex} \vec { a }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b }.{/tex} Also, given {tex} | \vec { a } \times \vec { b } | = 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad | \vec { a } | | \vec { b } | \sin \theta = 1 \Rightarrow 3 \times \frac { 2 } { 3 } \sin \theta = 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 \sin \theta = 1 \Rightarrow \sin \theta = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Rightarrow \theta = \frac { \pi } { 6 }{/tex}
solution: We have to find the angle between {tex} \vec { a }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b }.{/tex} Given, {tex} | \vec { a } | = 3{/tex} and {tex} | \vec { b } | = 2 / 3{/tex} Let {tex} \theta{/tex} be the angle between {tex} \vec { a }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b }.{/tex} Also, given {tex} | \vec { a } \times \vec { b } | = 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad | \vec { a } | | \vec { b } | \sin \theta = 1 \Rightarrow 3 \times \frac { 2 } { 3 } \sin \theta = 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 \sin \theta = 1 \Rightarrow \sin \theta = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Rightarrow \theta = \frac { \pi } { 6 }{/tex}
solution_html:
We have to find the angle between {tex} \vec { a }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b }.{/tex}Given, {tex} | \vec { a } | = 3{/tex}and {tex} | \vec { b } | = 2 / 3{/tex}
Let {tex} \theta{/tex} be the angle between {tex} \vec { a }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b }.{/tex}
Also, given {tex} | \vec { a } \times \vec { b } | = 1{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad | \vec { a } | | \vec { b } | \sin \theta = 1 \Rightarrow 3 \times \frac { 2 } { 3 } \sin \theta = 1{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 \sin \theta = 1 \Rightarrow \sin \theta = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Rightarrow \theta = \frac { \pi } { 6 }{/tex}
