question_html: Find a unit vector perpendicular to each one of the vectors {tex}\vec{a}=(4 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=(2 \hat{i}-2\hat{j}+ \hat{k}){/tex}.
question_text: Find a unit vector perpendicular to each one of the vectors {tex}\vec{a}=(4 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=(2 \hat{i}-2\hat{j}+ \hat{k}){/tex} .
explanation: We know that {tex}(\vec{a} \times \vec{b}){/tex} is a vector perpendicular to each one of {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} . Therefore, the required vector is {tex}\frac{(\vec{a} \times \vec{b})}{|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|}{/tex} Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{(a} \times \vec{b})=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 4 & -1 & 3 \\ 2 & 2 & -1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = (1 - 6) {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} - (-4 - 6) {tex}\hat{j}{/tex} + (8 + 2) {tex}\hat{k}{/tex} = (-5 {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} + 10 {tex}\hat{j}{/tex} + 10 {tex}\hat{k}{/tex} ) {tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=\sqrt{(-5)^{2}+(10)^{2}+(10)^{2}}=\sqrt{225}{/tex} = 15 Therefore the required unit vector = {tex}\frac{(-5 \hat{i}+10 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k})}{15}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{3}(-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex}
solution: We know that {tex}(\vec{a} \times \vec{b}){/tex} is a vector perpendicular to each one of {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} . Therefore, the required vector is {tex}\frac{(\vec{a} \times \vec{b})}{|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|}{/tex} Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{(a} \times \vec{b})=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 4 & -1 & 3 \\ 2 & 2 & -1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = (1 - 6) {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} - (-4 - 6) {tex}\hat{j}{/tex} + (8 + 2) {tex}\hat{k}{/tex} = (-5 {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} + 10 {tex}\hat{j}{/tex} + 10 {tex}\hat{k}{/tex} ) {tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=\sqrt{(-5)^{2}+(10)^{2}+(10)^{2}}=\sqrt{225}{/tex} = 15 Therefore the required unit vector = {tex}\frac{(-5 \hat{i}+10 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k})}{15}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{3}(-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex}
solution_html:
We know that {tex}(\vec{a} \times \vec{b}){/tex} is a vector perpendicular to each one of {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex}. Therefore, the required vector is {tex}\frac{(\vec{a} \times \vec{b})}{|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{(a} \times \vec{b})=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 4 & -1 & 3 \\ 2 & 2 & -1 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
= (1 - 6) {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} - (-4 - 6) {tex}\hat{j}{/tex} + (8 + 2) {tex}\hat{k}{/tex}
= (-5 {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} + 10 {tex}\hat{j}{/tex} + 10 {tex}\hat{k}{/tex})
{tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=\sqrt{(-5)^{2}+(10)^{2}+(10)^{2}}=\sqrt{225}{/tex} = 15
Therefore the required unit vector = {tex}\frac{(-5 \hat{i}+10 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k})}{15}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{3}(-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex}

question_html: Find the angle between unit vectors {tex}\vec{\text a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\text b}{/tex} so that {tex}\sqrt{3} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} is also a unit vector.
question_text: Find the angle between unit vectors {tex}\vec{\text a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\text b}{/tex} so that {tex}\sqrt{3} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} is also a unit vector.
explanation: {tex}|\vec{a}|{/tex} = {tex}|\vec{b}|{/tex} = 1 and {tex}|\sqrt{3} \vec{a}{/tex} - {tex}\vec{b}|{/tex} = 1 By scalar product, {tex}|\vec{a}. \vec{b}|{/tex} = {tex}|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} By substituting the values, we get {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} = cos {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}|\sqrt{3} a-b|^2{/tex} = 1 {tex}(\sqrt{3} a)^2-2 \sqrt{3} a b{/tex} + b 2 = 1 3a 2 - 2 {tex} \sqrt{3}{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + b 2 = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 - 2 {tex} \sqrt{3}{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + 1 = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4 - 1 = 2 {tex} \sqrt{3}{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 = 2 {tex} \sqrt{3}{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}
solution: {tex}|\vec{a}|{/tex} = {tex}|\vec{b}|{/tex} = 1 and {tex}|\sqrt{3} \vec{a}{/tex} - {tex}\vec{b}|{/tex} = 1 By scalar product, {tex}|\vec{a}. \vec{b}|{/tex} = {tex}|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} By substituting the values, we get {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} = cos {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}|\sqrt{3} a-b|^2{/tex} = 1 {tex}(\sqrt{3} a)^2-2 \sqrt{3} a b{/tex} + b 2 = 1 3a 2 - 2 {tex} \sqrt{3}{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + b 2 = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 - 2 {tex} \sqrt{3}{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + 1 = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4 - 1 = 2 {tex} \sqrt{3}{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 = 2 {tex} \sqrt{3}{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}|\vec{a}|{/tex} = {tex}|\vec{b}|{/tex} = 1 and {tex}|\sqrt{3} \vec{a}{/tex} - {tex}\vec{b}|{/tex} = 1
By scalar product, {tex}|\vec{a}. \vec{b}|{/tex} = {tex}|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex}
By substituting the values, we get
{tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} = cos {tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}|\sqrt{3} a-b|^2{/tex} = 1
{tex}(\sqrt{3} a)^2-2 \sqrt{3} a b{/tex} + b2 = 1
3a2 - 2{tex} \sqrt{3}{/tex} cos{tex}\theta{/tex} + b2 = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 - 2{tex} \sqrt{3}{/tex} cos{tex}\theta{/tex} + 1 = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4 - 1 = 2{tex} \sqrt{3}{/tex} cos{tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 = 2{tex} \sqrt{3}{/tex} cos{tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos{tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}

question_html:
Show that for any two non-zero vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex}and {tex}\vec b{/tex}.
{tex}|\vec{a}+\vec{b}|=|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|{/tex} iff {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} are perpendicular vectors.
question_text: Show that for any two non-zero vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} . {tex}|\vec{a}+\vec{b}|=|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|{/tex} iff {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} are perpendicular vectors.
explanation: {tex}|\vec{\text a}+\vec{\text b}|=|\vec{\text a}-\vec{\text b}|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec{\text a}+\vec{\text b}|^2=|\vec{\text a}-\vec{\text b}|^2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}4 \vec{\text a} \cdot \vec{\text b}{/tex} = 0 or {tex}\vec{\text a} \cdot \vec{\text b}{/tex} = 0 or {tex}\vec{\text a} \perp \vec{\text b}{/tex} Let {tex}\vec{\text a} \perp \vec{\text b}{/tex} Then {tex}\vec{\text a} \cdot \vec{\text b}{/tex} = 0 Thus, {tex}|\vec{\text a}+\vec{\text b}|^2{/tex} = {tex}|\vec{\text a}|^2+|\vec{\text b}|^2{/tex} and {tex}|\vec{\text a}-\vec{\text b}|^2{/tex} = {tex}|\vec{\text a}|^2+|\vec{\text b}|^2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec{\text a}+\vec{\text b}|=|\vec{\text a}-\vec{\text b}|{/tex}
solution: {tex}|\vec{\text a}+\vec{\text b}|=|\vec{\text a}-\vec{\text b}|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec{\text a}+\vec{\text b}|^2=|\vec{\text a}-\vec{\text b}|^2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}4 \vec{\text a} \cdot \vec{\text b}{/tex} = 0 or {tex}\vec{\text a} \cdot \vec{\text b}{/tex} = 0 or {tex}\vec{\text a} \perp \vec{\text b}{/tex} Let {tex}\vec{\text a} \perp \vec{\text b}{/tex} Then {tex}\vec{\text a} \cdot \vec{\text b}{/tex} = 0 Thus, {tex}|\vec{\text a}+\vec{\text b}|^2{/tex} = {tex}|\vec{\text a}|^2+|\vec{\text b}|^2{/tex} and {tex}|\vec{\text a}-\vec{\text b}|^2{/tex} = {tex}|\vec{\text a}|^2+|\vec{\text b}|^2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec{\text a}+\vec{\text b}|=|\vec{\text a}-\vec{\text b}|{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}|\vec{\text a}+\vec{\text b}|=|\vec{\text a}-\vec{\text b}|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec{\text a}+\vec{\text b}|^2=|\vec{\text a}-\vec{\text b}|^2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}4 \vec{\text a} \cdot \vec{\text b}{/tex} = 0 or {tex}\vec{\text a} \cdot \vec{\text b}{/tex} = 0 or {tex}\vec{\text a} \perp \vec{\text b}{/tex}
Let {tex}\vec{\text a} \perp \vec{\text b}{/tex}
Then {tex}\vec{\text a} \cdot \vec{\text b}{/tex} = 0
Thus, {tex}|\vec{\text a}+\vec{\text b}|^2{/tex} = {tex}|\vec{\text a}|^2+|\vec{\text b}|^2{/tex} and {tex}|\vec{\text a}-\vec{\text b}|^2{/tex} = {tex}|\vec{\text a}|^2+|\vec{\text b}|^2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec{\text a}+\vec{\text b}|=|\vec{\text a}-\vec{\text b}|{/tex}

question_html: Show that the lines {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a}+\lambda \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{b}+\mu \vec{a}{/tex} are coplanar and the plane containing them is given by {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}})=0{/tex}
question_text: Show that the lines {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a}+\lambda \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{b}+\mu \vec{a}{/tex} are coplanar and the plane containing them is given by {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}})=0{/tex}
explanation: Two lines {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a}_1+\lambda \vec{b}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a}_2+\mu \vec{b}_2{/tex} are coplanar if {tex}\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right){/tex} = 0 Now, {tex}\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right){/tex} = {tex}(\vec{b}-\vec{a}) \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex} {tex}\left(\because \vec{a}_1=\vec{a}, \vec{b}_1=\vec{b}, \vec{a}_2=\vec{b}, \vec{b}_2=\vec{a}\right){/tex} = {tex}\vec{b} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a})-\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} \vec{b}\; \vec{b} \; \vec{a} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{lll} \vec{a} \; \vec{b} \; \vec{a} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = 0 Hence the lines are coplanar. Equation of plane containing them is given by, {tex}(\vec{r}-\vec{a}) \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex} = 0 [ {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}(\vec{r}-\vec{a}) \cdot \vec{n}{/tex} = 0] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec{r} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a})-\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec{r} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex} = 0 {tex}\left(\because\left[\begin{array}{lll} \vec{a}\; \vec{b} \; \vec{a} \end{array}\right]=0\right){/tex}
solution: Two lines {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a}_1+\lambda \vec{b}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a}_2+\mu \vec{b}_2{/tex} are coplanar if {tex}\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right){/tex} = 0 Now, {tex}\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right){/tex} = {tex}(\vec{b}-\vec{a}) \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex} {tex}\left(\because \vec{a}_1=\vec{a}, \vec{b}_1=\vec{b}, \vec{a}_2=\vec{b}, \vec{b}_2=\vec{a}\right){/tex} = {tex}\vec{b} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a})-\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} \vec{b}\; \vec{b} \; \vec{a} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{lll} \vec{a} \; \vec{b} \; \vec{a} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = 0 Hence the lines are coplanar. Equation of plane containing them is given by, {tex}(\vec{r}-\vec{a}) \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex} = 0 [ {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}(\vec{r}-\vec{a}) \cdot \vec{n}{/tex} = 0] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec{r} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a})-\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec{r} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex} = 0 {tex}\left(\because\left[\begin{array}{lll} \vec{a}\; \vec{b} \; \vec{a} \end{array}\right]=0\right){/tex}
solution_html:
Two lines {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a}_1+\lambda \vec{b}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a}_2+\mu \vec{b}_2{/tex} are coplanar if {tex}\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right){/tex} = 0
Now, {tex}\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right){/tex}
= {tex}(\vec{b}-\vec{a}) \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex} {tex}\left(\because \vec{a}_1=\vec{a}, \vec{b}_1=\vec{b}, \vec{a}_2=\vec{b}, \vec{b}_2=\vec{a}\right){/tex}
= {tex}\vec{b} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a})-\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} \vec{b}\; \vec{b} \; \vec{a} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{lll} \vec{a} \; \vec{b} \; \vec{a} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = 0
Hence the lines are coplanar.
Equation of plane containing them is given by,
{tex}(\vec{r}-\vec{a}) \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex} = 0 [{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}(\vec{r}-\vec{a}) \cdot \vec{n}{/tex} = 0]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec{r} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a})-\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec{r} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex} = 0 {tex}\left(\because\left[\begin{array}{lll} \vec{a}\; \vec{b} \; \vec{a} \end{array}\right]=0\right){/tex}

question_html: Show that the vectors {tex}2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}, {tex}3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+7 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}5 \hat{\text i}+6 \hat{\text j}+2 \hat{\text k}{/tex} form the sides of a right-angled triangle.
question_text: Show that the vectors {tex}2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} , {tex}3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+7 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}5 \hat{\text i}+6 \hat{\text j}+2 \hat{\text k}{/tex} form the sides of a right-angled triangle.
explanation: Let {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} , {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+7 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{c}}=5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} Since {tex}\vec{\mathrm{c}}=\vec{\mathrm{a}}+\vec{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} , three vectors form a triangle. Also, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} = 0. So, triangle is a right angled triangle.
solution: Let {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} , {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+7 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{c}}=5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} Since {tex}\vec{\mathrm{c}}=\vec{\mathrm{a}}+\vec{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} , three vectors form a triangle. Also, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} = 0. So, triangle is a right angled triangle.
solution_html:
Let {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+7 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{c}}=5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
Since {tex}\vec{\mathrm{c}}=\vec{\mathrm{a}}+\vec{\mathrm{b}}{/tex}, three vectors form a triangle.
Also, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} = 0.
So, triangle is a right angled triangle.

question_html: Find |{tex}|\vec{a}|{/tex} and {tex}|\vec{b}|{/tex}, if {tex}|\vec{a}|{/tex} = {tex}2|\vec{b}|{/tex} and {tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \cdot(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex} = 12.
question_text: Find | {tex}|\vec{a}|{/tex} and {tex}|\vec{b}|{/tex} , if {tex}|\vec{a}|{/tex} = {tex}2|\vec{b}|{/tex} and {tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \cdot(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex} = 12.
explanation: {tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b})(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex} = 12 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec{a}|^2-|\vec{b}|^2{/tex} = 12 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}3|\vec{b}|^2{/tex} = 12 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec{b}|{/tex} = 2 Now, {tex}|\vec{a}|^2{/tex} = 12 + {tex}|\vec{b}|^2{/tex} = 16 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec{a}|{/tex} = 4
solution: {tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b})(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex} = 12 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec{a}|^2-|\vec{b}|^2{/tex} = 12 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}3|\vec{b}|^2{/tex} = 12 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec{b}|{/tex} = 2 Now, {tex}|\vec{a}|^2{/tex} = 12 + {tex}|\vec{b}|^2{/tex} = 16 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec{a}|{/tex} = 4
solution_html:
{tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b})(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex} = 12 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec{a}|^2-|\vec{b}|^2{/tex} = 12
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}3|\vec{b}|^2{/tex} = 12 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec{b}|{/tex} = 2
Now, {tex}|\vec{a}|^2{/tex} = 12 + {tex}|\vec{b}|^2{/tex} = 16 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec{a}|{/tex} = 4

question_text: Using vectors, find the area of the triangle ABC with vertices A(1, 2, 3), B(2, -1, 4) and C(4, 5, -1).
explanation: area of {tex}\triangle \text {ABC}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} |cross product of any two side vectors| {tex}\overrightarrow{\text {AB}}{/tex} = {tex}\hat{\text i}-3 \hat{\text j}+\hat{\text k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\text {BC}}{/tex} = {tex}2 \hat{\text i}+6 \hat{\text j}-5 \hat{\text k}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{B C}=9 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+12 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} area of {tex}\triangle \text {ABC}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex} \sqrt{81+49+144}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{274}{/tex}
solution: area of {tex}\triangle \text {ABC}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} |cross product of any two side vectors| {tex}\overrightarrow{\text {AB}}{/tex} = {tex}\hat{\text i}-3 \hat{\text j}+\hat{\text k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\text {BC}}{/tex} = {tex}2 \hat{\text i}+6 \hat{\text j}-5 \hat{\text k}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{B C}=9 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+12 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} area of {tex}\triangle \text {ABC}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex} \sqrt{81+49+144}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{274}{/tex}
solution_html:
area of {tex}\triangle \text {ABC}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}|cross product of any two side vectors|
{tex}\overrightarrow{\text {AB}}{/tex} = {tex}\hat{\text i}-3 \hat{\text j}+\hat{\text k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\text {BC}}{/tex} = {tex}2 \hat{\text i}+6 \hat{\text j}-5 \hat{\text k}{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{B C}=9 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+12 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} area of {tex}\triangle \text {ABC}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}{tex} \sqrt{81+49+144}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{274}{/tex}

question_text: If {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|{/tex} = 3, {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}|{/tex} = 4 and {tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|{/tex} = 6, then the value of {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} is
explanation: {tex}|\vec{a}|=3 |\vec{b}|=4{/tex} and {tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=6{/tex} {tex} |\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=|\vec{a}||\vec{b}| \sin \theta . {/tex} {tex} 6=3 \times 4 \sin \theta . {/tex} {tex} \frac{b x \not 2}{3 \times 4_2}=\sin \theta . {/tex} {tex} \sin \theta=\frac{1}{2} {/tex} {tex} \theta=\frac{\pi}{6} {/tex} {tex} \vec{a} \cdot \bar{b} =|\vec{a}| \cdot|\vec{b}| \cos \theta {/tex} {tex} =3 \times 4 \times \cos \frac{\pi}{6} {/tex} {tex} =3 \times 4 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} {/tex} {tex} =6 \sqrt{3} . {/tex}

question_html: Find a unit vector perpendicular to each of the vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} where {tex}\vec{a}=5 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=7 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}.
question_text: Find a unit vector perpendicular to each of the vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} where {tex}\vec{a}=5 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=7 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex} \vec{a} =5 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k} . {/tex} {tex}\vec{b} =7 \hat{i}+6 j+2 \hat{k} {/tex} {tex} \vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}i & j & k \\ 5 & 6 & -2 \\ 7 & 6 & 2\end{array}\right| {/tex} {tex} \vec{a} \times \vec{b}=i\left|\begin{array}{cc}6 & -2 \\ 6 & 2\end{array}\right|-j\left|\begin{array}{cc}5 & -2 \\ 7 & 2\end{array}\right|+k\left|\begin{array}{cc}5 & 6 \\ 7 & 6\end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex} =i(12+12)-j(10+14)+k(30-42) {/tex} {tex} =24 i-24 j-12 k . {/tex} {tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}| =\sqrt{(24)^2+(24)^2+(12)^2} {/tex} {tex} =\sqrt{576+576+144} {/tex} {tex} =\sqrt{1296} {/tex} {tex} =36 {/tex} {tex}\hat{n} =\frac{\vec{a} \times \vec{b}}{|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{24 i-24 j-12 k}{36} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{12(2 i-2 j-k)}{36} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{3}(2 i-2 j-k){/tex}
solution: {tex} \vec{a} =5 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k} . {/tex} {tex}\vec{b} =7 \hat{i}+6 j+2 \hat{k} {/tex} {tex} \vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}i & j & k \\ 5 & 6 & -2 \\ 7 & 6 & 2\end{array}\right| {/tex} {tex} \vec{a} \times \vec{b}=i\left|\begin{array}{cc}6 & -2 \\ 6 & 2\end{array}\right|-j\left|\begin{array}{cc}5 & -2 \\ 7 & 2\end{array}\right|+k\left|\begin{array}{cc}5 & 6 \\ 7 & 6\end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex} =i(12+12)-j(10+14)+k(30-42) {/tex} {tex} =24 i-24 j-12 k . {/tex} {tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}| =\sqrt{(24)^2+(24)^2+(12)^2} {/tex} {tex} =\sqrt{576+576+144} {/tex} {tex} =\sqrt{1296} {/tex} {tex} =36 {/tex} {tex}\hat{n} =\frac{\vec{a} \times \vec{b}}{|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{24 i-24 j-12 k}{36} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{12(2 i-2 j-k)}{36} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{3}(2 i-2 j-k){/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \vec{a} =5 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k} . {/tex}
{tex}\vec{b} =7 \hat{i}+6 j+2 \hat{k} {/tex}
{tex} \vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}i & j & k \\ 5 & 6 & -2 \\ 7 & 6 & 2\end{array}\right| {/tex}
{tex} \vec{a} \times \vec{b}=i\left|\begin{array}{cc}6 & -2 \\ 6 & 2\end{array}\right|-j\left|\begin{array}{cc}5 & -2 \\ 7 & 2\end{array}\right|+k\left|\begin{array}{cc}5 & 6 \\ 7 & 6\end{array}\right|{/tex}
{tex} =i(12+12)-j(10+14)+k(30-42) {/tex}
{tex} =24 i-24 j-12 k . {/tex}
{tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}| =\sqrt{(24)^2+(24)^2+(12)^2} {/tex}
{tex} =\sqrt{576+576+144} {/tex}
{tex} =\sqrt{1296} {/tex}
{tex} =36 {/tex}
{tex}\hat{n} =\frac{\vec{a} \times \vec{b}}{|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{24 i-24 j-12 k}{36} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{12(2 i-2 j-k)}{36} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{1}{3}(2 i-2 j-k){/tex}

question_html: Find the unit vector perpendicular to each of the vectors {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}.
question_text: Find the unit vector perpendicular to each of the vectors {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} .
explanation: Compute the cross product: {tex} \overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{{i}} & \hat{{j}} & \hat{{k}} \\ 4 & 3 & 1 \\ 2 & -1 & 2 \end{array}\right|{/tex} Expanding along the first row: {tex} {/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}=\hat{{i}}\left|\begin{array}{cc}3 & 1 \\ -1 & 2\end{array}\right|-\hat{{j}}\left|\begin{array}{cc}4 & 1 \\ 2 & 2\end{array}\right|+\hat{{k}}\left|\begin{array}{cc}4 & 3 \\ 2 & -1\end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=\hat{{i}}(3 \times 2-1 \times(-1))-\hat{{j}}(4 \times 2-1 \times 2)+\hat{{k}}(4 \times(-1)-3 \times 2) {/tex} {tex}=\hat{{i}}(6+1)-\hat{{j}}(8-2)+\hat{{k}}(-4-6) {/tex} {tex}=7 \hat{{i}}-6 \hat{{j}}-10 \hat{{k}} {/tex} Compute the magnitude of the cross product: {tex} \mid \overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}} \mid=\sqrt{7^2+(-6)^2+(-10)^2} {/tex} {tex} =\sqrt{49+36+100}=\sqrt{185} {/tex} Compute the unit vector: {tex} \hat{{n}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{185}}(7 \hat{{i}}-6 \hat{{j}}-10 \hat{{k}}){/tex} Thus, the required unit vector is: {tex} \frac{7}{\sqrt{185}} \hat{{i}}-\frac{6}{\sqrt{185}} \hat{{j}}-\frac{10}{\sqrt{185}} \hat{{k}}{/tex}
solution: Compute the cross product: {tex} \overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{{i}} & \hat{{j}} & \hat{{k}} \\ 4 & 3 & 1 \\ 2 & -1 & 2 \end{array}\right|{/tex} Expanding along the first row: {tex} {/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}=\hat{{i}}\left|\begin{array}{cc}3 & 1 \\ -1 & 2\end{array}\right|-\hat{{j}}\left|\begin{array}{cc}4 & 1 \\ 2 & 2\end{array}\right|+\hat{{k}}\left|\begin{array}{cc}4 & 3 \\ 2 & -1\end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=\hat{{i}}(3 \times 2-1 \times(-1))-\hat{{j}}(4 \times 2-1 \times 2)+\hat{{k}}(4 \times(-1)-3 \times 2) {/tex} {tex}=\hat{{i}}(6+1)-\hat{{j}}(8-2)+\hat{{k}}(-4-6) {/tex} {tex}=7 \hat{{i}}-6 \hat{{j}}-10 \hat{{k}} {/tex} Compute the magnitude of the cross product: {tex} \mid \overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}} \mid=\sqrt{7^2+(-6)^2+(-10)^2} {/tex} {tex} =\sqrt{49+36+100}=\sqrt{185} {/tex} Compute the unit vector: {tex} \hat{{n}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{185}}(7 \hat{{i}}-6 \hat{{j}}-10 \hat{{k}}){/tex} Thus, the required unit vector is: {tex} \frac{7}{\sqrt{185}} \hat{{i}}-\frac{6}{\sqrt{185}} \hat{{j}}-\frac{10}{\sqrt{185}} \hat{{k}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Compute the cross product:
{tex} \overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{{i}} & \hat{{j}} & \hat{{k}} \\ 4 & 3 & 1 \\ 2 & -1 & 2 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
Expanding along the first row:
{tex} {/tex}{tex}\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}=\hat{{i}}\left|\begin{array}{cc}3 & 1 \\ -1 & 2\end{array}\right|-\hat{{j}}\left|\begin{array}{cc}4 & 1 \\ 2 & 2\end{array}\right|+\hat{{k}}\left|\begin{array}{cc}4 & 3 \\ 2 & -1\end{array}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=\hat{{i}}(3 \times 2-1 \times(-1))-\hat{{j}}(4 \times 2-1 \times 2)+\hat{{k}}(4 \times(-1)-3 \times 2) {/tex}
{tex}=\hat{{i}}(6+1)-\hat{{j}}(8-2)+\hat{{k}}(-4-6) {/tex}
{tex}=7 \hat{{i}}-6 \hat{{j}}-10 \hat{{k}} {/tex}
Compute the magnitude of the cross product:
{tex} \mid \overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}} \mid=\sqrt{7^2+(-6)^2+(-10)^2} {/tex}
{tex} =\sqrt{49+36+100}=\sqrt{185} {/tex}
Compute the unit vector:
{tex} \hat{{n}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{185}}(7 \hat{{i}}-6 \hat{{j}}-10 \hat{{k}}){/tex}
Thus, the required unit vector is:
{tex} \frac{7}{\sqrt{185}} \hat{{i}}-\frac{6}{\sqrt{185}} \hat{{j}}-\frac{10}{\sqrt{185}} \hat{{k}}{/tex}

question_html: Find the distance of the point P(-2, - 4, 7) from the point of intersection Q of the line {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}})+\lambda(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} and the plane {tex}\vec{r} \cdot(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k})=6{/tex}. Also, write the vector equation of the line PQ.
question_text: Find the distance of the point P(-2, - 4, 7) from the point of intersection Q of the line {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}})+\lambda(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} and the plane {tex}\vec{r} \cdot(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k})=6{/tex} . Also, write the vector equation of the line PQ.
explanation: Given : Line: {tex}\vec{r}=(3 \widehat{i}-2 \widehat{j}+6 \widehat{k})+\lambda(2 \widehat{i}-\widehat{j}+2 \widehat{k}){/tex} {tex} \vec{r}=(3+2 \lambda) \hat{{i}}+(-2-\lambda) \hat{{j}}+(6+2 \lambda) \hat{{k}}{/tex} Plane: {tex}\vec{r} \cdot(\hat{i}-\widehat{j}+\widehat{k})=6{/tex} Intersection Point: {tex} {[(3+2 \lambda) \hat{i}+(-2-\lambda) \hat{j}+(6+2 \lambda) \hat{k}] \cdot(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k})=6} {/tex} {tex} 3+2 \lambda+2+\lambda+6+2 \lambda=6 {/tex} {tex} 5 \lambda=-5 \Rightarrow \lambda=-1 {/tex} Point Q {tex}(1,-1,4){/tex} Point {tex}P(-2,-4,7){/tex} {tex} {PQ}=\sqrt{(1+2)^2+(-1+4)^2+(4-7)^2}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{9+9+9}=3 \sqrt{3}{/tex} Line {tex}{PQ}: \overrightarrow{{r}}=-2 \hat{{i}}-4 \hat{{j}}+7 \hat{{k}}+\mu(3 \hat{{i}}+3 \hat{{j}}-3 \hat{{k}}){/tex}
solution: Given : Line: {tex}\vec{r}=(3 \widehat{i}-2 \widehat{j}+6 \widehat{k})+\lambda(2 \widehat{i}-\widehat{j}+2 \widehat{k}){/tex} {tex} \vec{r}=(3+2 \lambda) \hat{{i}}+(-2-\lambda) \hat{{j}}+(6+2 \lambda) \hat{{k}}{/tex} Plane: {tex}\vec{r} \cdot(\hat{i}-\widehat{j}+\widehat{k})=6{/tex} Intersection Point: {tex} {[(3+2 \lambda) \hat{i}+(-2-\lambda) \hat{j}+(6+2 \lambda) \hat{k}] \cdot(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k})=6} {/tex} {tex} 3+2 \lambda+2+\lambda+6+2 \lambda=6 {/tex} {tex} 5 \lambda=-5 \Rightarrow \lambda=-1 {/tex} Point Q {tex}(1,-1,4){/tex} Point {tex}P(-2,-4,7){/tex} {tex} {PQ}=\sqrt{(1+2)^2+(-1+4)^2+(4-7)^2}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{9+9+9}=3 \sqrt{3}{/tex} Line {tex}{PQ}: \overrightarrow{{r}}=-2 \hat{{i}}-4 \hat{{j}}+7 \hat{{k}}+\mu(3 \hat{{i}}+3 \hat{{j}}-3 \hat{{k}}){/tex}
solution_html:
Given :
Line: {tex}\vec{r}=(3 \widehat{i}-2 \widehat{j}+6 \widehat{k})+\lambda(2 \widehat{i}-\widehat{j}+2 \widehat{k}){/tex}
{tex} \vec{r}=(3+2 \lambda) \hat{{i}}+(-2-\lambda) \hat{{j}}+(6+2 \lambda) \hat{{k}}{/tex}
Plane: {tex}\vec{r} \cdot(\hat{i}-\widehat{j}+\widehat{k})=6{/tex}
Intersection Point:
{tex} {[(3+2 \lambda) \hat{i}+(-2-\lambda) \hat{j}+(6+2 \lambda) \hat{k}] \cdot(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k})=6} {/tex}
{tex} 3+2 \lambda+2+\lambda+6+2 \lambda=6 {/tex}
{tex} 5 \lambda=-5 \Rightarrow \lambda=-1 {/tex}
Point Q {tex}(1,-1,4){/tex}
Point {tex}P(-2,-4,7){/tex}
{tex} {PQ}=\sqrt{(1+2)^2+(-1+4)^2+(4-7)^2}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{9+9+9}=3 \sqrt{3}{/tex}
Line {tex}{PQ}: \overrightarrow{{r}}=-2 \hat{{i}}-4 \hat{{j}}+7 \hat{{k}}+\mu(3 \hat{{i}}+3 \hat{{j}}-3 \hat{{k}}){/tex}

question_html: If {tex}\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}, 2 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}, 3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{i}-6 \hat{j}-\hat{k} {/tex} respectively are the position vectors of points A, B, C and D, then find the angle between the straight lines AB and CD. Find whether {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AB}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{CD}}{/tex} are collinear or not.
question_text: If {tex}\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}, 2 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}, 3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{i}-6 \hat{j}-\hat{k} {/tex} respectively are the position vectors of points A, B, C and D, then find the angle between the straight lines AB and CD. Find whether {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AB}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{CD}}{/tex} are collinear or not.
explanation: Let {tex} \theta {/tex} be the angle between the lines AB and CD. Then, {tex} \theta {/tex} is also the angle between vectors {tex} \overrightarrow{A B} \text { and } \overrightarrow{C D} {/tex} Now, {tex} \overrightarrow{A B}=(2 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j})-(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})=\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-\hat{k} {/tex} and, {tex} \overrightarrow{C D}=(\hat{i}-6 \hat{j}-\hat{k})-(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k})=-2 \hat{i}-8 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k} {/tex} {tex} \therefore|\overrightarrow{A B}|=\sqrt{1+16+1}=3 \sqrt{2},|\overrightarrow{C D}|=\sqrt{4+64+4}=6 \sqrt{2} {/tex} {tex} \text { and, } (\overrightarrow{A B} \cdot \overrightarrow{C D})=(\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-\hat{k}) \cdot(-2 \hat{i}-8 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) {/tex} = - 2 - 32 - 2 = - 36 Now, using this formula. {tex} \cos \theta=\frac{\overrightarrow{A B} \cdot \overrightarrow{C D}}{|\overrightarrow{A B}||\overrightarrow{C D}|} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{-36}{3 \sqrt{2} \times 6 \sqrt{2}}=-1=\cos \pi {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \theta=\pi {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{A B} \text { and } \overrightarrow{C D} {/tex} and unlike parallel vectors. Hence, lines AB and CD are parallel.
solution: Let {tex} \theta {/tex} be the angle between the lines AB and CD. Then, {tex} \theta {/tex} is also the angle between vectors {tex} \overrightarrow{A B} \text { and } \overrightarrow{C D} {/tex} Now, {tex} \overrightarrow{A B}=(2 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j})-(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})=\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-\hat{k} {/tex} and, {tex} \overrightarrow{C D}=(\hat{i}-6 \hat{j}-\hat{k})-(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k})=-2 \hat{i}-8 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k} {/tex} {tex} \therefore|\overrightarrow{A B}|=\sqrt{1+16+1}=3 \sqrt{2},|\overrightarrow{C D}|=\sqrt{4+64+4}=6 \sqrt{2} {/tex} {tex} \text { and, } (\overrightarrow{A B} \cdot \overrightarrow{C D})=(\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-\hat{k}) \cdot(-2 \hat{i}-8 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) {/tex} = - 2 - 32 - 2 = - 36 Now, using this formula. {tex} \cos \theta=\frac{\overrightarrow{A B} \cdot \overrightarrow{C D}}{|\overrightarrow{A B}||\overrightarrow{C D}|} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{-36}{3 \sqrt{2} \times 6 \sqrt{2}}=-1=\cos \pi {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \theta=\pi {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{A B} \text { and } \overrightarrow{C D} {/tex} and unlike parallel vectors. Hence, lines AB and CD are parallel.
solution_html:
Let {tex} \theta {/tex} be the angle between the lines AB and CD. Then, {tex} \theta {/tex} is also the angle between vectors {tex} \overrightarrow{A B} \text { and } \overrightarrow{C D} {/tex} 
Now, 
{tex} \overrightarrow{A B}=(2 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j})-(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})=\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-\hat{k} {/tex}
and,
 {tex} \overrightarrow{C D}=(\hat{i}-6 \hat{j}-\hat{k})-(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k})=-2 \hat{i}-8 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k} {/tex}
{tex} \therefore|\overrightarrow{A B}|=\sqrt{1+16+1}=3 \sqrt{2},|\overrightarrow{C D}|=\sqrt{4+64+4}=6 \sqrt{2} {/tex}
{tex} \text { and, } (\overrightarrow{A B} \cdot \overrightarrow{C D})=(\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-\hat{k}) \cdot(-2 \hat{i}-8 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) {/tex} = - 2 - 32 - 2 = - 36
Now, using this formula. {tex} \cos \theta=\frac{\overrightarrow{A B} \cdot \overrightarrow{C D}}{|\overrightarrow{A B}||\overrightarrow{C D}|} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{-36}{3 \sqrt{2} \times 6 \sqrt{2}}=-1=\cos \pi {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \theta=\pi {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{A B} \text { and } \overrightarrow{C D} {/tex} and unlike parallel vectors.
Hence, lines AB and CD are parallel.

question_html: If the sum of two unit vectors {tex}\hat { a }{/tex} and {tex}\hat { b }{/tex} is a unit vector, show that the magnitude of their difference is {tex}\sqrt { 3 }.{/tex}
question_text: If the sum of two unit vectors {tex}\hat { a }{/tex} and {tex}\hat { b }{/tex} is a unit vector, show that the magnitude of their difference is {tex}\sqrt { 3 }.{/tex}
explanation: Let {tex}\vec { c } = \hat { a } + \hat { b }.{/tex} Then, according to given condition {tex}\vec { c }{/tex} is a unit vector, i.e. {tex}| \vec { c } | = 1.{/tex} To show {tex}| \hat { a } - \hat { b } | = \sqrt { 3 }{/tex} Consider, {tex}\vec { c } = \hat { a } + \hat { b }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \vec { c } | = | \hat { a } + \hat { b } |{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1 = | \hat { a } + \hat { b } |{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \hat { a } + \hat { b } | ^ { 2 } = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( \hat { a } + \hat { b } ) .( \hat { a } + \hat { b } ) = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \hat { a } | ^ { 2 } + 2 \hat { a } \cdot \hat { b } + | \hat { b } | ^ { 2 } = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1 + 2 \hat { a } \cdot \hat { b } + 1 = 1 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \hat { a } .\hat { b } = - 1{/tex} ...(i) Now consider, {tex}| \hat { a } - \hat { b } | ^ { 2 } = ( \hat { a } - \hat { b } ).( \hat { a } - \hat { b } ){/tex} {tex}=\quad | \hat { a } | ^ { 2 } - 2 \hat { a } \cdot \hat { b } + | \hat { b } | ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \quad 1 - (-1) + 1= 3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \hat { a } - \hat { b } | = \sqrt { 3 }{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}\vec { c } = \hat { a } + \hat { b }.{/tex} Then, according to given condition {tex}\vec { c }{/tex} is a unit vector, i.e. {tex}| \vec { c } | = 1.{/tex} To show {tex}| \hat { a } - \hat { b } | = \sqrt { 3 }{/tex} Consider, {tex}\vec { c } = \hat { a } + \hat { b }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \vec { c } | = | \hat { a } + \hat { b } |{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1 = | \hat { a } + \hat { b } |{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \hat { a } + \hat { b } | ^ { 2 } = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( \hat { a } + \hat { b } ) .( \hat { a } + \hat { b } ) = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \hat { a } | ^ { 2 } + 2 \hat { a } \cdot \hat { b } + | \hat { b } | ^ { 2 } = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1 + 2 \hat { a } \cdot \hat { b } + 1 = 1 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \hat { a } .\hat { b } = - 1{/tex} ...(i) Now consider, {tex}| \hat { a } - \hat { b } | ^ { 2 } = ( \hat { a } - \hat { b } ).( \hat { a } - \hat { b } ){/tex} {tex}=\quad | \hat { a } | ^ { 2 } - 2 \hat { a } \cdot \hat { b } + | \hat { b } | ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \quad 1 - (-1) + 1= 3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \hat { a } - \hat { b } | = \sqrt { 3 }{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\vec { c } = \hat { a } + \hat { b }.{/tex} 
Then, according to given condition {tex}\vec { c }{/tex} is a unit vector, i.e. {tex}| \vec { c } | = 1.{/tex}
To show {tex}| \hat { a } - \hat { b } | = \sqrt { 3 }{/tex}
Consider, {tex}\vec { c } = \hat { a } + \hat { b }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \vec { c } | = | \hat { a } + \hat { b } |{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1 = | \hat { a } + \hat { b } |{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \hat { a } + \hat { b } | ^ { 2 } = 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( \hat { a } + \hat { b } ) .( \hat { a } + \hat { b } ) = 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \hat { a } | ^ { 2 } + 2 \hat { a } \cdot \hat { b } + | \hat { b } | ^ { 2 } = 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1 + 2 \hat { a } \cdot \hat { b } + 1 = 1 {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \hat { a } .\hat { b } = - 1{/tex}...(i)
Now consider, {tex}| \hat { a } - \hat { b } | ^ { 2 } = ( \hat { a } - \hat { b } ).( \hat { a } - \hat { b } ){/tex}
{tex}=\quad | \hat { a } | ^ { 2 } - 2 \hat { a } \cdot \hat { b } + | \hat { b } | ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= \quad 1 - (-1) + 1= 3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \hat { a } - \hat { b } | = \sqrt { 3 }{/tex}

question_html: The scalar product of the vector {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} with a unit vector along the sum of vectors {tex}\vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c } = \lambda \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} is equal to one. Find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} and hence, find the unit vector along {tex}\vec { b } + \vec { c }.{/tex}
question_text: The scalar product of the vector {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} with a unit vector along the sum of vectors {tex}\vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c } = \lambda \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} is equal to one. Find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} and hence, find the unit vector along {tex}\vec { b } + \vec { c }.{/tex}
explanation: According to the question, {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } , \vec { b } = { 2 } \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c } = \lambda \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }.{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec { b } + \vec { c } = 2 \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k } + \lambda \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}= ( 2 + \lambda ) \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad | \vec { b } + \vec { c } | = \sqrt { ( 2 + \lambda ) ^ { 2 } + ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 2 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 4 + \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 36 + 4 }{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 }{/tex} The unit vector along {tex}\vec { b } + \vec { c }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \vec { b } + \vec { c } } { | \vec { b } + \vec { c } | } = \frac { ( 2 + \lambda ) \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 } }{/tex} ...(i) According to the question, the scalar product of {tex}( \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } ){/tex} with unit vector {tex}\vec { b } + \vec { c }{/tex} is 1. {tex}\therefore \quad ( \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } ) \cdot \frac { \vec { b } + \vec { c } } { | \vec { b } + \vec { c } | } = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } ) \cdot \frac { ( 2 + \lambda ) \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 } } = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 ( 2 + \lambda ) + 1 ( 6 ) + 1 ( - 2 ) } { \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 } } = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { ( 2 + \lambda ) + 6 - 2 } { \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 } } = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda + 6 = \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( \lambda + 6 ) ^ { 2 } = \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44{/tex} [squaring both sides] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda ^ { 2 } + 36 + 12 \lambda = \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 8 \lambda = 8{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda = 1{/tex} The value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} is 1. Substituting the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get unit vector along {tex}\vec { b } + \vec { c }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { ( 2 + 1 ) \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + 4 ( 1 ) + 44 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 3 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 1 + 4 + 44 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 3 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 49 } } = \frac { 3 } { 7 } \hat { i } + \frac { 6 } { 7 } \hat { j } - \frac { 2 } { 7 } \hat { k }{/tex}
solution: According to the question, {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } , \vec { b } = { 2 } \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c } = \lambda \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }.{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec { b } + \vec { c } = 2 \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k } + \lambda \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}= ( 2 + \lambda ) \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad | \vec { b } + \vec { c } | = \sqrt { ( 2 + \lambda ) ^ { 2 } + ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 2 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 4 + \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 36 + 4 }{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 }{/tex} The unit vector along {tex}\vec { b } + \vec { c }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \vec { b } + \vec { c } } { | \vec { b } + \vec { c } | } = \frac { ( 2 + \lambda ) \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 } }{/tex} ...(i) According to the question, the scalar product of {tex}( \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } ){/tex} with unit vector {tex}\vec { b } + \vec { c }{/tex} is 1. {tex}\therefore \quad ( \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } ) \cdot \frac { \vec { b } + \vec { c } } { | \vec { b } + \vec { c } | } = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } ) \cdot \frac { ( 2 + \lambda ) \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 } } = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 ( 2 + \lambda ) + 1 ( 6 ) + 1 ( - 2 ) } { \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 } } = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { ( 2 + \lambda ) + 6 - 2 } { \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 } } = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda + 6 = \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( \lambda + 6 ) ^ { 2 } = \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44{/tex} [squaring both sides] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda ^ { 2 } + 36 + 12 \lambda = \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 8 \lambda = 8{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda = 1{/tex} The value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} is 1. Substituting the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get unit vector along {tex}\vec { b } + \vec { c }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { ( 2 + 1 ) \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + 4 ( 1 ) + 44 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 3 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 1 + 4 + 44 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 3 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 49 } } = \frac { 3 } { 7 } \hat { i } + \frac { 6 } { 7 } \hat { j } - \frac { 2 } { 7 } \hat { k }{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } , \vec { b } = { 2 } \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k }{/tex}
and {tex}\vec { c } = \lambda \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }.{/tex}
Now, {tex}\vec { b } + \vec { c } = 2 \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k } + \lambda \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex}
{tex}= ( 2 + \lambda ) \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad | \vec { b } + \vec { c } | = \sqrt { ( 2 + \lambda ) ^ { 2 } + ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 2 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt { 4 + \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 36 + 4 }{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 }{/tex}
The unit vector along {tex}\vec { b } + \vec { c }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \vec { b } + \vec { c } } { | \vec { b } + \vec { c } | } = \frac { ( 2 + \lambda ) \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 } }{/tex} ...(i)
According to the question, the scalar product of {tex}( \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } ){/tex}with unit vector {tex}\vec { b } + \vec { c }{/tex} is 1.
{tex}\therefore \quad ( \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } ) \cdot \frac { \vec { b } + \vec { c } } { | \vec { b } + \vec { c } | } = 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } ) \cdot \frac { ( 2 + \lambda ) \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 } } = 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 ( 2 + \lambda ) + 1 ( 6 ) + 1 ( - 2 ) } { \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 } } = 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { ( 2 + \lambda ) + 6 - 2 } { \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 } } = 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda + 6 = \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( \lambda + 6 ) ^ { 2 } = \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44{/tex}
[squaring both sides]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda ^ { 2 } + 36 + 12 \lambda = \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 8 \lambda = 8{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda = 1{/tex}
The value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} is 1.
Substituting the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get unit vector along {tex}\vec { b } + \vec { c }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { ( 2 + 1 ) \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + 4 ( 1 ) + 44 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 3 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 1 + 4 + 44 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 3 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 49 } } = \frac { 3 } { 7 } \hat { i } + \frac { 6 } { 7 } \hat { j } - \frac { 2 } { 7 } \hat { k }{/tex}

question_html: If the sum of two unit vectors is a unit vector, prove that the magnitude of their difference is {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}.
question_text: If the sum of two unit vectors is a unit vector, prove that the magnitude of their difference is {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} .
explanation: Let {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} are two unit vectors. Given, {tex}|\vec{a}|=1,|\vec{b}|=1,|\vec{a}+\vec{b}|=1{/tex} We know that, {tex}|\vec{a}+\vec{b}|^{2}+|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|^{2}=2\left(|\vec{a}|^{2}+|\vec{b}|^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}(1)^{2}+|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|^{2}=2\left(1^{2}+1^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow (1)^{2}+|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|^{2}=4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow |\vec{a}-\vec{b}|^{2}=3{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|=\sqrt{3} {/tex}
solution: Let {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} are two unit vectors. Given, {tex}|\vec{a}|=1,|\vec{b}|=1,|\vec{a}+\vec{b}|=1{/tex} We know that, {tex}|\vec{a}+\vec{b}|^{2}+|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|^{2}=2\left(|\vec{a}|^{2}+|\vec{b}|^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}(1)^{2}+|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|^{2}=2\left(1^{2}+1^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow (1)^{2}+|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|^{2}=4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow |\vec{a}-\vec{b}|^{2}=3{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|=\sqrt{3} {/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} are two unit vectors.
Given, {tex}|\vec{a}|=1,|\vec{b}|=1,|\vec{a}+\vec{b}|=1{/tex}
We know that,
{tex}|\vec{a}+\vec{b}|^{2}+|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|^{2}=2\left(|\vec{a}|^{2}+|\vec{b}|^{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}(1)^{2}+|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|^{2}=2\left(1^{2}+1^{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow (1)^{2}+|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|^{2}=4{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow |\vec{a}-\vec{b}|^{2}=3{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|=\sqrt{3} {/tex}

question_html: Find the volume of a cuboid whose edges are given by -3{tex}\hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}{/tex}, {tex}-5 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}7 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex}.
question_text: Find the volume of a cuboid whose edges are given by -3 {tex}\hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}{/tex} , {tex}-5 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}7 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} .
explanation: If a, b, c are edges of a cuboid. Then, volume of cuboid = {tex}\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{c}){/tex} Here, {tex}\vec{a}=-3 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}=-5 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{c}=7 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} Then, {tex}\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{c})=\left|\begin{array}\\ {-3} & {7} & {5} \\ {-5} & {7} & {-3} \\ \;\;{7} & {-5} & {-3}\end{array}\right|{/tex} = -3(-21 - 15) - 7(15 + 21) + 5(25 - 49) = -3 {tex} \times{/tex} (-36) - 7 {tex} \times{/tex} 36 + 5 {tex} \times{/tex} (-24) = 108 - 252 - 120 = -264 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Volume of cuboid = 264 cubic units
solution: If a, b, c are edges of a cuboid. Then, volume of cuboid = {tex}\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{c}){/tex} Here, {tex}\vec{a}=-3 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}=-5 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{c}=7 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} Then, {tex}\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{c})=\left|\begin{array}\\ {-3} & {7} & {5} \\ {-5} & {7} & {-3} \\ \;\;{7} & {-5} & {-3}\end{array}\right|{/tex} = -3(-21 - 15) - 7(15 + 21) + 5(25 - 49) = -3 {tex} \times{/tex} (-36) - 7 {tex} \times{/tex} 36 + 5 {tex} \times{/tex} (-24) = 108 - 252 - 120 = -264 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Volume of cuboid = 264 cubic units
solution_html:
If a, b, c are edges of a cuboid.
Then, volume of cuboid = {tex}\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{c}){/tex}
Here,
{tex}\vec{a}=-3 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{b}=-5 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{c}=7 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex}
Then,
{tex}\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{c})=\left|\begin{array}\\ {-3} & {7} & {5} \\ {-5} & {7} & {-3} \\ \;\;{7} & {-5} & {-3}\end{array}\right|{/tex}
= -3(-21 - 15) - 7(15 + 21) + 5(25 - 49)
= -3{tex} \times{/tex}(-36) - 7 {tex} \times{/tex} 36 + 5 {tex} \times{/tex}(-24)
= 108 - 252 - 120
= -264
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Volume of cuboid = 264 cubic units

question_html: Find a unit vector perpendicular to both the vectors {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}{/tex}, where {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}-7 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex}
question_text: Find a unit vector perpendicular to both the vectors {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} , where {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}-7 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex}
explanation: Given, {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}-7 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} Let {tex}\vec{n}{/tex} be the vector perpendicular to {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} . {tex}\vec{n}=\vec{a} \times \vec{b}{/tex} {tex}\vec{n}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}{\hat{i}} & {\hat{j}} & {\hat{k}} \\ {1} & {-7} & {7} \\ {3} & {-2} & {2}\end{array}\right|=19 \hat{j}+19 \hat{k}{/tex} Now, the unit vector perpendicular to {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} , {tex}\widehat{n}=\frac{19 j+19 k}{\sqrt{19^{2}+19^{2}}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex}
solution: Given, {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}-7 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} Let {tex}\vec{n}{/tex} be the vector perpendicular to {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} . {tex}\vec{n}=\vec{a} \times \vec{b}{/tex} {tex}\vec{n}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}{\hat{i}} & {\hat{j}} & {\hat{k}} \\ {1} & {-7} & {7} \\ {3} & {-2} & {2}\end{array}\right|=19 \hat{j}+19 \hat{k}{/tex} Now, the unit vector perpendicular to {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} , {tex}\widehat{n}=\frac{19 j+19 k}{\sqrt{19^{2}+19^{2}}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex}
solution_html:
Given,
{tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}-7 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex}
Let {tex}\vec{n}{/tex} be the vector perpendicular to {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex}.
{tex}\vec{n}=\vec{a} \times \vec{b}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{n}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}{\hat{i}} & {\hat{j}} & {\hat{k}} \\ {1} & {-7} & {7} \\ {3} & {-2} & {2}\end{array}\right|=19 \hat{j}+19 \hat{k}{/tex}
Now, the unit vector perpendicular to {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex}, {tex}\widehat{n}=\frac{19 j+19 k}{\sqrt{19^{2}+19^{2}}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Show that the vectors {tex}\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}},-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} are coplanar.
question_text: Show that the vectors {tex}\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}},-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} are coplanar.
explanation: Let {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \vec{\mathrm{b}}=-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \vec{\mathrm{c}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}{/tex} are coplanar if {tex} \vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}} \times \vec{\mathrm{c}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}} \times \vec{\mathrm{c}}=\left| \begin{array}{ccc}{1} & {-2} & {3} \\ {-2} & {3} & {-4} \\ {1} & {-3} & {5}\end{array}\right|{/tex} = 1(3) + 2(-6) + 3(3) = 3 - 12 + 9 = 0 Hence {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}{/tex} are coplanar.
solution: Let {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \vec{\mathrm{b}}=-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \vec{\mathrm{c}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}{/tex} are coplanar if {tex} \vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}} \times \vec{\mathrm{c}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}} \times \vec{\mathrm{c}}=\left| \begin{array}{ccc}{1} & {-2} & {3} \\ {-2} & {3} & {-4} \\ {1} & {-3} & {5}\end{array}\right|{/tex} = 1(3) + 2(-6) + 3(3) = 3 - 12 + 9 = 0 Hence {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}{/tex} are coplanar.
solution_html:
Let {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \vec{\mathrm{b}}=-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \vec{\mathrm{c}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}{/tex}  are coplanar if {tex} \vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}} \times \vec{\mathrm{c}} = 0{/tex}
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}} \times \vec{\mathrm{c}}=\left| \begin{array}{ccc}{1} & {-2} & {3} \\ {-2} & {3} & {-4} \\ {1} & {-3} & {5}\end{array}\right|{/tex}
= 1(3) + 2(-6) + 3(3)
= 3 - 12 + 9 = 0
Hence {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}{/tex} are coplanar.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}, 2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+5 \hat{\mathbf{j}}, 3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{\mathrm{i}}-6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} are the position vectors of points A, B, C and D respectively, then find the angle between {tex}\vec {AB}{/tex} and {tex}\vec {CD}{/tex}. Deduce that {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{C D}{/tex} are collinear.
question_text: If {tex}\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}, 2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+5 \hat{\mathbf{j}}, 3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{\mathrm{i}}-6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} are the position vectors of points A, B, C and D respectively, then find the angle between {tex}\vec {AB}{/tex} and {tex}\vec {CD}{/tex} . Deduce that {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{C D}{/tex} are collinear.
explanation: {tex}A(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}), \mathrm{B}(2 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}), \mathrm{C}(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}D(\hat{i}-6 \hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\begin{aligned} \overrightarrow{A B} &=(2-1) \hat{\imath}+(5-1) \hat{\jmath}+(0-1) \hat{k} \\ &=1 \hat{\imath}+4 \hat{\jmath}-\hat{k} \end{aligned}{/tex} {tex}\begin{aligned} \overrightarrow{C D} &=(1-3) \hat{\imath}+(-6-2) \hat{\jmath}+(-1-(-3)) \hat{k} \\ &=-2 \hat{\imath}-8 \hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{k} \end{aligned}{/tex} Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between {tex}\vec{\mathrm{AB}} {/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{CD}}{/tex} So, {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{\overrightarrow{A B} \cdot \overrightarrow{C D}}{|\overrightarrow{A B}||\overrightarrow{C D}|}{/tex} {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{(\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-\hat{k}) \cdot(-2 \hat{i}-8 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{1+16+1} \sqrt{4+64+4}}{/tex} {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{-2-32-2}{\sqrt{18} \sqrt{72}}{/tex} {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{-36}{\sqrt{2} \cdot 6 \sqrt{2}}{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = -1 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = cos {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\pi{/tex} Since {tex}0 \leq \theta \leq \pi{/tex} it follows that {tex}\theta=\pi{/tex} . This shows that {tex}\vec {AB}{/tex} and {tex}\vec {CD}{/tex} are collinear.
solution: {tex}A(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}), \mathrm{B}(2 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}), \mathrm{C}(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}D(\hat{i}-6 \hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\begin{aligned} \overrightarrow{A B} &=(2-1) \hat{\imath}+(5-1) \hat{\jmath}+(0-1) \hat{k} \\ &=1 \hat{\imath}+4 \hat{\jmath}-\hat{k} \end{aligned}{/tex} {tex}\begin{aligned} \overrightarrow{C D} &=(1-3) \hat{\imath}+(-6-2) \hat{\jmath}+(-1-(-3)) \hat{k} \\ &=-2 \hat{\imath}-8 \hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{k} \end{aligned}{/tex} Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between {tex}\vec{\mathrm{AB}} {/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{CD}}{/tex} So, {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{\overrightarrow{A B} \cdot \overrightarrow{C D}}{|\overrightarrow{A B}||\overrightarrow{C D}|}{/tex} {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{(\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-\hat{k}) \cdot(-2 \hat{i}-8 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{1+16+1} \sqrt{4+64+4}}{/tex} {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{-2-32-2}{\sqrt{18} \sqrt{72}}{/tex} {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{-36}{\sqrt{2} \cdot 6 \sqrt{2}}{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = -1 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = cos {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\pi{/tex} Since {tex}0 \leq \theta \leq \pi{/tex} it follows that {tex}\theta=\pi{/tex} . This shows that {tex}\vec {AB}{/tex} and {tex}\vec {CD}{/tex} are collinear.
solution_html:
{tex}A(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}), \mathrm{B}(2 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}), \mathrm{C}(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}D(\hat{i}-6 \hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex}
 {tex}\begin{aligned} \overrightarrow{A B} &=(2-1) \hat{\imath}+(5-1) \hat{\jmath}+(0-1) \hat{k} \\ &=1 \hat{\imath}+4 \hat{\jmath}-\hat{k} \end{aligned}{/tex}
{tex}\begin{aligned} \overrightarrow{C D} &=(1-3) \hat{\imath}+(-6-2) \hat{\jmath}+(-1-(-3)) \hat{k} \\ &=-2 \hat{\imath}-8 \hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{k} \end{aligned}{/tex}
Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between {tex}\vec{\mathrm{AB}} {/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{CD}}{/tex}
So, {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{\overrightarrow{A B} \cdot \overrightarrow{C D}}{|\overrightarrow{A B}||\overrightarrow{C D}|}{/tex}
{tex}\cos \theta=\frac{(\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-\hat{k}) \cdot(-2 \hat{i}-8 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{1+16+1} \sqrt{4+64+4}}{/tex}
{tex}\cos \theta=\frac{-2-32-2}{\sqrt{18} \sqrt{72}}{/tex}
{tex}\cos \theta=\frac{-36}{\sqrt{2} \cdot 6 \sqrt{2}}{/tex}
cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = -1
cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = cos {tex}\pi{/tex}
{tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\pi{/tex} 
Since {tex}0 \leq \theta \leq \pi{/tex} it follows that {tex}\theta=\pi{/tex}. This shows that {tex}\vec {AB}{/tex} and {tex}\vec {CD}{/tex} are collinear.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}|\vec{a}|{/tex} = 2, {tex}|\vec{b}|{/tex} = 7 and {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}{/tex}, find the angle between {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}|\vec{a}|{/tex} = 2, {tex}|\vec{b}|{/tex} = 7 and {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}{/tex} , find the angle between {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} .
explanation: Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} . {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}{/tex} (Given) {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=\sqrt{9+4+36}{/tex} = 7 We know that , {tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=|\vec{a}||\vec{b}| \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 7=(2)(7) \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sin \theta=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} . {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}{/tex} (Given) {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=\sqrt{9+4+36}{/tex} = 7 We know that , {tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=|\vec{a}||\vec{b}| \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 7=(2)(7) \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sin \theta=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex}.
{tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}{/tex} (Given)
{tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=\sqrt{9+4+36}{/tex} = 7
We know that ,
{tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=|\vec{a}||\vec{b}| \sin \theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 7=(2)(7) \sin \theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sin \theta=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Let {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}{/tex}be three vectors such that {tex}|\vec{a}|=1,| \vec{b} |=2 \text { and }|\vec{c}|=3 . {/tex} If the projection of {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} along {tex}\vec{a} \text { and } \vec{b}, \vec{c}{/tex}are perpendicular to each other, find {tex}|3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}+2 \vec{c}|{/tex}
question_text: Let {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}{/tex} be three vectors such that {tex}|\vec{a}|=1,| \vec{b} |=2 \text { and }|\vec{c}|=3 . {/tex} If the projection of {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} along {tex}\vec{a} \text { and } \vec{b}, \vec{c}{/tex} are perpendicular to each other, find {tex}|3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}+2 \vec{c}|{/tex}
explanation: It is given that projection of {tex}\vec{b}\space along \space\vec{a}={/tex} projection of {tex}\vec{c} \text { along } \vec{a}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\vec{b} \cdot \vec{a}}{|\vec{a}|}=\frac{\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}}{|\vec{a}|} \Rightarrow \vec{b} \cdot \vec{a}=\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a} \Rightarrow \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}{/tex} ...(i) Also, {tex}\vec{b}\space and \space\vec{c}{/tex} are perpendicular to each other. {tex}\therefore \quad \vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}=0{/tex} ...(ii) Now, {tex}|3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}+2 \vec{c}|^{2}=9|\vec{a}|^{2}+4|\vec{b}|^{2}+4|\vec{c}|^{2}+2\{-6(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})-4(\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c})+6(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c})\}{/tex} {tex}|3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}+2 \vec{c}|^{2}=9 \times 1^{2}+4 \times 2^{2}+4 \times 3^{2}+2\{-6(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})-4 \times 0+6(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})\}{/tex} {tex}|3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}+2 \vec{c}|^{2}=9+16+36+2 \times 0=61{/tex} {tex}|3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}+2 \vec{c}|=\sqrt{61}{/tex}
solution: It is given that projection of {tex}\vec{b}\space along \space\vec{a}={/tex} projection of {tex}\vec{c} \text { along } \vec{a}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\vec{b} \cdot \vec{a}}{|\vec{a}|}=\frac{\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}}{|\vec{a}|} \Rightarrow \vec{b} \cdot \vec{a}=\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a} \Rightarrow \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}{/tex} ...(i) Also, {tex}\vec{b}\space and \space\vec{c}{/tex} are perpendicular to each other. {tex}\therefore \quad \vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}=0{/tex} ...(ii) Now, {tex}|3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}+2 \vec{c}|^{2}=9|\vec{a}|^{2}+4|\vec{b}|^{2}+4|\vec{c}|^{2}+2\{-6(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})-4(\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c})+6(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c})\}{/tex} {tex}|3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}+2 \vec{c}|^{2}=9 \times 1^{2}+4 \times 2^{2}+4 \times 3^{2}+2\{-6(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})-4 \times 0+6(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})\}{/tex} {tex}|3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}+2 \vec{c}|^{2}=9+16+36+2 \times 0=61{/tex} {tex}|3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}+2 \vec{c}|=\sqrt{61}{/tex}
solution_html:
It is given that
projection of {tex}\vec{b}\space along \space\vec{a}={/tex} projection of {tex}\vec{c} \text { along } \vec{a}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\vec{b} \cdot \vec{a}}{|\vec{a}|}=\frac{\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}}{|\vec{a}|} \Rightarrow \vec{b} \cdot \vec{a}=\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a} \Rightarrow \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}{/tex} ...(i)
Also, {tex}\vec{b}\space and \space\vec{c}{/tex}are perpendicular to each other.
{tex}\therefore \quad \vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}=0{/tex} ...(ii)
Now,
{tex}|3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}+2 \vec{c}|^{2}=9|\vec{a}|^{2}+4|\vec{b}|^{2}+4|\vec{c}|^{2}+2\{-6(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})-4(\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c})+6(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c})\}{/tex}
{tex}|3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}+2 \vec{c}|^{2}=9 \times 1^{2}+4 \times 2^{2}+4 \times 3^{2}+2\{-6(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})-4 \times 0+6(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})\}{/tex}
{tex}|3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}+2 \vec{c}|^{2}=9+16+36+2 \times 0=61{/tex}
{tex}|3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}+2 \vec{c}|=\sqrt{61}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k}, \vec{b}=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}=-3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex}, find {tex}[\vec{a}\; \vec{b}\; \vec{c}]{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k}, \vec{b}=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}=-3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} , find {tex}[\vec{a}\; \vec{b}\; \vec{c}]{/tex}
explanation: Given: {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k}, \vec{b}=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}=-3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} , {tex}[\vec{a}\; \vec{b}\; \vec{c}]=[\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{c})]{/tex} {tex}=\left|\begin{array}{rrr}{2} & {3} & {1} \\ {1} & {-2} & {1} \\ {-3} & {1} & {2}\end{array}\right|{/tex} = 2(-4 - 1) - 3(2 + 3) + 1(1 - 6) = -10 - 15 - 5 = -30
solution: Given: {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k}, \vec{b}=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}=-3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} , {tex}[\vec{a}\; \vec{b}\; \vec{c}]=[\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{c})]{/tex} {tex}=\left|\begin{array}{rrr}{2} & {3} & {1} \\ {1} & {-2} & {1} \\ {-3} & {1} & {2}\end{array}\right|{/tex} = 2(-4 - 1) - 3(2 + 3) + 1(1 - 6) = -10 - 15 - 5 = -30
solution_html:
Given: {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k}, \vec{b}=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}=-3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex},
{tex}[\vec{a}\; \vec{b}\; \vec{c}]=[\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{c})]{/tex}
{tex}=\left|\begin{array}{rrr}{2} & {3} & {1} \\ {1} & {-2} & {1} \\ {-3} & {1} & {2}\end{array}\right|{/tex}
= 2(-4 - 1) - 3(2 + 3) + 1(1 - 6)
= -10 - 15 - 5
= -30

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}|\vec{a}|=2,|\vec{b}|=7{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}{/tex} find the angle between {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}|\vec{a}|=2,|\vec{b}|=7{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}{/tex} find the angle between {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} .
explanation: Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} , then {tex}\sin \theta=\frac{|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|}{|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|}{/tex} ...(i) Then, {tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=\sqrt{(3)^{2}+(2)^{2}+(6)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{49}{/tex} = 7 {tex}\therefore \quad \sin \theta=\frac{7}{2 \times 7}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin \theta=\sin \frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} , then {tex}\sin \theta=\frac{|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|}{|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|}{/tex} ...(i) Then, {tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=\sqrt{(3)^{2}+(2)^{2}+(6)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{49}{/tex} = 7 {tex}\therefore \quad \sin \theta=\frac{7}{2 \times 7}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin \theta=\sin \frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}{/tex}, then
{tex}\sin \theta=\frac{|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|}{|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|}{/tex} ...(i)
Then, {tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=\sqrt{(3)^{2}+(2)^{2}+(6)^{2}}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{49}{/tex} = 7
{tex}\therefore \quad \sin \theta=\frac{7}{2 \times 7}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin \theta=\sin \frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}

question_text: X and Y are two points with position vectors {tex}3 \vec{a}+\vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a}-3 \vec{b}{/tex} respectively. Write the position vector of a point Z which divides the line segment XY in the ratio 2 : 1 externally.
explanation: Position vector of z = {tex}\frac{2(\vec{a}-3 \vec{b})-1(3 \vec{a}+\vec{b})}{2-1}{/tex} = {tex}-\vec{a}-7 \vec{b}{/tex} , This is the required solution.

question_html: Find a unit vector perpendicular to both {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}{/tex}, where {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=4 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+8 \hat{k}, \vec{b}=-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex}.
question_text: Find a unit vector perpendicular to both {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} , where {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=4 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+8 \hat{k}, \vec{b}=-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} .
explanation: Here we have {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=4 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+8 \hat{k}, \vec{b}=-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 4 & -1 & 8 \\ 0 & -1 & 1\end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}7 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}{/tex} Required unit vector = {tex}\frac{(\vec{a} \times \vec{b})}{|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{9}(7 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}){/tex}
solution: Here we have {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=4 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+8 \hat{k}, \vec{b}=-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 4 & -1 & 8 \\ 0 & -1 & 1\end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}7 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}{/tex} Required unit vector = {tex}\frac{(\vec{a} \times \vec{b})}{|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{9}(7 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}){/tex}
solution_html:
Here we have {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=4 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+8 \hat{k}, \vec{b}=-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}{/tex}={tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 4 & -1 & 8 \\ 0 & -1 & 1\end{array}\right|{/tex}= {tex}7 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}{/tex}
Required unit vector = {tex}\frac{(\vec{a} \times \vec{b})}{|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{9}(7 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Let {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}{/tex} be three vectors such that {tex}|\vec{a}|=1,|\vec{b}|=2{/tex} and {tex}|\vec{c}|=3{/tex}. If the projection of {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} along {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} is equal to the projection of {tex}\vec{c}{/tex} along {tex}\vec{a}{/tex}; and {tex}\vec{b}, \vec{c}{/tex} are perpendicular to each other, then find {tex}|3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}+2 \vec{c}|{/tex}.
question_text: Let {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}{/tex} be three vectors such that {tex}|\vec{a}|=1,|\vec{b}|=2{/tex} and {tex}|\vec{c}|=3{/tex} . If the projection of {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} along {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} is equal to the projection of {tex}\vec{c}{/tex} along {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} ; and {tex}\vec{b}, \vec{c}{/tex} are perpendicular to each other, then find {tex}|3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}+2 \vec{c}|{/tex} .
explanation: Given {tex}\frac{\vec{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}}{|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|}=\frac{\vec{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}}{|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}=\vec{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}} \perp \vec{\mathrm{c}} \Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{c}} = 0{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}(|3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}+2 \vec{c}|)^{2}{/tex} {tex}=9|\vec{a}|^{2}+4|\vec{b}|^{2}+4|\vec{c}|^{2}-12 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}-8 \vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+12 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}{/tex} = 9(1) 2 + 4(2) 2 + 4(3) 2 [using (i) and (ii)] = 9 + 16 + 36 = 61 {tex}\Rightarrow|3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}+2 \vec{c}|=\sqrt{61}{/tex}
solution: Given {tex}\frac{\vec{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}}{|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|}=\frac{\vec{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}}{|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}=\vec{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}} \perp \vec{\mathrm{c}} \Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{c}} = 0{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}(|3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}+2 \vec{c}|)^{2}{/tex} {tex}=9|\vec{a}|^{2}+4|\vec{b}|^{2}+4|\vec{c}|^{2}-12 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}-8 \vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+12 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}{/tex} = 9(1) 2 + 4(2) 2 + 4(3) 2 [using (i) and (ii)] = 9 + 16 + 36 = 61 {tex}\Rightarrow|3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}+2 \vec{c}|=\sqrt{61}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given {tex}\frac{\vec{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}}{|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|}=\frac{\vec{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}}{|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}=\vec{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}} \perp \vec{\mathrm{c}} \Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{c}} = 0{/tex} ...(ii)
{tex}(|3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}+2 \vec{c}|)^{2}{/tex}{tex}=9|\vec{a}|^{2}+4|\vec{b}|^{2}+4|\vec{c}|^{2}-12 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}-8 \vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+12 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}{/tex}
= 9(1)2 + 4(2)2 + 4(3)2 [using (i) and (ii)]
= 9 + 16 + 36 = 61
{tex}\Rightarrow|3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}+2 \vec{c}|=\sqrt{61}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Let {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}  be two vectors. Show that the vectors {tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b}){/tex} and {tex}(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex} are perpendicular to each other.
question_text: Let {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} be two vectors. Show that the vectors {tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b}){/tex} and {tex}(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex} are perpendicular to each other.
explanation: We have, {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}=4 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \cdot(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex} = 8 + 5 + 3 = 0 Therefore {tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \perp(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex}
solution: We have, {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}=4 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \cdot(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex} = 8 + 5 + 3 = 0 Therefore {tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \perp(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex}
solution_html:
We have,{tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}=4 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \cdot(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex} = 8 + 5 + 3 = 0
Therefore {tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \perp(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Using vectors, prove that the points (2, -1, 3), (3, -5, 1) and (-1, 11, 9) are collinear.
question_text: Using vectors, prove that the points (2, -1, 3), (3, -5, 1) and (-1, 11, 9) are collinear.
explanation: Let P(2, -1,3), Q(3, -5,1) and R(-1,11, 9) be the three points given. Then we have {tex}\overrightarrow{{PQ}}=\hat{{i}}-4 \hat{{j}}-2 \hat{{k}}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{PR}}=-3 \hat{{i}}+12 \hat{{j}}+6 \hat{{k}}{/tex} = {tex}-3(\hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{PR}}=-3 \overrightarrow{{PQ}}{/tex} , since P is common. Therefore the points P, Q and R are collinear.
solution: Let P(2, -1,3), Q(3, -5,1) and R(-1,11, 9) be the three points given. Then we have {tex}\overrightarrow{{PQ}}=\hat{{i}}-4 \hat{{j}}-2 \hat{{k}}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{PR}}=-3 \hat{{i}}+12 \hat{{j}}+6 \hat{{k}}{/tex} = {tex}-3(\hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{PR}}=-3 \overrightarrow{{PQ}}{/tex} , since P is common. Therefore the points P, Q and R are collinear.
solution_html:
Let P(2, -1,3), Q(3, -5,1) and R(-1,11, 9) be the three points given. Then we have
{tex}\overrightarrow{{PQ}}=\hat{{i}}-4 \hat{{j}}-2 \hat{{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{{PR}}=-3 \hat{{i}}+12 \hat{{j}}+6 \hat{{k}}{/tex}
= {tex}-3(\hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{PR}}=-3 \overrightarrow{{PQ}}{/tex},  since P is common.
Therefore the points P, Q and R are collinear.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Show that the four points A, B, C and D with position vectors {tex}\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}, 3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}, 2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} respectively are coplanar.
question_text: Show that the four points A, B, C and D with position vectors {tex}\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}, 3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}, 2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} respectively are coplanar.
explanation: We need to show that the four points corresponding to four given position vectors {tex}\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}, 3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}, 2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} respectively are coplanar. Here we have, A(1, 2, -1), B(3, -1, 0), C(2, 3, 2), D(4, 0, 3) Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{{AB}}=2 \hat{{i}}-3 \hat{{j}}+\hat{{k}}{/tex} , {tex}\overrightarrow{{AC}}=\hat{{i}}+\hat{{j}}+3 \hat{{k}}{/tex} , {tex}\overrightarrow{{AD}}=3 \hat{{i}}-2 \hat{{j}}+4 \hat{{k}}{/tex} So, {tex}[\overrightarrow{{AB}} \overrightarrow{{AC}} \overrightarrow{{AD}}]{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{rrr}2 & -3 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 3 \\ 3 & -2 & 4\end{array}\right|{/tex} = 2(10) +3(-5) +1(-5) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A, B, C and D are coplanar
solution: We need to show that the four points corresponding to four given position vectors {tex}\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}, 3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}, 2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} respectively are coplanar. Here we have, A(1, 2, -1), B(3, -1, 0), C(2, 3, 2), D(4, 0, 3) Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{{AB}}=2 \hat{{i}}-3 \hat{{j}}+\hat{{k}}{/tex} , {tex}\overrightarrow{{AC}}=\hat{{i}}+\hat{{j}}+3 \hat{{k}}{/tex} , {tex}\overrightarrow{{AD}}=3 \hat{{i}}-2 \hat{{j}}+4 \hat{{k}}{/tex} So, {tex}[\overrightarrow{{AB}} \overrightarrow{{AC}} \overrightarrow{{AD}}]{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{rrr}2 & -3 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 3 \\ 3 & -2 & 4\end{array}\right|{/tex} = 2(10) +3(-5) +1(-5) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A, B, C and D are coplanar
solution_html:
We need to show that the four points corresponding to four given position vectors {tex}\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}, 3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}, 2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} respectively are coplanar.
Here we have,
A(1, 2, -1), B(3, -1, 0), C(2, 3, 2), D(4, 0, 3)
Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{{AB}}=2 \hat{{i}}-3 \hat{{j}}+\hat{{k}}{/tex}, {tex}\overrightarrow{{AC}}=\hat{{i}}+\hat{{j}}+3 \hat{{k}}{/tex}, {tex}\overrightarrow{{AD}}=3 \hat{{i}}-2 \hat{{j}}+4 \hat{{k}}{/tex}
So, {tex}[\overrightarrow{{AB}} \overrightarrow{{AC}} \overrightarrow{{AD}}]{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{rrr}2 & -3 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 3 \\ 3 & -2 & 4\end{array}\right|{/tex} = 2(10) +3(-5) +1(-5)  = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A, B, C and D are coplanar
