subject: Mathematics
question_html: Show that the points {tex}A(-2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}), B(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}C(7 \hat{i}-\hat{k}){/tex} are collinear.
question_text: Show that the points {tex}A(-2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}), B(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}C(7 \hat{i}-\hat{k}){/tex} are collinear.
explanation: Therefore,we have {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}{/tex} =(position vector of B) - (position vector of A) = {tex}(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})-(-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k})=(3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{B C}{/tex} =(position vector of C) - (position vector of B) = (7i - 3k) - (i + 2j + 3k) = (6i - 2j - 4k) {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}=2 \overrightarrow{B C}{/tex} , which shows that {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AB}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{B C}{/tex} are parallel vectors, having a common end point B. Therefore, the points A, B and C are collinear.
solution: Therefore,we have {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}{/tex} =(position vector of B) - (position vector of A) = {tex}(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})-(-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k})=(3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{B C}{/tex} =(position vector of C) - (position vector of B) = (7i - 3k) - (i + 2j + 3k) = (6i - 2j - 4k) {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}=2 \overrightarrow{B C}{/tex} , which shows that {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AB}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{B C}{/tex} are parallel vectors, having a common end point B. Therefore, the points A, B and C are collinear.
solution_html:
Therefore,we have
{tex}\overrightarrow{A B}{/tex} =(position vector of B) - (position vector of A)
= {tex}(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})-(-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k})=(3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{B C}{/tex} =(position vector of C) - (position vector of B)
= (7i - 3k) - (i + 2j + 3k) = (6i - 2j - 4k)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}=2 \overrightarrow{B C}{/tex} , which shows that {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AB}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{B C}{/tex} are parallel vectors, having a common end point B. 
Therefore, the points A, B and C are collinear.

question_html: For any two vectors {tex}\vec{a} {/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex}, prove that {tex}(\vec{a} \times \vec{b})^{2}=\vec{{a}^{2}} \vec{{b}^{2}}-(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})^{2}{/tex}.
question_text: For any two vectors {tex}\vec{a} {/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} , prove that {tex}(\vec{a} \times \vec{b})^{2}=\vec{{a}^{2}} \vec{{b}^{2}}-(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})^{2}{/tex} .
explanation: (a {tex}\times{/tex} b) 2 = (ab sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ) 2 = a 2 b 2 sin 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} = a 2 b 2 (1 - cos 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} ) = a 2 b 2 - a 2 b 2 cos 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} = a 2 b 2 - (a {tex}\cdot{/tex} b) 2
solution: (a {tex}\times{/tex} b) 2 = (ab sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ) 2 = a 2 b 2 sin 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} = a 2 b 2 (1 - cos 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} ) = a 2 b 2 - a 2 b 2 cos 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} = a 2 b 2 - (a {tex}\cdot{/tex} b) 2
solution_html:
(a {tex}\times{/tex} b)2 = (ab sin{tex}\theta{/tex})2
= a2b2 sin2{tex}\theta{/tex}
= a2b2 (1 - cos2{tex}\theta{/tex})
= a2b2 - a2b2 cos2{tex}\theta{/tex}
= a2b2 - (a{tex}\cdot{/tex}b)2

question_html: Find the value of x such that the four points with position vectors, {tex}\mathrm{A}(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}), {/tex} {tex}\mathrm{B}(4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{x} \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}),{/tex} {tex} \mathrm{C}(4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{D}(6 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} are coplanar.
question_text: Find the value of x such that the four points with position vectors, {tex}\mathrm{A}(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}), {/tex} {tex}\mathrm{B}(4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{x} \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}),{/tex} {tex} \mathrm{C}(4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{D}(6 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} are coplanar.
explanation: Here {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AB}}=\hat{i}+(x-2) \hat{j}+4 \hat{k},{/tex} {tex} \overrightarrow{A C}=\hat{i}-3 \hat{k}, \overrightarrow{A D}=3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex} As A, B, C, D are coplanar {tex}\therefore \left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & \mathrm{x}-2 & 4 \\ 1 & 0 & -3 \\ 3 & 3 & -2\end{array}\right|=0{/tex} -9 - (2 - x)7 - 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 0 x = 5
solution: Here {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AB}}=\hat{i}+(x-2) \hat{j}+4 \hat{k},{/tex} {tex} \overrightarrow{A C}=\hat{i}-3 \hat{k}, \overrightarrow{A D}=3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex} As A, B, C, D are coplanar {tex}\therefore \left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & \mathrm{x}-2 & 4 \\ 1 & 0 & -3 \\ 3 & 3 & -2\end{array}\right|=0{/tex} -9 - (2 - x)7 - 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 0 x = 5
solution_html:
Here {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AB}}=\hat{i}+(x-2) \hat{j}+4 \hat{k},{/tex} {tex} \overrightarrow{A C}=\hat{i}-3 \hat{k}, \overrightarrow{A D}=3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex}
As A, B, C, D are coplanar
{tex}\therefore \left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & \mathrm{x}-2 & 4 \\ 1 & 0 & -3 \\ 3 & 3 & -2\end{array}\right|=0{/tex}
-9 - (2 - x)7 - 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 0
x = 5

subject: Mathematics
question_html: The scalar product of the vector {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} with a unit vector along the sum of the vectors {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}=\lambda \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} is equal to 1. Find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} and hence find the unit vector along {tex}\vec{b}+\vec{c}{/tex}.
question_text: The scalar product of the vector {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} with a unit vector along the sum of the vectors {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}=\lambda \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} is equal to 1. Find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} and hence find the unit vector along {tex}\vec{b}+\vec{c}{/tex} .
explanation: We have, {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+k, \vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k} \text { and } \vec{c}=\lambda \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 k . \text { Then }{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}+\vec{c}=(2+\lambda) \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\text { Let } \hat{r}{/tex} denote the unit vector along {tex}\vec{b}+\vec{c} .{/tex} Then, {tex}\hat{r}=\frac{\vec{b}+\vec{c}}{|\vec{b}+\vec{k}|}=\frac{(2+\lambda) \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{(2+\lambda)^{2}+36+4}}=\frac{(2+\lambda) \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{(2+\lambda)^{2}+40}}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\text { Now, } (\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \cdot \hat{r}=1{/tex} {given} {tex}\Rightarrow (\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \cdot \frac{(2+\lambda) \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{(2+\lambda)^{2}+40}}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow (\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \cdot\{(2+\lambda) \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}\}=\sqrt{(2+\lambda)^{2}+40}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2+\lambda+6-2=\sqrt{(2+\lambda)^{2}+40}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow (\lambda+6)^{2}=(2+\lambda)^{2}+40{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 8 \lambda=8 \Rightarrow \lambda=1{/tex} {tex}\text { Putting } \lambda=1 \text { in (i), we obtain } \hat{r}=\frac{1}{7}(3 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}){/tex}
solution: We have, {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+k, \vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k} \text { and } \vec{c}=\lambda \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 k . \text { Then }{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}+\vec{c}=(2+\lambda) \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\text { Let } \hat{r}{/tex} denote the unit vector along {tex}\vec{b}+\vec{c} .{/tex} Then, {tex}\hat{r}=\frac{\vec{b}+\vec{c}}{|\vec{b}+\vec{k}|}=\frac{(2+\lambda) \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{(2+\lambda)^{2}+36+4}}=\frac{(2+\lambda) \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{(2+\lambda)^{2}+40}}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\text { Now, } (\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \cdot \hat{r}=1{/tex} {given} {tex}\Rightarrow (\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \cdot \frac{(2+\lambda) \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{(2+\lambda)^{2}+40}}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow (\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \cdot\{(2+\lambda) \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}\}=\sqrt{(2+\lambda)^{2}+40}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2+\lambda+6-2=\sqrt{(2+\lambda)^{2}+40}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow (\lambda+6)^{2}=(2+\lambda)^{2}+40{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 8 \lambda=8 \Rightarrow \lambda=1{/tex} {tex}\text { Putting } \lambda=1 \text { in (i), we obtain } \hat{r}=\frac{1}{7}(3 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}){/tex}
solution_html:
We have,
{tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+k, \vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k} \text { and } \vec{c}=\lambda \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 k . \text { Then }{/tex}
{tex}\vec{b}+\vec{c}=(2+\lambda) \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\text { Let } \hat{r}{/tex}  denote the unit vector along  {tex}\vec{b}+\vec{c} .{/tex} Then,
{tex}\hat{r}=\frac{\vec{b}+\vec{c}}{|\vec{b}+\vec{k}|}=\frac{(2+\lambda) \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{(2+\lambda)^{2}+36+4}}=\frac{(2+\lambda) \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{(2+\lambda)^{2}+40}}{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\text { Now, } (\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \cdot \hat{r}=1{/tex}   {given}
{tex}\Rightarrow (\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \cdot \frac{(2+\lambda) \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{(2+\lambda)^{2}+40}}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow (\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \cdot\{(2+\lambda) \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}\}=\sqrt{(2+\lambda)^{2}+40}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2+\lambda+6-2=\sqrt{(2+\lambda)^{2}+40}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow (\lambda+6)^{2}=(2+\lambda)^{2}+40{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 8 \lambda=8 \Rightarrow \lambda=1{/tex}
{tex}\text { Putting } \lambda=1 \text { in (i), we obtain } \hat{r}=\frac{1}{7}(3 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the value of x such that the points A(3,2,1), B(4,x,5), C(4,2,-2) and D(6, 5,-1) are coplanar.
question_text: Find the value of x such that the points A(3,2,1), B(4,x,5), C(4,2,-2) and D(6, 5,-1) are coplanar.
explanation: According to the question , A= (3,2,1), B= (4,x,5), C =(4,2,-2) and D = (6,5,-1). Now , {tex} \vec { A B } = \hat { i } + ( x - 2 ) \hat{j } + 4 \hat { k } {/tex} {tex} \vec { A C } = \hat { i } - 3 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { A D } = 3 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k }{/tex} Since, {tex}\vec { A B } , \vec { A C }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { A D }{/tex} are coplanar {tex}\therefore [ \vec { A B } \ \vec { A C } \ \vec { A D } ] = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left| \begin{array} { c c } { 1 } & { ( x - 2 ) } & { 4 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } & { - 3 } \\ { 3 } & { 3 } & { - 2 } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1 ( 0 + 9 ) - (x-2)(-2+ 9) + 4 (3- 0) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9- (x - 2)(7) + 12 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9 - 7x + 14 + 12 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 35 - 7x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x = \frac { 35 } { 7 } = 5{/tex}
solution: According to the question , A= (3,2,1), B= (4,x,5), C =(4,2,-2) and D = (6,5,-1). Now , {tex} \vec { A B } = \hat { i } + ( x - 2 ) \hat{j } + 4 \hat { k } {/tex} {tex} \vec { A C } = \hat { i } - 3 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { A D } = 3 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k }{/tex} Since, {tex}\vec { A B } , \vec { A C }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { A D }{/tex} are coplanar {tex}\therefore [ \vec { A B } \ \vec { A C } \ \vec { A D } ] = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left| \begin{array} { c c } { 1 } & { ( x - 2 ) } & { 4 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } & { - 3 } \\ { 3 } & { 3 } & { - 2 } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1 ( 0 + 9 ) - (x-2)(-2+ 9) + 4 (3- 0) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9- (x - 2)(7) + 12 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9 - 7x + 14 + 12 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 35 - 7x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x = \frac { 35 } { 7 } = 5{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question ,

 A= (3,2,1),

B= (4,x,5),

C =(4,2,-2) and

D = (6,5,-1).
Now ,

{tex} \vec { A B } = \hat { i } + ( x - 2 ) \hat{j } + 4 \hat { k } {/tex}

{tex} \vec { A C } = \hat { i } - 3 \hat { k }{/tex} and 

{tex}\vec { A D } = 3 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k }{/tex}
Since, {tex}\vec { A B } , \vec { A C }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { A D }{/tex} are coplanar
{tex}\therefore [ \vec { A B } \ \vec { A C } \ \vec { A D } ] = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left| \begin{array} { c c } { 1 } & { ( x - 2 ) } & { 4 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } & { - 3 } \\ { 3 } & { 3 } & { - 2 } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1 ( 0 + 9 ) - (x-2)(-2+ 9) + 4 (3- 0) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9- (x - 2)(7) + 12 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9 - 7x + 14 + 12 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 35 - 7x = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}  {tex}x = \frac { 35 } { 7 } = 5{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Let {tex}\vec { a } = 4 \hat { i } + 5 \hat { j } - \hat { k }{/tex}, {tex}\vec { b } = \hat { i } - 4 \hat { j } + 5 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c } = 3 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - \hat { k }{/tex}. Find a vector {tex}\overrightarrow { \mathrm { d } }{/tex} which is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec { c }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } {/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow { \mathrm { d } } \cdot \overrightarrow { \mathrm { a } } = 21{/tex}.
question_text: Let {tex}\vec { a } = 4 \hat { i } + 5 \hat { j } - \hat { k }{/tex} , {tex}\vec { b } = \hat { i } - 4 \hat { j } + 5 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c } = 3 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - \hat { k }{/tex} . Find a vector {tex}\overrightarrow { \mathrm { d } }{/tex} which is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec { c }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } {/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow { \mathrm { d } } \cdot \overrightarrow { \mathrm { a } } = 21{/tex} .
explanation: According to the question, {tex}\vec { a } = 4 \hat { i } + 5 \hat { j } - \hat { k } , \vec { b } = \hat { i } - 4 \hat { j } + 5 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c } = 3 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - \hat { k }{/tex} Since {tex}\vec { d }{/tex} is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec { c } \text { and } \vec { b }{/tex} . {tex}\therefore \vec { d } = \lambda ( \vec { c } \times \vec { b } ) = \lambda \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { 3 } & { 1 } & { - 1 } \\ { 1 } & { - 4 } & { 5 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \lambda [ \hat { i } ( 5 - 4 ) - \hat { j } ( 15 + 1 ) + \hat { k } ( - 12 - 1 ) ]{/tex} {tex}= \lambda ( \hat { i } - 16 \hat { j } - 13 \hat { k } ){/tex} .......(i) According to the question, {tex}\vec { d } \cdot \vec { a } = 21{/tex} {tex}\therefore \lambda ( \hat { i } - 16 \hat { j } - 13 \hat { k } ) \cdot ( 4 \hat { i } + 5 \hat { j } - \hat { k } ) = 21{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda ( 4 - 80 + 13 ) = 21{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\lambda ( - 63 ) = 21{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda = \frac { - 1 } { 3 }{/tex} Now, from Eq.(i), we get {tex}\therefore \vec { d } = - \frac { 1 } { 3 } ( \hat { i } - 16 \hat { j } - 13 \hat { k } ){/tex}
solution: According to the question, {tex}\vec { a } = 4 \hat { i } + 5 \hat { j } - \hat { k } , \vec { b } = \hat { i } - 4 \hat { j } + 5 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c } = 3 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - \hat { k }{/tex} Since {tex}\vec { d }{/tex} is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec { c } \text { and } \vec { b }{/tex} . {tex}\therefore \vec { d } = \lambda ( \vec { c } \times \vec { b } ) = \lambda \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { 3 } & { 1 } & { - 1 } \\ { 1 } & { - 4 } & { 5 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \lambda [ \hat { i } ( 5 - 4 ) - \hat { j } ( 15 + 1 ) + \hat { k } ( - 12 - 1 ) ]{/tex} {tex}= \lambda ( \hat { i } - 16 \hat { j } - 13 \hat { k } ){/tex} .......(i) According to the question, {tex}\vec { d } \cdot \vec { a } = 21{/tex} {tex}\therefore \lambda ( \hat { i } - 16 \hat { j } - 13 \hat { k } ) \cdot ( 4 \hat { i } + 5 \hat { j } - \hat { k } ) = 21{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda ( 4 - 80 + 13 ) = 21{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\lambda ( - 63 ) = 21{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda = \frac { - 1 } { 3 }{/tex} Now, from Eq.(i), we get {tex}\therefore \vec { d } = - \frac { 1 } { 3 } ( \hat { i } - 16 \hat { j } - 13 \hat { k } ){/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question,  {tex}\vec { a } = 4 \hat { i } + 5 \hat { j } - \hat { k } , \vec { b } = \hat { i } - 4 \hat { j } + 5 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c } = 3 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - \hat { k }{/tex}
Since {tex}\vec { d }{/tex} is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec { c } \text { and } \vec { b }{/tex}.
{tex}\therefore \vec { d } = \lambda ( \vec { c } \times \vec { b } ) = \lambda \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { 3 } & { 1 } & { - 1 } \\ { 1 } & { - 4 } & { 5 } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
{tex}= \lambda [ \hat { i } ( 5 - 4 ) - \hat { j } ( 15 + 1 ) + \hat { k } ( - 12 - 1 ) ]{/tex}
{tex}= \lambda ( \hat { i } - 16 \hat { j } - 13 \hat { k } ){/tex}.......(i)
According to the question, {tex}\vec { d } \cdot \vec { a } = 21{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore \lambda ( \hat { i } - 16 \hat { j } - 13 \hat { k } ) \cdot ( 4 \hat { i } + 5 \hat { j } - \hat { k } ) = 21{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda ( 4 - 80 + 13 ) = 21{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\lambda ( - 63 ) = 21{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda = \frac { - 1 } { 3 }{/tex}
Now, from Eq.(i), we get
{tex}\therefore \vec { d } = - \frac { 1 } { 3 } ( \hat { i } - 16 \hat { j } - 13 \hat { k } ){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}\theta {/tex} is the angle between two vectors {tex}\hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} and {tex}3\hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex}, find sin{tex}\theta{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}\theta {/tex} is the angle between two vectors {tex}\hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} and {tex}3\hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex} , find sin {tex}\theta{/tex} .
explanation: According to the question, {tex}\vec a =\hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = 3\hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex} We know that, {tex}\sin \theta = \frac{{\left| {\vec a \times \vec b } \right|}}{{\left| {\vec a} \right|\left| {\vec b} \right|}}{/tex} ..........(i) {tex}\left[ {\because \left| {\vec a \times \vec b} \right| = \left| {\vec a} \right|\left| {\vec b} \right|\sin \theta } \right]{/tex} Here, {tex}\left| {\vec a} \right|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt {{1^2} + {{( - 2)}^2} + {3^2}} = \sqrt {1 + 4 + 9} = \sqrt {14} {/tex} {tex}\left| {\vec b} \right|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt {{3^2} + {{( - 2)}^2} + {1^2}} = \sqrt {9 + 4 + 1} = \sqrt {14} {/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow a \times \overrightarrow b = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} \hat i&\hat j&\hat k \\ 1&{ - 2}&3 \\ 3&{ - 2}&1 \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = \hat i( - 2 + 6) -\hat j(1 - 9) +\hat k( - 2 + 6){/tex} {tex} = 4\hat i + 8\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} {tex} = 4(\hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left| {\vec a \times \vec b} \right| = 4\sqrt {{1^2} + {2^2} + {1^2}} {/tex} {tex} = 4\sqrt {1 + 4 + 1} {/tex} {tex} = 4\sqrt {6} {/tex} Now, from Eq.(i), we get sin {tex}\theta = \frac{{4\sqrt 6 }}{{\sqrt {14} .\sqrt {14} }}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{4\sqrt 6 }}{{14}} = \frac{{2\sqrt 6 }}{7}{/tex}
solution: According to the question, {tex}\vec a =\hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = 3\hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex} We know that, {tex}\sin \theta = \frac{{\left| {\vec a \times \vec b } \right|}}{{\left| {\vec a} \right|\left| {\vec b} \right|}}{/tex} ..........(i) {tex}\left[ {\because \left| {\vec a \times \vec b} \right| = \left| {\vec a} \right|\left| {\vec b} \right|\sin \theta } \right]{/tex} Here, {tex}\left| {\vec a} \right|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt {{1^2} + {{( - 2)}^2} + {3^2}} = \sqrt {1 + 4 + 9} = \sqrt {14} {/tex} {tex}\left| {\vec b} \right|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt {{3^2} + {{( - 2)}^2} + {1^2}} = \sqrt {9 + 4 + 1} = \sqrt {14} {/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow a \times \overrightarrow b = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} \hat i&\hat j&\hat k \\ 1&{ - 2}&3 \\ 3&{ - 2}&1 \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = \hat i( - 2 + 6) -\hat j(1 - 9) +\hat k( - 2 + 6){/tex} {tex} = 4\hat i + 8\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} {tex} = 4(\hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left| {\vec a \times \vec b} \right| = 4\sqrt {{1^2} + {2^2} + {1^2}} {/tex} {tex} = 4\sqrt {1 + 4 + 1} {/tex} {tex} = 4\sqrt {6} {/tex} Now, from Eq.(i), we get sin {tex}\theta = \frac{{4\sqrt 6 }}{{\sqrt {14} .\sqrt {14} }}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{4\sqrt 6 }}{{14}} = \frac{{2\sqrt 6 }}{7}{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}\vec a =\hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = 3\hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex}
We know that, {tex}\sin \theta = \frac{{\left| {\vec a \times \vec b } \right|}}{{\left| {\vec a} \right|\left| {\vec b} \right|}}{/tex}..........(i){tex}\left[ {\because \left| {\vec a \times \vec b} \right| = \left| {\vec a} \right|\left| {\vec b} \right|\sin \theta } \right]{/tex}
Here, {tex}\left| {\vec a} \right|{/tex}={tex}\sqrt {{1^2} + {{( - 2)}^2} + {3^2}} = \sqrt {1 + 4 + 9} = \sqrt {14} {/tex}
{tex}\left| {\vec b} \right|{/tex}={tex}\sqrt {{3^2} + {{( - 2)}^2} + {1^2}} = \sqrt {9 + 4 + 1} = \sqrt {14} {/tex}
and {tex}\overrightarrow a \times \overrightarrow b = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} \hat i&\hat j&\hat k \\ 1&{ - 2}&3 \\ 3&{ - 2}&1 \end{array}} \right|{/tex}
{tex} = \hat i( - 2 + 6) -\hat j(1 - 9) +\hat k( - 2 + 6){/tex}
{tex} = 4\hat i + 8\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex}
{tex} = 4(\hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \left| {\vec a \times \vec b} \right| = 4\sqrt {{1^2} + {2^2} + {1^2}} {/tex}
{tex} = 4\sqrt {1 + 4 + 1} {/tex}
{tex} = 4\sqrt {6} {/tex}
Now, from Eq.(i), we get
sin{tex}\theta = \frac{{4\sqrt 6 }}{{\sqrt {14} .\sqrt {14} }}{/tex}{tex} = \frac{{4\sqrt 6 }}{{14}} = \frac{{2\sqrt 6 }}{7}{/tex}

question_html: Find the magnitude of each of the two vectors {tex}\vec a \text{ and }\vec b{/tex}, having the same magnitude such that the angle between them is 60° and their scalar product is {tex}\frac 92{/tex}.
question_text: Find the magnitude of each of the two vectors {tex}\vec a \text{ and }\vec b{/tex} , having the same magnitude such that the angle between them is 60° and their scalar product is {tex}\frac 92{/tex} .
explanation: Let two vectors be {tex}\vec a \text{ and }\vec b{/tex} . According to the question, {tex}\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right| = \left| {\overrightarrow b } \right|{/tex} Given, {tex}\overrightarrow a .\overrightarrow b = \frac{9}{2}{/tex} and angle between them is 60° We know that, {tex}\vec a \cdot\vec b{/tex} = {tex}\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right| \left| {\overrightarrow b } \right|cos\theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{9}{2} = \left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|.\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|\cos {60^ \circ }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}.{\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|^2} = \frac{9}{2}\left[ {\because \cos {{60}^ \circ } = \frac{1}{2}} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|^2} = 9{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left| {\overrightarrow a } \right| = 3{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} magnitude cannot be negative) Therefore, {tex}\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right| = \left| {\overrightarrow b } \right| = 3{/tex}
solution: Let two vectors be {tex}\vec a \text{ and }\vec b{/tex} . According to the question, {tex}\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right| = \left| {\overrightarrow b } \right|{/tex} Given, {tex}\overrightarrow a .\overrightarrow b = \frac{9}{2}{/tex} and angle between them is 60° We know that, {tex}\vec a \cdot\vec b{/tex} = {tex}\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right| \left| {\overrightarrow b } \right|cos\theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{9}{2} = \left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|.\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|\cos {60^ \circ }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}.{\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|^2} = \frac{9}{2}\left[ {\because \cos {{60}^ \circ } = \frac{1}{2}} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|^2} = 9{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left| {\overrightarrow a } \right| = 3{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} magnitude cannot be negative) Therefore, {tex}\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right| = \left| {\overrightarrow b } \right| = 3{/tex}
solution_html:
Let two vectors be {tex}\vec a \text{ and }\vec b{/tex}.
According to the question, {tex}\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right| = \left| {\overrightarrow b } \right|{/tex}
Given, {tex}\overrightarrow a .\overrightarrow b = \frac{9}{2}{/tex} and angle between them is 60°
We know that, {tex}\vec a \cdot\vec b{/tex} ={tex}\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right| \left| {\overrightarrow b } \right|cos\theta{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{9}{2} = \left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|.\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|\cos {60^ \circ }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}.{\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|^2} = \frac{9}{2}\left[ {\because \cos {{60}^ \circ } = \frac{1}{2}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|^2} = 9{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \left| {\overrightarrow a } \right| = 3{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} magnitude cannot be negative)
Therefore, {tex}\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right| = \left| {\overrightarrow b } \right| = 3{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the value of {tex}[\hat{\mathrm{i}}, \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \hat{\mathrm{j}}]{/tex}.
question_text: Find the value of {tex}[\hat{\mathrm{i}}, \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \hat{\mathrm{j}}]{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}[\hat{i} \hat{k} \hat{j}]{/tex} = {tex}\hat{\mathbf{i}} \cdot(\hat{\mathbf{k}} \times \hat{\mathbf{j}}){/tex} = {tex}-\hat{i} \cdot(\hat{j} \times \hat{k}){/tex} = -1
solution: {tex}[\hat{i} \hat{k} \hat{j}]{/tex} = {tex}\hat{\mathbf{i}} \cdot(\hat{\mathbf{k}} \times \hat{\mathbf{j}}){/tex} = {tex}-\hat{i} \cdot(\hat{j} \times \hat{k}){/tex} = -1
solution_html:
{tex}[\hat{i} \hat{k} \hat{j}]{/tex} = {tex}\hat{\mathbf{i}} \cdot(\hat{\mathbf{k}} \times \hat{\mathbf{j}}){/tex}
= {tex}-\hat{i} \cdot(\hat{j} \times \hat{k}){/tex}
= -1

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=\overrightarrow{0}{/tex} and {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|=5,|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}|=6{/tex} and {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|=9{/tex}, then find the angle between {tex}\text { } \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=\overrightarrow{0}{/tex} and {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|=5,|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}|=6{/tex} and {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|=9{/tex} , then find the angle between {tex}\text { } \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} .
explanation: Here we have, {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=\overrightarrow{0}{/tex} and {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|=5,|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}|=6{/tex} and {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|=9{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=0{/tex} {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}=-\vec{c}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a}^{2}+\vec{b}^{2}+2 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\vec{c}^{2}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\frac{|\vec{c}|^{2}-|\vec{a}|^{2}-|\vec{b}|^{2}}{2}{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|}=\frac{|\vec{c}|^{2}-|\vec{a}|^{2}-|\vec{b}|^{2}}{2|a||b|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{9^{2}-5^{2}-6^{2}}{2(5)(6)}{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{81-25-36}{60}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right){/tex}
solution: Here we have, {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=\overrightarrow{0}{/tex} and {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|=5,|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}|=6{/tex} and {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|=9{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=0{/tex} {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}=-\vec{c}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a}^{2}+\vec{b}^{2}+2 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\vec{c}^{2}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\frac{|\vec{c}|^{2}-|\vec{a}|^{2}-|\vec{b}|^{2}}{2}{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|}=\frac{|\vec{c}|^{2}-|\vec{a}|^{2}-|\vec{b}|^{2}}{2|a||b|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{9^{2}-5^{2}-6^{2}}{2(5)(6)}{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{81-25-36}{60}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
Here we have,  {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=\overrightarrow{0}{/tex}  and  {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|=5,|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}|=6{/tex}  and  {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|=9{/tex}
Now, {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=0{/tex}
{tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}=-\vec{c}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{a}^{2}+\vec{b}^{2}+2 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\vec{c}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\frac{|\vec{c}|^{2}-|\vec{a}|^{2}-|\vec{b}|^{2}}{2}{/tex}
cos {tex}\theta{/tex}  = {tex}\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|}=\frac{|\vec{c}|^{2}-|\vec{a}|^{2}-|\vec{b}|^{2}}{2|a||b|}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{9^{2}-5^{2}-6^{2}}{2(5)(6)}{/tex}
cos {tex}\theta{/tex}  = {tex}\frac{81-25-36}{60}{/tex}={tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\theta{/tex}  = {tex}\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: The adjacent sides of a parallelogram are determined by the vectors {tex}\vec{{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-7 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}. Find the vectors determining its diagonals and hence find the area of the parallelogram.
question_text: The adjacent sides of a parallelogram are determined by the vectors {tex}\vec{{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-7 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} . Find the vectors determining its diagonals and hence find the area of the parallelogram.
explanation: Adjacent sides of a parallelogram are determined by the vectors {tex}\vec{{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-7 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} . Diagonals of parallelogram are {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}+\overrightarrow{{b}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{{b}}-\overrightarrow{{a}}~~~[\text { or } \overrightarrow{{a}}-\overrightarrow{{b}}]{/tex} Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}+\overrightarrow{{b}}{/tex} = {tex}3 \hat{i}-8 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{{b}}-\overrightarrow{{a}}=\hat{{i}}-6 \hat{{j}}-2 \hat{{k}}{/tex} Required area = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{rrr} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & -8 & 4 \\ 1 & -6 & -2 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}|40 \hat{i}+10 \hat{j}-10 \hat{k}|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{1800}{/tex} = {tex}15 \sqrt{2}{/tex} sq. units
solution: Adjacent sides of a parallelogram are determined by the vectors {tex}\vec{{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-7 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} . Diagonals of parallelogram are {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}+\overrightarrow{{b}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{{b}}-\overrightarrow{{a}}~~~[\text { or } \overrightarrow{{a}}-\overrightarrow{{b}}]{/tex} Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}+\overrightarrow{{b}}{/tex} = {tex}3 \hat{i}-8 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{{b}}-\overrightarrow{{a}}=\hat{{i}}-6 \hat{{j}}-2 \hat{{k}}{/tex} Required area = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{rrr} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & -8 & 4 \\ 1 & -6 & -2 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}|40 \hat{i}+10 \hat{j}-10 \hat{k}|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{1800}{/tex} = {tex}15 \sqrt{2}{/tex} sq. units
solution_html:
Adjacent sides of a parallelogram are determined by the vectors {tex}\vec{{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-7 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}.
Diagonals of parallelogram are {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}+\overrightarrow{{b}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{{b}}-\overrightarrow{{a}}~~~[\text { or } \overrightarrow{{a}}-\overrightarrow{{b}}]{/tex}
Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}+\overrightarrow{{b}}{/tex} = {tex}3 \hat{i}-8 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{{b}}-\overrightarrow{{a}}=\hat{{i}}-6 \hat{{j}}-2 \hat{{k}}{/tex}
Required area = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{rrr} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & -8 & 4 \\ 1 & -6 & -2 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}|40 \hat{i}+10 \hat{j}-10 \hat{k}|{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{1800}{/tex} = {tex}15 \sqrt{2}{/tex} sq. units

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}{/tex} are three mutually perpendicular vectors of equal magnitude, prove that {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c} {/tex} is equally inclined with vectors {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b} \text { and } \vec{c}{/tex}. Also, find the angle.
question_text: If {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}{/tex} are three mutually perpendicular vectors of equal magnitude, prove that {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c} {/tex} is equally inclined with vectors {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b} \text { and } \vec{c}{/tex} . Also, find the angle.
explanation: Let {tex}|\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|=|\vec{c}|=\lambda{/tex} (say) Since {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}{/tex} are mutually perpendicular vectors. Thus, {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}=\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}=0{/tex} ...(i) We know that {tex}|\vec{x}|^{2}=\vec{x} \cdot \vec{x}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|^{2}=(\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}) \cdot(\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|^{2}=\vec{a} \cdot \vec{a}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{c}+2 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+2 \vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+2 \vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\quad|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|^{2}=\left.|\vec{a}|^{2}|+| \vec{b}\right|^{2}+|\vec{c}|^{2}{/tex} [Using (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|^{2}=3 \lambda^{2} [\because|\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|=|\vec{c}|=\lambda]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|=\sqrt{3} \lambda{/tex} Let {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}{/tex} makes angles {tex}\theta_{1}, \theta_{2}, \theta_{3}{/tex} with {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b} \text { and } \vec{c}{/tex} respectively. Then, {tex}\cos \theta_{1}=\frac{\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c})}{|\vec{a}||\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|}=\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{a}+\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}}{|\vec{a}||\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta_{1}=\frac{|a|^{2}}{|\vec{a}||\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|}=\frac{|\vec{a}|}{|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|}=\frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{3} \lambda}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\space{/tex} [Using (ii)] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \theta_{1}=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right){/tex} Similarly, we obtain {tex}\theta_{2}=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \text { and } \theta_{3}=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \theta_{1}=\theta_{2}=\theta_{3}{/tex} Therefor, {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}{/tex} is equally inclinded with vectors .
solution: Let {tex}|\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|=|\vec{c}|=\lambda{/tex} (say) Since {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}{/tex} are mutually perpendicular vectors. Thus, {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}=\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}=0{/tex} ...(i) We know that {tex}|\vec{x}|^{2}=\vec{x} \cdot \vec{x}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|^{2}=(\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}) \cdot(\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|^{2}=\vec{a} \cdot \vec{a}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{c}+2 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+2 \vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+2 \vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\quad|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|^{2}=\left.|\vec{a}|^{2}|+| \vec{b}\right|^{2}+|\vec{c}|^{2}{/tex} [Using (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|^{2}=3 \lambda^{2} [\because|\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|=|\vec{c}|=\lambda]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|=\sqrt{3} \lambda{/tex} Let {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}{/tex} makes angles {tex}\theta_{1}, \theta_{2}, \theta_{3}{/tex} with {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b} \text { and } \vec{c}{/tex} respectively. Then, {tex}\cos \theta_{1}=\frac{\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c})}{|\vec{a}||\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|}=\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{a}+\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}}{|\vec{a}||\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta_{1}=\frac{|a|^{2}}{|\vec{a}||\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|}=\frac{|\vec{a}|}{|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|}=\frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{3} \lambda}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\space{/tex} [Using (ii)] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \theta_{1}=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right){/tex} Similarly, we obtain {tex}\theta_{2}=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \text { and } \theta_{3}=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \theta_{1}=\theta_{2}=\theta_{3}{/tex} Therefor, {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}{/tex} is equally inclinded with vectors .
solution_html:
Let {tex}|\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|=|\vec{c}|=\lambda{/tex} (say)
Since {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}{/tex}  are mutually perpendicular vectors. Thus,
{tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}=\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}=0{/tex} ...(i)
We know that {tex}|\vec{x}|^{2}=\vec{x} \cdot \vec{x}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|^{2}=(\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}) \cdot(\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|^{2}=\vec{a} \cdot \vec{a}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{c}+2 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+2 \vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+2 \vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\quad|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|^{2}=\left.|\vec{a}|^{2}|+| \vec{b}\right|^{2}+|\vec{c}|^{2}{/tex}    [Using (i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|^{2}=3 \lambda^{2} [\because|\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|=|\vec{c}|=\lambda]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|=\sqrt{3} \lambda{/tex}
Let  {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}{/tex} makes angles {tex}\theta_{1}, \theta_{2}, \theta_{3}{/tex} with {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b} \text { and } \vec{c}{/tex}respectively. Then,
{tex}\cos \theta_{1}=\frac{\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c})}{|\vec{a}||\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|}=\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{a}+\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}}{|\vec{a}||\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta_{1}=\frac{|a|^{2}}{|\vec{a}||\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|}=\frac{|\vec{a}|}{|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|}=\frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{3} \lambda}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\space{/tex} [Using (ii)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \theta_{1}=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right){/tex}
Similarly, we obtain
{tex}\theta_{2}=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \text { and } \theta_{3}=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \theta_{1}=\theta_{2}=\theta_{3}{/tex}
Therefor, {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}{/tex}is equally inclinded with vectors .

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Write the angle between the vectors {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b} \times \vec{a}{/tex}.
question_text: Write the angle between the vectors {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b} \times \vec{a}{/tex} .
explanation: Since, {tex}\vec{b} \times \vec{a}=-\vec{a} \times \vec{b}{/tex} So, {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b} \times \vec{a}{/tex} are vectors of same magnitude but opposite in directions.Hence, they are antiparallel vectors. Thus, the angle between the vectors {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b} \times \vec{a}{/tex} is 180 o .
solution: Since, {tex}\vec{b} \times \vec{a}=-\vec{a} \times \vec{b}{/tex} So, {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b} \times \vec{a}{/tex} are vectors of same magnitude but opposite in directions.Hence, they are antiparallel vectors. Thus, the angle between the vectors {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b} \times \vec{a}{/tex} is 180 o .
solution_html:
Since,
{tex}\vec{b} \times \vec{a}=-\vec{a} \times \vec{b}{/tex}
So,
{tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b} \times \vec{a}{/tex} are vectors of same magnitude but opposite in directions.Hence, they are antiparallel vectors.
Thus, the angle between the vectors {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b} \times \vec{a}{/tex} is 180o.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If the points with position vectors {tex}10 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}, 12 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}{/tex} and {tex}a \hat{i}+11 \hat{j}{/tex} are collinear, find the value of a.
question_text: If the points with position vectors {tex}10 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}, 12 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}{/tex} and {tex}a \hat{i}+11 \hat{j}{/tex} are collinear, find the value of a.
explanation: Let A, B, C be the points with position vectors {tex}10 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}, 12 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}, a \hat{i}+11 \hat{j}{/tex} Then, {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}={/tex} Position vector of B - Position vector of A {tex}=12 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}-10 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}{/tex} {tex}=2 \hat{i}-8 \hat{j}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{B C}{/tex} = Position vector of C - Position vector of B {tex}=a \hat{i}+11 \hat{j}-12 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}{/tex} {tex}=(a-12) \hat{i}+16 \hat{j}{/tex} Since, A, B and C are collinear. {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}=\lambda \overrightarrow{B C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \hat{i}-8 \hat{j}=\lambda(a-12) \hat{i}+\lambda 16 \hat{j}{/tex} Equating coefficients of i & j; {tex}\Rightarrow 2=\lambda(a-12),-8=\lambda 16{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2=\lambda(a-12), \lambda=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2=-\frac{1}{2}(a-12){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -a + 12 = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 8
solution: Let A, B, C be the points with position vectors {tex}10 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}, 12 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}, a \hat{i}+11 \hat{j}{/tex} Then, {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}={/tex} Position vector of B - Position vector of A {tex}=12 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}-10 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}{/tex} {tex}=2 \hat{i}-8 \hat{j}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{B C}{/tex} = Position vector of C - Position vector of B {tex}=a \hat{i}+11 \hat{j}-12 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}{/tex} {tex}=(a-12) \hat{i}+16 \hat{j}{/tex} Since, A, B and C are collinear. {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}=\lambda \overrightarrow{B C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \hat{i}-8 \hat{j}=\lambda(a-12) \hat{i}+\lambda 16 \hat{j}{/tex} Equating coefficients of i & j; {tex}\Rightarrow 2=\lambda(a-12),-8=\lambda 16{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2=\lambda(a-12), \lambda=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2=-\frac{1}{2}(a-12){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -a + 12 = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 8
solution_html:
Let A, B, C be the points with position vectors {tex}10 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}, 12 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}, a \hat{i}+11 \hat{j}{/tex}
Then, {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}={/tex} Position vector of B - Position vector of A
{tex}=12 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}-10 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}{/tex}
{tex}=2 \hat{i}-8 \hat{j}{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{B C}{/tex} = Position vector of C - Position vector of B
{tex}=a \hat{i}+11 \hat{j}-12 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}{/tex}
{tex}=(a-12) \hat{i}+16 \hat{j}{/tex}
Since,
A, B and C are collinear.
{tex}\overrightarrow{A B}=\lambda \overrightarrow{B C}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \hat{i}-8 \hat{j}=\lambda(a-12) \hat{i}+\lambda 16 \hat{j}{/tex}

Equating coefficients of i & j;
{tex}\Rightarrow 2=\lambda(a-12),-8=\lambda 16{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2=\lambda(a-12), \lambda=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2=-\frac{1}{2}(a-12){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -a + 12 = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 8

question_html:
Let {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } , \vec { b } = \hat { i }{/tex}and
{tex}\vec { c } = c _ { 1 } \hat { i } + c _ { 2 } \hat { j } + c _ { 3 } \hat { k },{/tex}then

  1. Let c1 = 1 and c2 = 2, find c3 which makes {tex}\vec { a } , \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c }{/tex} coplanar.
  2. If c2 = -1 and c3 = 1, show that no value of c1 can make {tex}\vec { a } , \vec { b }{/tex}and {tex}\vec { c }{/tex} coplanar.
question_text: Let {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } , \vec { b } = \hat { i }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c } = c _ { 1 } \hat { i } + c _ { 2 } \hat { j } + c _ { 3 } \hat { k },{/tex} then Let c 1 = 1 and c 2 = 2, find c 3 which makes {tex}\vec { a } , \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c }{/tex} coplanar. If c 2 = -1 and c 3 = 1, show that no value of c 1 can make {tex}\vec { a } , \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c }{/tex} coplanar.
explanation: According to the question, {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } ,{/tex} {tex}\vec { b } = \hat { i } + 0 \hat { j } + 0 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c } = c _ { 1 } \hat { i } + c _ { 2 } \hat { \jmath } + c _ { 3 } \hat { k }{/tex} The given vectors are coplanar if {tex} [ \vec { a }\ \vec { b }\ \vec { c } ] = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left| \begin{array} { l l l } { 1 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { c _ { 1 } } & { c _ { 2 } } & { c _ { 3 } } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex} If c 1 = 1 and c 2 = 2 {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 1 } & { 2 } & { c _ { 3 } } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - 1 \left( c _ { 3 } - 0 \right) + 1 ( 2 - 0 ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -c 3 + 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - c 3 = - 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c 3 = 2 If c 2 = -1 and c 3 = 1 {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { c _ { 1 } } & { - 1 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1(0) - 1(1 - 0) + 1 (-1 - 0) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0 - 1 +0 -1 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow -2\ =0{/tex} , this is not possible {tex}\therefore {/tex} no value of c 1 can make {tex}\vec { a } , \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c }{/tex} coplanar.
solution: According to the question, {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } ,{/tex} {tex}\vec { b } = \hat { i } + 0 \hat { j } + 0 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c } = c _ { 1 } \hat { i } + c _ { 2 } \hat { \jmath } + c _ { 3 } \hat { k }{/tex} The given vectors are coplanar if {tex} [ \vec { a }\ \vec { b }\ \vec { c } ] = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left| \begin{array} { l l l } { 1 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { c _ { 1 } } & { c _ { 2 } } & { c _ { 3 } } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex} If c 1 = 1 and c 2 = 2 {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 1 } & { 2 } & { c _ { 3 } } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - 1 \left( c _ { 3 } - 0 \right) + 1 ( 2 - 0 ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -c 3 + 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - c 3 = - 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c 3 = 2 If c 2 = -1 and c 3 = 1 {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { c _ { 1 } } & { - 1 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1(0) - 1(1 - 0) + 1 (-1 - 0) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0 - 1 +0 -1 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow -2\ =0{/tex} , this is not possible {tex}\therefore {/tex} no value of c 1 can make {tex}\vec { a } , \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c }{/tex} coplanar.
solution_html:
According to the question, 

{tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } ,{/tex}

{tex}\vec { b } = \hat { i } + 0 \hat { j } + 0 \hat { k }{/tex} and 

{tex}\vec { c } = c _ { 1 } \hat { i } + c _ { 2 } \hat { \jmath } + c _ { 3 } \hat { k }{/tex}
The given vectors are coplanar if {tex} [ \vec { a }\ \vec { b }\ \vec { c } ] = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left| \begin{array} { l l l } { 1 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { c _ { 1 } } & { c _ { 2 } } & { c _ { 3 } } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex}

  1. If c1 = 1 and c2 = 2
    {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 1 } & { 2 } & { c _ { 3 } } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow - 1 \left( c _ { 3 } - 0 \right) + 1 ( 2 - 0 ) = 0{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -c3 + 2 = 0 
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - c3 = - 2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c3 = 2
  2. If c2 = -1 and c3 = 1
    {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { c _ { 1 } } & { - 1 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1(0) - 1(1 - 0) + 1 (-1 - 0) = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}  0 - 1 +0 -1 = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow -2\ =0{/tex} , this is not possible
    {tex}\therefore {/tex}no value of c1 can make {tex}\vec { a } , \vec { b }{/tex}and {tex}\vec { c }{/tex} coplanar.

question_html: Show that the points A, B, C with position vectors {tex}2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + \hat { k } , \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}3 \hat { i } - 4 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k }{/tex} respectively, are the vertices of a right-angled triangle. Hence find the area of the triangle.
question_text: Show that the points A, B, C with position vectors {tex}2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + \hat { k } , \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}3 \hat { i } - 4 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k }{/tex} respectively, are the vertices of a right-angled triangle. Hence find the area of the triangle.
explanation: According to the question , {tex}A= 2\hat { i } - \hat { j } + \hat { k } {/tex} , {tex}B = \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}C = 3 \hat { i } - 4 \hat {j } - 4 \hat { k }{/tex} We know that, if two lines are perpendicular then , {tex}a_1a_2 + b_1b_2 + c_1 c_2 = 0{/tex} Now, for lines with position vector A and B, we get {tex}2 \times 1 - 1 \times ( - 3 ) + 1 \times ( - 5) = 2 + 3 - 5 = 5 - 5 = 0{/tex} Now, for lines with position vector B and C, we get {tex}1 \times 3 - 3 \times ( - 4 ) - 5 \times ( - 4 ) = 3 + 12 + 20 = 35 \neq 0{/tex} Now, for lines with position vector A and C, we get {tex}2 \times 3 - 1 \times ( - 4 ) + 1 \times ( - 4 ) = 6 + 4 - 4 = 6 \neq 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} A, B, C are the vertices of a right angled {tex}\Delta A B C,{/tex} and right angle is present at B. {tex}|A| = \sqrt {2^2+(-1)^2+1^2} = \sqrt{6}\\|B| = \sqrt {1^2+(-3)^2+(-5)^2} = \sqrt{35}\\{/tex} Area of triangle {tex}=\frac{1}{2} |A||B| {/tex} {tex}\\= \frac{1}{2}\sqrt {6} . \sqrt {35} \\=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{210}{/tex}
solution: According to the question , {tex}A= 2\hat { i } - \hat { j } + \hat { k } {/tex} , {tex}B = \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}C = 3 \hat { i } - 4 \hat {j } - 4 \hat { k }{/tex} We know that, if two lines are perpendicular then , {tex}a_1a_2 + b_1b_2 + c_1 c_2 = 0{/tex} Now, for lines with position vector A and B, we get {tex}2 \times 1 - 1 \times ( - 3 ) + 1 \times ( - 5) = 2 + 3 - 5 = 5 - 5 = 0{/tex} Now, for lines with position vector B and C, we get {tex}1 \times 3 - 3 \times ( - 4 ) - 5 \times ( - 4 ) = 3 + 12 + 20 = 35 \neq 0{/tex} Now, for lines with position vector A and C, we get {tex}2 \times 3 - 1 \times ( - 4 ) + 1 \times ( - 4 ) = 6 + 4 - 4 = 6 \neq 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} A, B, C are the vertices of a right angled {tex}\Delta A B C,{/tex} and right angle is present at B. {tex}|A| = \sqrt {2^2+(-1)^2+1^2} = \sqrt{6}\\|B| = \sqrt {1^2+(-3)^2+(-5)^2} = \sqrt{35}\\{/tex} Area of triangle {tex}=\frac{1}{2} |A||B| {/tex} {tex}\\= \frac{1}{2}\sqrt {6} . \sqrt {35} \\=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{210}{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question ,

{tex}A= 2\hat { i } - \hat { j } + \hat { k } {/tex} ,

{tex}B = \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k }{/tex} and 

{tex}C = 3 \hat { i } - 4 \hat {j } - 4 \hat { k }{/tex}
We know that,

if two lines are perpendicular then ,
{tex}a_1a_2 + b_1b_2 + c_1 c_2 = 0{/tex}
Now, for lines with position vector A and B, we get
{tex}2 \times 1 - 1 \times ( - 3 ) + 1 \times ( - 5) = 2 + 3 - 5 = 5 - 5 = 0{/tex}

Now, for lines with position vector B and C, we get
{tex}1 \times 3 - 3 \times ( - 4 ) - 5 \times ( - 4 ) = 3 + 12 + 20 = 35 \neq 0{/tex}
Now, for lines with position vector A and C, we get
{tex}2 \times 3 - 1 \times ( - 4 ) + 1 \times ( - 4 ) = 6 + 4 - 4 = 6 \neq 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} A, B, C are the vertices of  a right angled {tex}\Delta A B C,{/tex} and right angle is present at B.
{tex}|A| = \sqrt {2^2+(-1)^2+1^2} = \sqrt{6}\\|B| = \sqrt {1^2+(-3)^2+(-5)^2} = \sqrt{35}\\{/tex}
Area of triangle {tex}=\frac{1}{2} |A||B| {/tex}
{tex}\\= \frac{1}{2}\sqrt {6} . \sqrt {35} \\=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{210}{/tex}

question_html: Find {tex}\lambda{/tex} if the vectors {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex}, {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}=\lambda \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} are coplanar.
question_text: Find {tex}\lambda{/tex} if the vectors {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} , {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}=\lambda \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} are coplanar.
explanation: Since {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}{/tex} are coplanar vectors, we can write {tex}[\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}]=0{/tex} i.e., {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 3 & 1 \\ 2 & -1 & -1 \\ \lambda & 7 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 (-3 + 7) - 3 (6 + {tex}\lambda{/tex} ) + 1 (14 + {tex}\lambda{/tex} ) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0.
solution: Since {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}{/tex} are coplanar vectors, we can write {tex}[\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}]=0{/tex} i.e., {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 3 & 1 \\ 2 & -1 & -1 \\ \lambda & 7 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 (-3 + 7) - 3 (6 + {tex}\lambda{/tex} ) + 1 (14 + {tex}\lambda{/tex} ) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0.
solution_html:
Since {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}{/tex} are coplanar vectors, we can write {tex}[\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}]=0{/tex} i.e.,
{tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 3 & 1 \\ 2 & -1 & -1 \\ \lambda & 7 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 (-3 + 7) - 3 (6 + {tex}\lambda{/tex}) + 1 (14 + {tex}\lambda{/tex}) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0.

question_html: If {tex}(\vec{a} \times \vec{b})^{2}+(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})^{2}{/tex} = 225 and{tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|{/tex} = 5, then write the value of {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}(\vec{a} \times \vec{b})^{2}+(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})^{2}{/tex} = 225 and {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|{/tex} = 5, then write the value of {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|{/tex} .
explanation: We have {tex}(\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}})^{2}{/tex} + {tex}(\vec{a}-\vec{b})^{2}{/tex} = 225 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec{a}|^{2}|\vec{b}|^{2}\left(\sin ^{2} \theta+\cos ^{2} \theta\right){/tex} = 225 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(5)^{2}|\overrightarrow{{b}}|^{2}{/tex} = 225 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec{{b}}|=3{/tex}
solution: We have {tex}(\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}})^{2}{/tex} + {tex}(\vec{a}-\vec{b})^{2}{/tex} = 225 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec{a}|^{2}|\vec{b}|^{2}\left(\sin ^{2} \theta+\cos ^{2} \theta\right){/tex} = 225 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(5)^{2}|\overrightarrow{{b}}|^{2}{/tex} = 225 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec{{b}}|=3{/tex}
solution_html:
We have{tex}(\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}})^{2}{/tex} + {tex}(\vec{a}-\vec{b})^{2}{/tex} = 225
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec{a}|^{2}|\vec{b}|^{2}\left(\sin ^{2} \theta+\cos ^{2} \theta\right){/tex} = 225
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(5)^{2}|\overrightarrow{{b}}|^{2}{/tex} = 225
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec{{b}}|=3{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}\vec{a}{/tex}, {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}{/tex} are mutually perpendicular vectors of equal magnitudes, find the angles which the vector 2{tex}\vec{a}{/tex} + {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} + 2{tex}\vec{c}{/tex} makes with the vectors {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} + {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} , {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}{/tex} are mutually perpendicular vectors of equal magnitudes, find the angles which the vector 2 {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} + {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} + 2 {tex}\vec{c}{/tex} makes with the vectors {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} + {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}{/tex} .
explanation: Here it is given that {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} , {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}{/tex} are mutually perpendicular vectors of equal magnitudes Let the vector {tex}\overrightarrow{{p}}=(2 \overrightarrow{{a}}+\overrightarrow{{b}}+2 \overrightarrow{{c}}){/tex} makes angles {tex}\alpha{/tex} , {tex}\beta{/tex} , {tex}\gamma{/tex} respectively with the vector {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}, \overrightarrow{{b}}, \overrightarrow{{c}}{/tex} Given that {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}|=| \overrightarrow{{b}}|=| \overrightarrow{{c}} |{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\cos \alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(2 \vec{a}+\vec{b}+2 \vec{c})-\vec{a}}{|2 \vec{a}+\vec{b}+2 \vec{c}||\vec{a}|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2|a|^{2}}{3|\vec{a}||\vec{a}|}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha=\cos ^{-1} \frac{2}{3}{/tex} {tex}\cos \beta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(2 \vec{a}+\vec{b}+2 \vec{c}) \cdot \vec{b}}{|2 \vec{a}+\vec{b}+2 \vec{c}||\vec{b}|}=\frac{|\vec{b}|^{2}}{3|\vec{b}||\vec{b}|}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\beta=\cos ^{-1} \frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\cos \gamma{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(2 \overrightarrow{{a}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}+2 \overrightarrow{{c}}) \cdot \overrightarrow{{c}}}{|2 \overrightarrow{{a}}+\overrightarrow{{b}}+2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}||\overrightarrow{{c}}|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2|\overrightarrow{{c}}|^{2}}{3|\overrightarrow{{c}}||\overrightarrow{{c}}|}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
solution: Here it is given that {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} , {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}{/tex} are mutually perpendicular vectors of equal magnitudes Let the vector {tex}\overrightarrow{{p}}=(2 \overrightarrow{{a}}+\overrightarrow{{b}}+2 \overrightarrow{{c}}){/tex} makes angles {tex}\alpha{/tex} , {tex}\beta{/tex} , {tex}\gamma{/tex} respectively with the vector {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}, \overrightarrow{{b}}, \overrightarrow{{c}}{/tex} Given that {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}|=| \overrightarrow{{b}}|=| \overrightarrow{{c}} |{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\cos \alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(2 \vec{a}+\vec{b}+2 \vec{c})-\vec{a}}{|2 \vec{a}+\vec{b}+2 \vec{c}||\vec{a}|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2|a|^{2}}{3|\vec{a}||\vec{a}|}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha=\cos ^{-1} \frac{2}{3}{/tex} {tex}\cos \beta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(2 \vec{a}+\vec{b}+2 \vec{c}) \cdot \vec{b}}{|2 \vec{a}+\vec{b}+2 \vec{c}||\vec{b}|}=\frac{|\vec{b}|^{2}}{3|\vec{b}||\vec{b}|}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\beta=\cos ^{-1} \frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\cos \gamma{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(2 \overrightarrow{{a}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}+2 \overrightarrow{{c}}) \cdot \overrightarrow{{c}}}{|2 \overrightarrow{{a}}+\overrightarrow{{b}}+2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}||\overrightarrow{{c}}|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2|\overrightarrow{{c}}|^{2}}{3|\overrightarrow{{c}}||\overrightarrow{{c}}|}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here it is given that {tex}\vec{a}{/tex}, {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}{/tex} are mutually perpendicular vectors of equal magnitudes
Let the vector {tex}\overrightarrow{{p}}=(2 \overrightarrow{{a}}+\overrightarrow{{b}}+2 \overrightarrow{{c}}){/tex} makes angles {tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\beta{/tex}, {tex}\gamma{/tex} respectively with the vector {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}, \overrightarrow{{b}}, \overrightarrow{{c}}{/tex}
Given that {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}|=| \overrightarrow{{b}}|=| \overrightarrow{{c}} |{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\cos \alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(2 \vec{a}+\vec{b}+2 \vec{c})-\vec{a}}{|2 \vec{a}+\vec{b}+2 \vec{c}||\vec{a}|}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{2|a|^{2}}{3|\vec{a}||\vec{a}|}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha=\cos ^{-1} \frac{2}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\cos \beta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(2 \vec{a}+\vec{b}+2 \vec{c}) \cdot \vec{b}}{|2 \vec{a}+\vec{b}+2 \vec{c}||\vec{b}|}=\frac{|\vec{b}|^{2}}{3|\vec{b}||\vec{b}|}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\beta=\cos ^{-1} \frac{1}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\cos \gamma{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(2 \overrightarrow{{a}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}+2 \overrightarrow{{c}}) \cdot \overrightarrow{{c}}}{|2 \overrightarrow{{a}}+\overrightarrow{{b}}+2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}||\overrightarrow{{c}}|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2|\overrightarrow{{c}}|^{2}}{3|\overrightarrow{{c}}||\overrightarrow{{c}}|}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: The position vectors of points A, B and C are {tex}\lambda \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}, 12 \hat{i}+\mu \hat{j}{/tex} and {tex}11 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}{/tex} respectively. If C divides the line segment joining A and B in the ratio 3: 1, find the values of {tex}\lambda{/tex} and {tex}\mu{/tex}.
question_text: The position vectors of points A, B and C are {tex}\lambda \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}, 12 \hat{i}+\mu \hat{j}{/tex} and {tex}11 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}{/tex} respectively. If C divides the line segment joining A and B in the ratio 3: 1, find the values of {tex}\lambda{/tex} and {tex}\mu{/tex} .
explanation: We have,the position vectors of points A, B and C are {tex}\lambda \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}, 12 \hat{i}+\mu \hat{j}{/tex} and {tex}11 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}{/tex} respectively. It is given that, C divides the line segment joining A and B in the ratio 3: 1. {tex}11 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}=\frac{3 \times(12 \hat{i}+\mu \hat{j})+1 \times(\lambda \hat{i}+3 \hat{j})}{3+1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 11 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}=\frac{(36+\lambda) \hat{i}+(3 \mu+3) \hat{j}}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 44 \hat{i}-12 \hat{j}=(36+\lambda) \hat{i}+(3 \mu+3) \hat{j}{/tex} Equating the corresponding components, we get {tex}36+\lambda=44{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=44-36=8{/tex} and {tex}\Rightarrow 3 \mu+3=-12{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3 \mu=-12-3=-15{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \boldsymbol{\mu}=-5{/tex} Therefore, the values of {tex}\lambda{/tex} and {tex}\mu{/tex} are 8 and -5, respectively.
solution: We have,the position vectors of points A, B and C are {tex}\lambda \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}, 12 \hat{i}+\mu \hat{j}{/tex} and {tex}11 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}{/tex} respectively. It is given that, C divides the line segment joining A and B in the ratio 3: 1. {tex}11 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}=\frac{3 \times(12 \hat{i}+\mu \hat{j})+1 \times(\lambda \hat{i}+3 \hat{j})}{3+1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 11 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}=\frac{(36+\lambda) \hat{i}+(3 \mu+3) \hat{j}}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 44 \hat{i}-12 \hat{j}=(36+\lambda) \hat{i}+(3 \mu+3) \hat{j}{/tex} Equating the corresponding components, we get {tex}36+\lambda=44{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=44-36=8{/tex} and {tex}\Rightarrow 3 \mu+3=-12{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3 \mu=-12-3=-15{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \boldsymbol{\mu}=-5{/tex} Therefore, the values of {tex}\lambda{/tex} and {tex}\mu{/tex} are 8 and -5, respectively.
solution_html:
We have,the position vectors of points A, B and C are {tex}\lambda \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}, 12 \hat{i}+\mu \hat{j}{/tex} and {tex}11 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}{/tex} respectively.
It is given that, C divides the line segment joining A and B in the ratio 3: 1.
{tex}11 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}=\frac{3 \times(12 \hat{i}+\mu \hat{j})+1 \times(\lambda \hat{i}+3 \hat{j})}{3+1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 11 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}=\frac{(36+\lambda) \hat{i}+(3 \mu+3) \hat{j}}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 44 \hat{i}-12 \hat{j}=(36+\lambda) \hat{i}+(3 \mu+3) \hat{j}{/tex}
Equating the corresponding components, we get {tex}36+\lambda=44{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=44-36=8{/tex}
and
{tex}\Rightarrow 3 \mu+3=-12{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 3 \mu=-12-3=-15{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \boldsymbol{\mu}=-5{/tex}
Therefore, the values of {tex}\lambda{/tex} and {tex}\mu{/tex} are 8 and -5, respectively.

question_text: If {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=7 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} then express {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} in the form of {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{b}_1+\vec{b}_2{/tex} , where {tex}\vec{b}_1{/tex} is parallel to {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}_2{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} .

solution: {tex}\overrightarrow{b_1} \| \vec{a} {/tex} or let {tex} \vec{b_1}=\lambda(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\vec{b}_2=\vec{b}-\vec {b_1}{/tex} {tex}=(7 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}-(2 \lambda \hat{i}-\lambda \hat{j}-2 \lambda \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=(7-2 \lambda) \hat{i}+(2+\lambda) \hat{j}{/tex} {tex}-(3-2 \lambda) \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}_2 \perp \vec{a} {/tex} or {tex}2(7-2 \lambda)-1(2+\lambda){/tex} {tex}+2(3-2 \lambda)=0{/tex} or {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}_1=4 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{b_2}=3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} or {tex}(7 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=(4 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}+(3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\overrightarrow{b_1} \| \vec{a} {/tex} or let {tex} \vec{b_1}=\lambda(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}\vec{b}_2=\vec{b}-\vec {b_1}{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}\vec { a } \times \vec { b } = \vec { c } \times \vec { d }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { a } \times \vec { c } = \vec { b } \times \vec { d },{/tex}then show that {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { d }{/tex} is parallel to {tex}\vec { b } - \vec { c },{/tex} where {tex}\vec { a } \neq \vec { d }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } \neq \vec { c }.{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}\vec { a } \times \vec { b } = \vec { c } \times \vec { d }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { a } \times \vec { c } = \vec { b } \times \vec { d },{/tex} then show that {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { d }{/tex} is parallel to {tex}\vec { b } - \vec { c },{/tex} where {tex}\vec { a } \neq \vec { d }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } \neq \vec { c }.{/tex}
explanation: According to the question, {tex}\vec { a } \times \vec { b } = \vec { c } \times \vec { d }{/tex} ...(i) and {tex}\vec { a } \times \vec { c } = \vec { b } \times \vec { d }{/tex} ...(ii) Subtracting Eq. (ii) from Eq. (i), {tex}( \vec { a } \times \vec { b } ) - ( \vec { a } \times \vec { c } ) = ( \vec { c } \times \vec { d } ) - ( \vec { b } \times \vec { d } ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow ( \vec { a } \times \vec { b } ) - ( \vec { a } \times \vec { c } ) + ( \vec { b } \times \vec { d } ) - ( \vec { c } \times \vec { d } ) = \vec { 0 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec { a } \times ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) + ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) \times \vec { d } = \vec { 0 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec { a } \times ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) - \vec { d } \times ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) = \vec { 0 }{/tex} {tex}[ \because \vec { a } \times \vec { b } = - \vec { b } \times \vec { a } ]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad ( \vec { a } - \vec { d } ) \times ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) = \vec { 0 }{/tex} {tex}[ \because \vec { a } \neq \vec { d }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } \neq \vec { c },{/tex} given] The cross-product of vectors {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { d }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } - \vec { c }{/tex} is a zero vector, so {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { d }{/tex} is parallel {tex}\vec { b } - \vec { c }.{/tex}
solution: According to the question, {tex}\vec { a } \times \vec { b } = \vec { c } \times \vec { d }{/tex} ...(i) and {tex}\vec { a } \times \vec { c } = \vec { b } \times \vec { d }{/tex} ...(ii) Subtracting Eq. (ii) from Eq. (i), {tex}( \vec { a } \times \vec { b } ) - ( \vec { a } \times \vec { c } ) = ( \vec { c } \times \vec { d } ) - ( \vec { b } \times \vec { d } ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow ( \vec { a } \times \vec { b } ) - ( \vec { a } \times \vec { c } ) + ( \vec { b } \times \vec { d } ) - ( \vec { c } \times \vec { d } ) = \vec { 0 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec { a } \times ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) + ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) \times \vec { d } = \vec { 0 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec { a } \times ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) - \vec { d } \times ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) = \vec { 0 }{/tex} {tex}[ \because \vec { a } \times \vec { b } = - \vec { b } \times \vec { a } ]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad ( \vec { a } - \vec { d } ) \times ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) = \vec { 0 }{/tex} {tex}[ \because \vec { a } \neq \vec { d }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } \neq \vec { c },{/tex} given] The cross-product of vectors {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { d }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } - \vec { c }{/tex} is a zero vector, so {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { d }{/tex} is parallel {tex}\vec { b } - \vec { c }.{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}\vec { a } \times \vec { b } = \vec { c } \times \vec { d }{/tex} ...(i)
and {tex}\vec { a } \times \vec { c } = \vec { b } \times \vec { d }{/tex} ...(ii)
Subtracting Eq. (ii) from Eq. (i), 
{tex}( \vec { a } \times \vec { b } ) - ( \vec { a } \times \vec { c } ) = ( \vec { c } \times \vec { d } ) - ( \vec { b } \times \vec { d } ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow ( \vec { a } \times \vec { b } ) - ( \vec { a } \times \vec { c } ) + ( \vec { b } \times \vec { d } ) - ( \vec { c } \times \vec { d } ) = \vec { 0 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec { a } \times ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) + ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) \times \vec { d } = \vec { 0 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec { a } \times ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) - \vec { d } \times ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) = \vec { 0 }{/tex}
{tex}[ \because \vec { a } \times \vec { b } = - \vec { b } \times \vec { a } ]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad ( \vec { a } - \vec { d } ) \times ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) = \vec { 0 }{/tex}
{tex}[ \because \vec { a } \neq \vec { d }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } \neq \vec { c },{/tex} given]
The cross-product of vectors {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { d }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } - \vec { c }{/tex} is a zero vector, so {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { d }{/tex} is parallel {tex}\vec { b } - \vec { c }.{/tex}

( \vec { c } \times \vec { a } ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow [ \vec { a } \ \vec { b } \ \vec { c } ] + [ \vec { a } \ \vec { b } \ \vec { a } ] + [ \vec { a } \ \vec { c }\ \vec { a } ] +[ \vec { b } \ \vec { b }\ \vec { c } ] + [ \vec { b } \ \vec { b }\ \vec { a } ] + [ \vec { b } \ \vec { c } \ \vec { a } ] =0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow [\vec { a }\ \vec { b }\ \vec { c } ] + [ \vec { b } \ \vec { c } \ \vec { a } ] =0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow [ \vec { a } \ \vec { b } \ \vec { c } ] + [ \vec { a } \ \vec { b }\ \vec { c } ]=0{/tex} {tex}[ \because [ \vec { b } \ \vec { c }\ \vec { a } ] = - [ \vec { b } \ \vec { a } \ \vec { c } ] = [ \vec { a }\ \vec { b }\ \vec { c } ] \ ]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 2 \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { \vec { a } } & { \vec { b } } & { \vec { c } } \end{array} \right] = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { \vec { a } } & { \vec { b } } & { \vec { c } } \end{array} \right] = 0{/tex} Hence, the vectors {tex}\vec { a } , \vec { b } , \vec { c }{/tex} are coplanar.

( \vec { b }\times \vec { c } ) + \vec { a .} ( \vec { b } \times \vec { a } ) + \vec { a } \cdot ( \vec { c } \times \vec { a } ) {/tex} {tex}+ \vec { b } \cdot ( \vec { b } \times \vec { c } ) + \vec { b } \cdot (\vec { b } \times \vec { a } ) + \vec { b }. ( \vec { c } \times \vec { a } ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow [ \vec { a } \ \vec { b } \ \vec { c } ] + [ \vec { a } \ \vec { b } \ \vec { a } ] + [ \vec { a } \ \vec { c }\ \vec { a } ] +[ \vec { b } \ \vec { b }\ \vec { c } ] + [ \vec { b } \ \vec { b }\ \vec { a } ] + [ \vec { b } \ \vec { c } \ \vec { a } ] =0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow [\vec { a }\ \vec { b }\ \vec { c } ] + [ \vec { b } \ \vec { c } \ \vec { a } ] =0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow [ \vec { a } \ \vec { b } \ \vec { c } ] + [ \vec { a } \ \vec { b }\ \vec { c } ]=0{/tex} {tex}[ \because [ \vec { b } \ \vec { c }\ \vec { a } ] = - [ \vec { b } \ \vec { a } \ \vec { c } ] = [ \vec { a }\ \vec { b }\ \vec { c } ] \ ]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 2 \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { \vec { a } } & { \vec { b } } & { \vec { c } } \end{array} \right] = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { \vec { a } } & { \vec { b } } & { \vec { c } } \end{array} \right] = 0{/tex} Hence, the vectors {tex}\vec { a } , \vec { b } , \vec { c }{/tex} are coplanar.

( \vec { c } \times \vec { a } ) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow [ \vec { a } \ \vec { b } \ \vec { c } ] + [ \vec { a } \ \vec { b } \ \vec { a } ] + [ \vec { a } \ \vec { c }\ \vec { a } ] +[ \vec { b } \ \vec { b }\ \vec { c } ] + [ \vec { b } \ \vec { b }\ \vec { a } ] + [ \vec { b } \ \vec { c } \ \vec { a } ] =0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow 2 \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { \vec { a } } & { \vec { b } } & { \vec { c } } \end{array} \right] = 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { \vec { a } } & { \vec { b } } & { \vec { c } } \end{array} \right] = 0{/tex}

question_html: Show that the four points A ( 4,5,1), B(0,-1,-1), C(3,9,4) and D (-4,4,4) are coplanar.
question_text: Show that the four points A ( 4,5,1), B(0,-1,-1), C(3,9,4) and D (-4,4,4) are coplanar.
explanation: According to the question , Given points are A ( 4,5,1), B(0,-1,-1), C(3,9,4) and D (-4,4,4). Let the position vector of points A, B, C and D are {tex}\vec { O A } = 4 \hat { i } + 5 \hat {j } + \hat { k } , {/tex} {tex}\vec { O B } =0 \hat {i} - \hat { j } - \hat { k },{/tex} {tex}\vec { O C } = 3 \hat { i } + 9 \hat {j} + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { O D } = - 4 \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec { A B } = \vec { O B } - \vec { O A }{/tex} {tex}= 0 \hat {i} - \hat { j } - \hat { k } - ( 4 \hat { i} + 5 \hat { j } + \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= - 4 \hat {i} - 6 \hat {j} - 2 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\vec { A C } = \vec { O C } - \vec { O A }{/tex} {tex}= 3 \hat { i } + 9 \hat { j} + 4 \hat { k } - ( 4 \hat { i } + 5 \hat { j } + \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= - \hat { i } + 4 \hat {j} + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\vec { A D } = \vec { O D } - \vec { O A }{/tex} {tex}= - 4 \hat i + 4 \hat j + 4 \hat { k } - ( 4 \hat i + 5 \hat { j} + \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= - 8 \hat {i} - \hat {j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} Use the condition that the four points are coplanar, if {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l l l }\vec { A B } & { \vec { A C } } & { \vec { A D } } \end{array} \right] = 0.{/tex} Now, {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { \vec { A B } } & { \vec { A C } } & { \vec { A D } } \end{array} \right] = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { - 4 } & { - 6 } & { - 2 } \\ { - 1 } & { 4 } & { 3 } \\ { - 8 } & { - 1 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex}= - 4 (12 + 3) + 6 (- 3 + 24) - 2(1 + 32) = - 60 + 126 - 66 =0{/tex} Hence, the four points A, B, C and D are coplanar.
solution: According to the question , Given points are A ( 4,5,1), B(0,-1,-1), C(3,9,4) and D (-4,4,4). Let the position vector of points A, B, C and D are {tex}\vec { O A } = 4 \hat { i } + 5 \hat {j } + \hat { k } , {/tex} {tex}\vec { O B } =0 \hat {i} - \hat { j } - \hat { k },{/tex} {tex}\vec { O C } = 3 \hat { i } + 9 \hat {j} + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { O D } = - 4 \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec { A B } = \vec { O B } - \vec { O A }{/tex} {tex}= 0 \hat {i} - \hat { j } - \hat { k } - ( 4 \hat { i} + 5 \hat { j } + \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= - 4 \hat {i} - 6 \hat {j} - 2 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\vec { A C } = \vec { O C } - \vec { O A }{/tex} {tex}= 3 \hat { i } + 9 \hat { j} + 4 \hat { k } - ( 4 \hat { i } + 5 \hat { j } + \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= - \hat { i } + 4 \hat {j} + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\vec { A D } = \vec { O D } - \vec { O A }{/tex} {tex}= - 4 \hat i + 4 \hat j + 4 \hat { k } - ( 4 \hat i + 5 \hat { j} + \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= - 8 \hat {i} - \hat {j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} Use the condition that the four points are coplanar, if {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l l l }\vec { A B } & { \vec { A C } } & { \vec { A D } } \end{array} \right] = 0.{/tex} Now, {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { \vec { A B } } & { \vec { A C } } & { \vec { A D } } \end{array} \right] = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { - 4 } & { - 6 } & { - 2 } \\ { - 1 } & { 4 } & { 3 } \\ { - 8 } & { - 1 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex}= - 4 (12 + 3) + 6 (- 3 + 24) - 2(1 + 32) = - 60 + 126 - 66 =0{/tex} Hence, the four points A, B, C and D are coplanar.
solution_html:
According to the question ,

Given points are  A ( 4,5,1), B(0,-1,-1), C(3,9,4) and D (-4,4,4).

Let the position vector of points A, B, C and D are
{tex}\vec { O A } = 4 \hat { i } + 5 \hat {j } + \hat { k } , {/tex}
{tex}\vec { O B } =0 \hat {i} - \hat { j } - \hat { k },{/tex}
{tex}\vec { O C } = 3 \hat { i } + 9 \hat {j} + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} and
{tex}\vec { O D } = - 4 \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} 


Now, {tex}\vec { A B } = \vec { O B } - \vec { O A }{/tex}
{tex}= 0 \hat {i} - \hat { j } - \hat { k } - ( 4 \hat { i} + 5 \hat { j } + \hat { k } ){/tex}
{tex}= - 4 \hat {i} - 6 \hat {j} - 2 \hat { k }{/tex}
{tex}\vec { A C } = \vec { O C } - \vec { O A }{/tex}
{tex}= 3 \hat { i } + 9 \hat { j} + 4 \hat { k } - ( 4 \hat { i } + 5 \hat { j } + \hat { k } ){/tex}
{tex}= - \hat { i } + 4 \hat {j} + 3 \hat { k }{/tex}
{tex}\vec { A D } = \vec { O D } - \vec { O A }{/tex}
{tex}= - 4 \hat i + 4 \hat j + 4 \hat { k } - ( 4 \hat i + 5 \hat { j} + \hat { k } ){/tex}
{tex}= - 8 \hat {i} - \hat {j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex}
Use the condition that the four points are coplanar, if {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l l l }\vec { A B } & { \vec { A C } } & { \vec { A D } } \end{array} \right] = 0.{/tex}
Now, {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { \vec { A B } } & { \vec { A C } } & { \vec { A D } } \end{array} \right] = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { - 4 } & { - 6 } & { - 2 } \\ { - 1 } & { 4 } & { 3 } \\ { - 8 } & { - 1 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
{tex}= - 4 (12 + 3) + 6 (- 3 + 24) - 2(1 + 32) = - 60 + 126 - 66  =0{/tex}
Hence, the four points A, B, C and D are coplanar.

question_html: The two vectors {tex}\hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and 3{tex}\hat { i } - \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} represent the two sides AB and AC respectively of triangle ABC. Find the length of the median through A.
question_text: The two vectors {tex}\hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and 3 {tex}\hat { i } - \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} represent the two sides AB and AC respectively of triangle ABC. Find the length of the median through A.
explanation: We are given that {tex}\vec { A B } = \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { A C } = 3 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} Clearly, median vector, {tex}\vec { A D } = \frac { \vec { A B } + \vec { A C } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { ( \hat { j } + \hat { k } ) + ( \hat { 3 } \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } ) } { 2 } = \frac { 3 \hat { i } + 5 \hat { k } } { 2 }{/tex} Now, length of median = {tex}\vec { | A D | }{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { \left( \frac { 3 } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { 5 } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sqrt { 9 + 25 }= \frac { \sqrt { 34 } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \sqrt { 17 } \times \sqrt { 2 } } { 2 } \equiv \sqrt { \frac { 17 } { 2 } }{/tex} units.
solution: We are given that {tex}\vec { A B } = \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { A C } = 3 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} Clearly, median vector, {tex}\vec { A D } = \frac { \vec { A B } + \vec { A C } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { ( \hat { j } + \hat { k } ) + ( \hat { 3 } \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } ) } { 2 } = \frac { 3 \hat { i } + 5 \hat { k } } { 2 }{/tex} Now, length of median = {tex}\vec { | A D | }{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { \left( \frac { 3 } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { 5 } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sqrt { 9 + 25 }= \frac { \sqrt { 34 } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \sqrt { 17 } \times \sqrt { 2 } } { 2 } \equiv \sqrt { \frac { 17 } { 2 } }{/tex} units.
solution_html:
We are given that {tex}\vec { A B } = \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { A C } = 3 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex}

Clearly, median vector, {tex}\vec { A D } = \frac { \vec { A B } + \vec { A C } } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { ( \hat { j } + \hat { k } ) + ( \hat { 3 } \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } ) } { 2 } = \frac { 3 \hat { i } + 5 \hat { k } } { 2 }{/tex}
Now, length of median = {tex}\vec { | A D | }{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt { \left( \frac { 3 } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { 5 } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sqrt { 9 + 25 }= \frac { \sqrt { 34 } } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \sqrt { 17 } \times \sqrt { 2 } } { 2 } \equiv \sqrt { \frac { 17 } { 2 } }{/tex}units.
