question_html: The two vectors {tex}\hat j{/tex} + {tex}\hat i{/tex} and 3{tex}\hat i{/tex} - {tex}\hat j{/tex} + 4{tex}\hat k{/tex} represent the sides {tex}\vec {AB}{/tex}  and {tex}\vec {AC}{/tex} respectively of triangle ABC. Find the length of the median through A.
question_text: The two vectors {tex}\hat j{/tex} + {tex}\hat i{/tex} and 3 {tex}\hat i{/tex} - {tex}\hat j{/tex} + 4 {tex}\hat k{/tex} represent the sides {tex}\vec {AB}{/tex} and {tex}\vec {AC}{/tex} respectively of triangle ABC. Find the length of the median through A.
explanation: Let D be the mid-point of side BC of triangle ABC. Then, {tex}\vec {AB}{/tex} + {tex}\vec {AC}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\vec {AD}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ( {tex}\vec j{/tex} + {tex}\vec i{/tex} ) + (3 {tex}\hat i{/tex} - {tex}\hat j{/tex} + 4 {tex}\hat k{/tex} ) = 2 {tex}\vec {AD}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec {AD}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\vec i{/tex} + 0 {tex}\hat j{/tex} + 2 {tex}\hat k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec {AD} |{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{4 + 0 + 4}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}
solution: Let D be the mid-point of side BC of triangle ABC. Then, {tex}\vec {AB}{/tex} + {tex}\vec {AC}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\vec {AD}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ( {tex}\vec j{/tex} + {tex}\vec i{/tex} ) + (3 {tex}\hat i{/tex} - {tex}\hat j{/tex} + 4 {tex}\hat k{/tex} ) = 2 {tex}\vec {AD}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec {AD}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\vec i{/tex} + 0 {tex}\hat j{/tex} + 2 {tex}\hat k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec {AD} |{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{4 + 0 + 4}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let D be the mid-point of side BC of triangle ABC. Then,
{tex}\vec {AB}{/tex} + {tex}\vec {AC}{/tex} = 2{tex}\vec {AD}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ({tex}\vec j{/tex} + {tex}\vec i{/tex}) + (3{tex}\hat i{/tex} - {tex}\hat j{/tex} + 4{tex}\hat k{/tex}) = 2{tex}\vec {AD}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec {AD}{/tex} = 2{tex}\vec i{/tex} + 0{tex}\hat j{/tex} + 2{tex}\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec {AD} |{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{4 + 0 + 4}{/tex} = 2{tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} are unit vectors, then what is the angle between {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} for {tex}\vec{a}-\sqrt{2} \vec{b}{/tex} to be unit vectors?
question_text: If {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} are unit vectors, then what is the angle between {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} for {tex}\vec{a}-\sqrt{2} \vec{b}{/tex} to be unit vectors?
explanation: We have, {tex} |\vec{a}|=1 |\vec{b}|=1 {/tex} {tex} |\vec{a}-\sqrt{2} \vec{b}|=1 {/tex} {tex} |\vec{a}-\sqrt{2} \vec{b}|^2=1 {/tex} {tex} (\vec{a}-\sqrt{2} \vec{b}) \cdot(\vec{a}-\sqrt{2} \vec{b})=1 {/tex} {tex} |\vec{a}|^2+2|\vec{b}|^2-2 \sqrt{2} \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=1 {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=|\vec{a}||\vec{b}| \cos \theta {/tex} {tex} \cos \theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} {/tex} {tex} \theta=\frac{\pi}{4}=45^{\circ} {/tex}
solution: We have, {tex} |\vec{a}|=1 |\vec{b}|=1 {/tex} {tex} |\vec{a}-\sqrt{2} \vec{b}|=1 {/tex} {tex} |\vec{a}-\sqrt{2} \vec{b}|^2=1 {/tex} {tex} (\vec{a}-\sqrt{2} \vec{b}) \cdot(\vec{a}-\sqrt{2} \vec{b})=1 {/tex} {tex} |\vec{a}|^2+2|\vec{b}|^2-2 \sqrt{2} \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=1 {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=|\vec{a}||\vec{b}| \cos \theta {/tex} {tex} \cos \theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} {/tex} {tex} \theta=\frac{\pi}{4}=45^{\circ} {/tex}
solution_html:
We have,
{tex} |\vec{a}|=1 |\vec{b}|=1 {/tex}
{tex} |\vec{a}-\sqrt{2} \vec{b}|=1 {/tex}
{tex} |\vec{a}-\sqrt{2} \vec{b}|^2=1 {/tex}
{tex} (\vec{a}-\sqrt{2} \vec{b}) \cdot(\vec{a}-\sqrt{2} \vec{b})=1 {/tex}
{tex} |\vec{a}|^2+2|\vec{b}|^2-2 \sqrt{2} \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=1 {/tex}
{tex} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=|\vec{a}||\vec{b}| \cos \theta {/tex}
{tex} \cos \theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} {/tex}
{tex} \theta=\frac{\pi}{4}=45^{\circ} {/tex}

question_html: Find the position vector of a point which divides the join of points with position vectors {tex} \vec { a } - 2 \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex} 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} externally in the ratio 2:1.
question_text: Find the position vector of a point which divides the join of points with position vectors {tex} \vec { a } - 2 \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex} 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} externally in the ratio 2:1.
explanation: Given position vectors are {tex} \vec { O A } = \vec { a } - 2 \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { O B } = 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} . Let {tex}\vec { O C }{/tex} be the position vector of a point C which divides the join of points, with position vectors {tex} \vec { O A }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { O B },{/tex} externally in the ratio 2:1. {tex} \therefore \vec { O C } = \frac { 2 \vec { O B } - 1 \vec { O A } } { 2 - 1 } = \frac { 2 ( 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) - 1 ( \vec { a } - 2 \vec { b } ) } { 1 }{/tex} [by external section formula] {tex} = 4 \vec { a } + 2 \vec { b } - \vec { a } + 2 \vec { b } = 3 \vec { a } + 4 \vec { b }{/tex}
solution: Given position vectors are {tex} \vec { O A } = \vec { a } - 2 \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { O B } = 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} . Let {tex}\vec { O C }{/tex} be the position vector of a point C which divides the join of points, with position vectors {tex} \vec { O A }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { O B },{/tex} externally in the ratio 2:1. {tex} \therefore \vec { O C } = \frac { 2 \vec { O B } - 1 \vec { O A } } { 2 - 1 } = \frac { 2 ( 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) - 1 ( \vec { a } - 2 \vec { b } ) } { 1 }{/tex} [by external section formula] {tex} = 4 \vec { a } + 2 \vec { b } - \vec { a } + 2 \vec { b } = 3 \vec { a } + 4 \vec { b }{/tex}
solution_html:
Given position vectors are {tex} \vec { O A } = \vec { a } - 2 \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { O B } = 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex}.
Let {tex}\vec { O C }{/tex} be the position vector of a point C which divides the join of points, with position vectors {tex} \vec { O A }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { O B },{/tex} externally in the ratio 2:1.
{tex} \therefore \vec { O C } = \frac { 2 \vec { O B } - 1 \vec { O A } } { 2 - 1 } = \frac { 2 ( 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) - 1 ( \vec { a } - 2 \vec { b } ) } { 1 }{/tex}
[by external section formula]
{tex} = 4 \vec { a } + 2 \vec { b } - \vec { a } + 2 \vec { b } = 3 \vec { a } + 4 \vec { b }{/tex}

question_html: If {tex} \vec { a } = 4 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k },{/tex}then find a unit vector parallel to the vector {tex} \vec { a } + \vec { b }.{/tex}
question_text: If {tex} \vec { a } = 4 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k },{/tex} then find a unit vector parallel to the vector {tex} \vec { a } + \vec { b }.{/tex}
explanation: Here we are given that {tex} \vec { a } = 4 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + \hat { k } , \vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k }.{/tex} Now, {tex} \vec { a } + \vec { b } = ( 4 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + \hat { k } )+ ( 2 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex} = 6 \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} | \vec { a } + \vec { b } | = \sqrt { ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 3 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = \sqrt { 36 + 9 + 4 } = \sqrt { 49 }{/tex} = 7 units {tex} \therefore{/tex} The unit vector parallel to the vector {tex} \vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} is given as {tex} \frac { \vec { a } + \vec { b } } { | \vec { a } + \vec { b } | } = \frac { 6 \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } } { 7 }{/tex}
solution: Here we are given that {tex} \vec { a } = 4 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + \hat { k } , \vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k }.{/tex} Now, {tex} \vec { a } + \vec { b } = ( 4 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + \hat { k } )+ ( 2 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex} = 6 \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} | \vec { a } + \vec { b } | = \sqrt { ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 3 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = \sqrt { 36 + 9 + 4 } = \sqrt { 49 }{/tex} = 7 units {tex} \therefore{/tex} The unit vector parallel to the vector {tex} \vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} is given as {tex} \frac { \vec { a } + \vec { b } } { | \vec { a } + \vec { b } | } = \frac { 6 \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } } { 7 }{/tex}
solution_html:
Here we are given that {tex} \vec { a } = 4 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + \hat { k } , \vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k }.{/tex}
Now, {tex} \vec { a } + \vec { b } = ( 4 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + \hat { k } )+ ( 2 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k } ){/tex}
{tex} = 6 \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k }{/tex}
and {tex} | \vec { a } + \vec { b } | = \sqrt { ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 3 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt { 36 + 9 + 4 } = \sqrt { 49 }{/tex}= 7 units
{tex} \therefore{/tex} The unit vector parallel to the vector {tex} \vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} is given as
{tex} \frac { \vec { a } + \vec { b } } { | \vec { a } + \vec { b } | } = \frac { 6 \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } } { 7 }{/tex}

question_html:
Find {tex} \lambda{/tex} and{tex}\mathrm { } \mu{/tex}, if
{tex}( \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + 9 \hat { k } ) \times ( 3 \hat { i } - \lambda \hat { j } + \mu \hat { k } ) = \vec { 0 }.{/tex}
question_text: Find {tex} \lambda{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm { } \mu{/tex} , if {tex}( \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + 9 \hat { k } ) \times ( 3 \hat { i } - \lambda \hat { j } + \mu \hat { k } ) = \vec { 0 }.{/tex}
explanation: In order to find the two unknowns, we evaluate and simplify the cross product. Given: {tex} ( \hat { i} + 3 \hat { j } + 9 \hat { k } ) \times ( 3 \hat { i } - \lambda \hat { j } + \mu \hat { k } ) = \vec { 0 }{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { \hat { i} } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { 1 } & { 3 } & { 9 } \\ { 3 } & { - \lambda } & { \mu } \end{array} \right| = \vec { 0 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \hat { i } ( 3 \mu + 9 \lambda ) - \hat { j } ( \mu - 27 ) + \hat { k } ( - \lambda - 9 ){/tex} {tex}= 0 \hat { i } + 0 \hat { j } + 0 \hat { k }{/tex} On comparing the coefficients of {tex}\hat { i } , \hat { j }{/tex} and {tex}\hat { k },{/tex} we get {tex}3 \mu + 9 \lambda = 0 , - \mu + 27 = 0 \text { and } - \lambda - 9 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mu = 27 \text { and } - \lambda = 9{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mu = 27 \text { and } \lambda = - 9{/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex}{/tex}
solution: In order to find the two unknowns, we evaluate and simplify the cross product. Given: {tex} ( \hat { i} + 3 \hat { j } + 9 \hat { k } ) \times ( 3 \hat { i } - \lambda \hat { j } + \mu \hat { k } ) = \vec { 0 }{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { \hat { i} } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { 1 } & { 3 } & { 9 } \\ { 3 } & { - \lambda } & { \mu } \end{array} \right| = \vec { 0 }{/tex}

{tex}\vec { a } = 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } \text { and } \vec { b } = \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex}.
question_text: Write the number of vectors of unit length perpendicular to both the vectors {tex}\vec { a } = 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } \text { and } \vec { b } = \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} .
explanation: The unit vector can be perpendicular either in the positive direction or negative direction. Thus, unit vectors perpendicular to {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} are given as {tex}\pm \left( \frac { \vec { a } \times \vec { b } } { | \vec { a } \times \vec { b } | } \right){/tex} So, obviously, there are two unit vectors perpendicular to the given vectors.

question_html: If {tex} \vec { a } , \vec { b } , \vec { c }{/tex} are unit vectors such that {tex} \vec { a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } = \vec { 0 },{/tex} then write the value of {tex} \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } + \vec { b } \vec { c } + \vec { c } \cdot \vec { a }.{/tex}
question_text: If {tex} \vec { a } , \vec { b } , \vec { c }{/tex} are unit vectors such that {tex} \vec { a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } = \vec { 0 },{/tex} then write the value of {tex} \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } + \vec { b } \vec { c } + \vec { c } \cdot \vec { a }.{/tex}
explanation: Here, we have to find the value of {tex} \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } + \vec { b } \cdot\vec { c } + \vec { c } \cdot \vec { a }.{/tex} Given, {tex} | \vec { a } | = | \vec { b } | = | \vec { c } | = 1{/tex} and {tex} \vec { a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } = \vec { 0 }.{/tex} Now,consider, {tex} \vec { a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } = \vec { 0 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad ( \vec { a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } ) ^ { 2 } = ( \vec { 0 } ) ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \vec { ( a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } ). \ \vec { ( a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } ) = \vec { 0 } .\vec { 0 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \vec { a } .\vec { a } + \vec { a }.\vec { b } + \vec { a } .\vec { c } + \vec { b }. \vec { a } + \vec { b } .\vec { b } + \vec { b } \cdot \vec { c }+ \vec { a }.\vec { c }{/tex} {tex} +\vec { c }. \vec { b } + \vec { c } .\vec { c } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 } + | \vec { b } | ^ { 2 } + | \vec { c } | ^ { 2 } + 2 ( \vec { a }. \vec { b } + \vec { b }. \vec { c } + \vec { c } . \vec { c })= 0{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \vec { x }. \vec { y } = \vec { y } .\vec { x } \text { and } \vec { x }. \vec { x } = | \vec { x } | ^ { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 1 + 1 + 1 + 2 \vec { ( a }. \vec { b } + \vec { b }. \vec { c } + \vec { c }. \vec { a } ) = 0{/tex} {tex} [ \because | \vec { a } | = | \vec { b } | = | \vec { c } | = 1 ]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \vec { a }. \vec { b } + \vec { b }. \vec { c } + \vec { c }. \vec { a } = \frac { - 3 } { 2 }{/tex}
solution: Here, we have to find the value of {tex} \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } + \vec { b } \cdot\vec { c } + \vec { c } \cdot \vec { a }.{/tex} Given, {tex} | \vec { a } | = | \vec { b } | = | \vec { c } | = 1{/tex} and {tex} \vec { a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } = \vec { 0 }.{/tex} Now,consider, {tex} \vec { a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } = \vec { 0 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad ( \vec { a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } ) ^ { 2 } = ( \vec { 0 } ) ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \vec { ( a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } ). \ \vec { ( a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } ) = \vec { 0 } .\vec { 0 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \vec { a } .\vec { a } + \vec { a }.\vec { b } + \vec { a } .\vec { c } + \vec { b }. \vec { a } + \vec { b } .\vec { b } + \vec { b } \cdot \vec { c }+ \vec { a }.\vec { c }{/tex} {tex} +\vec { c }. \vec { b } + \vec { c } .\vec { c } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 } + | \vec { b } | ^ { 2 } + | \vec { c } | ^ { 2 } + 2 ( \vec { a }. \vec { b } + \vec { b }. \vec { c } + \vec { c } . \vec { c })= 0{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \vec { x }. \vec { y } = \vec { y } .\vec { x } \text { and } \vec { x }. \vec { x } = | \vec { x } | ^ { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 1 + 1 + 1 + 2 \vec { ( a }. \vec { b } + \vec { b }. \vec { c } + \vec { c }. \vec { a } ) = 0{/tex} {tex} [ \because | \vec { a } | = | \vec { b } | = | \vec { c } | = 1 ]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \vec { a }. \vec { b } + \vec { b }. \vec { c } + \vec { c }. \vec { a } = \frac { - 3 } { 2 }{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, we have to find  the value of {tex} \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } + \vec { b } \cdot\vec { c } + \vec { c } \cdot \vec { a }.{/tex}Given, {tex} | \vec { a } | = | \vec { b } | = | \vec { c } | = 1{/tex}and {tex} \vec { a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } = \vec { 0 }.{/tex}
Now,consider, {tex} \vec { a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } = \vec { 0 }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad ( \vec { a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } ) ^ { 2 } = ( \vec { 0 } ) ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \vec { ( a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } ). \ \vec { ( a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } ) = \vec { 0 } .\vec { 0 }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \vec { a } .\vec { a } + \vec { a }.\vec { b } + \vec { a } .\vec { c } + \vec { b }. \vec { a } + \vec { b } .\vec { b } + \vec { b } \cdot \vec { c }+ \vec { a }.\vec { c }{/tex}{tex} +\vec { c }. \vec { b } + \vec { c } .\vec { c } = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 } + | \vec { b } | ^ { 2 } + | \vec { c } | ^ { 2 } + 2 ( \vec { a }. \vec { b } + \vec { b }. \vec { c } + \vec { c } . \vec { c })= 0{/tex}
{tex} \left[ \because \vec { x }. \vec { y } = \vec { y } .\vec { x } \text { and } \vec { x }. \vec { x } = | \vec { x } | ^ { 2 } \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 1 + 1 + 1 + 2 \vec { ( a }. \vec { b } + \vec { b }. \vec { c } + \vec { c }. \vec { a } ) = 0{/tex}
{tex} [ \because | \vec { a } | = | \vec { b } | = | \vec { c } | = 1 ]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \vec { a }. \vec { b } + \vec { b }. \vec { c } + \vec { c }. \vec { a } = \frac { - 3 } { 2 }{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Write the position vector of the point which divides the join of points with position vectors {tex}3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}{/tex} in the ratio 2 : 1.
question_text: Write the position vector of the point which divides the join of points with position vectors {tex}3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}{/tex} in the ratio 2 : 1.
explanation: Suppose R be the point which divides the line joining the points with position vectors {tex}3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}{/tex} in the ratio 2 :1. And, {tex}\overrightarrow{O A}=3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{O B}=2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}{/tex} Here, m : n = 2 : 1 Therefore, position vector {tex}\overrightarrow{O R}{/tex} is given below {tex}\overrightarrow{O R}= \frac { m \overrightarrow{O B}+n \overrightarrow{O A}} {m+n}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2(2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b})+1(3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b})}{2+1}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{7 \vec{a}+4 \vec{b}}{3}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{7}{3} \vec{a}+\frac{4}{3} \vec{b}{/tex}
solution: Suppose R be the point which divides the line joining the points with position vectors {tex}3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}{/tex} in the ratio 2 :1. And, {tex}\overrightarrow{O A}=3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{O B}=2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}{/tex} Here, m : n = 2 : 1 Therefore, position vector {tex}\overrightarrow{O R}{/tex} is given below {tex}\overrightarrow{O R}= \frac { m \overrightarrow{O B}+n \overrightarrow{O A}} {m+n}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2(2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b})+1(3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b})}{2+1}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{7 \vec{a}+4 \vec{b}}{3}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{7}{3} \vec{a}+\frac{4}{3} \vec{b}{/tex}
solution_html:
Suppose R be the point which divides the line joining the points with position vectors  {tex}3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}{/tex} in the ratio 2 :1.
And, {tex}\overrightarrow{O A}=3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}{/tex}and {tex}\overrightarrow{O B}=2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}{/tex}
Here, m : n = 2 : 1
Therefore, position vector {tex}\overrightarrow{O R}{/tex} is given below
{tex}\overrightarrow{O R}= \frac { m \overrightarrow{O B}+n \overrightarrow{O A}} {m+n}{/tex} 
{tex}=\frac{2(2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b})+1(3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b})}{2+1}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{7 \vec{a}+4 \vec{b}}{3}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{7}{3} \vec{a}+\frac{4}{3} \vec{b}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the position vector of a point R which divides the line joining the two points P and Q with position vectors {tex}\overrightarrow{O P}=2 \vec{a}+\vec{b} \text { and } \overrightarrow{O Q}=\vec{a}-2 \vec{b}{/tex}, respectively in the ratio 1 :2 internally and externally.
question_text: Find the position vector of a point R which divides the line joining the two points P and Q with position vectors {tex}\overrightarrow{O P}=2 \vec{a}+\vec{b} \text { and } \overrightarrow{O Q}=\vec{a}-2 \vec{b}{/tex} , respectively in the ratio 1 :2 internally and externally.
explanation: Point R divides the line joining the two points $P$ and $Q$ in the ratio 1: 2 internally. Position vector of point R {tex}=\frac{1(\overrightarrow{a}-2 \overrightarrow{b})+2(2 \overrightarrow{a}+\overrightarrow{b})}{1+2}=\frac{5 \overrightarrow{a}}{3}{/tex} Point R divides the line joining the two points P and Q in the ratio 1: 2 externally Position vector of point R {tex}=\frac{1(\overrightarrow {a}-2 \overrightarrow{b})-2(2 \overrightarrow{a}+\overrightarrow{b})}{1-2}=\frac{-3 \overrightarrow{a}-4 \overrightarrow{b}}{-1}=3 \overrightarrow{a}+4 \overrightarrow{b}{/tex}
solution: Point R divides the line joining the two points $P$ and $Q$ in the ratio 1: 2 internally. Position vector of point R {tex}=\frac{1(\overrightarrow{a}-2 \overrightarrow{b})+2(2 \overrightarrow{a}+\overrightarrow{b})}{1+2}=\frac{5 \overrightarrow{a}}{3}{/tex} Point R divides the line joining the two points P and Q in the ratio 1: 2 externally Position vector of point R {tex}=\frac{1(\overrightarrow {a}-2 \overrightarrow{b})-2(2 \overrightarrow{a}+\overrightarrow{b})}{1-2}=\frac{-3 \overrightarrow{a}-4 \overrightarrow{b}}{-1}=3 \overrightarrow{a}+4 \overrightarrow{b}{/tex}
solution_html:
Point R divides the line joining the two points $P$ and $Q$ in the ratio 1: 2 internally.
Position vector of point R {tex}=\frac{1(\overrightarrow{a}-2 \overrightarrow{b})+2(2 \overrightarrow{a}+\overrightarrow{b})}{1+2}=\frac{5 \overrightarrow{a}}{3}{/tex}
Point R divides the line joining the two points P and Q in the ratio 1: 2 externally
Position vector of point R {tex}=\frac{1(\overrightarrow {a}-2 \overrightarrow{b})-2(2 \overrightarrow{a}+\overrightarrow{b})}{1-2}=\frac{-3 \overrightarrow{a}-4 \overrightarrow{b}}{-1}=3 \overrightarrow{a}+4 \overrightarrow{b}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If four points A, B , C and D with position vectors {tex}4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}, 5 \hat{i}+x \hat{j}+7 k, 5 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}{/tex} and {tex}7 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} respectively are coplanar, then find the value of x.
question_text: If four points A, B , C and D with position vectors {tex}4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}, 5 \hat{i}+x \hat{j}+7 k, 5 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}{/tex} and {tex}7 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} respectively are coplanar, then find the value of x.
explanation: Suppose that {tex}\overrightarrow{O A}=4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}, \overrightarrow{O B}{/tex} = {tex}5 \hat{i}+x \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}, \overrightarrow{O C}{/tex} = {tex}5 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{O D}=7 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}=(5 \hat{i}+x \hat{j}+7 \hat{k})-(4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})=\hat{i}+(x-3) \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{A C}=(5 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j})-(4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})=\hat{i}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{A D}=(7 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+\hat{k})-(4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})=3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex} Since the given four points are coplanar, so the vectors {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}, \overrightarrow{A C}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{A D}{/tex} are also coplanar.therefore {tex}[\overrightarrow{A B} \quad \overrightarrow{A C} \quad \overrightarrow{A D}]{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & x-3 & 4 \\ 1 & 0 & -3 \\ 3 & 3 & -2 \end{array}\right|=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1(0+9)-(x-3)(-2+9)+4(3-0)=0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9-7 x+21+12=0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 7 x=42 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x=6 Th, the value of x is 6
solution: Suppose that {tex}\overrightarrow{O A}=4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}, \overrightarrow{O B}{/tex} = {tex}5 \hat{i}+x \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}, \overrightarrow{O C}{/tex} = {tex}5 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{O D}=7 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}=(5 \hat{i}+x \hat{j}+7 \hat{k})-(4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})=\hat{i}+(x-3) \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{A C}=(5 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j})-(4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})=\hat{i}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{A D}=(7 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+\hat{k})-(4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})=3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex} Since the given four points are coplanar, so the vectors {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}, \overrightarrow{A C}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{A D}{/tex} are also coplanar.therefore {tex}[\overrightarrow{A B} \quad \overrightarrow{A C} \quad \overrightarrow{A D}]{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & x-3 & 4 \\ 1 & 0 & -3 \\ 3 & 3 & -2 \end{array}\right|=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1(0+9)-(x-3)(-2+9)+4(3-0)=0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9-7 x+21+12=0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 7 x=42 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x=6 Th, the value of x is 6
solution_html:
Suppose that {tex}\overrightarrow{O A}=4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}, \overrightarrow{O B}{/tex} = {tex}5 \hat{i}+x \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}, \overrightarrow{O C}{/tex} = {tex}5 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{O D}=7 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}=(5 \hat{i}+x \hat{j}+7 \hat{k})-(4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})=\hat{i}+(x-3) \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{A C}=(5 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j})-(4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})=\hat{i}-3 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{A D}=(7 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+\hat{k})-(4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})=3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex}
Since the given four points are coplanar, so the vectors {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}, \overrightarrow{A C}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{A D}{/tex} are also coplanar.therefore
{tex}[\overrightarrow{A B} \quad \overrightarrow{A C} \quad \overrightarrow{A D}]{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & x-3 & 4 \\ 1 & 0 & -3 \\ 3 & 3 & -2 \end{array}\right|=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1(0+9)-(x-3)(-2+9)+4(3-0)=0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9-7 x+21+12=0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 7 x=42
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x=6
Th, the value of x is 6

question_html: Find the angle between two vectors {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex}having the same length {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}  and their vector product is {tex}-\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex}.
question_text: Find the angle between two vectors {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} having the same length {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} and their vector product is {tex}-\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\sin \theta=\frac{|-\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}|}{\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}\sin \theta=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex} or {tex}\theta{/tex} = 60 o or {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\sin \theta=\frac{|-\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}|}{\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}\sin \theta=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex} or {tex}\theta{/tex} = 60 o or {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\sin \theta=\frac{|-\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}|}{\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\sin \theta=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}
or {tex}\theta{/tex} = 60o
or {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}

question_html: Let {tex}\vec a = \hat i + 4\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex}, {tex}\vec b = 3\hat i - 2\hat j + 7\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = 2\hat i - \hat j + 4\hat k{/tex}. Find a vector {tex}\vec d{/tex} which is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex}, and {tex}\vec c.\vec d = 15{/tex}.
question_text: Let {tex}\vec a = \hat i + 4\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} , {tex}\vec b = 3\hat i - 2\hat j + 7\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = 2\hat i - \hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} . Find a vector {tex}\vec d{/tex} which is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} , and {tex}\vec c.\vec d = 15{/tex} .
explanation: Given: Vectors {tex}\vec a = \hat i + 4\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = 3\hat i - 2\hat j + 7\hat k{/tex} We know that the cross-product of two vectors, {tex}\vec a \times \vec b{/tex} is a vector perpendicular to both {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} Hence, vector {tex}\vec d{/tex} which is also perpendicular to both {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} is {tex}\vec d = \lambda \left( {\vec a \times \vec b} \right){/tex} where {tex}\lambda = 1{/tex} or some other scalar. Therefore, {tex}\vec d = \lambda \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} \vec i&\vec j&\vec k \\ 1&4&2 \\ 3&{ - 2}&7 \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \lambda \left[ {\hat i\left( {28 + 4} \right) - \hat j\left( {7 - 6} \right) + \hat k\left( { - 2 - 12} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \vec d = 32\lambda \hat i - \lambda \hat j - 14\lambda \hat k{/tex} ...(i) Now given {tex}\vec c = 2\hat i - \hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c.\vec d = 15{/tex} {tex}\vec c.\vec d = 15{/tex} {tex}= 2\left( {32\lambda } \right) + \left( { - 1} \right)\left( { - \lambda } \right) + 4\left( { - 14\lambda } \right) = 15{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 64\lambda + \lambda - 56\lambda = 15{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 9\lambda = 15{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{15}}{9}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{5}{3}{/tex} Putting {tex}\lambda = \frac{5}{3}{/tex} in eq. (i), we get {tex}\vec d = \frac{5}{3}\left[ {32\hat i - \hat j - 14\hat k} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec d = \frac{1}{3}\left[ {160\hat i - 5\hat j - 70\hat k} \right]{/tex}
solution: Given: Vectors {tex}\vec a = \hat i + 4\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = 3\hat i - 2\hat j + 7\hat k{/tex} We know that the cross-product of two vectors, {tex}\vec a \times \vec b{/tex} is a vector perpendicular to both {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} Hence, vector {tex}\vec d{/tex} which is also perpendicular to both {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} is {tex}\vec d = \lambda \left( {\vec a \times \vec b} \right){/tex} where {tex}\lambda = 1{/tex} or some other scalar. Therefore, {tex}\vec d = \lambda \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} \vec i&\vec j&\vec k \\ 1&4&2 \\ 3&{ - 2}&7 \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \lambda \left[ {\hat i\left( {28 + 4} \right) - \hat j\left( {7 - 6} \right) + \hat k\left( { - 2 - 12} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \vec d = 32\lambda \hat i - \lambda \hat j - 14\lambda \hat k{/tex} ...(i) Now given {tex}\vec c = 2\hat i - \hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c.\vec d = 15{/tex} {tex}\vec c.\vec d = 15{/tex} {tex}= 2\left( {32\lambda } \right) + \left( { - 1} \right)\left( { - \lambda } \right) + 4\left( { - 14\lambda } \right) = 15{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 64\lambda + \lambda - 56\lambda = 15{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 9\lambda = 15{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{15}}{9}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{5}{3}{/tex} Putting {tex}\lambda = \frac{5}{3}{/tex} in eq. (i), we get {tex}\vec d = \frac{5}{3}\left[ {32\hat i - \hat j - 14\hat k} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec d = \frac{1}{3}\left[ {160\hat i - 5\hat j - 70\hat k} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: Vectors {tex}\vec a = \hat i + 4\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = 3\hat i - 2\hat j + 7\hat k{/tex}
We know that the cross-product of two vectors, {tex}\vec a \times \vec b{/tex} is a vector perpendicular to both {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex}
Hence, vector {tex}\vec d{/tex} which is also perpendicular to both {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} is {tex}\vec d = \lambda \left( {\vec a \times \vec b} \right){/tex} where {tex}\lambda = 1{/tex} or some other scalar.
Therefore, {tex}\vec d = \lambda \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} \vec i&\vec j&\vec k \\ 1&4&2 \\ 3&{ - 2}&7 \end{array}} \right|{/tex}
{tex}= \lambda \left[ {\hat i\left( {28 + 4} \right) - \hat j\left( {7 - 6} \right) + \hat k\left( { - 2 - 12} \right)} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \vec d = 32\lambda \hat i - \lambda \hat j - 14\lambda \hat k{/tex}...(i)
Now given {tex}\vec c = 2\hat i - \hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c.\vec d = 15{/tex}
{tex}\vec c.\vec d = 15{/tex}
{tex}= 2\left( {32\lambda } \right) + \left( { - 1} \right)\left( { - \lambda } \right) + 4\left( { - 14\lambda } \right) = 15{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 64\lambda + \lambda - 56\lambda = 15{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 9\lambda = 15{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{15}}{9}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{5}{3}{/tex}
Putting {tex}\lambda = \frac{5}{3}{/tex} in eq. (i), we get
{tex}\vec d = \frac{5}{3}\left[ {32\hat i - \hat j - 14\hat k} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec d = \frac{1}{3}\left[ {160\hat i - 5\hat j - 70\hat k} \right]{/tex}

question_html: Write the projection of the vector {tex}7 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k }{/tex} on the vector {tex}2 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }.{/tex}
question_text: Write the projection of the vector {tex}7 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k }{/tex} on the vector {tex}2 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }.{/tex}
explanation: Let {tex}\vec a{/tex} = 7 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + {tex}\hat j{/tex} - 4 {tex}\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} = 2 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 6 {tex}\hat j{/tex} + 3 {tex}\hat k{/tex} The projection of {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} is given by {tex}\frac{1}{\left| \vec b \right|}(\vec{a} . \vec{b}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{(7 \times 2)+(1 \times 6)+(-4 \times 3)}{\sqrt{2^{2}+6^{2}+3^{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{8}{7}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}\vec a{/tex} = 7 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + {tex}\hat j{/tex} - 4 {tex}\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} = 2 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 6 {tex}\hat j{/tex} + 3 {tex}\hat k{/tex} The projection of {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} is given by {tex}\frac{1}{\left| \vec b \right|}(\vec{a} . \vec{b}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{(7 \times 2)+(1 \times 6)+(-4 \times 3)}{\sqrt{2^{2}+6^{2}+3^{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{8}{7}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\vec a{/tex} = 7{tex}\hat i{/tex} + {tex}\hat j{/tex} - 4{tex}\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} = 2{tex}\hat i{/tex} + 6{tex}\hat j{/tex} + 3{tex}\hat k{/tex}
The projection of {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} is given by
{tex}\frac{1}{\left| \vec b \right|}(\vec{a} . \vec{b}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{(7 \times 2)+(1 \times 6)+(-4 \times 3)}{\sqrt{2^{2}+6^{2}+3^{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{8}{7}{/tex}

question_html: Write the direction ratios of the vector 3{tex}\vec { a } + 2 \vec { b },{/tex} where {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } - 4 \hat { j } + 5 \hat { k }.{/tex}
question_text: Write the direction ratios of the vector 3 {tex}\vec { a } + 2 \vec { b },{/tex} where {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } - 4 \hat { j } + 5 \hat { k }.{/tex}
explanation: Here, we are given that {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } - 4 \hat { j } + 5 \hat { k }.{/tex} Therefore, we have {tex}3 \vec { a } + 2 \vec { b } = 3 ( \hat { i } + \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } ) + 2 ( 2 \hat { i } - 4 \hat { j } + 5 \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= ( 3 \hat{i} + 3 \hat { j } - 6 \hat { k } ) + ( 4 \hat { i } - 8 \hat { j } + 10 \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= { 7 } \hat { i} - 5 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} Hence, direction ratio of vectors {tex}3 \vec { a } + 2 \vec { b }{/tex} are 7, - 5 and 4.
solution: Here, we are given that {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } - 4 \hat { j } + 5 \hat { k }.{/tex} Therefore, we have {tex}3 \vec { a } + 2 \vec { b } = 3 ( \hat { i } + \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } ) + 2 ( 2 \hat { i } - 4 \hat { j } + 5 \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= ( 3 \hat{i} + 3 \hat { j } - 6 \hat { k } ) + ( 4 \hat { i } - 8 \hat { j } + 10 \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= { 7 } \hat { i} - 5 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} Hence, direction ratio of vectors {tex}3 \vec { a } + 2 \vec { b }{/tex} are 7, - 5 and 4.
solution_html:
Here, we are given that {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k }{/tex}

{tex}\vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } - 4 \hat { j } + 5 \hat { k }.{/tex}Therefore, we have

{tex}3 \vec { a } + 2 \vec { b } = 3 ( \hat { i } + \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } ) + 2 ( 2 \hat { i } - 4 \hat { j } + 5 \hat { k } ){/tex}
{tex}= ( 3 \hat{i} + 3 \hat { j } - 6 \hat { k } ) + ( 4 \hat { i } - 8 \hat { j } + 10 \hat { k } ){/tex}
{tex}= { 7 } \hat { i} - 5 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex}
Hence, direction ratio of vectors {tex}3 \vec { a } + 2 \vec { b }{/tex} are 7, - 5 and 4.

question_html: Find the unit vector in the direction of the sum of the vectors {tex}2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } - \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}4 \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k }.{/tex}
question_text: Find the unit vector in the direction of the sum of the vectors {tex}2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } - \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}4 \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k }.{/tex}
explanation: Here,we put the given vectors equal to {tex}\vec { a } = 2 \hat { i} + 3 \hat { j} - k \text { and } \vec { b } = 4 \hat { i} - 3 \hat { j} + 2 \hat { k }{/tex} Now, sum of two vectors, {tex}\vec { a } + \vec { b } = ( 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } - \hat { k } ) + ( 4 \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } ) = 6 \hat { i } + \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required unit vector = {tex}\frac { \vec { a } + \vec { b } } { | \vec { a } + \vec { b } | }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 6 \hat { i} + \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 6 ^ { 2 } + 1 ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { 6\hat { i} + \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 36 + 1 } } = \frac { 6 \hat { i } + \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 37 } } = \frac { 6 } { \sqrt { 37 } } \hat { i } + \frac { \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 37 } }{/tex}
solution: Here,we put the given vectors equal to {tex}\vec { a } = 2 \hat { i} + 3 \hat { j} - k \text { and } \vec { b } = 4 \hat { i} - 3 \hat { j} + 2 \hat { k }{/tex} Now, sum of two vectors, {tex}\vec { a } + \vec { b } = ( 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } - \hat { k } ) + ( 4 \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } ) = 6 \hat { i } + \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required unit vector = {tex}\frac { \vec { a } + \vec { b } } { | \vec { a } + \vec { b } | }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 6 \hat { i} + \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 6 ^ { 2 } + 1 ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { 6\hat { i} + \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 36 + 1 } } = \frac { 6 \hat { i } + \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 37 } } = \frac { 6 } { \sqrt { 37 } } \hat { i } + \frac { \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 37 } }{/tex}
solution_html:
Here,we put the given vectors equal to {tex}\vec { a } = 2 \hat { i} + 3 \hat { j} - k \text { and } \vec { b } = 4 \hat { i} - 3 \hat { j} + 2 \hat { k }{/tex}
Now, sum of two vectors,
{tex}\vec { a } + \vec { b } = ( 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } - \hat { k } ) + ( 4 \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } ) = 6 \hat { i } + \hat { k }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required unit vector = {tex}\frac { \vec { a } + \vec { b } } { | \vec { a } + \vec { b } | }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 6 \hat { i} + \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 6 ^ { 2 } + 1 ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { 6\hat { i} + \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 36 + 1 } } = \frac { 6 \hat { i } + \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 37 } } = \frac { 6 } { \sqrt { 37 } } \hat { i } + \frac { \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 37 } }{/tex}

question_html: If {tex} \vec { a } = 7 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} then find the projection of {tex} \vec { a } {/tex} on {tex}\vec { b }{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex} \vec { a } = 7 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} then find the projection of {tex} \vec { a } {/tex} on {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} .
explanation: Here,we are given that {tex} \vec { a } = 7 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} Now, the projection of {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} on {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { \vec { a } .\vec { b } } { | \vec { b } | }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { ( 7 \hat { i }+ \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k } ). ( 2 \hat { i} + 6 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ) } { \sqrt { 2 ^ { 2 } + 6 ^ { 2 } + 3 ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { 14 + 6 - 12 } { \sqrt { 49 } } = \frac { 8 } { 7 }{/tex}
solution: Here,we are given that {tex} \vec { a } = 7 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} Now, the projection of {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} on {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { \vec { a } .\vec { b } } { | \vec { b } | }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { ( 7 \hat { i }+ \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k } ). ( 2 \hat { i} + 6 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ) } { \sqrt { 2 ^ { 2 } + 6 ^ { 2 } + 3 ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { 14 + 6 - 12 } { \sqrt { 49 } } = \frac { 8 } { 7 }{/tex}
solution_html:
Here,we are given that  {tex} \vec { a } = 7 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k }{/tex}
and {tex} \vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex}
Now, the projection of{tex}\vec { a }{/tex} on {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { \vec { a } .\vec { b } } { | \vec { b } | }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { ( 7 \hat { i }+ \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k } ). ( 2 \hat { i} + 6 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ) } { \sqrt { 2 ^ { 2 } + 6 ^ { 2 } + 3 ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { 14 + 6 - 12 } { \sqrt { 49 } } = \frac { 8 } { 7 }{/tex}

question_html: If {tex} \hat { a } , \hat { b }{/tex} and {tex} \hat { c }{/tex} are mutually perpendicular unit vectors, then find the value of {tex} | 2 \hat { a } + \hat { b } + \hat { c } |.{/tex}
question_text: If {tex} \hat { a } , \hat { b }{/tex} and {tex} \hat { c }{/tex} are mutually perpendicular unit vectors, then find the value of {tex} | 2 \hat { a } + \hat { b } + \hat { c } |.{/tex}
explanation: We have to find the value of {tex} | 2 \hat { a } + \hat { b } + \hat { c } |.{/tex} Given, {tex}\hat { a } , \hat { b }{/tex} and {tex}\hat { \mathcal { c } }{/tex} are mutually perpendicular unit vectors, which implies that their dot product is zero.Thus, {tex}\hat { a }. \hat { b } = \hat { b } .\hat { c } = \hat { c } .\hat { a } = 0{/tex} ...(i) and {tex}| \hat { a } | = | \hat { b } | = | \hat { c } | = 1{/tex} ...(ii) Now, {tex}| 2 \hat { a } + \hat { b } + \hat { c } | ^{ 2 } = ( 2 \hat { a } + \hat { b } + \hat { c } ) .( 2 \hat { a } + \hat { b } + \hat { c } ){/tex} {tex}\left[\therefore {|\vec a{|^2} = \vec a\;\vec a} \right]{/tex} {tex}= 4 ( \hat { a }. \hat { a } ) + 2 (\hat { a }. \hat { b } ) + 2 ( \hat { a } .\hat { c } ) + 2 (\hat { b }. \hat { a } ){/tex} {tex}+ ( \hat { b }. \hat { b } ) + ( \hat { b } .\hat { c } ) + 2 (\hat { c }. \hat { a } ) + ( \hat { c } \cdot \hat { b } ) + ( \hat { c }. \hat { c } ){/tex} [ {tex}\therefore{/tex} dot product is distributive over addition] {tex} = 4\left( {|\hat a{|^2}} \right) + 2(0) + 2(0) + 2(0) + |\hat b{|^2} + (0){/tex} {tex} + 2(0) + (0) + |\hat c{|^2}{/tex} [from Eq (i) and {tex} \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = \vec { b } \vec { a } ]{/tex} = 4(1) + 1 + 1 = 4 + 1 + 1 = 6 {tex}\therefore \quad | 2 \hat { a } + \hat { b } + \hat { c } | = \sqrt { 6 }{/tex} [ {tex} \because {/tex} length cannot be negative]
solution: We have to find the value of {tex} | 2 \hat { a } + \hat { b } + \hat { c } |.{/tex} Given, {tex}\hat { a } , \hat { b }{/tex} and {tex}\hat { \mathcal { c } }{/tex} are mutually perpendicular unit vectors, which implies that their dot product is zero.Thus, {tex}\hat { a }. \hat { b } = \hat { b } .\hat { c } = \hat { c } .\hat { a } = 0{/tex} ...(i) and {tex}| \hat { a } | = | \hat { b } | = | \hat { c } | = 1{/tex} ...(ii) Now, {tex}| 2 \hat { a } + \hat { b } + \hat { c } | ^{ 2 } = ( 2 \hat { a } + \hat { b } + \hat { c } ) .( 2 \hat { a } + \hat { b } + \hat { c } ){/tex} {tex}\left[\therefore {|\vec a{|^2} = \vec a\;\vec a} \right]{/tex} {tex}= 4 ( \hat { a }. \hat { a } ) + 2 (\hat { a }. \hat { b } ) + 2 ( \hat { a } .\hat { c } ) + 2 (\hat { b }. \hat { a } ){/tex} {tex}+ ( \hat { b }. \hat { b } ) + ( \hat { b } .\hat { c } ) + 2 (\hat { c }. \hat { a } ) + ( \hat { c } \cdot \hat { b } ) + ( \hat { c }. \hat { c } ){/tex} [ {tex}\therefore{/tex} dot product is distributive over addition] {tex} = 4\left( {|\hat a{|^2}} \right) + 2(0) + 2(0) + 2(0) + |\hat b{|^2} + (0){/tex} {tex} + 2(0) + (0) + |\hat c{|^2}{/tex} [from Eq (i) and {tex} \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = \vec { b } \vec { a } ]{/tex} = 4(1) + 1 + 1 = 4 + 1 + 1 = 6 {tex}\therefore \quad | 2 \hat { a } + \hat { b } + \hat { c } | = \sqrt { 6 }{/tex} [ {tex} \because {/tex} length cannot be negative]
solution_html:
We have to find the value of {tex} | 2 \hat { a } + \hat { b } + \hat { c } |.{/tex}
Given, {tex}\hat { a } , \hat { b }{/tex} and {tex}\hat { \mathcal { c } }{/tex} are mutually perpendicular unit vectors, which implies that their dot product is zero.Thus,
{tex}\hat { a }. \hat { b } = \hat { b } .\hat { c } = \hat { c } .\hat { a } = 0{/tex} ...(i)
and {tex}| \hat { a } | = | \hat { b } | = | \hat { c } | = 1{/tex} ...(ii)
Now, {tex}| 2 \hat { a } + \hat { b } + \hat { c } | ^{ 2 } = ( 2 \hat { a } + \hat { b } + \hat { c } ) .( 2 \hat { a } + \hat { b } + \hat { c } ){/tex}
{tex}\left[\therefore {|\vec a{|^2} = \vec a\;\vec a} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= 4 ( \hat { a }. \hat { a } ) + 2 (\hat { a }. \hat { b } ) + 2 ( \hat { a } .\hat { c } ) + 2 (\hat { b }. \hat { a } ){/tex}
{tex}+ ( \hat { b }. \hat { b } ) + ( \hat { b } .\hat { c } ) + 2 (\hat { c }. \hat { a } ) + ( \hat { c } \cdot \hat { b } ) + ( \hat { c }. \hat { c } ){/tex}
[{tex}\therefore{/tex} dot product is distributive over addition]
{tex} = 4\left( {|\hat a{|^2}} \right) + 2(0) + 2(0) + 2(0) + |\hat b{|^2} + (0){/tex}{tex} + 2(0) + (0) + |\hat c{|^2}{/tex}
[from Eq (i) and {tex} \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = \vec { b } \vec { a } ]{/tex}
= 4(1) + 1 + 1
= 4 + 1 + 1 = 6
{tex}\therefore \quad | 2 \hat { a } + \hat { b } + \hat { c } | = \sqrt { 6 }{/tex}
[{tex} \because {/tex} length cannot be negative]

question_html: Write a unit vector perpendicular to both the vectors {tex} \vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b } = \hat { i } + \hat { j }.{/tex}
question_text: Write a unit vector perpendicular to both the vectors {tex} \vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b } = \hat { i } + \hat { j }.{/tex}
explanation: We have to find a unit vector perpendicular to both the vectors {tex} \vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b } = \hat { i } + \hat { j }.{/tex} Now,the given vectors are {tex} \vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b } = \hat { i } + \hat { j }{/tex} As we know that, the vector {tex} \vec { a } \times \vec { b }{/tex} is perpendicular to both the vectors, so let us first evaluate {tex} \vec { a } \times \vec { b }.{/tex} Then, {tex} \vec { a } \times \vec { b } = \left| \begin{array} { l l l } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { 1 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex} = \hat { i } ( 0 - 1 ) - \hat { j } ( 0 - 1 ) + \hat { k } ( 1 - 1 ){/tex} {tex} = - \hat { i } + \hat { j }{/tex} Then, the unit vector perpendicular to both {tex} \vec { a }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b }{/tex} is given by {tex} \frac { \vec { a } \times \vec { b } } { | \vec { a } \times \vec { b } | } = \frac { - \hat { i } + \hat { j } } { \sqrt { ( - 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { - \hat { i } + \hat { j } } { \sqrt { 2 } } = \frac { - \hat { i } } { \sqrt { 2 } } + \frac { \hat { j } } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} .
solution: We have to find a unit vector perpendicular to both the vectors {tex} \vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b } = \hat { i } + \hat { j }.{/tex} Now,the given vectors are {tex} \vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b } = \hat { i } + \hat { j }{/tex} As we know that, the vector {tex} \vec { a } \times \vec { b }{/tex} is perpendicular to both the vectors, so let us first evaluate {tex} \vec { a } \times \vec { b }.{/tex} Then, {tex} \vec { a } \times \vec { b } = \left| \begin{array} { l l l } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { 1 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex} = \hat { i } ( 0 - 1 ) - \hat { j } ( 0 - 1 ) + \hat { k } ( 1 - 1 ){/tex} {tex} = - \hat { i } + \hat { j }{/tex} Then, the unit vector perpendicular to both {tex} \vec { a }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b }{/tex} is given by {tex} \frac { \vec { a } \times \vec { b } } { | \vec { a } \times \vec { b } | } = \frac { - \hat { i } + \hat { j } } { \sqrt { ( - 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { - \hat { i } + \hat { j } } { \sqrt { 2 } } = \frac { - \hat { i } } { \sqrt { 2 } } + \frac { \hat { j } } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} .
solution_html:
We have to find a unit vector perpendicular to both the vectors {tex} \vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b } = \hat { i } + \hat { j }.{/tex}

Now,the given vectors are {tex} \vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and  {tex} \vec { b } = \hat { i } + \hat { j }{/tex}
As we know that, the vector {tex} \vec { a } \times \vec { b }{/tex} is
perpendicular to both the vectors, so let us first evaluate {tex} \vec { a } \times \vec { b }.{/tex}
Then, {tex} \vec { a } \times \vec { b } = \left| \begin{array} { l l l } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { 1 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
{tex} = \hat { i } ( 0 - 1 ) - \hat { j } ( 0 - 1 ) + \hat { k } ( 1 - 1 ){/tex}
{tex} = - \hat { i } + \hat { j }{/tex}
Then, the unit vector perpendicular to both {tex} \vec { a }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b }{/tex} is given by
{tex} \frac { \vec { a } \times \vec { b } } { | \vec { a } \times \vec { b } | } = \frac { - \hat { i } + \hat { j } } { \sqrt { ( - 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { - \hat { i } + \hat { j } } { \sqrt { 2 } } = \frac { - \hat { i } } { \sqrt { 2 } } + \frac { \hat { j } } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex}.

question_html: Find {tex} \lambda,{/tex}if the vectors {tex} \vec { a } = \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + \hat { k },{/tex}{tex} \vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } - \hat { k }{/tex}and {tex} \vec { c } = \lambda \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex}are coplanar.
question_text: Find {tex} \lambda,{/tex} if the vectors {tex} \vec { a } = \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + \hat { k },{/tex} {tex} \vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } - \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { c } = \lambda \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} are coplanar.
explanation: We have to find {tex} \lambda,{/tex} where we are given the following vectors : {tex} \vec { a } = \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j} + \hat { k },{/tex} {tex} \vec { b } = 2 \hat { i} - \hat { j } - \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { c } = \lambda \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }.{/tex} Since, the vectors {tex} \vec { a } , \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { c }{/tex} are coplanar, Therefore, we have {tex} [\vec { a } \vec { b } \vec { c } ] = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { 3 } & { 1 } \\ { 2 } & { - 1 } & { - 1 } \\ { 0 } & { \lambda } & { 3 } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 1 ( - 3 + \lambda ) - 3 ( 6 ) + 1 ( 2 \lambda ) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad - 3 + \lambda - 18 + 2 \lambda = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 \lambda = 21{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad \lambda = 7{/tex}
solution: We have to find {tex} \lambda,{/tex} where we are given the following vectors : {tex} \vec { a } = \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j} + \hat { k },{/tex} {tex} \vec { b } = 2 \hat { i} - \hat { j } - \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { c } = \lambda \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }.{/tex} Since, the vectors {tex} \vec { a } , \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { c }{/tex} are coplanar, Therefore, we have {tex} [\vec { a } \vec { b } \vec { c } ] = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { 3 } & { 1 } \\ { 2 } & { - 1 } & { - 1 } \\ { 0 } & { \lambda } & { 3 } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 1 ( - 3 + \lambda ) - 3 ( 6 ) + 1 ( 2 \lambda ) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad - 3 + \lambda - 18 + 2 \lambda = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 \lambda = 21{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad \lambda = 7{/tex}
solution_html:
We have to find {tex} \lambda,{/tex} where we are given the following vectors  :{tex} \vec { a } = \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j} + \hat { k },{/tex}
{tex} \vec { b } = 2 \hat { i} - \hat { j } - \hat { k }{/tex}and {tex} \vec { c } = \lambda \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }.{/tex}
Since, the vectors {tex} \vec { a } , \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { c }{/tex} are coplanar,
Therefore, we have {tex} [\vec { a } \vec { b } \vec { c } ] = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { 3 } & { 1 } \\ { 2 } & { - 1 } & { - 1 } \\ { 0 } & { \lambda } & { 3 } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad 1 ( - 3 + \lambda ) - 3 ( 6 ) + 1 ( 2 \lambda ) = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad - 3 + \lambda - 18 + 2 \lambda = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 \lambda = 21{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad \lambda = 7{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}\vec { r } = x \hat { i } + y \hat { j } + z \hat { k },{/tex} find {tex}( \vec { r } \times \hat { i } ) \cdot ( \vec { r } \times \hat { j } ) + x y.{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}\vec { r } = x \hat { i } + y \hat { j } + z \hat { k },{/tex} find {tex}( \vec { r } \times \hat { i } ) \cdot ( \vec { r } \times \hat { j } ) + x y.{/tex}
explanation: According to the question , {tex}\vec { r } = x \hat { i} + y\hat { j } + z \hat { k }{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec { r } \times \hat { i} = ( x \hat { i } + y\hat { j} + z \hat { k } ) \times \hat { i}{/tex} {tex}= x ( \hat { i } \times \hat { i} ) + y ( \hat { j } \times \hat { i } ) + z ( \hat { k } \times \hat {i } ){/tex} {tex}= x ( 0 )+ y ( - \hat { k } ) + z ( \hat { j } ) {/tex} {tex}= - y \hat { k } + z \hat { j }{/tex} Now, {tex}(\vec { r } \times \hat { j} ) = ( x \hat { i } + y \hat { j} + z \hat { k } ) \times \hat {j }{/tex} {tex}= x ( \hat { i } \times \hat {j } ) + y ( \hat { j } \times \hat { j } ) + z ( \hat { k } \times \hat { j } ){/tex} {tex}= x \hat { k } + y (0 )+ z ( - \hat { i } ){/tex} {tex}= x \hat { k } - z \hat { i}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad ( \vec { r } \times \hat {i} ) \cdot ( \vec { r } \times \hat { j } ) = ( - y \hat { k } + z \hat { j } ) \cdot ( x \hat { k } - z \hat { i } ){/tex} {tex}= - y x + 0+0+0= -xy{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad ( \vec { r } \times \hat { i } ) \cdot ( \vec { r } \times \hat { j } ) + x y = 0{/tex}
solution: According to the question , {tex}\vec { r } = x \hat { i} + y\hat { j } + z \hat { k }{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec { r } \times \hat { i} = ( x \hat { i } + y\hat { j} + z \hat { k } ) \times \hat { i}{/tex} {tex}= x ( \hat { i } \times \hat { i} ) + y ( \hat { j } \times \hat { i } ) + z ( \hat { k } \times \hat {i } ){/tex} {tex}= x ( 0 )+ y ( - \hat { k } ) + z ( \hat { j } ) {/tex} {tex}= - y \hat { k } + z \hat { j }{/tex} Now, {tex}(\vec { r } \times \hat { j} ) = ( x \hat { i } + y \hat { j} + z \hat { k } ) \times \hat {j }{/tex} {tex}= x ( \hat { i } \times \hat {j } ) + y ( \hat { j } \times \hat { j } ) + z ( \hat { k } \times \hat { j } ){/tex} {tex}= x \hat { k } + y (0 )+ z ( - \hat { i } ){/tex} {tex}= x \hat { k } - z \hat { i}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad ( \vec { r } \times \hat {i} ) \cdot ( \vec { r } \times \hat { j } ) = ( - y \hat { k } + z \hat { j } ) \cdot ( x \hat { k } - z \hat { i } ){/tex} {tex}= - y x + 0+0+0= -xy{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad ( \vec { r } \times \hat { i } ) \cdot ( \vec { r } \times \hat { j } ) + x y = 0{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question ,

{tex}\vec { r } = x \hat { i} + y\hat { j } + z \hat { k }{/tex}
Now, {tex}\vec { r } \times \hat { i} = ( x \hat { i } + y\hat { j} + z \hat { k } ) \times \hat { i}{/tex}
{tex}= x ( \hat { i } \times \hat { i} ) + y ( \hat { j } \times \hat { i } ) + z ( \hat { k } \times \hat {i } ){/tex}
{tex}= x ( 0 )+ y ( - \hat { k } ) + z ( \hat { j } ) {/tex}
{tex}= - y \hat { k } + z \hat { j }{/tex}

Now,{tex}(\vec { r } \times \hat { j} ) = ( x \hat { i } + y \hat { j} + z \hat { k } ) \times \hat {j }{/tex}
{tex}= x ( \hat { i } \times \hat {j } ) + y ( \hat { j } \times \hat { j } ) + z ( \hat { k } \times \hat { j } ){/tex}
{tex}= x \hat { k } + y (0 )+ z ( - \hat { i } ){/tex}
{tex}= x \hat { k } - z \hat { i}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \quad ( \vec { r } \times \hat {i} ) \cdot ( \vec { r } \times \hat { j } ) = ( - y \hat { k } + z \hat { j } ) \cdot ( x \hat { k } - z \hat { i } ){/tex}
{tex}= - y x + 0+0+0= -xy{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad ( \vec { r } \times \hat { i } ) \cdot ( \vec { r } \times \hat { j } ) + x y = 0{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k } , \vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c } = 3 \hat { i } - 4 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k },{/tex} then find a unit vector perpendicular to both of the vectors {tex}( \vec { a } - \vec { b } ){/tex}and {tex}( \vec { c } - \vec { b } ).{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k } , \vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c } = 3 \hat { i } - 4 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k },{/tex} then find a unit vector perpendicular to both of the vectors {tex}( \vec { a } - \vec { b } ){/tex} and {tex}( \vec { c } - \vec { b } ).{/tex}
explanation: According to the question, {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i} + 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k } ,{/tex} {tex} \vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c } = 3 \hat { i } - 4 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k }{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { b } = ( i + 2 \hat { j} + \hat { k } ) - ( 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } ){/tex} {tex}= - \hat {i} + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec { c } - \vec { b } = ( 3 \hat {i } - 4 \hat { j} - 5 \hat { k } ) - (2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } ){/tex} {tex}= \hat { i } - 5 \hat { j} - 5 \hat { k }{/tex} Now, a vector perpendicular to {tex}(\vec { a } - \vec { b } ){/tex} and {tex}( \vec { c } - \vec { b } ){/tex} is given by {tex}\vec { ( a } - \vec { b } ) \times ( \vec { c } - \vec { b } ) = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { - 1 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { - 5 } & { - 5 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \hat { i } ( - 5 + 5 ) - \hat { j } ( 5 - 1 ) + \hat { k } ( 5 - 1 ){/tex} {tex}= \hat { i } ( 0 ) - \hat { j } ( 4 ) + \hat { k } ( 4 ) {/tex} {tex}= - 4 \hat {j} + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} Unit vector along {tex}( \vec { a } - \vec { b } ) \times ( \vec { c } - \vec { b } ){/tex} is given by {tex}\frac { - 4 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } } { | - 4 \hat {j } + 4 \hat { k } | }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { - 4 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { ( - 4 ) ^ { 2 } + 4 ^ { 2 } } } {/tex} {tex}= \frac { - 4 \hat {j } + 4 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 32 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { - 4 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } } { 4 \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = - \frac { \hat { j } } { \sqrt { 2 } } + \frac { \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex}
solution: According to the question, {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i} + 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k } ,{/tex} {tex} \vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c } = 3 \hat { i } - 4 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k }{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { b } = ( i + 2 \hat { j} + \hat { k } ) - ( 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } ){/tex} {tex}= - \hat {i} + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec { c } - \vec { b } = ( 3 \hat {i } - 4 \hat { j} - 5 \hat { k } ) - (2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } ){/tex} {tex}= \hat { i } - 5 \hat { j} - 5 \hat { k }{/tex} Now, a vector perpendicular to {tex}(\vec { a } - \vec { b } ){/tex} and {tex}( \vec { c } - \vec { b } ){/tex} is given by {tex}\vec { ( a } - \vec { b } ) \times ( \vec { c } - \vec { b } ) = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { - 1 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { - 5 } & { - 5 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \hat { i } ( - 5 + 5 ) - \hat { j } ( 5 - 1 ) + \hat { k } ( 5 - 1 ){/tex} {tex}= \hat { i } ( 0 ) - \hat { j } ( 4 ) + \hat { k } ( 4 ) {/tex} {tex}= - 4 \hat {j} + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} Unit vector along {tex}( \vec { a } - \vec { b } ) \times ( \vec { c } - \vec { b } ){/tex} is given by {tex}\frac { - 4 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } } { | - 4 \hat {j } + 4 \hat { k } | }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { - 4 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { ( - 4 ) ^ { 2 } + 4 ^ { 2 } } } {/tex} {tex}= \frac { - 4 \hat {j } + 4 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 32 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { - 4 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } } { 4 \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = - \frac { \hat { j } } { \sqrt { 2 } } + \frac { \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question,

 {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i} + 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k } ,{/tex}
{tex} \vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j }{/tex} and 

{tex}\vec { c } = 3 \hat { i } - 4 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k }{/tex}
Now, {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { b } = ( i + 2 \hat { j} + \hat { k } ) - ( 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } ){/tex}{tex}= - \hat {i} + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex}
Now,  {tex}\vec { c } - \vec { b } = ( 3 \hat {i } - 4 \hat { j} - 5 \hat { k } ) - (2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } ){/tex}{tex}= \hat { i } - 5 \hat { j} - 5 \hat { k }{/tex}
Now, a vector perpendicular to {tex}(\vec { a } - \vec { b } ){/tex}and {tex}( \vec { c } - \vec { b } ){/tex} is given by
{tex}\vec { ( a } - \vec { b } ) \times ( \vec { c } - \vec { b } ) = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { - 1 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { - 5 } & { - 5 } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
{tex}= \hat { i } ( - 5 + 5 ) - \hat { j } ( 5 - 1 ) + \hat { k } ( 5 - 1 ){/tex}
{tex}= \hat { i } ( 0 ) - \hat { j } ( 4 ) + \hat { k } ( 4 ) {/tex}

{tex}= - 4 \hat {j} + 4 \hat { k }{/tex}
Unit vector along {tex}( \vec { a } - \vec { b } ) \times ( \vec { c } - \vec { b } ){/tex}is given by
{tex}\frac { - 4 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } } { | - 4 \hat {j } + 4 \hat { k } | }{/tex}

{tex} = \frac { - 4 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { ( - 4 ) ^ { 2 } + 4 ^ { 2 } } } {/tex}

{tex}= \frac { - 4 \hat {j } + 4 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 32 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { - 4 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } } { 4 \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex}

{tex} = - \frac { \hat { j } } { \sqrt { 2 } } + \frac { \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex}

question_html: Find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex}so that the four points A, B, C and D with position vectors {tex}4 \hat { i } + 5 \hat { j } + \hat { k }, - \hat { j } - \hat { k } , 3 \hat { i } + \lambda \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex}and {tex}- 4 \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k },{/tex}respectively are coplanar.
question_text: Find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} so that the four points A, B, C and D with position vectors {tex}4 \hat { i } + 5 \hat { j } + \hat { k }, - \hat { j } - \hat { k } , 3 \hat { i } + \lambda \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}- 4 \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k },{/tex} respectively are coplanar.
explanation: According to the question, Given, {tex}\vec { O A } = 4 \hat { i } + 5 \hat { j } + \hat { k } , {/tex} {tex}\vec { O B } = - \hat { j } - \hat { k } ,{/tex} {tex}\vec { O C } = 3 \hat { i } + \lambda \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } \text { and }{/tex} {tex} \vec { O D } = - 4 \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }.{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec { A B } = \vec { O B } - \vec { O A } = - \hat { j } - \hat { k } - ( 4 \hat {i } + 5 \hat { j} + \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= - 4 \hat { i } - 6 \hat { j} - 2 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\vec { A C } = \vec { O C } - \vec { O A } = 3 \hat { i} + \lambda \hat {j } + 4 \hat { k } - ( 4 \hat { i} + 5 \hat {j} + \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= - \hat { i } + ( \lambda - 5 )\hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { A D } = \vec { O D } - \vec { O A }{/tex} {tex}= - 4 \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j} + 4 \hat { k } - ( 4 \hat { i } + 5 \hat { j } + \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= - 8 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} Since, vectors {tex}\vec { O A } , \vec { O B } , \vec { O C }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { O D }{/tex} are coplanar. {tex}\therefore \quad [ \vec { A B }\ \vec { A C }\ \vec { A D } ] = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { - 4 } & { - 6 } & { - 2 } \\ { - 1 } & { ( \lambda - 5 ) } & { 3 } \\ { - 8 } & { - 1 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex} {tex}- 4 ( 3 \lambda - 15 + 3 ) + 6 ( - 3 + 24 ) - 2 ( 1 + 8 \lambda - 40 ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - 4 ( 3 \lambda - 12 ) + 6 ( 21 ) - 2 ( 8 \lambda - 39 ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - 12 \lambda + 48 + 126 - 16 \lambda + 78 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - 28 \lambda + 252 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda = 9{/tex}
solution: According to the question, Given, {tex}\vec { O A } = 4 \hat { i } + 5 \hat { j } + \hat { k } , {/tex} {tex}\vec { O B } = - \hat { j } - \hat { k } ,{/tex} {tex}\vec { O C } = 3 \hat { i } + \lambda \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } \text { and }{/tex} {tex} \vec { O D } = - 4 \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }.{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec { A B } = \vec { O B } - \vec { O A } = - \hat { j } - \hat { k } - ( 4 \hat {i } + 5 \hat { j} + \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= - 4 \hat { i } - 6 \hat { j} - 2 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\vec { A C } = \vec { O C } - \vec { O A } = 3 \hat { i} + \lambda \hat {j } + 4 \hat { k } - ( 4 \hat { i} + 5 \hat {j} + \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= - \hat { i } + ( \lambda - 5 )\hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { A D } = \vec { O D } - \vec { O A }{/tex} {tex}= - 4 \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j} + 4 \hat { k } - ( 4 \hat { i } + 5 \hat { j } + \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= - 8 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} Since, vectors {tex}\vec { O A } , \vec { O B } , \vec { O C }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { O D }{/tex} are coplanar. {tex}\therefore \quad [ \vec { A B }\ \vec { A C }\ \vec { A D } ] = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { - 4 } & { - 6 } & { - 2 } \\ { - 1 } & { ( \lambda - 5 ) } & { 3 } \\ { - 8 } & { - 1 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex} {tex}- 4 ( 3 \lambda - 15 + 3 ) + 6 ( - 3 + 24 ) - 2 ( 1 + 8 \lambda - 40 ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - 4 ( 3 \lambda - 12 ) + 6 ( 21 ) - 2 ( 8 \lambda - 39 ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - 12 \lambda + 48 + 126 - 16 \lambda + 78 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - 28 \lambda + 252 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda = 9{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question,

Given, {tex}\vec { O A } = 4 \hat { i } + 5 \hat { j } + \hat { k } , {/tex}

{tex}\vec { O B } = - \hat { j } - \hat { k } ,{/tex} 
{tex}\vec { O C } = 3 \hat { i } + \lambda \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } \text { and }{/tex}

{tex} \vec { O D } = - 4 \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }.{/tex}
Now, {tex}\vec { A B } = \vec { O B } - \vec { O A } = - \hat { j } - \hat { k } - ( 4 \hat {i } + 5 \hat { j} + \hat { k } ){/tex}
{tex}= - 4 \hat { i } - 6 \hat { j} - 2 \hat { k }{/tex}
{tex}\vec { A C } = \vec { O C } - \vec { O A } = 3 \hat { i} + \lambda \hat {j } + 4 \hat { k } - ( 4 \hat { i} + 5 \hat {j} + \hat { k } ){/tex}
{tex}= - \hat { i } + ( \lambda - 5 )\hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex}
and {tex}\vec { A D } = \vec { O D } - \vec { O A }{/tex}
{tex}= - 4 \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j} + 4 \hat { k } - ( 4 \hat { i } + 5 \hat { j } + \hat { k } ){/tex}
{tex}= - 8 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex}
Since, vectors {tex}\vec { O A } , \vec { O B } , \vec { O C }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { O D }{/tex} are coplanar.
{tex}\therefore \quad [ \vec { A B }\ \vec { A C }\ \vec { A D } ] = 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { - 4 } & { - 6 } & { - 2 } \\ { - 1 } & { ( \lambda - 5 ) } & { 3 } \\ { - 8 } & { - 1 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex}
{tex}- 4 ( 3 \lambda - 15 + 3 ) + 6 ( - 3 + 24 ) - 2 ( 1 + 8 \lambda - 40 ) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad - 4 ( 3 \lambda - 12 ) + 6 ( 21 ) - 2 ( 8 \lambda - 39 ) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad - 12 \lambda + 48 + 126 - 16 \lambda + 78 = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad - 28 \lambda + 252 = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda = 9{/tex}

question_html: Prove that {tex}[\vec { a }.\ \vec { b } + \vec { c }.\ \vec { d }\ ]{/tex} = {tex} \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { \vec { a } } & { \vec { b } } & { \vec { d } } \end{array} \right] + \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { \vec { a } } & { \vec { c } } & { \vec { d } } \end{array} \right].{/tex}
question_text: Prove that {tex}[\vec { a }.\ \vec { b } + \vec { c }.\ \vec { d }\ ]{/tex} = {tex} \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { \vec { a } } & { \vec { b } } & { \vec { d } } \end{array} \right] + \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { \vec { a } } & { \vec { c } } & { \vec { d } } \end{array} \right].{/tex}
explanation: According to the question, To prove {tex}[\vec { a }.\ \vec { b } + \vec { c }.\ \vec { d }\ ]{/tex} = {tex} \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { \vec { a } } & { \vec { b } } & { \vec { d } } \end{array} \right] + \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { \vec { a } } & { \vec { c } } & { \vec { d } } \end{array} \right].{/tex} LHS = {tex}[ \vec { a }. \vec { b } + \vec { c }. \vec { d } ] = \vec { a } .\{ ( \vec { b } + \vec { c } ) \times \vec { d } \}{/tex} by definition of scalar triple product , we get {tex}= \vec { a } .( \vec { b } \times \vec { d } + \vec { c } \times \vec { d } ){/tex} {tex}= \vec { a }. ( \vec { b } \times \vec { d } ) + \vec { a }. ( \vec { c } \times \vec { d } ) = \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { \vec { a } } & { \vec { b } } & { \vec { d } } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}+ \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { \vec { a } } & { \vec { c } } & { \vec { d } } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
solution: According to the question, To prove {tex}[\vec { a }.\ \vec { b } + \vec { c }.\ \vec { d }\ ]{/tex} = {tex} \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { \vec { a } } & { \vec { b } } & { \vec { d } } \end{array} \right] + \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { \vec { a } } & { \vec { c } } & { \vec { d } } \end{array} \right].{/tex} LHS = {tex}[ \vec { a }. \vec { b } + \vec { c }. \vec { d } ] = \vec { a } .\{ ( \vec { b } + \vec { c } ) \times \vec { d } \}{/tex} by definition of scalar triple product , we get {tex}= \vec { a } .( \vec { b } \times \vec { d } + \vec { c } \times \vec { d } ){/tex} {tex}= \vec { a }. ( \vec { b } \times \vec { d } ) + \vec { a }. ( \vec { c } \times \vec { d } ) = \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { \vec { a } } & { \vec { b } } & { \vec { d } } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}+ \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { \vec { a } } & { \vec { c } } & { \vec { d } } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question,

To prove  {tex}[\vec { a }.\ \vec { b } + \vec { c }.\ \vec { d }\ ]{/tex} = {tex} \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { \vec { a } } & { \vec { b } } & { \vec { d } } \end{array} \right] + \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { \vec { a } } & { \vec { c } } & { \vec { d } } \end{array} \right].{/tex}
LHS = {tex}[ \vec { a }. \vec { b } + \vec { c }. \vec { d } ] = \vec { a } .\{ ( \vec { b } + \vec { c } ) \times \vec { d } \}{/tex}

by definition of scalar triple product , we get
{tex}= \vec { a } .( \vec { b } \times \vec { d } + \vec { c } \times \vec { d } ){/tex}
{tex}= \vec { a }. ( \vec { b } \times \vec { d } ) + \vec { a }. ( \vec { c } \times \vec { d } ) = \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { \vec { a } } & { \vec { b } } & { \vec { d } } \end{array} \right]{/tex}{tex}+ \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { \vec { a } } & { \vec { c } } & { \vec { d } } \end{array} \right]{/tex}

question_html: Find the area of a parallelogram whose adjacent sides are represented by the vectors {tex} 2 \hat { i } - 3 \hat { k } {/tex} and {tex}4 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k }.{/tex}
question_text: Find the area of a parallelogram whose adjacent sides are represented by the vectors {tex} 2 \hat { i } - 3 \hat { k } {/tex} and {tex}4 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k }.{/tex}
explanation: We have to find the area of the parallelogram whose sides are given by the vectors: {tex} \vec { a } = \hat { 2 i} - 3 \hat { k } \text { and } \vec { b } = 4 \hat { j} + 2 \hat { k }.{/tex} {tex} \therefore{/tex} Area of parallelogram {tex} = | \vec { a } \times \vec { b } |{/tex} {tex}\vec a\times\vec b=\ \begin{vmatrix} \hat i & \hat j&\hat k\\ 2 & 0 & -3\\ 0 & 4 & 2\end{vmatrix}\ {/tex} {tex}= | \hat { i }(0+12) - \hat { j }(4-0 ) + \hat {k } (8-0 ) |{/tex} {tex}= 12\cdot \hat { i } -4\cdot\hat { j } +8\cdot \hat { k } {/tex} Magnitude of {tex}|\vec a\times\vec b|=\sqrt{(12)^2+(-4)^2+8^2}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{144+16+64}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{224}{/tex} = {tex}4\sqrt{14}{/tex} Area of parallelogram {tex} = | \vec { a } \times \vec { b } |{/tex} = {tex}4\sqrt{14}{/tex} sq. unit
solution: We have to find the area of the parallelogram whose sides are given by the vectors: {tex} \vec { a } = \hat { 2 i} - 3 \hat { k } \text { and } \vec { b } = 4 \hat { j} + 2 \hat { k }.{/tex} {tex} \therefore{/tex} Area of parallelogram {tex} = | \vec { a } \times \vec { b } |{/tex} {tex}\vec a\times\vec b=\ \begin{vmatrix} \hat i & \hat j&\hat k\\ 2 & 0 & -3\\ 0 & 4 & 2\end{vmatrix}\ {/tex} {tex}= | \hat { i }(0+12) - \hat { j }(4-0 ) + \hat {k } (8-0 ) |{/tex} {tex}= 12\cdot \hat { i } -4\cdot\hat { j } +8\cdot \hat { k } {/tex} Magnitude of {tex}|\vec a\times\vec b|=\sqrt{(12)^2+(-4)^2+8^2}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{144+16+64}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{224}{/tex} = {tex}4\sqrt{14}{/tex} Area of parallelogram {tex} = | \vec { a } \times \vec { b } |{/tex} = {tex}4\sqrt{14}{/tex} sq. unit
solution_html:
We have to find the area of the parallelogram whose sides are given by the vectors:
{tex} \vec { a } = \hat { 2 i} - 3 \hat { k } \text { and } \vec { b } = 4 \hat { j} + 2 \hat { k }.{/tex}
{tex} \therefore{/tex} Area of parallelogram {tex} = | \vec { a } \times \vec { b } |{/tex}

{tex}\vec a\times\vec b=\ \begin{vmatrix} \hat i & \hat j&\hat k\\ 2 & 0 & -3\\ 0 & 4 & 2\end{vmatrix}\ {/tex}
{tex}= | \hat { i }(0+12) - \hat { j }(4-0 ) + \hat {k } (8-0 ) |{/tex}
{tex}= 12\cdot \hat { i } -4\cdot\hat { j } +8\cdot \hat { k } {/tex}
Magnitude of {tex}|\vec a\times\vec b|=\sqrt{(12)^2+(-4)^2+8^2}{/tex} 
{tex}=\sqrt{144+16+64}{/tex} 

{tex}=\sqrt{224}{/tex} = {tex}4\sqrt{14}{/tex}

Area of parallelogram {tex} = | \vec { a } \times \vec { b } |{/tex} = {tex}4\sqrt{14}{/tex} sq. unit

question_html: Find a vector in the direction of vector {tex}\vec a = \hat i - 2\hat j{/tex} that has magnitude 7 units.
question_text: Find a vector in the direction of vector {tex}\vec a = \hat i - 2\hat j{/tex} that has magnitude 7 units.
explanation: {tex}\hat a = \frac{{\vec a}}{{\left| {\vec a} \right|}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\left( {\hat i - 2\hat j} \right)}}{{\sqrt 5 }}{/tex} Vector having magnitude equal to 7 and in the direction of {tex}\vec a{/tex} is {tex}7\hat a = 7\left( {\frac{{\hat i - 2\hat j}}{{\sqrt 5 }}} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{7}{{\sqrt 5 }}\left( {\hat i - 2\hat j} \right){/tex}
solution: {tex}\hat a = \frac{{\vec a}}{{\left| {\vec a} \right|}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\left( {\hat i - 2\hat j} \right)}}{{\sqrt 5 }}{/tex} Vector having magnitude equal to 7 and in the direction of {tex}\vec a{/tex} is {tex}7\hat a = 7\left( {\frac{{\hat i - 2\hat j}}{{\sqrt 5 }}} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{7}{{\sqrt 5 }}\left( {\hat i - 2\hat j} \right){/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\hat a = \frac{{\vec a}}{{\left| {\vec a} \right|}}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{\left( {\hat i - 2\hat j} \right)}}{{\sqrt 5 }}{/tex}

Vector having magnitude equal to 7 and in the direction of {tex}\vec a{/tex}is

{tex}7\hat a = 7\left( {\frac{{\hat i - 2\hat j}}{{\sqrt 5 }}} \right){/tex}

{tex}= \frac{7}{{\sqrt 5 }}\left( {\hat i - 2\hat j} \right){/tex}

question_html: Show that the four points with position vectors {tex}4 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}+12 \hat{k}, 2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}, 3 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}5 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}{/tex} are coplanar.
question_text: Show that the four points with position vectors {tex}4 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}+12 \hat{k}, 2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}, 3 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}5 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}{/tex} are coplanar.
explanation: Let {tex}\overrightarrow a = 4\hat i + 8\hat j + 12\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow b = 2\hat i + 4\hat j + 6\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow c = 3\hat i + 5\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow d = 5\hat i + 8\hat j + 5\hat k{/tex} Now, {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\vec a}&{\vec b}&{\vec c} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left| {\begin{array}{*{0}{c}} 4&8&12 \\ 2&4&6 \\ 3&5&4 \end{array}} \right|{/tex} = 4(16 - 30) -8(8 - 18) + 12(10 - 12) = 4(-14) - 8(-10) + 12(-2) = -56 + 80 - 24 = -80 + 80 = 0 So, {tex}\hat a,\hat b,\;\& \;\hat c{/tex} are Co-planar Also, {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} b&{\overrightarrow c }&{\overrightarrow d } \end{array}} \right] = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&4&6 \\ 3&5&4 \\ 5&8&5 \end{array}} \right|{/tex} = 2(25 - 32) -4(15 - 20) + 6(24 - 25) = 2(-7) - 4(-5) - 6 = -14 - 6 + 20 = 0 i.e., {tex}\overrightarrow b ,\overrightarrow c \;\& \;\overrightarrow d {/tex} are co-planar Hence, {tex}\overrightarrow a ,\overrightarrow b ,\overrightarrow c \;\& \;\overrightarrow d {/tex} are co-planar
solution: Let {tex}\overrightarrow a = 4\hat i + 8\hat j + 12\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow b = 2\hat i + 4\hat j + 6\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow c = 3\hat i + 5\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow d = 5\hat i + 8\hat j + 5\hat k{/tex} Now, {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\vec a}&{\vec b}&{\vec c} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left| {\begin{array}{*{0}{c}} 4&8&12 \\ 2&4&6 \\ 3&5&4 \end{array}} \right|{/tex} = 4(16 - 30) -8(8 - 18) + 12(10 - 12) = 4(-14) - 8(-10) + 12(-2) = -56 + 80 - 24 = -80 + 80 = 0 So, {tex}\hat a,\hat b,\;\& \;\hat c{/tex} are Co-planar Also, {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} b&{\overrightarrow c }&{\overrightarrow d } \end{array}} \right] = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&4&6 \\ 3&5&4 \\ 5&8&5 \end{array}} \right|{/tex} = 2(25 - 32) -4(15 - 20) + 6(24 - 25) = 2(-7) - 4(-5) - 6 = -14 - 6 + 20 = 0 i.e., {tex}\overrightarrow b ,\overrightarrow c \;\& \;\overrightarrow d {/tex} are co-planar Hence, {tex}\overrightarrow a ,\overrightarrow b ,\overrightarrow c \;\& \;\overrightarrow d {/tex} are co-planar
solution_html:
Let {tex}\overrightarrow a = 4\hat i + 8\hat j + 12\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow b = 2\hat i + 4\hat j + 6\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow c = 3\hat i + 5\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow d = 5\hat i + 8\hat j + 5\hat k{/tex}
Now, {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\vec a}&{\vec b}&{\vec c} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex}= \left| {\begin{array}{*{0}{c}} 4&8&12 \\ 2&4&6 \\ 3&5&4 \end{array}} \right|{/tex}
= 4(16 - 30) -8(8 - 18) + 12(10 - 12)
= 4(-14) - 8(-10) + 12(-2)
= -56 + 80 - 24
= -80 + 80
= 0
So, {tex}\hat a,\hat b,\;\& \;\hat c{/tex} are Co-planar
Also, {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} b&{\overrightarrow c }&{\overrightarrow d } \end{array}} \right] = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&4&6 \\ 3&5&4 \\ 5&8&5 \end{array}} \right|{/tex}
= 2(25 - 32) -4(15 - 20) + 6(24 - 25)
= 2(-7) - 4(-5) - 6
= -14 - 6 + 20
= 0
i.e., {tex}\overrightarrow b ,\overrightarrow c \;\& \;\overrightarrow d {/tex} are co-planar
Hence, {tex}\overrightarrow a ,\overrightarrow b ,\overrightarrow c \;\& \;\overrightarrow d {/tex} are co-planar

question_html: If {tex}\vec{r}=x \hat{i}+y \hat{j}+z \hat{k}{/tex}, find the value of {tex}(\vec{r} \times \hat{i}) \cdot(\vec{r} \times \hat{j})+x y{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}\vec{r}=x \hat{i}+y \hat{j}+z \hat{k}{/tex} , find the value of {tex}(\vec{r} \times \hat{i}) \cdot(\vec{r} \times \hat{j})+x y{/tex} .
explanation: We obtain {tex}\vec{r} \times \hat{i}=-y \hat{k}+z \hat{j}=0 \hat{i}+z \hat{j}-y \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r} \times \hat{j}=x \hat{k}-z \hat{i}=-z \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}+x \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore (\vec{r} \times \hat{i}) \cdot(\vec{r} \times \hat{j})=(0 \hat{i} \times z \hat{j}-y \hat{k}) \cdot(-z \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}+x \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=0 \times-z+z \times 0+(-y) x=-y x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow (\vec{r} \times \hat{i}) \cdot(\vec{r} \times \hat{j}){/tex} +xy = -xy + xy = 0
solution: We obtain {tex}\vec{r} \times \hat{i}=-y \hat{k}+z \hat{j}=0 \hat{i}+z \hat{j}-y \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r} \times \hat{j}=x \hat{k}-z \hat{i}=-z \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}+x \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore (\vec{r} \times \hat{i}) \cdot(\vec{r} \times \hat{j})=(0 \hat{i} \times z \hat{j}-y \hat{k}) \cdot(-z \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}+x \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=0 \times-z+z \times 0+(-y) x=-y x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow (\vec{r} \times \hat{i}) \cdot(\vec{r} \times \hat{j}){/tex} +xy = -xy + xy = 0
solution_html:
We obtain
{tex}\vec{r} \times \hat{i}=-y \hat{k}+z \hat{j}=0 \hat{i}+z \hat{j}-y \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r} \times \hat{j}=x \hat{k}-z \hat{i}=-z \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}+x \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore (\vec{r} \times \hat{i}) \cdot(\vec{r} \times \hat{j})=(0 \hat{i} \times z \hat{j}-y \hat{k}) \cdot(-z \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}+x \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=0 \times-z+z \times 0+(-y) x=-y x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow (\vec{r} \times \hat{i}) \cdot(\vec{r} \times \hat{j}){/tex} +xy =  -xy + xy = 0
