question_html: Find a unit vector perpendicular to the plane ABC where A, B and C are the points ( 3,- 1, 2) and(1, -1, -3), (4, -3,1 ) respectively
question_text: Find a unit vector perpendicular to the plane ABC where A, B and C are the points ( 3,- 1, 2) and(1, -1, -3), (4, -3,1 ) respectively
explanation: The vector {tex}\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}{/tex} is perpendicular to the vector {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{A C}{/tex} both. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required unit vector {tex}=\frac{\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}}{|\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}|}{/tex} Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}{/tex} = P.V. of B - P.V. of A {tex}=(\hat{i}-\hat{j}-3 \hat{k})-(3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} = {tex}-2 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{A C}{/tex} = P.V. of C - P.V. of A {tex}=(4 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+\hat{k})-(3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}=\left|\begin{array}{rrr} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ -2 & 0 & -5 \\ 1 & -2 & -1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=(0-10) \hat{i}-(2+5) \hat{j}+(4-0) \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}=-10 \hat{i}-7 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow |\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}|{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{(-10)^{2}+(-7)^{2}+4^{2}}=\sqrt{165}{/tex} Hence, required vector {tex}=\frac{\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}}{|\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}|}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{165}}{/tex} {tex}(-10 \hat{i}-7 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex}
solution: The vector {tex}\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}{/tex} is perpendicular to the vector {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{A C}{/tex} both. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required unit vector {tex}=\frac{\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}}{|\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}|}{/tex} Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}{/tex} = P.V. of B - P.V. of A {tex}=(\hat{i}-\hat{j}-3 \hat{k})-(3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} = {tex}-2 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{A C}{/tex} = P.V. of C - P.V. of A {tex}=(4 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+\hat{k})-(3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}=\left|\begin{array}{rrr} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ -2 & 0 & -5 \\ 1 & -2 & -1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=(0-10) \hat{i}-(2+5) \hat{j}+(4-0) \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}=-10 \hat{i}-7 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow |\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}|{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{(-10)^{2}+(-7)^{2}+4^{2}}=\sqrt{165}{/tex} Hence, required vector {tex}=\frac{\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}}{|\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}|}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{165}}{/tex} {tex}(-10 \hat{i}-7 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex}
solution_html:
The vector {tex}\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}{/tex} is perpendicular to the vector {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{A C}{/tex} both.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required unit vector {tex}=\frac{\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}}{|\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}|}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}{/tex} = P.V. of B - P.V. of A {tex}=(\hat{i}-\hat{j}-3 \hat{k})-(3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex}  = {tex}-2 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}{/tex}
and {tex}\overrightarrow{A C}{/tex} = P.V. of C - P.V. of A {tex}=(4 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+\hat{k})-(3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}=\left|\begin{array}{rrr} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ -2 & 0 & -5 \\ 1 & -2 & -1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=(0-10) \hat{i}-(2+5) \hat{j}+(4-0) \hat{k}{/tex}{tex}=-10 \hat{i}-7 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow |\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}|{/tex}{tex}=\sqrt{(-10)^{2}+(-7)^{2}+4^{2}}=\sqrt{165}{/tex}
Hence, required vector {tex}=\frac{\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}}{|\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}|}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{165}}{/tex}{tex}(-10 \hat{i}-7 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex}

question_html: Find  a vector magnitude {tex}\sqrt{171} {/tex} which is perpendicular to both of the vectors {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex}.
question_text: Find a vector magnitude {tex}\sqrt{171} {/tex} which is perpendicular to both of the vectors {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} .
explanation: The given vectors are {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} unit vectors perpendicular to both {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} {tex}=\pm \frac{\vec{a} \times \vec{b}}{|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|}{/tex} Now,we have {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}={/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 3 & -1 & 2 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=\hat{i}-11 \hat{j}-7 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=|\hat{i}-11 \hat{j}-7 \hat{k}|{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{1^{2}+(-11)^{2}+(-7)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{1+121+49}=\sqrt{171}{/tex} Unit vectors perpendicular to both {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} {tex}=\pm \frac{i-11 \hat{j}-7 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{171}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required vectors {tex}=\sqrt{171}\left(\pm \frac{\hat{i}-11 \hat{j}-7 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{171}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\pm(\hat{i}-11 \hat{j}-7 \hat{k}){/tex} Therefore, the vectors of magnitude {tex}\sqrt{171}{/tex} which are perpendicular to both the given vectors are {tex}\pm(\hat{i}-11 \hat{j}-7 \hat{k}){/tex} .
solution: The given vectors are {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} unit vectors perpendicular to both {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} {tex}=\pm \frac{\vec{a} \times \vec{b}}{|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|}{/tex} Now,we have {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}={/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 3 & -1 & 2 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=\hat{i}-11 \hat{j}-7 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=|\hat{i}-11 \hat{j}-7 \hat{k}|{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{1^{2}+(-11)^{2}+(-7)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{1+121+49}=\sqrt{171}{/tex} Unit vectors perpendicular to both {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} {tex}=\pm \frac{i-11 \hat{j}-7 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{171}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required vectors {tex}=\sqrt{171}\left(\pm \frac{\hat{i}-11 \hat{j}-7 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{171}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\pm(\hat{i}-11 \hat{j}-7 \hat{k}){/tex} Therefore, the vectors of magnitude {tex}\sqrt{171}{/tex} which are perpendicular to both the given vectors are {tex}\pm(\hat{i}-11 \hat{j}-7 \hat{k}){/tex} .
solution_html:
The given vectors are {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} unit vectors perpendicular to both {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} {tex}=\pm \frac{\vec{a} \times \vec{b}}{|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|}{/tex}
Now,we have
{tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}={/tex}{tex}{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 3 & -1 & 2 \end{array}\right|{/tex}{tex}=\hat{i}-11 \hat{j}-7 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=|\hat{i}-11 \hat{j}-7 \hat{k}|{/tex}{tex}=\sqrt{1^{2}+(-11)^{2}+(-7)^{2}}{/tex}{tex}=\sqrt{1+121+49}=\sqrt{171}{/tex}
Unit vectors perpendicular to both {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} {tex}=\pm \frac{i-11 \hat{j}-7 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{171}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required vectors {tex}=\sqrt{171}\left(\pm \frac{\hat{i}-11 \hat{j}-7 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{171}}\right){/tex}{tex}=\pm(\hat{i}-11 \hat{j}-7 \hat{k}){/tex}
Therefore, the vectors of magnitude {tex}\sqrt{171}{/tex} which are perpendicular to both the given vectors are {tex}\pm(\hat{i}-11 \hat{j}-7 \hat{k}){/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: In a triangle OAC, if B is the mid-point of side AC and {tex}\overrightarrow{O A}=\vec{a}, \overrightarrow{O B}=\vec{b}{/tex}, then what is {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OC}}{/tex}?
question_text: In a triangle OAC, if B is the mid-point of side AC and {tex}\overrightarrow{O A}=\vec{a}, \overrightarrow{O B}=\vec{b}{/tex} , then what is {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OC}}{/tex} ?
explanation: In {tex}\triangle{/tex} AOC, {tex}\overrightarrow{O A}=\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{O B}=\vec{b}{/tex} Here,It is given that B is the mid-point of AC. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Positiion vector of B {tex}=\frac{\text { Position vector of } \mathrm{A}+\text { Position vector of } \mathrm{C}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{O B}=\frac{\overrightarrow{O A}+\overrightarrow{O C}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{b}=\frac{\vec{a}+\overrightarrow{O C}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{a}+\overrightarrow{O C}=2 \vec{b}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{O C}=2 \vec{b}-\vec{a}{/tex}
solution: In {tex}\triangle{/tex} AOC, {tex}\overrightarrow{O A}=\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{O B}=\vec{b}{/tex} Here,It is given that B is the mid-point of AC. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Positiion vector of B {tex}=\frac{\text { Position vector of } \mathrm{A}+\text { Position vector of } \mathrm{C}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{O B}=\frac{\overrightarrow{O A}+\overrightarrow{O C}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{b}=\frac{\vec{a}+\overrightarrow{O C}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{a}+\overrightarrow{O C}=2 \vec{b}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{O C}=2 \vec{b}-\vec{a}{/tex}
solution_html:
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}AOC, {tex}\overrightarrow{O A}=\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{O B}=\vec{b}{/tex}
Here,It is given that B is the mid-point of AC.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Positiion vector of B {tex}=\frac{\text { Position vector of } \mathrm{A}+\text { Position vector of } \mathrm{C}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{O B}=\frac{\overrightarrow{O A}+\overrightarrow{O C}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{b}=\frac{\vec{a}+\overrightarrow{O C}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{a}+\overrightarrow{O C}=2 \vec{b}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{O C}=2 \vec{b}-\vec{a}{/tex}

question_html: Write the value of {tex}\hat{i} \cdot(\hat{j} \times \hat{k})+\hat{j} \cdot(\hat{k} \times \hat{i})+\hat{k} \cdot(\hat{j} \times \hat{i}){/tex}.
question_text: Write the value of {tex}\hat{i} \cdot(\hat{j} \times \hat{k})+\hat{j} \cdot(\hat{k} \times \hat{i})+\hat{k} \cdot(\hat{j} \times \hat{i}){/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\hat{i} \cdot(\hat{j} \times \hat{k})+\hat{j} \cdot(\hat{k} \times \hat{i})+\hat{k} \cdot(\hat{j} \times \hat{i}){/tex} {tex}=\hat{i} \cdot \hat{i}+\hat{j} \cdot j+\hat{k} \cdot(-\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=|\hat{i}|^{2}+|\hat{j}|^{2}-|\hat{k}|^{2}{/tex} = 1 + 1 - 1 {tex}(\because|\hat{i}|=1,|\hat{j}|=1{/tex} and {tex}|\hat{k}|=1){/tex} = 1
solution: {tex}\hat{i} \cdot(\hat{j} \times \hat{k})+\hat{j} \cdot(\hat{k} \times \hat{i})+\hat{k} \cdot(\hat{j} \times \hat{i}){/tex} {tex}=\hat{i} \cdot \hat{i}+\hat{j} \cdot j+\hat{k} \cdot(-\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=|\hat{i}|^{2}+|\hat{j}|^{2}-|\hat{k}|^{2}{/tex} = 1 + 1 - 1 {tex}(\because|\hat{i}|=1,|\hat{j}|=1{/tex} and {tex}|\hat{k}|=1){/tex} = 1
solution_html:
{tex}\hat{i} \cdot(\hat{j} \times \hat{k})+\hat{j} \cdot(\hat{k} \times \hat{i})+\hat{k} \cdot(\hat{j} \times \hat{i}){/tex}
{tex}=\hat{i} \cdot \hat{i}+\hat{j} \cdot j+\hat{k} \cdot(-\hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}=|\hat{i}|^{2}+|\hat{j}|^{2}-|\hat{k}|^{2}{/tex}
= 1 + 1 - 1 {tex}(\because|\hat{i}|=1,|\hat{j}|=1{/tex} and {tex}|\hat{k}|=1){/tex}
 = 1

subject: Mathematics
question_html: For any two vectors {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex}, find {tex}\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex}.
question_text: For any two vectors {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} , find {tex}\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex} .
explanation: Let: {tex}\vec{a}=a_{1} \hat{i}+a_{2} \hat{j}+a_{3} \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}=b_{1} \hat{i}+b_{2} \hat{j}+b_{3} \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b} \times \vec{a}{/tex} {tex}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ b_{1} & b_{2} & b_{3} \\ a_{1} & a_{2} & a_{3} \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=\hat{i}\left(b_{2} a_{3}-b_{3} a_{2}\right)-\hat{j}\left(b_{1} a_{3}-b_{3} a_{1}\right){/tex} {tex}+\hat{k}\left(b_{1} a_{2}-b_{2} a_{1}\right){/tex} Now, {tex}\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex} {tex}=\left(a_{1} \hat{i}+a_{2} \hat{j}+a_{3} \hat{k}\right) \cdot\left[\hat{i}\left(b_{2} a_{3}-b_{3} a_{2}\right)-\hat{j}\right.{/tex} {tex}\left.\left(b_{1} a_{3}-b_{3} a_{1}\right)+\hat{k}\left(b_{1} a_{2}-b_{2} a_{1}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}=a_{1}\left(b_{2} a_{3}-b_{3} a_{2}\right)-a_{2}\left(b_{1} a_{3}-b_{3} a_{1}\right){/tex} {tex}+a_{3}\left(b_{1} a_{2}-b_{2} a_{1}\right){/tex} {tex}=a_{1} b_{2} a_{3}-a_{1} b_{3} a_{2}-a_{2} b_{1} a_{3}+a_{2} b_{3} a_{1}{/tex} {tex}+a_{3} b_{1} a_{2}-a_{3} b_{2} a_{1}{/tex} = 0 Hence, {tex}\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex} = 0
solution: Let: {tex}\vec{a}=a_{1} \hat{i}+a_{2} \hat{j}+a_{3} \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}=b_{1} \hat{i}+b_{2} \hat{j}+b_{3} \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b} \times \vec{a}{/tex} {tex}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ b_{1} & b_{2} & b_{3} \\ a_{1} & a_{2} & a_{3} \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=\hat{i}\left(b_{2} a_{3}-b_{3} a_{2}\right)-\hat{j}\left(b_{1} a_{3}-b_{3} a_{1}\right){/tex} {tex}+\hat{k}\left(b_{1} a_{2}-b_{2} a_{1}\right){/tex} Now, {tex}\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex} {tex}=\left(a_{1} \hat{i}+a_{2} \hat{j}+a_{3} \hat{k}\right) \cdot\left[\hat{i}\left(b_{2} a_{3}-b_{3} a_{2}\right)-\hat{j}\right.{/tex} {tex}\left.\left(b_{1} a_{3}-b_{3} a_{1}\right)+\hat{k}\left(b_{1} a_{2}-b_{2} a_{1}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}=a_{1}\left(b_{2} a_{3}-b_{3} a_{2}\right)-a_{2}\left(b_{1} a_{3}-b_{3} a_{1}\right){/tex} {tex}+a_{3}\left(b_{1} a_{2}-b_{2} a_{1}\right){/tex} {tex}=a_{1} b_{2} a_{3}-a_{1} b_{3} a_{2}-a_{2} b_{1} a_{3}+a_{2} b_{3} a_{1}{/tex} {tex}+a_{3} b_{1} a_{2}-a_{3} b_{2} a_{1}{/tex} = 0 Hence, {tex}\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex} = 0
solution_html:
Let:
{tex}\vec{a}=a_{1} \hat{i}+a_{2} \hat{j}+a_{3} \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{b}=b_{1} \hat{i}+b_{2} \hat{j}+b_{3} \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{b} \times \vec{a}{/tex}{tex}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ b_{1} & b_{2} & b_{3} \\ a_{1} & a_{2} & a_{3} \end{array}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=\hat{i}\left(b_{2} a_{3}-b_{3} a_{2}\right)-\hat{j}\left(b_{1} a_{3}-b_{3} a_{1}\right){/tex}{tex}+\hat{k}\left(b_{1} a_{2}-b_{2} a_{1}\right){/tex}
Now,
{tex}\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex}
{tex}=\left(a_{1} \hat{i}+a_{2} \hat{j}+a_{3} \hat{k}\right) \cdot\left[\hat{i}\left(b_{2} a_{3}-b_{3} a_{2}\right)-\hat{j}\right.{/tex}{tex}\left.\left(b_{1} a_{3}-b_{3} a_{1}\right)+\hat{k}\left(b_{1} a_{2}-b_{2} a_{1}\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}=a_{1}\left(b_{2} a_{3}-b_{3} a_{2}\right)-a_{2}\left(b_{1} a_{3}-b_{3} a_{1}\right){/tex}{tex}+a_{3}\left(b_{1} a_{2}-b_{2} a_{1}\right){/tex}
{tex}=a_{1} b_{2} a_{3}-a_{1} b_{3} a_{2}-a_{2} b_{1} a_{3}+a_{2} b_{3} a_{1}{/tex}{tex}+a_{3} b_{1} a_{2}-a_{3} b_{2} a_{1}{/tex}
= 0
Hence, {tex}\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex} = 0

question_html: Find the angle between the vectors {tex}\hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex}.
question_text: Find the angle between the vectors {tex}\hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex} .
explanation: We know that, cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}) \cdot(\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}})}{\sqrt{1+1} \sqrt{1+1}}{/tex} , where {tex}\theta{/tex} is required angle {tex}\therefore{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex} .
solution: We know that, cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}) \cdot(\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}})}{\sqrt{1+1} \sqrt{1+1}}{/tex} , where {tex}\theta{/tex} is required angle {tex}\therefore{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex} .
solution_html:
We know that,

cos{tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}) \cdot(\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}})}{\sqrt{1+1} \sqrt{1+1}}{/tex}, where {tex}\theta{/tex} is required angle
{tex}\therefore{/tex} cos{tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex}.

question_html: Write the projection of vector 2{tex}\hat i{/tex} + 3{tex}\hat j{/tex} – {tex}\hat k{/tex} along the vector {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 2{tex}\hat j{/tex}
question_text: Write the projection of vector 2 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 3 {tex}\hat j{/tex} – {tex}\hat k{/tex} along the vector {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 2 {tex}\hat j{/tex}
explanation: Projection of vector {tex}\vec{a} \text { on } \vec{b}=\frac{1}{|\vec{b}|}(\vec{a}. \vec{b}){/tex} Given, {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{\imath}+3 \hat{\jmath}- \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}=1 \hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}{/tex} Now, {tex}(\vec{a} . \vec{b}){/tex} = (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1) + (3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2) + (-1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0) = 2 + 6 = 8 &, | b | =√2 So, projection of vector a long vector b = 8/√2 = 4√2
solution: Projection of vector {tex}\vec{a} \text { on } \vec{b}=\frac{1}{|\vec{b}|}(\vec{a}. \vec{b}){/tex} Given, {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{\imath}+3 \hat{\jmath}- \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}=1 \hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}{/tex} Now, {tex}(\vec{a} . \vec{b}){/tex} = (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1) + (3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2) + (-1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0) = 2 + 6 = 8 &, | b | =√2 So, projection of vector a long vector b = 8/√2 = 4√2
solution_html:
Projection of vector {tex}\vec{a} \text { on } \vec{b}=\frac{1}{|\vec{b}|}(\vec{a}. \vec{b}){/tex}
Given, 
{tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{\imath}+3 \hat{\jmath}- \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{b}=1 \hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}{/tex}
Now,
{tex}(\vec{a} . \vec{b}){/tex} = (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1) + (3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2) + (-1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0)
= 2 + 6 = 8
&, |b| =√2
So, projection of vector a long vector b = 8/√2 = 4√2

question_html: If {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}, then find {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}|{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} , then find {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}|{/tex}
explanation: Cross product of vectors a and b is given by :- {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 3 & 5 & -2 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}-17 \hat{i}+13 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{289+169+49}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{507}{/tex}
solution: Cross product of vectors a and b is given by :- {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 3 & 5 & -2 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}-17 \hat{i}+13 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{289+169+49}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{507}{/tex}
solution_html:
Cross product of vectors a and b is given by :-

 {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 3 & 5 & -2 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}-17 \hat{i}+13 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{289+169+49}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{507}{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}|\vec{a}|=10,|\vec{b}|=2{/tex} and {tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=16{/tex}, find {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}|\vec{a}|=10,|\vec{b}|=2{/tex} and {tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=16{/tex} , find {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} .
explanation: We know that, {tex}(\vec{a} . \vec{b})^{2}+|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|^{2}=|\vec{a}|^{2}|\vec{b}|^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(\vec{a} . \vec{b})^{2}+(16)^{2}=(10)^{2} \times 2^{2}{/tex} {tex}(\because|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=16,|\vec{a}|=10 \text { and }|\vec{b}|=2){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})^{2}{/tex} + 256 = 400 {tex}\Rightarrow(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})^{2}{/tex} = 144 {tex}\Rightarrow(\vec{a} . \vec{b})=\pm 12{/tex}
solution: We know that, {tex}(\vec{a} . \vec{b})^{2}+|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|^{2}=|\vec{a}|^{2}|\vec{b}|^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(\vec{a} . \vec{b})^{2}+(16)^{2}=(10)^{2} \times 2^{2}{/tex} {tex}(\because|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=16,|\vec{a}|=10 \text { and }|\vec{b}|=2){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})^{2}{/tex} + 256 = 400 {tex}\Rightarrow(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})^{2}{/tex} = 144 {tex}\Rightarrow(\vec{a} . \vec{b})=\pm 12{/tex}
solution_html:
We know that, 
{tex}(\vec{a} . \vec{b})^{2}+|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|^{2}=|\vec{a}|^{2}|\vec{b}|^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(\vec{a} . \vec{b})^{2}+(16)^{2}=(10)^{2} \times 2^{2}{/tex} {tex}(\because|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=16,|\vec{a}|=10 \text { and }|\vec{b}|=2){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})^{2}{/tex} + 256 = 400
{tex}\Rightarrow(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})^{2}{/tex} = 144
{tex}\Rightarrow(\vec{a} . \vec{b})=\pm 12{/tex}

question_html: Find a vector of magnitude {tex}\sqrt{171}{/tex} which is perpendicular to both of the vectors {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} .
question_text: Find a vector of magnitude {tex}\sqrt{171}{/tex} which is perpendicular to both of the vectors {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\vec{a}=\hat i+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=3 \hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\imath} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 3 & 4 & 2 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=\hat{\imath}(4-3)-\hat{\jmath}(2+9)+\hat{k}(-1-6){/tex} {tex}\vec{d}=\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\hat{\imath}-7 \hat{\jmath}-7 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}d=\frac{\vec{d}}{|\overrightarrow{d l}|}=\frac{\hat{\imath}-7 \hat{\jmath}-7 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{1^{2}+(7)^{2}+(-7)^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\hat{d}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{99}}(\hat{\imath}-\hat{7} \hat{\jmath}-7 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} vector = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{171}}{\sqrt{99}}(\hat{\imath}-7 \hat{\jmath}-7 \hat{k}){/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{171}{99}}(i-7 \hat{j}-7 \hat{k}){/tex}
solution: {tex}\vec{a}=\hat i+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=3 \hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\imath} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 3 & 4 & 2 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=\hat{\imath}(4-3)-\hat{\jmath}(2+9)+\hat{k}(-1-6){/tex} {tex}\vec{d}=\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\hat{\imath}-7 \hat{\jmath}-7 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}d=\frac{\vec{d}}{|\overrightarrow{d l}|}=\frac{\hat{\imath}-7 \hat{\jmath}-7 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{1^{2}+(7)^{2}+(-7)^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\hat{d}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{99}}(\hat{\imath}-\hat{7} \hat{\jmath}-7 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} vector = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{171}}{\sqrt{99}}(\hat{\imath}-7 \hat{\jmath}-7 \hat{k}){/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{171}{99}}(i-7 \hat{j}-7 \hat{k}){/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\vec{a}=\hat i+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=3 \hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\imath} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 3 & 4 & 2 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=\hat{\imath}(4-3)-\hat{\jmath}(2+9)+\hat{k}(-1-6){/tex}
{tex}\vec{d}=\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\hat{\imath}-7 \hat{\jmath}-7 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}d=\frac{\vec{d}}{|\overrightarrow{d l}|}=\frac{\hat{\imath}-7 \hat{\jmath}-7 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{1^{2}+(7)^{2}+(-7)^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\hat{d}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{99}}(\hat{\imath}-\hat{7} \hat{\jmath}-7 \hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} vector = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{171}}{\sqrt{99}}(\hat{\imath}-7 \hat{\jmath}-7 \hat{k}){/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{\frac{171}{99}}(i-7 \hat{j}-7 \hat{k}){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|{/tex} = a, then find the value of the following: {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \hat{\mathrm{i}}|^{2}+|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \hat{\mathrm{j}}|^{2}+|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \hat{\mathrm{k}}|^{2}{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|{/tex} = a, then find the value of the following: {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \hat{\mathrm{i}}|^{2}+|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \hat{\mathrm{j}}|^{2}+|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \hat{\mathrm{k}}|^{2}{/tex} .
explanation: We have {tex}|\vec a \times \hat i|^{2}+|\vec a \times\hat j|^{2}+|\vec a \times \hat k|^{2}{/tex} {tex}=(|\vec a||\hat i|)^{2}+(|\vec a||\hat j|)^{2}+(|\vec a||\hat k|)^{2}{/tex} {tex}=|\vec a|^{2} \times\left(|\hat i|^{2}+|\hat j|^{2}+|\hat k|^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=a^{2} \times\left(|\hat i|^{2}+|\hat j|^{2}+|\hat k|^{2}\right){/tex} As we know {tex}|\hat i|^{2}=|\hat j|^{2}=|\hat k|^{2}=1{/tex} Hence {tex}|\vec a \times \hat i|^{2}+|\vec a \times \hat j|^{2}+|\vec a \times \hat k|^{2}=a^{2} \times 3{/tex} {tex}\therefore|\vec a \times \hat i|^{2}+|\vec a \times\hat j|^{2}+|\vec a \times \hat k|^{2}=3a^{2}{/tex}
solution: We have {tex}|\vec a \times \hat i|^{2}+|\vec a \times\hat j|^{2}+|\vec a \times \hat k|^{2}{/tex} {tex}=(|\vec a||\hat i|)^{2}+(|\vec a||\hat j|)^{2}+(|\vec a||\hat k|)^{2}{/tex} {tex}=|\vec a|^{2} \times\left(|\hat i|^{2}+|\hat j|^{2}+|\hat k|^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=a^{2} \times\left(|\hat i|^{2}+|\hat j|^{2}+|\hat k|^{2}\right){/tex} As we know {tex}|\hat i|^{2}=|\hat j|^{2}=|\hat k|^{2}=1{/tex} Hence {tex}|\vec a \times \hat i|^{2}+|\vec a \times \hat j|^{2}+|\vec a \times \hat k|^{2}=a^{2} \times 3{/tex} {tex}\therefore|\vec a \times \hat i|^{2}+|\vec a \times\hat j|^{2}+|\vec a \times \hat k|^{2}=3a^{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have
{tex}|\vec a \times \hat i|^{2}+|\vec a \times\hat j|^{2}+|\vec a \times \hat k|^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=(|\vec a||\hat i|)^{2}+(|\vec a||\hat j|)^{2}+(|\vec a||\hat k|)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=|\vec a|^{2} \times\left(|\hat i|^{2}+|\hat j|^{2}+|\hat k|^{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}=a^{2} \times\left(|\hat i|^{2}+|\hat j|^{2}+|\hat k|^{2}\right){/tex}
As we know {tex}|\hat i|^{2}=|\hat j|^{2}=|\hat k|^{2}=1{/tex}
Hence 
{tex}|\vec a \times \hat i|^{2}+|\vec a \times \hat j|^{2}+|\vec a \times \hat k|^{2}=a^{2} \times 3{/tex}
{tex}\therefore|\vec a \times \hat i|^{2}+|\vec a \times\hat j|^{2}+|\vec a \times \hat k|^{2}=3a^{2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the value of a + b, if the points A(2, a, 3), B(3, -5, b) and C(-1, 11, 9) are collinear.
question_text: Find the value of a + b, if the points A(2, a, 3), B(3, -5, b) and C(-1, 11, 9) are collinear.
explanation: Given that A(2, a, 3), B(3, -5, b) and C(-1, 11, 9) are collinear. Here {tex}\vec {AB}= (1, -5-a, b-3){/tex} , and {tex}\vec {AC} = (-3, 11-a, 6) = -3(1, \frac{a-11}3, -2){/tex} As, A, B, and C are collinear, {tex}\therefore \vec {AC}={/tex} {tex}-3\vec {AB}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}(1, -5-a, b-3)=(1, \frac{a-11}3, -2){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow -5 - a = \frac{a - 11}{3}{/tex} and b - 3 = -2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = -1 and b = 1 Hence, a + b = 0
solution: Given that A(2, a, 3), B(3, -5, b) and C(-1, 11, 9) are collinear. Here {tex}\vec {AB}= (1, -5-a, b-3){/tex} , and {tex}\vec {AC} = (-3, 11-a, 6) = -3(1, \frac{a-11}3, -2){/tex} As, A, B, and C are collinear, {tex}\therefore \vec {AC}={/tex} {tex}-3\vec {AB}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}(1, -5-a, b-3)=(1, \frac{a-11}3, -2){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow -5 - a = \frac{a - 11}{3}{/tex} and b - 3 = -2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = -1 and b = 1 Hence, a + b = 0
solution_html:
Given that A(2, a, 3), B(3, -5, b) and C(-1, 11, 9) are collinear.
Here {tex}\vec {AB}= (1, -5-a, b-3){/tex} , and {tex}\vec {AC} = (-3, 11-a, 6) = -3(1, \frac{a-11}3, -2){/tex}
As, A, B, and C are collinear, 
{tex}\therefore \vec {AC}={/tex} {tex}-3\vec {AB}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}(1, -5-a, b-3)=(1, \frac{a-11}3, -2){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow -5 - a = \frac{a - 11}{3}{/tex} and b - 3 = -2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = -1 and b = 1
Hence, a + b = 0

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Let {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \ \vec{\mathrm{b}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+7 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{c}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} . Find a vector {tex}\vec{\mathrm{d}}{/tex} which is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c} \cdot \vec{d}=27{/tex}
question_text: Let {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \ \vec{\mathrm{b}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+7 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{c}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} . Find a vector {tex}\vec{\mathrm{d}}{/tex} which is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c} \cdot \vec{d}=27{/tex}
explanation: Here we have, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \ \vec{\mathrm{b}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+7 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{c}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} . Now, {tex}\vec{d}=d_{1} \hat{i}+d_{2} \hat{j}+d_{,} \hat{k}{/tex} Since {tex}\vec{d}{/tex} is perpendicular to both {tex} \vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} {tex}\vec{d} \cdot \vec{a}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow d_{1}+4 d_{2}+2 d_{3}=0{/tex} ............(i) And, {tex}\vec{d} \cdot \vec{b}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3 d_{1}-2 d_{2}+7 d_{3}=0{/tex} .......(ii) Also, it is given that: {tex}\vec{c} \cdot \vec{d}=15{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 d_{1}-d_{2}+4 d_{3}=15{/tex} .................(iii) On solving (i), (ii), and (iii), we get: {tex}d_{1}=\frac{160}{3}, d_{2}=-\frac{5}{3} ~~and~~ d_{3}=-\frac{70}{3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{d}=\frac{160}{3} \hat{i}-\frac{5}{3} j-\frac{70}{3} \hat{k}=\frac{1}{3}(160 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}-70 \hat{k}){/tex} Hence, the required vector is {tex} \frac{1}{3}(160 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}-70 \hat{k}){/tex}
solution: Here we have, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \ \vec{\mathrm{b}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+7 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{c}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} . Now, {tex}\vec{d}=d_{1} \hat{i}+d_{2} \hat{j}+d_{,} \hat{k}{/tex} Since {tex}\vec{d}{/tex} is perpendicular to both {tex} \vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} {tex}\vec{d} \cdot \vec{a}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow d_{1}+4 d_{2}+2 d_{3}=0{/tex} ............(i) And, {tex}\vec{d} \cdot \vec{b}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3 d_{1}-2 d_{2}+7 d_{3}=0{/tex} .......(ii) Also, it is given that: {tex}\vec{c} \cdot \vec{d}=15{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 d_{1}-d_{2}+4 d_{3}=15{/tex} .................(iii) On solving (i), (ii), and (iii), we get: {tex}d_{1}=\frac{160}{3}, d_{2}=-\frac{5}{3} ~~and~~ d_{3}=-\frac{70}{3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{d}=\frac{160}{3} \hat{i}-\frac{5}{3} j-\frac{70}{3} \hat{k}=\frac{1}{3}(160 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}-70 \hat{k}){/tex} Hence, the required vector is {tex} \frac{1}{3}(160 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}-70 \hat{k}){/tex}
solution_html:
Here we have,  {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \ \vec{\mathrm{b}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+7 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{c}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}.
Now, {tex}\vec{d}=d_{1} \hat{i}+d_{2} \hat{j}+d_{,} \hat{k}{/tex}
Since {tex}\vec{d}{/tex} is perpendicular to both {tex} \vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{d} \cdot \vec{a}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow d_{1}+4 d_{2}+2 d_{3}=0{/tex} ............(i)
And,
{tex}\vec{d} \cdot \vec{b}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 3 d_{1}-2 d_{2}+7 d_{3}=0{/tex}  .......(ii)
Also, it is given that:
{tex}\vec{c} \cdot \vec{d}=15{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 d_{1}-d_{2}+4 d_{3}=15{/tex}  .................(iii)
On solving (i), (ii), and (iii), we get:
{tex}d_{1}=\frac{160}{3}, d_{2}=-\frac{5}{3} ~~and~~ d_{3}=-\frac{70}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{d}=\frac{160}{3} \hat{i}-\frac{5}{3} j-\frac{70}{3} \hat{k}=\frac{1}{3}(160 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}-70 \hat{k}){/tex}
Hence, the required vector is {tex} \frac{1}{3}(160 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}-70 \hat{k}){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=7 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}, then find the projection of {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} on {tex}\vec b{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=7 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} , then find the projection of {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} on {tex}\vec b{/tex}
explanation: We have, {tex}\vec{a}=7 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-4 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} The projection of {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} is given by {tex}\frac{1}{\left|\vec b\right|}(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})=\frac{(7 \times 2)+(1 \times 6)+(-4 \times 3)}{\sqrt{2^{2}+6^{2}+3^{2}}}=\frac{8}{7}{/tex}
solution: We have, {tex}\vec{a}=7 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-4 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} The projection of {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} is given by {tex}\frac{1}{\left|\vec b\right|}(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})=\frac{(7 \times 2)+(1 \times 6)+(-4 \times 3)}{\sqrt{2^{2}+6^{2}+3^{2}}}=\frac{8}{7}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have,

{tex}\vec{a}=7 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-4 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex}
The projection of {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} is given by
{tex}\frac{1}{\left|\vec b\right|}(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})=\frac{(7 \times 2)+(1 \times 6)+(-4 \times 3)}{\sqrt{2^{2}+6^{2}+3^{2}}}=\frac{8}{7}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find {tex}\lambda{/tex}, if the vectors {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}, \vec{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}=\lambda \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} are coplanar.
question_text: Find {tex}\lambda{/tex} , if the vectors {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}, \vec{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}=\lambda \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} are coplanar.
explanation: It is given that the vectors {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}, \vec{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}=\lambda \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} are coplanar. Three vectors {tex} \vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}{/tex} are co-planar if {tex}[\vec{a}~~\vec{b} ~~\vec{c}]=0{/tex} Given, {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{\imath}+3 \hat{\jmath}+\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b} =2 \hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}-\hat{k} {/tex} {tex}\vec{c} =\lambda \hat{\imath}+7 \hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{k} {/tex} {tex}\therefore ~\left[\begin{array}{lll} \vec{a} & \vec{b} & \vec{c} \end{array}\right]=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 3 & 1 \\ 2 & -1 & -1 \\ \lambda & 7 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}0=1[(-1 \times 3)-(7 \times-1)]-3[(2 \times 3)-(\lambda \times-1)]+1[(2 \times 7)-(\lambda \times-1)]{/tex} {tex}0=1[-3+7]-3[6+\lambda]+1[14+\lambda]{/tex} {tex}0=4-18-3 \lambda+14+\lambda{/tex} {tex}0=18-18-3 \lambda+\lambda{/tex} 0 = -2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} -2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0 {tex}\lambda=\frac{0}{-2}{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0 Therefore, {tex}\vec{a}, b, \vec{c} {/tex} are co=planar if {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0
solution: It is given that the vectors {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}, \vec{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}=\lambda \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} are coplanar. Three vectors {tex} \vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}{/tex} are co-planar if {tex}[\vec{a}~~\vec{b} ~~\vec{c}]=0{/tex} Given, {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{\imath}+3 \hat{\jmath}+\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b} =2 \hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}-\hat{k} {/tex} {tex}\vec{c} =\lambda \hat{\imath}+7 \hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{k} {/tex} {tex}\therefore ~\left[\begin{array}{lll} \vec{a} & \vec{b} & \vec{c} \end{array}\right]=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 3 & 1 \\ 2 & -1 & -1 \\ \lambda & 7 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}0=1[(-1 \times 3)-(7 \times-1)]-3[(2 \times 3)-(\lambda \times-1)]+1[(2 \times 7)-(\lambda \times-1)]{/tex} {tex}0=1[-3+7]-3[6+\lambda]+1[14+\lambda]{/tex} {tex}0=4-18-3 \lambda+14+\lambda{/tex} {tex}0=18-18-3 \lambda+\lambda{/tex} 0 = -2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} -2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0 {tex}\lambda=\frac{0}{-2}{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0 Therefore, {tex}\vec{a}, b, \vec{c} {/tex} are co=planar if {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0
solution_html:
It is given that the vectors {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}, \vec{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}=\lambda \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} are coplanar.
Three vectors {tex} \vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}{/tex} are co-planar if
{tex}[\vec{a}~~\vec{b} ~~\vec{c}]=0{/tex}
Given,
{tex}\vec{a}=\hat{\imath}+3 \hat{\jmath}+\hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{b} =2 \hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}-\hat{k} {/tex}
{tex}\vec{c} =\lambda \hat{\imath}+7 \hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{k} {/tex}
{tex}\therefore ~\left[\begin{array}{lll} \vec{a} & \vec{b} & \vec{c} \end{array}\right]=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 3 & 1 \\ 2 & -1 & -1 \\ \lambda & 7 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
{tex}0=1[(-1 \times 3)-(7 \times-1)]-3[(2 \times 3)-(\lambda \times-1)]+1[(2 \times 7)-(\lambda \times-1)]{/tex}
{tex}0=1[-3+7]-3[6+\lambda]+1[14+\lambda]{/tex}
{tex}0=4-18-3 \lambda+14+\lambda{/tex}
{tex}0=18-18-3 \lambda+\lambda{/tex}
0 = -2{tex}\lambda{/tex}
-2{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0
{tex}\lambda=\frac{0}{-2}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda{/tex}= 0
Therefore, {tex}\vec{a}, b, \vec{c} {/tex}are co=planar if {tex}\lambda{/tex}= 0

question_html: If {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}, \vec{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}} {/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{c}}=5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} , then find the value of {tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \cdot \vec{c}{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}, \vec{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}} {/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{c}}=5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} , then find the value of {tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \cdot \vec{c}{/tex}
explanation: Given: {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}, \vec{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}} {/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{c}}=5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \cdot \vec{c}{/tex} {tex}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}+2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \cdot(5 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=(3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}) \cdot(5 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} (-4) + 0 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 15 - 12 = 3
solution: Given: {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}, \vec{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}} {/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{c}}=5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \cdot \vec{c}{/tex} {tex}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}+2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \cdot(5 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=(3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}) \cdot(5 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} (-4) + 0 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 15 - 12 = 3
solution_html:
Given:  {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}, \vec{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}} {/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{c}}=5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \cdot \vec{c}{/tex}
{tex}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}+2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \cdot(5 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}=(3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}) \cdot(5 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex}
= 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 + 3 {tex}\times{/tex}(-4) + 0 {tex}\times{/tex} 3
= 15 - 12
= 3

question_html: Vectors {tex} \vec { a } , \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { c }{/tex} are such that {tex} \vec { a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } = \vec { 0 }{/tex} and {tex} | \vec { a } | = 3 , | \vec { b } | = 5{/tex} and {tex} | \vec { c } | = 7.{/tex} Find the angle between {tex} \vec { a }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b }.{/tex}
question_text: Vectors {tex} \vec { a } , \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { c }{/tex} are such that {tex} \vec { a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } = \vec { 0 }{/tex} and {tex} | \vec { a } | = 3 , | \vec { b } | = 5{/tex} and {tex} | \vec { c } | = 7.{/tex} Find the angle between {tex} \vec { a }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b }.{/tex}
explanation: According to the question , {tex} | \vec { a } | = 3 , {/tex} {tex}| \vec { b } | = 5 \text { and } {/tex} {tex}| \vec { c } | = 7.{/tex} Let the angle between {tex} \vec { a }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b }{/tex} is {tex}\theta.{/tex} Also, {tex} \vec { a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } = 0 \Rightarrow \vec { a } + \vec { b } = - \vec { c }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad ( \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) ^ { 2 } = ( - \vec { c } ) ^ { 2 }{/tex} [squaring both sides] {tex} \Rightarrow \quad ( \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) .( \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) = ( - \vec { c } ) \cdot ( - \vec { c } ){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \vec { a }. \vec { a } + \vec { a }. \vec { b } + \vec { b } . \vec { a } + \vec { b } .\vec { b } = \vec { c } .\vec { c }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 } + \vec { a } .\vec { b } + \vec { a } .\vec { b } + | \vec { b } | ^ { 2 } = | \vec { c } | ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \vec { x } .\vec { x } = | \vec { x } | ^ { 2 }\right.{/tex} and {tex} \vec { a }. \vec { b } = \vec { b } \cdot \vec { a } \Big]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 } + 2 \vec { a }. \vec { b } + | \vec { b } | ^ { 2 } = | \vec { c } | ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 } + 2 | \vec { a } | | \vec { b } | \cos \theta{/tex} {tex} + | \vec { b } | ^ { 2 } = | \vec { c } | ^ { 2 } \quad \ldots ( \mathrm { i } ){/tex} {tex} [ \because \vec { a } .\vec { b } = | \vec { a } | | \vec { b } | \cos \theta ]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } + 2 \times 3 \times 5 \cos \theta + ( 5 ) ^ { 2 } = ( 7 ) ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 9 + 30 \cos \theta + 25 = 49{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 30 \cos \theta = 49 - 34{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \frac { 15 } { 30 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \cos \frac { \pi } { 3 } \left[ \because \frac { 1 } { 2 } = \cos \frac { \pi } { 3 } \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \theta = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex}
solution: According to the question , {tex} | \vec { a } | = 3 , {/tex} {tex}| \vec { b } | = 5 \text { and } {/tex} {tex}| \vec { c } | = 7.{/tex} Let the angle between {tex} \vec { a }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b }{/tex} is {tex}\theta.{/tex} Also, {tex} \vec { a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } = 0 \Rightarrow \vec { a } + \vec { b } = - \vec { c }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad ( \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) ^ { 2 } = ( - \vec { c } ) ^ { 2 }{/tex} [squaring both sides] {tex} \Rightarrow \quad ( \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) .( \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) = ( - \vec { c } ) \cdot ( - \vec { c } ){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \vec { a }. \vec { a } + \vec { a }. \vec { b } + \vec { b } . \vec { a } + \vec { b } .\vec { b } = \vec { c } .\vec { c }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 } + \vec { a } .\vec { b } + \vec { a } .\vec { b } + | \vec { b } | ^ { 2 } = | \vec { c } | ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \vec { x } .\vec { x } = | \vec { x } | ^ { 2 }\right.{/tex} and {tex} \vec { a }. \vec { b } = \vec { b } \cdot \vec { a } \Big]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 } + 2 \vec { a }. \vec { b } + | \vec { b } | ^ { 2 } = | \vec { c } | ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 } + 2 | \vec { a } | | \vec { b } | \cos \theta{/tex} {tex} + | \vec { b } | ^ { 2 } = | \vec { c } | ^ { 2 } \quad \ldots ( \mathrm { i } ){/tex} {tex} [ \because \vec { a } .\vec { b } = | \vec { a } | | \vec { b } | \cos \theta ]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } + 2 \times 3 \times 5 \cos \theta + ( 5 ) ^ { 2 } = ( 7 ) ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 9 + 30 \cos \theta + 25 = 49{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 30 \cos \theta = 49 - 34{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \frac { 15 } { 30 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \cos \frac { \pi } { 3 } \left[ \because \frac { 1 } { 2 } = \cos \frac { \pi } { 3 } \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \theta = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question , 

{tex} | \vec { a } | = 3 , {/tex}

{tex}| \vec { b } | = 5 \text { and } {/tex}

{tex}| \vec { c } | = 7.{/tex}

Let the angle between {tex} \vec { a }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b }{/tex} is {tex}\theta.{/tex}
Also, {tex} \vec { a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } = 0 \Rightarrow \vec { a } + \vec { b } = - \vec { c }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad ( \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) ^ { 2 } = ( - \vec { c } ) ^ { 2 }{/tex} [squaring both sides]
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad ( \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) .( \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) = ( - \vec { c } ) \cdot ( - \vec { c } ){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \vec { a }. \vec { a } + \vec { a }. \vec { b } + \vec { b } . \vec { a } + \vec { b } .\vec { b } = \vec { c } .\vec { c }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 } + \vec { a } .\vec { b } + \vec { a } .\vec { b } + | \vec { b } | ^ { 2 } = | \vec { c } | ^ { 2 }{/tex}{tex} \left[ \because \vec { x } .\vec { x } = | \vec { x } | ^ { 2 }\right.{/tex} and {tex} \vec { a }. \vec { b } = \vec { b } \cdot \vec { a } \Big]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 } + 2 \vec { a }. \vec { b } + | \vec { b } | ^ { 2 } = | \vec { c } | ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 } + 2 | \vec { a } | | \vec { b } | \cos \theta{/tex}{tex} + | \vec { b } | ^ { 2 } = | \vec { c } | ^ { 2 } \quad \ldots ( \mathrm { i } ){/tex}{tex} [ \because \vec { a } .\vec { b } = | \vec { a } | | \vec { b } | \cos \theta ]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } + 2 \times 3 \times 5 \cos \theta + ( 5 ) ^ { 2 } = ( 7 ) ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad 9 + 30 \cos \theta + 25 = 49{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad 30 \cos \theta = 49 - 34{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \frac { 15 } { 30 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \cos \frac { \pi } { 3 } \left[ \because \frac { 1 } { 2 } = \cos \frac { \pi } { 3 } \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \theta = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex}

question_html: The scalar product of the vector {tex}\hat i + \hat j + \hat k{/tex} with a unit vector along the sum of vectors {tex}2\hat i + 4\hat j - 5\hat k,{/tex} and {tex}\lambda \hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} is equal the one. Find the value of {tex}\lambda {/tex}.
question_text: The scalar product of the vector {tex}\hat i + \hat j + \hat k{/tex} with a unit vector along the sum of vectors {tex}2\hat i + 4\hat j - 5\hat k,{/tex} and {tex}\lambda \hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} is equal the one. Find the value of {tex}\lambda {/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i + 4\hat j - 5\hat k{/tex} {tex}\vec b = \lambda \hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}\vec a + \vec b = \left( {2 + \lambda } \right)\hat i + 6\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} Unit vector along {tex}\vec a + \vec b = \frac{{\vec a + \vec b}}{{\left| {\vec a + \vec b} \right|}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)\hat i + 6\hat j - 2\hat k}}{{\sqrt {{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + {{\left( 6 \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 2} \right)}^2}} }}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)\hat i + 6\hat j - 2\hat k}}{{\sqrt {{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + 40} }}{/tex} According to question, {tex}\vec c.\left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right) = 1{/tex} {tex}\left( {\hat i + \hat j + \hat k} \right).\left( {\frac{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)\hat i + 6\hat j - 2\hat k}}{{{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + 40}}} \right) = 1{/tex} {tex}\frac{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right) + 6 - 2}}{{\sqrt {{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + 40} }} = 1{/tex} {tex}2 + \lambda + 4 = \sqrt {{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + 40} {/tex} Squaring on both sides, {tex}{\lambda ^2} + 36 + 12\lambda = {\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)^2} + 40{/tex} {tex}\lambda = 1{/tex}
solution: {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i + 4\hat j - 5\hat k{/tex} {tex}\vec b = \lambda \hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}\vec a + \vec b = \left( {2 + \lambda } \right)\hat i + 6\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} Unit vector along {tex}\vec a + \vec b = \frac{{\vec a + \vec b}}{{\left| {\vec a + \vec b} \right|}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)\hat i + 6\hat j - 2\hat k}}{{\sqrt {{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + {{\left( 6 \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 2} \right)}^2}} }}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)\hat i + 6\hat j - 2\hat k}}{{\sqrt {{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + 40} }}{/tex} According to question, {tex}\vec c.\left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right) = 1{/tex} {tex}\left( {\hat i + \hat j + \hat k} \right).\left( {\frac{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)\hat i + 6\hat j - 2\hat k}}{{{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + 40}}} \right) = 1{/tex} {tex}\frac{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right) + 6 - 2}}{{\sqrt {{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + 40} }} = 1{/tex} {tex}2 + \lambda + 4 = \sqrt {{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + 40} {/tex} Squaring on both sides, {tex}{\lambda ^2} + 36 + 12\lambda = {\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)^2} + 40{/tex} {tex}\lambda = 1{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\vec a = 2\hat i + 4\hat j - 5\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\vec b = \lambda \hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\vec a + \vec b = \left( {2 + \lambda } \right)\hat i + 6\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex}
Unit vector along
{tex}\vec a + \vec b = \frac{{\vec a + \vec b}}{{\left| {\vec a + \vec b} \right|}}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)\hat i + 6\hat j - 2\hat k}}{{\sqrt {{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + {{\left( 6 \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 2} \right)}^2}} }}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)\hat i + 6\hat j - 2\hat k}}{{\sqrt {{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + 40} }}{/tex}
According to question, {tex}\vec c.\left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right) = 1{/tex}
{tex}\left( {\hat i + \hat j + \hat k} \right).\left( {\frac{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)\hat i + 6\hat j - 2\hat k}}{{{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + 40}}} \right) = 1{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right) + 6 - 2}}{{\sqrt {{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + 40} }} = 1{/tex}
{tex}2 + \lambda + 4 = \sqrt {{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + 40} {/tex}
Squaring on both sides,
{tex}{\lambda ^2} + 36 + 12\lambda = {\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)^2} + 40{/tex}
{tex}\lambda = 1{/tex}

question_html: Find a vector in the direction of vector {tex}2 \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex} which has magnitude 21 units.
question_text: Find a vector in the direction of vector {tex}2 \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex} which has magnitude 21 units.
explanation: We have to find a vector in the direction of vector {tex}2 \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex} which has magnitude 21 units. Let {tex}\vec { a } = 2 \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex} Then, {tex}| \vec { a } | = \sqrt { ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 3 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 4 + 9 + 36 } = \sqrt { 49 }{/tex} = 7 units Now unit vector in the direction of the given vectors {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} is given as {tex}\hat { a } = \frac { \vec { a } } { | \vec { a } | } = \frac { 1 } { 7 } ( 2 \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 } { 7 } \hat { i } - \frac { 3 } { 7 } \hat { j } + \frac { 6 } { 7 } \hat { k }{/tex} Now, the vector of magnitude equal to 21 units and in the direction of {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} is given by {tex}21 \hat { a } = 21 \left( \frac { 2 } { 7 } \hat { i } - \frac { 3 } { 7 } \hat { j } + \frac { 6 } { 7 } \hat { k } \right) = 6 \hat { i } - 9 \hat { j} + 18 \hat { k }{/tex}
solution: We have to find a vector in the direction of vector {tex}2 \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex} which has magnitude 21 units. solution_html:

Now unit vector in the direction of the given vectors {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} is given as

question_html: Find a vector {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} of magnitude {tex}5 \sqrt { 2 }{/tex}, making an angle of {tex}\frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex}with X-axis, {tex}\frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex}with Y-axis and an acute angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with Z-axis.
question_text: Find a vector {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} of magnitude {tex}5 \sqrt { 2 }{/tex} , making an angle of {tex}\frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex} with X-axis, {tex}\frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} with Y-axis and an acute angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with Z-axis.
explanation: Here, we have to find the vector of magnitude {tex}5 \sqrt { 2 }{/tex} making the given angles with the three axes respectively. Given that l = cos {tex}\frac { \pi } { 4 },{/tex} m = cos {tex}\frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} and n= cos {tex} \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow l = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } , m = 0 \text { and } n = \cos \theta{/tex} We know that, l 2 + m 2 + n 2 = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow \left( \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } + ( 0 ) ^ { 2 } + n ^ { 2 } = 1 \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 2 } + n ^ { 2 } = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad n ^ { 2 } = 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad n = \pm \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \Rightarrow n = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} [ {tex} \because \theta{/tex} is an acute angle with Z-axis] {tex}\therefore \quad \cos \theta = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \theta = \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex} Thus, direction cosines of a line are {tex}\frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } , 0 , \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} . {tex}\therefore{/tex} Vector {tex}\vec { a } = | \vec { a } | ( \hat { i } + m \hat { j } + n \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= 5 \sqrt { 2 } \left( \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \hat { i } + ( 0 ) \hat { j } + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \hat { k } \right) = \ { 5 } \hat { i} + 5 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}[ \because | \vec { a } | = 5 \sqrt { 2 } \text { given } ]{/tex}
solution: Here, we have to find the vector of magnitude {tex}5 \sqrt { 2 }{/tex} making the given angles with the three axes respectively. Given that l = cos {tex}\frac { \pi } { 4 },{/tex} m = cos {tex}\frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} and n= cos {tex} \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow l = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } , m = 0 \text { and } n = \cos \theta{/tex} We know that, l 2 + m 2 + n 2 = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow \left( \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } + ( 0 ) ^ { 2 } + n ^ { 2 } = 1 \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 2 } + n ^ { 2 } = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad n ^ { 2 } = 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad n = \pm \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \Rightarrow n = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} [ {tex} \because \theta{/tex} is an acute angle with Z-axis] {tex}\therefore \quad \cos \theta = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \theta = \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex} Thus, direction cosines of a line are {tex}\frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } , 0 , \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} . {tex}\therefore{/tex} Vector {tex}\vec { a } = | \vec { a } | ( \hat { i } + m \hat { j } + n \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= 5 \sqrt { 2 } \left( \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \hat { i } + ( 0 ) \hat { j } + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \hat { k } \right) = \ { 5 } \hat { i} + 5 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}[ \because | \vec { a } | = 5 \sqrt { 2 } \text { given } ]{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, we have to find the vector  of magnitude

{tex}5 \sqrt { 2 }{/tex}  making the given angles with the three axes respectively. Given that  l = cos{tex}\frac { \pi } { 4 },{/tex} m = cos {tex}\frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex}and n= cos {tex} \theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow l = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } , m = 0 \text { and } n = \cos \theta{/tex}
We know that, l2 + m2 + n2 = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow \left( \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } + ( 0 ) ^ { 2 } + n ^ { 2 } = 1 \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 2 } + n ^ { 2 } = 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad n ^ { 2 } = 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad n = \pm \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \Rightarrow n = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex}
[{tex} \because \theta{/tex} is an acute angle with Z-axis]
{tex}\therefore \quad \cos \theta = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \theta = \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex}
Thus, direction cosines of a line are{tex}\frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } , 0 , \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} .
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Vector {tex}\vec { a } = | \vec { a } | ( \hat { i } + m \hat { j } + n \hat { k } ){/tex}
{tex}= 5 \sqrt { 2 } \left( \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \hat { i } + ( 0 ) \hat { j } + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \hat { k } \right) = \ { 5 } \hat { i} + 5 \hat { k }{/tex}
{tex}[ \because | \vec { a } | = 5 \sqrt { 2 } \text { given } ]{/tex}

question_html: Find the value of p for which the vectors {tex}3 \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 9 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\hat { i } - 2 p \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} are parallel.
question_text: Find the value of p for which the vectors {tex}3 \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 9 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\hat { i } - 2 p \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} are parallel.
explanation: {tex}3 \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 9 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\hat { i } - 2 p \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} are two parallel vectors, so their direction ratios will be proportional. {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { 3 } { 1 } = \frac { 2 } { - 2 p } = \frac { 9 } { 3 } \Rightarrow \frac { 2 } { - 2 p } = \frac { 3 } { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - 6 p = 2 \Rightarrow p = \frac { 2 } { - 6 } \Rightarrow p = - \frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex}
solution: {tex}3 \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 9 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\hat { i } - 2 p \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} are two parallel vectors, so their direction ratios will be proportional. {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { 3 } { 1 } = \frac { 2 } { - 2 p } = \frac { 9 } { 3 } \Rightarrow \frac { 2 } { - 2 p } = \frac { 3 } { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - 6 p = 2 \Rightarrow p = \frac { 2 } { - 6 } \Rightarrow p = - \frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}3 \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 9 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\hat { i } - 2 p \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} are two parallel vectors, so their direction ratios will be proportional.
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac { 3 } { 1 } = \frac { 2 } { - 2 p } = \frac { 9 } { 3 } \Rightarrow \frac { 2 } { - 2 p } = \frac { 3 } { 1 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow - 6 p = 2 \Rightarrow p = \frac { 2 } { - 6 } \Rightarrow p = - \frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex}

solution: We have to find the value of cosine of the angle which the vector given, {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} makes with Y-axis .Now, unit vector in the direction of {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} is {tex}\hat { a } = \frac { \vec { a } } { | \vec { a } | } = \frac { \hat { i } + \hat { j} + \hat { k } } { \sqrt { ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 3 } } =\frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \hat { i } + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \hat { j } + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Cosine of the angle which given vector makes with Y-axis is {tex}\frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } }.{/tex}
solution_html:

question_text: Find the angle between X-axis and the vector {tex}\hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k }.{/tex}
explanation: Here, we have to find angle between the vector {tex} \hat{a}=\hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and X- axis. Now, unit vector in the direction of {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} is {tex}\hat { a } = \frac { \vec { a } } { | \vec { a } | } = \frac { \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 1 ^ { 2 } + 1 ^ { 2 } + 1 ^ { 2 } } } \Rightarrow \hat { a } = \frac { \hat { i } + \hat { j } } { \sqrt { 3 } } + \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \hat { a } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \hat { i } + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \hat { j } + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \hat { k }{/tex} So, angle between X-axis and the vector {tex}\hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } \text { is } \cos \alpha = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \cos ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right){/tex} {tex}[ \because \hat { a } = l\hat { i } + m \hat { j } + n \hat { k } \text { and } \cos \alpha = l{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow \alpha = \cos ^ { - 1 } l {/tex}
solution: Here, we have to find angle between the vector {tex} \hat{a}=\hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and X- axis. Now, unit vector in the direction of {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} is {tex}\hat { a } = \frac { \vec { a } } { | \vec { a } | } = \frac { \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 1 ^ { 2 } + 1 ^ { 2 } + 1 ^ { 2 } } } \Rightarrow \hat { a } = \frac { \hat { i } + \hat { j } } { \sqrt { 3 } } + \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \hat { a } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \hat { i } + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \hat { j } + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \hat { k }{/tex} So, angle between X-axis and the vector {tex}\hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } \text { is } \cos \alpha = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \cos ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right){/tex} {tex}[ \because \hat { a } = l\hat { i } + m \hat { j } + n \hat { k } \text { and } \cos \alpha = l{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow \alpha = \cos ^ { - 1 } l {/tex}
solution_html:
Here, we have to find angle between the vector {tex} \hat{a}=\hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex}  and X- axis.
Now, unit vector in the direction of {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} is
{tex}\hat { a } = \frac { \vec { a } } { | \vec { a } | } = \frac { \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 1 ^ { 2 } + 1 ^ { 2 } + 1 ^ { 2 } } } \Rightarrow \hat { a } = \frac { \hat { i } + \hat { j } } { \sqrt { 3 } } + \hat { k }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \hat { a } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \hat { i } + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \hat { j } + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \hat { k }{/tex}
So, angle between X-axis and the vector
{tex}\hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } \text { is } \cos \alpha = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \cos ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right){/tex}
{tex}[ \because \hat { a } = l\hat { i } + m \hat { j } + n \hat { k } \text { and } \cos \alpha = l{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha = \cos ^ { - 1 } l {/tex}

question_text: Write a unit vector in the direction of vector {tex}\vec { P Q },{/tex} where {tex}\vec { P }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { Q }{/tex} are the points (1, 3, 0) and ( 4, 5, 6), respectively.
explanation: Here, we are given the points which are {tex}\vec { P } ( 1,3,0 ) {/tex} and {tex}\vec { Q }{/tex} (4, 5, 6). Now, we have, x 1 = 1, y 1 = 3, z 1 = 0 and x 2 = 4, y 2 = 5, z 2 = 6 So, vector would be equal to {tex}\vec { P Q } = \left( x _ { 2 } - x _ { 1 } \right) \hat { i } + \left( y _ { 2 } - y _ { 1 } \right) \hat { j } + \left( z _ { 2 } - z _ { 1 } \right) \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}= ( 4 - 1 ) \hat { i } + ( 5 - 3 ) \hat { j } + ( 6 - 0 ) \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}= 3 \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Magnitude of given vector {tex}| \vec { P Q } | = \sqrt { 3 ^ { 2 } + 2 ^ { 2 } + 6 ^ { 2 } } = \sqrt { 9 + 4 + 36 }{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 49 } = 7{/tex} units Hence, the unit vector in the direction of {tex}\vec { P Q }{/tex} is given by {tex}\frac { \vec { P Q } } { | \vec { P Q } | } = \frac { { 3 } \hat { i} + 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } } { 7 } = \frac { 3 } { 7 } \hat { i } + \frac { 2 } { 7 } \hat { j } + \frac { 6 } { 7 } \hat { k }{/tex}
solution: Here, we are given the points which are {tex}\vec { P } ( 1,3,0 ) {/tex} and {tex}\vec { Q }{/tex} (4, 5, 6). Now, we have, x 1 = 1, y 1 = 3, z 1 = 0 and x 2 = 4, y 2 = 5, z 2 = 6 So, vector would be equal to {tex}\vec { P Q } = \left( x _ { 2 } - x _ { 1 } \right) \hat { i } + \left( y _ { 2 } - y _ { 1 } \right) \hat { j } + \left( z _ { 2 } - z _ { 1 } \right) \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}= ( 4 - 1 ) \hat { i } + ( 5 - 3 ) \hat { j } + ( 6 - 0 ) \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}= 3 \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Magnitude of given vector {tex}| \vec { P Q } | = \sqrt { 3 ^ { 2 } + 2 ^ { 2 } + 6 ^ { 2 } } = \sqrt { 9 + 4 + 36 }{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 49 } = 7{/tex} units Hence, the unit vector in the direction of {tex}\vec { P Q }{/tex} is given by {tex}\frac { \vec { P Q } } { | \vec { P Q } | } = \frac { { 3 } \hat { i} + 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } } { 7 } = \frac { 3 } { 7 } \hat { i } + \frac { 2 } { 7 } \hat { j } + \frac { 6 } { 7 } \hat { k }{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, we are given the points  which are{tex}\vec { P } ( 1,3,0 ) {/tex} and {tex}\vec { Q }{/tex}(4, 5, 6).
Now, we have, x1 = 1, y1 = 3, z1 = 0
and x2 = 4, y2 = 5, z2 = 6
So, vector would be equal to{tex}\vec { P Q } = \left( x _ { 2 } - x _ { 1 } \right) \hat { i } + \left( y _ { 2 } - y _ { 1 } \right) \hat { j } + \left( z _ { 2 } - z _ { 1 } \right) \hat { k }{/tex}
{tex}= ( 4 - 1 ) \hat { i } + ( 5 - 3 ) \hat { j } + ( 6 - 0 ) \hat { k }{/tex}
{tex}= 3 \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Magnitude of given vector
{tex}| \vec { P Q } | = \sqrt { 3 ^ { 2 } + 2 ^ { 2 } + 6 ^ { 2 } } = \sqrt { 9 + 4 + 36 }{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt { 49 } = 7{/tex}units
Hence, the unit vector in the direction of {tex}\vec { P Q }{/tex} is given by
{tex}\frac { \vec { P Q } } { | \vec { P Q } | } = \frac { { 3 } \hat { i} + 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } } { 7 } = \frac { 3 } { 7 } \hat { i } + \frac { 2 } { 7 } \hat { j } + \frac { 6 } { 7 } \hat { k }{/tex}
