question_html: If {tex}\vec { a } = x \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } - z \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } = 3 \hat { i } - y \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} are two equal vectors, then write the value of x + y + z.
question_text: If {tex}\vec { a } = x \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } - z \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } = 3 \hat { i } - y \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} are two equal vectors, then write the value of x + y + z.
explanation: Given, {tex}\vec { a } = \vec { b }{/tex} . Note that two vectors are equal if and only if their corresponding components are equal. {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \hat { xi } + 2 \hat { j } - z \hat { k } = \hat { 3 i} - y \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} On comparing the coefficient of components, we get x = 3, y = -2, z = -1 Now, x + y + z = 3 - 2 - 1 = 0
solution: Given, {tex}\vec { a } = \vec { b }{/tex} . Note that two vectors are equal if and only if their corresponding components are equal. {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \hat { xi } + 2 \hat { j } - z \hat { k } = \hat { 3 i} - y \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} On comparing the coefficient of components, we get x = 3, y = -2, z = -1 Now, x + y + z = 3 - 2 - 1 = 0
solution_html:
Given, {tex}\vec { a } = \vec { b }{/tex}. Note that two vectors are equal if and only if their corresponding components are equal.
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \hat { xi } + 2 \hat { j } - z \hat { k } = \hat { 3 i} - y \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex}
On comparing the coefficient of components, we get
x = 3, y = -2, z = -1
Now, x + y + z = 3 - 2 - 1 = 0

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A and B are two points with position vectors {tex}2 \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}6 \vec { b } - \vec { a },{/tex} respectively. Write the position vector of a point P which divides the line segment AB internally in the ratio 1 : 2.
question_text: A and B are two points with position vectors {tex}2 \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}6 \vec { b } - \vec { a },{/tex} respectively. Write the position vector of a point P which divides the line segment AB internally in the ratio 1 : 2.
explanation: Given, A and B are two points with position vectors {tex}2 \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b } \text { and } 6 \vec { b } - \vec { a }{/tex} respectively Also, point P divides the line segment AB in the ratio 1 : 2 internally. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Position vector of a point P dividing the line joining A and B internally in the ratio of 1 : 2 is given by: {tex}= \frac { 1 \times ( 6 \vec { b } - \vec { a } ) + 2 \times ( 2 \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b } ) } { 1 + 2 }{/tex} [by internal section formula] {tex}= \frac { 6 \vec { b } - \vec { a } + 4 \vec { a } - 6 \vec { b } } { 3 } = \frac { 3 \vec { a } } { 3 } = \vec { a }{/tex}
solution: Given, A and B are two points with position vectors {tex}2 \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b } \text { and } 6 \vec { b } - \vec { a }{/tex} respectively Also, point P divides the line segment AB in the ratio 1 : 2 internally. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Position vector of a point P dividing the line joining A and B internally in the ratio of 1 : 2 is given by: {tex}= \frac { 1 \times ( 6 \vec { b } - \vec { a } ) + 2 \times ( 2 \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b } ) } { 1 + 2 }{/tex} [by internal section formula] {tex}= \frac { 6 \vec { b } - \vec { a } + 4 \vec { a } - 6 \vec { b } } { 3 } = \frac { 3 \vec { a } } { 3 } = \vec { a }{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, A and B are two points with position vectors {tex}2 \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b } \text { and } 6 \vec { b } - \vec { a }{/tex} respectively Also, point P divides the line segment AB in the ratio 1 : 2 internally.

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Position vector of a point P dividing the line joining A and B internally in the ratio of 1 : 2 is given by:
{tex}= \frac { 1 \times ( 6 \vec { b } - \vec { a } ) + 2 \times ( 2 \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b } ) } { 1 + 2 }{/tex} [by internal section formula]
{tex}= \frac { 6 \vec { b } - \vec { a } + 4 \vec { a } - 6 \vec { b } } { 3 } = \frac { 3 \vec { a } } { 3 } = \vec { a }{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} are two vectors such that {tex}| \vec { a } + \vec { b } | = | \vec { a } |,{/tex}then prove that vector {tex}2 \vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} is perpendicular to vector b.
question_text: If {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} are two vectors such that {tex}| \vec { a } + \vec { b } | = | \vec { a } |,{/tex} then prove that vector {tex}2 \vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} is perpendicular to vector b.
explanation: We need to prove that vector {tex}2 \vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} is perpendicular to vector b. i.e {tex}( 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) \perp \vec { b }{/tex} Given, {tex}| \vec { a } + \vec { b } | = | \vec { a } |{/tex} On squaring both sides, we get {tex}| \vec { a } + \vec { b } | ^ { 2 } = | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 } + 2 \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } + | \vec { b } | ^ { 2 } = | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \vec { a }. \vec { b } + \vec { b }. \vec { b } = 0 \quad \left[ \because | \vec { x } | ^ { 2 } = \vec { x } \cdot \vec { x } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) \cdot \vec { b } = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad ( 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) \perp \vec { b }{/tex} {tex}[ \because \text { if } \vec { a } \perp \vec { b } \Leftrightarrow \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = 0 ]{/tex}
solution: We need to prove that vector {tex}2 \vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} is perpendicular to vector b. i.e {tex}( 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) \perp \vec { b }{/tex} Given, {tex}| \vec { a } + \vec { b } | = | \vec { a } |{/tex} On squaring both sides, we get {tex}| \vec { a } + \vec { b } | ^ { 2 } = | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 } + 2 \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } + | \vec { b } | ^ { 2 } = | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \vec { a }. \vec { b } + \vec { b }. \vec { b } = 0 \quad \left[ \because | \vec { x } | ^ { 2 } = \vec { x } \cdot \vec { x } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) \cdot \vec { b } = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad ( 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) \perp \vec { b }{/tex} {tex}[ \because \text { if } \vec { a } \perp \vec { b } \Leftrightarrow \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = 0 ]{/tex}
solution_html:
We need to prove that vector {tex}2 \vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} is perpendicular to vector b. i.e {tex}( 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) \perp \vec { b }{/tex}
Given, {tex}| \vec { a } + \vec { b } | = | \vec { a } |{/tex}
On squaring both sides, we get
{tex}| \vec { a } + \vec { b } | ^ { 2 } = | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 } + 2 \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } + | \vec { b } | ^ { 2 } = | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \vec { a }. \vec { b } + \vec { b }. \vec { b } = 0 \quad \left[ \because | \vec { x } | ^ { 2 } = \vec { x } \cdot \vec { x } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) \cdot \vec { b } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad ( 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) \perp \vec { b }{/tex}
{tex}[ \because \text { if } \vec { a } \perp \vec { b } \Leftrightarrow \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = 0 ]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find {tex}| \vec { x } |{/tex}, if for a unit Vector {tex}\hat { { a } }{/tex} {tex}(\vec { x}-\vec{a} ) \cdot ( \vec { x } + \vec { a } ) = 15.{/tex}
question_text: Find {tex}| \vec { x } |{/tex} , if for a unit Vector {tex}\hat { { a } }{/tex} {tex}(\vec { x}-\vec{a} ) \cdot ( \vec { x } + \vec { a } ) = 15.{/tex}
explanation: Here,we have to find {tex}| \vec { x } |.{/tex} Given , {tex}\hat { a }{/tex} is a unit vector. Then, {tex}| \hat { a } | = 1.{/tex} Now, we are given that dot product is equal to {tex}{( \vec { x } } - \vec { a } ). { ( \vec { x } } +\vec { a } ) = 15{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec { x } \cdot \vec { x } - \vec { a } \cdot \vec { x } + \vec { x } \cdot \vec { a } - \vec { a } \cdot \vec { a } = 15{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec { x } \cdot \vec { x } - \vec{ a } \cdot \vec { x } + \vec{ a } \cdot \vec { x } - \vec{ a } \cdot \vec{ a } = 15{/tex} [ {tex}\therefore{/tex} scalar product is commutative, i.e. {tex}\vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = \vec { b } \cdot \vec { a } ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \vec { x } | ^ { 2 } - | \vec{ a } | ^ { 2 } = 15 \quad \left[ \because \vec { z } \cdot \vec { z } = | \vec { z } | ^ { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \vec { x } | ^{ 2 } - 1 = 15 \quad [ \mathrm { given } , | \hat { a } | = 1 ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \vec { x } | ^ { 2 } = 16{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad | \vec { x } | = 4{/tex} [ {tex} \because{/tex} length cannot be negative]
solution: Here,we have to find {tex}| \vec { x } |.{/tex} Given , {tex}\hat { a }{/tex} is a unit vector. Then, {tex}| \hat { a } | = 1.{/tex} Now, we are given that dot product is equal to {tex}{( \vec { x } } - \vec { a } ). { ( \vec { x } } +\vec { a } ) = 15{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec { x } \cdot \vec { x } - \vec { a } \cdot \vec { x } + \vec { x } \cdot \vec { a } - \vec { a } \cdot \vec { a } = 15{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec { x } \cdot \vec { x } - \vec{ a } \cdot \vec { x } + \vec{ a } \cdot \vec { x } - \vec{ a } \cdot \vec{ a } = 15{/tex} [ {tex}\therefore{/tex} scalar product is commutative, i.e. {tex}\vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = \vec { b } \cdot \vec { a } ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \vec { x } | ^ { 2 } - | \vec{ a } | ^ { 2 } = 15 \quad \left[ \because \vec { z } \cdot \vec { z } = | \vec { z } | ^ { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \vec { x } | ^{ 2 } - 1 = 15 \quad [ \mathrm { given } , | \hat { a } | = 1 ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \vec { x } | ^ { 2 } = 16{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad | \vec { x } | = 4{/tex} [ {tex} \because{/tex} length cannot be negative]
solution_html:
Here,we have to find {tex}| \vec { x } |.{/tex} Given ,{tex}\hat { a }{/tex} is a unit vector. Then, {tex}| \hat { a } | = 1.{/tex}
Now, we are given that dot product is equal to{tex}{( \vec { x } } - \vec { a } ). { ( \vec { x } } +\vec { a } ) = 15{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec { x } \cdot \vec { x } - \vec { a } \cdot \vec { x } + \vec { x } \cdot \vec { a } - \vec { a } \cdot \vec { a } = 15{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec { x } \cdot \vec { x } - \vec{ a } \cdot \vec { x } + \vec{ a } \cdot \vec { x } - \vec{ a } \cdot \vec{ a } = 15{/tex}
[{tex}\therefore{/tex} scalar product is commutative, i.e. {tex}\vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = \vec { b } \cdot \vec { a } ]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \vec { x } | ^ { 2 } - | \vec{ a } | ^ { 2 } = 15 \quad \left[ \because \vec { z } \cdot \vec { z } = | \vec { z } | ^ { 2 } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \vec { x } | ^{ 2 } - 1 = 15 \quad [ \mathrm { given } , | \hat { a } | = 1 ]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \vec { x } | ^ { 2 } = 16{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad | \vec { x } | = 4{/tex}
[{tex} \because{/tex} length cannot be negative]

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the values of {tex}\lambda{/tex} for which the angle between the vectors {tex}\vec { a } = 2 \lambda ^ { 2 } \hat { i } + 4 \lambda \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } = 7 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + \lambda \hat { k }{/tex} is obtuse.
question_text: Find the values of {tex}\lambda{/tex} for which the angle between the vectors {tex}\vec { a } = 2 \lambda ^ { 2 } \hat { i } + 4 \lambda \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } = 7 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + \lambda \hat { k }{/tex} is obtuse.
explanation: According to the question, {tex}\vec { a } = 2 \lambda ^ { 2 } \hat { i } + 4 \lambda \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } = 7 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + \lambda \hat { k }{/tex} Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the obtuse angle between the vectors. Then, {tex}\cos \theta = \frac { \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } } { | \vec { a } | | \vec { b } | }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \frac { 14 \lambda ^ { 2 } - 8 \lambda + \lambda } { \sqrt { 4 \lambda ^ { 4 } + 16 \lambda ^ { 2 } + 1 } \sqrt { 49 + 4 + \lambda ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} since {tex} \theta{/tex} is obtuse, {tex}\therefore \quad \cos \theta < 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 14 \lambda ^ { 2 } - 7 \lambda } { \sqrt { 4 \lambda ^ { 4 } + 16 \lambda ^ { 2 } + 1 } \sqrt { 53 + \lambda ^ { 2 } } } < 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 14 \lambda ^ { 2 } - 7 \lambda < 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \lambda ^ { 2 } - \lambda < 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda ( 2 \lambda - 1 ) < 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 0 < \lambda < \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda \in \left( 0 , \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right){/tex}
solution: According to the question, {tex}\vec { a } = 2 \lambda ^ { 2 } \hat { i } + 4 \lambda \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } = 7 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + \lambda \hat { k }{/tex} Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the obtuse angle between the vectors. Then, {tex}\cos \theta = \frac { \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } } { | \vec { a } | | \vec { b } | }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \frac { 14 \lambda ^ { 2 } - 8 \lambda + \lambda } { \sqrt { 4 \lambda ^ { 4 } + 16 \lambda ^ { 2 } + 1 } \sqrt { 49 + 4 + \lambda ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} since {tex} \theta{/tex} is obtuse, {tex}\therefore \quad \cos \theta < 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 14 \lambda ^ { 2 } - 7 \lambda } { \sqrt { 4 \lambda ^ { 4 } + 16 \lambda ^ { 2 } + 1 } \sqrt { 53 + \lambda ^ { 2 } } } < 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 14 \lambda ^ { 2 } - 7 \lambda < 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \lambda ^ { 2 } - \lambda < 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda ( 2 \lambda - 1 ) < 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 0 < \lambda < \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda \in \left( 0 , \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right){/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}\vec { a } = 2 \lambda ^ { 2 } \hat { i } + 4 \lambda \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and 

{tex}\vec { b } = 7 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + \lambda \hat { k }{/tex}

Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the obtuse angle between the vectors.
Then, {tex}\cos \theta = \frac { \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } } { | \vec { a } | | \vec { b } | }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \frac { 14 \lambda ^ { 2 } - 8 \lambda + \lambda } { \sqrt { 4 \lambda ^ { 4 } + 16 \lambda ^ { 2 } + 1 } \sqrt { 49 + 4 + \lambda ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
since {tex} \theta{/tex} is obtuse,
{tex}\therefore \quad \cos \theta < 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 14 \lambda ^ { 2 } - 7 \lambda } { \sqrt { 4 \lambda ^ { 4 } + 16 \lambda ^ { 2 } + 1 } \sqrt { 53 + \lambda ^ { 2 } } } < 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 14 \lambda ^ { 2 } - 7 \lambda < 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \lambda ^ { 2 } - \lambda < 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda ( 2 \lambda - 1 ) < 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 0 < \lambda < \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda \in \left( 0 , \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}\vec { a } = 3 \hat { i } - \hat { j }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - 3 \hat { k },{/tex} then express {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} in the form {tex}\vec { b } = \vec { b } _ { 1 } + \vec { b } _ { 2 },{/tex} where {tex}\vec { b } _ { 1 } \left\| \vec { a } \text { and } \vec { b } _ { 2 } \perp \vec { a }\right..{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}\vec { a } = 3 \hat { i } - \hat { j }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - 3 \hat { k },{/tex} then express {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} in the form {tex}\vec { b } = \vec { b } _ { 1 } + \vec { b } _ { 2 },{/tex} where {tex}\vec { b } _ { 1 } \left\| \vec { a } \text { and } \vec { b } _ { 2 } \perp \vec { a }\right..{/tex}
explanation: According to the question, {tex}\vec { a } = 3 \hat { i } - \hat { j } {/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } + \hat {j } - 3 \hat { k }{/tex} Let {tex}\vec { b _ { 1 } } = x _ { 1 } \hat {i } + y _ { 1 } \hat { j } + z _ { 1 } \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b _ { 2 } } = x _ { 2 } \hat {i} + y _ { 2 } \hat { j} + z _ { 2 } \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\vec { b _ { 1 } } + \vec { b _ { 2 } } = \vec { b } , \vec { b _ { 1 } } \| \vec { a }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b _ { 2 } } \perp \vec { a }.{/tex} Consider, {tex}\vec { b _ { 1 } } + \vec { b _ { 2 } } = \vec { b }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left( x _ { 1 } + x _ { 2 } \right) \hat { i } + \left( y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } \right) \hat { j } + \left( z _ { 1 } + z _ { 2 } \right) \hat { k } = 2 \hat { i} + \hat { j } - 3 \hat { k }{/tex} On comparing the coefficient of {tex}\hat { i } , \hat { \jmath }{/tex} and {tex}\hat { k }{/tex} both sides; we get {tex}\Rightarrow x_1 + x_2 = 2{/tex} ...(i) {tex}y_1 + y_2 = 1 {/tex} ...(ii) and {tex}z_1 + z_2 =-3{/tex} ...(iii) Now, consider {tex}\vec { b _ { 1 } } \| \vec { a }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { x _ { 1 } } { 3 } = \frac { y _ { 1 } } { - 1 } = \frac { z _ { 1 } } { 0 } = \lambda ( \mathrm { say } ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x _ { 1 } = 3 \lambda , y _ { 1 } = - \lambda \text { and } z _ { 1 } = 0{/tex} ...(iv) On substituting the values of x,y and z, form Eq. (iv) to Eq. (i), (ii) and (iii), respectively, we get {tex}x _ { 2 } = 2 - 3 \lambda , y _ { 2 } = 1 + \lambda \text { and } z _ { 2 } = - 3{/tex} ...(v) Since, {tex}\vec { b _ { 2 } } \perp \vec { a },{/tex} therefore {tex}\vec { b } _ { 2 } \cdot \vec { a } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 x _ { 2 } - y _ { 2 } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 ( 2 - 3 \lambda ) - ( 1 + \lambda ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 6 - 9 \lambda - 1 - \lambda = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 5 - 10 \lambda = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} On substituting {tex}\lambda = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} in Eqs. (iv) and Eqs. (iv) and (v), we get {tex}x _ { 1 } = \frac { 3 } { 2 } , y _ { 1 } = \frac { - 1 } { 2 } , z _ { 1 } = 0{/tex} and {tex}x _ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } , y _ { 2 } = \frac { 3 } { 2 } \text { and } z _ { 2 } = - 3{/tex} Hence, {tex}\vec b=\vec { b } _ { 1 } + \vec { b } _ { 2 } = \left( \frac { 3 } { 2 } \hat { i } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \hat { j } \right) + \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \hat {i } + \frac { 3 } { 2 } \hat { j } - 3 \hat { k } \right){/tex} {tex}= 2\hat { i } + \hat { j } - 3 \hat { k } {/tex}
solution: According to the question, {tex}\vec { a } = 3 \hat { i } - \hat { j } {/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } + \hat {j } - 3 \hat { k }{/tex} Let {tex}\vec { b _ { 1 } } = x _ { 1 } \hat {i } + y _ { 1 } \hat { j } + z _ { 1 } \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b _ { 2 } } = x _ { 2 } \hat {i} + y _ { 2 } \hat { j} + z _ { 2 } \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\vec { b _ { 1 } } + \vec { b _ { 2 } } = \vec { b } , \vec { b _ { 1 } } \| \vec { a }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b _ { 2 } } \perp \vec { a }.{/tex} Consider, {tex}\vec { b _ { 1 } } + \vec { b _ { 2 } } = \vec { b }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left( x _ { 1 } + x _ { 2 } \right) \hat { i } + \left( y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } \right) \hat { j } + \left( z _ { 1 } + z _ { 2 } \right) \hat { k } = 2 \hat { i} + \hat { j } - 3 \hat { k }{/tex} On comparing the coefficient of {tex}\hat { i } , \hat { \jmath }{/tex} and {tex}\hat { k }{/tex} both sides; we get {tex}\Rightarrow x_1 + x_2 = 2{/tex} ...(i) {tex}y_1 + y_2 = 1 {/tex} ...(ii) and {tex}z_1 + z_2 =-3{/tex} ...(iii) Now, consider {tex}\vec { b _ { 1 } } \| \vec { a }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { x _ { 1 } } { 3 } = \frac { y _ { 1 } } { - 1 } = \frac { z _ { 1 } } { 0 } = \lambda ( \mathrm { say } ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x _ { 1 } = 3 \lambda , y _ { 1 } = - \lambda \text { and } z _ { 1 } = 0{/tex} ...(iv) On substituting the values of x,y and z, form Eq. (iv) to Eq. (i), (ii) and (iii), respectively, we get {tex}x _ { 2 } = 2 - 3 \lambda , y _ { 2 } = 1 + \lambda \text { and } z _ { 2 } = - 3{/tex} ...(v) Since, {tex}\vec { b _ { 2 } } \perp \vec { a },{/tex} therefore {tex}\vec { b } _ { 2 } \cdot \vec { a } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 x _ { 2 } - y _ { 2 } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 ( 2 - 3 \lambda ) - ( 1 + \lambda ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 6 - 9 \lambda - 1 - \lambda = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 5 - 10 \lambda = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} On substituting {tex}\lambda = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} in Eqs. (iv) and Eqs. (iv) and (v), we get {tex}x _ { 1 } = \frac { 3 } { 2 } , y _ { 1 } = \frac { - 1 } { 2 } , z _ { 1 } = 0{/tex} and {tex}x _ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } , y _ { 2 } = \frac { 3 } { 2 } \text { and } z _ { 2 } = - 3{/tex} Hence, {tex}\vec b=\vec { b } _ { 1 } + \vec { b } _ { 2 } = \left( \frac { 3 } { 2 } \hat { i } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \hat { j } \right) + \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \hat {i } + \frac { 3 } { 2 } \hat { j } - 3 \hat { k } \right){/tex} {tex}= 2\hat { i } + \hat { j } - 3 \hat { k } {/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question,

 {tex}\vec { a } = 3 \hat { i } - \hat { j } {/tex} and 
{tex}\vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } + \hat {j } - 3 \hat { k }{/tex}
Let {tex}\vec { b _ { 1 } } = x _ { 1 } \hat {i } + y _ { 1 } \hat { j } + z _ { 1 } \hat { k }{/tex} and 
{tex}\vec { b _ { 2 } } = x _ { 2 } \hat {i} + y _ { 2 } \hat { j} + z _ { 2 } \hat { k }{/tex}
{tex}\vec { b _ { 1 } } + \vec { b _ { 2 } } = \vec { b } , \vec { b _ { 1 } } \| \vec { a }{/tex} and 
{tex}\vec { b _ { 2 } } \perp \vec { a }.{/tex}

Consider, {tex}\vec { b _ { 1 } } + \vec { b _ { 2 } } = \vec { b }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left( x _ { 1 } + x _ { 2 } \right) \hat { i } + \left( y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } \right) \hat { j } + \left( z _ { 1 } + z _ { 2 } \right) \hat { k } = 2 \hat { i} + \hat { j } - 3 \hat { k }{/tex}
On comparing the coefficient of {tex}\hat { i } , \hat { \jmath }{/tex}  and {tex}\hat { k }{/tex} both sides; we get
{tex}\Rightarrow x_1 + x_2 = 2{/tex}   ...(i)
{tex}y_1 + y_2 = 1  {/tex}...(ii)
and {tex}z_1 + z_2 =-3{/tex} ...(iii)
Now, consider {tex}\vec { b _ { 1 } } \| \vec { a }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { x _ { 1 } } { 3 } = \frac { y _ { 1 } } { - 1 } = \frac { z _ { 1 } } { 0 } = \lambda ( \mathrm { say } ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x _ { 1 } = 3 \lambda , y _ { 1 } = - \lambda \text { and } z _ { 1 } = 0{/tex} ...(iv)
On substituting the values of x,y and z, form Eq. (iv) to Eq. (i), (ii) and (iii), respectively, we get
{tex}x _ { 2 } = 2 - 3 \lambda , y _ { 2 } = 1 + \lambda \text { and } z _ { 2 } = - 3{/tex} ...(v)
Since, {tex}\vec { b _ { 2 } } \perp \vec { a },{/tex} therefore {tex}\vec { b } _ { 2 } \cdot \vec { a } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 x _ { 2 } - y _ { 2 } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 ( 2 - 3 \lambda ) - ( 1 + \lambda ) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 6 - 9 \lambda - 1 - \lambda = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 5 - 10 \lambda = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}
On substituting {tex}\lambda = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} in Eqs. (iv) and Eqs. (iv) and (v), we get
{tex}x _ { 1 } = \frac { 3 } { 2 } , y _ { 1 } = \frac { - 1 } { 2 } , z _ { 1 } = 0{/tex}
and {tex}x _ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } , y _ { 2 } = \frac { 3 } { 2 } \text { and } z _ { 2 } = - 3{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\vec b=\vec { b } _ { 1 } + \vec { b } _ { 2 } = \left( \frac { 3 } { 2 } \hat { i } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \hat { j } \right) + \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \hat {i } + \frac { 3 } { 2 } \hat { j } - 3 \hat { k } \right){/tex}
{tex}= 2\hat { i } + \hat { j } - 3 \hat { k } {/tex}

question_html: Dot product of a vector with vectors {tex}\hat { i } - \hat { j } + \hat { k } , 2\hat { i } + \hat { j } - 3 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} are respectively 4, 0 and 2. Find the vector.
question_text: Dot product of a vector with vectors {tex}\hat { i } - \hat { j } + \hat { k } , 2\hat { i } + \hat { j } - 3 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} are respectively 4, 0 and 2. Find the vector.
explanation: According to the question, {tex}\vec { b } = \hat { i } - \hat { j } + \hat { k } , {/tex} {tex}\vec { c } = 2\hat { i } + \hat { j } - 3 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { d } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} let the required vector is {tex}\vec { a } = a _ { 1 } \hat {i} + a _ { 2 } \hat { j } + a _ { 3 } \hat { k }{/tex} Given, {tex}\vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = 4 , \ \vec { a } \cdot \vec { c } = 0{/tex} and {tex}\vec { a } \cdot \vec { d } = 2{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec { a } .\vec { b } = 4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow a _ { 1 } - a _ { 2 } + a _ { 3 } = 4{/tex} ....(i) {tex}\vec { a }. \vec { c } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2a_1 + a_2 - 3a_3 = 0 {/tex} ...(ii) and {tex}\vec { a }. \vec { d } = 2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow a_1 + a_2 + a_3 = 2 {/tex} ...(iii) On subtracting Eq. (iii) from Eq. (i), we get {tex}-2a_2 = 2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow a_2 = -1{/tex} On substituting a 2 = -1 in Eq. (ii) and (iii), we get 2a 1 - 3a 3 = 1 ...(iv) a 1 + a 3 = 3 ...(v) On multiplying Eq. (v) by 3 and then adding with Eq. (iv), we get {tex}5a_1 = 1 + 9 = 10{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a 1 = 2 On substituting a 1 = 2 in Eq. (v), we get a 3 = 1 {tex}\therefore {/tex} the vector is {tex}\vec { a } = 2\hat { i } - \hat { j } + \hat { k }.{/tex}
solution: According to the question, {tex}\vec { b } = \hat { i } - \hat { j } + \hat { k } , {/tex} {tex}\vec { c } = 2\hat { i } + \hat { j } - 3 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { d } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} let the required vector is {tex}\vec { a } = a _ { 1 } \hat {i} + a _ { 2 } \hat { j } + a _ { 3 } \hat { k }{/tex} Given, {tex}\vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = 4 , \ \vec { a } \cdot \vec { c } = 0{/tex} and {tex}\vec { a } \cdot \vec { d } = 2{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec { a } .\vec { b } = 4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow a _ { 1 } - a _ { 2 } + a _ { 3 } = 4{/tex} ....(i) {tex}\vec { a }. \vec { c } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2a_1 + a_2 - 3a_3 = 0 {/tex} ...(ii) and {tex}\vec { a }. \vec { d } = 2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow a_1 + a_2 + a_3 = 2 {/tex} ...(iii) On subtracting Eq. (iii) from Eq. (i), we get {tex}-2a_2 = 2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow a_2 = -1{/tex} On substituting a 2 = -1 in Eq. (ii) and (iii), we get 2a 1 - 3a 3 = 1 ...(iv) a 1 + a 3 = 3 ...(v) On multiplying Eq. (v) by 3 and then adding with Eq. (iv), we get {tex}5a_1 = 1 + 9 = 10{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a 1 = 2 On substituting a 1 = 2 in Eq. (v), we get a 3 = 1 {tex}\therefore {/tex} the vector is {tex}\vec { a } = 2\hat { i } - \hat { j } + \hat { k }.{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question,
{tex}\vec { b } = \hat { i } - \hat { j } + \hat { k } , {/tex}

{tex}\vec { c } = 2\hat { i } + \hat { j } - 3 \hat { k }{/tex} and 

{tex}\vec { d } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex}

let the required vector is {tex}\vec { a } = a _ { 1 } \hat {i} + a _ { 2 } \hat { j } + a _ { 3 } \hat { k }{/tex}
Given, {tex}\vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = 4 , \ \vec { a } \cdot \vec { c } = 0{/tex} and  {tex}\vec { a } \cdot \vec { d } = 2{/tex}
Now, {tex}\vec { a } .\vec { b } = 4{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow a _ { 1 } - a _ { 2 } + a _ { 3 } = 4{/tex}....(i)
{tex}\vec { a }. \vec { c } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2a_1 + a_2 - 3a_3 = 0  {/tex} ...(ii)
and {tex}\vec { a }. \vec { d } = 2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow a_1 + a_2 + a_3 = 2 {/tex}  ...(iii)
On subtracting Eq. (iii) from Eq. (i), we get
{tex}-2a_2 = 2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow a_2 = -1{/tex} 
On substituting a2 = -1 in Eq. (ii) and (iii),
we get
2a1 - 3a3 = 1  ...(iv)
a1 + a3 = 3  ...(v)
On multiplying Eq. (v) by 3 and then adding with Eq. (iv), we get
{tex}5a_1 = 1 + 9 = 10{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a1 = 2
On substituting a1 = 2 in Eq. (v), we get
a3 = 1
{tex}\therefore {/tex}the vector is {tex}\vec { a } = 2\hat { i } - \hat { j } + \hat { k }.{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } = 5 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + \lambda \hat { k },{/tex} then find the value of {tex}\lambda,{/tex} so that {tex}\vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { b }{/tex} are perpendicular vectors.
question_text: If {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } = 5 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + \lambda \hat { k },{/tex} then find the value of {tex}\lambda,{/tex} so that {tex}\vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { b }{/tex} are perpendicular vectors.
explanation: According to the question , Given vectors are , {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } = 5 \hat { i} - \hat { j } + \lambda \hat { k }{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec { a } + \vec { b } = ( \hat i - \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k } ) + ( 5\hat { i } - \hat { j } + \lambda \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= 6 \hat {i } - 2 \hat {j} + ( 7 + \lambda ) \hat { k }{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { b } = ( \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k } ) - (5 \hat { i } - \hat {j } + \lambda \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= - 4 \hat { i } + ( 7 - \lambda ) \hat { k }{/tex} Since, {tex}( \vec { a } + \vec { b } ){/tex} and {tex}( \vec { a } - \vec { b } ){/tex} are perpendicular vectors, then dot product of these vectors will be zero, i.e. {tex}( \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) \cdot ( \vec { a } - \vec { b } ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad [ 6 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + ( 7 + \lambda ) \hat { k } ] \cdot [ - 4 \hat { i } + ( 7 - \lambda ) \hat { k } ] = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - 24 + ( 7 + \lambda ) ( 7 - \lambda ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 49 - \lambda ^ { 2 } = 24{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda ^ { 2 } = 25{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad\lambda = \pm 5{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \lambda = \pm 5{/tex}
solution: According to the question , Given vectors are , {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } = 5 \hat { i} - \hat { j } + \lambda \hat { k }{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec { a } + \vec { b } = ( \hat i - \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k } ) + ( 5\hat { i } - \hat { j } + \lambda \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= 6 \hat {i } - 2 \hat {j} + ( 7 + \lambda ) \hat { k }{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { b } = ( \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k } ) - (5 \hat { i } - \hat {j } + \lambda \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= - 4 \hat { i } + ( 7 - \lambda ) \hat { k }{/tex} Since, {tex}( \vec { a } + \vec { b } ){/tex} and {tex}( \vec { a } - \vec { b } ){/tex} are perpendicular vectors, then dot product of these vectors will be zero, i.e. {tex}( \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) \cdot ( \vec { a } - \vec { b } ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad [ 6 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + ( 7 + \lambda ) \hat { k } ] \cdot [ - 4 \hat { i } + ( 7 - \lambda ) \hat { k } ] = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - 24 + ( 7 + \lambda ) ( 7 - \lambda ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 49 - \lambda ^ { 2 } = 24{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda ^ { 2 } = 25{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad\lambda = \pm 5{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \lambda = \pm 5{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question ,
Given vectors are ,  {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k }{/tex} and
 {tex}\vec { b } = 5 \hat { i} - \hat { j } + \lambda \hat { k }{/tex}
Now,
{tex}\vec { a } + \vec { b } = ( \hat i - \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k } ) + ( 5\hat { i } - \hat { j } + \lambda \hat { k } ){/tex}
{tex}= 6 \hat {i } - 2 \hat {j} + ( 7 + \lambda ) \hat { k }{/tex}
Now, 
 {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { b } = ( \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k } ) - (5 \hat { i } - \hat {j } + \lambda \hat { k } ){/tex}
{tex}= - 4 \hat { i } + ( 7 - \lambda ) \hat { k }{/tex}
Since, {tex}( \vec { a } + \vec { b } ){/tex} and {tex}( \vec { a } - \vec { b } ){/tex} are perpendicular vectors, then dot product of these vectors will be zero, i.e.  {tex}( \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) \cdot ( \vec { a } - \vec { b } ) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad [ 6 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + ( 7 + \lambda ) \hat { k } ] \cdot [ - 4 \hat { i } + ( 7 - \lambda ) \hat { k } ] = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad - 24 + ( 7 + \lambda ) ( 7 - \lambda ) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 49 - \lambda ^ { 2 } = 24{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda ^ { 2 } = 25{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad\lambda = \pm 5{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \lambda = \pm 5{/tex}

question_html: Write the direction cosines of vector {tex} - 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k }.{/tex}
question_text: Write the direction cosines of vector {tex} - 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k }.{/tex}
explanation: Let {tex}\vec { a } = - 2 \hat { i} + \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k }{/tex} .Here ,a= -2, b = 1, c=-5 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Directions cosines of {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} are given as {tex}\frac { - 2 } { \sqrt { ( - 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 5 ) ^ { 2 } } } ,\frac { 1 } { \sqrt { ( - 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 5 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} and {tex}\frac { - 5 } { \sqrt { ( - 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 5 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} i.e. {tex}\frac { - 2 } { \sqrt { 30 } } , \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 30 } } , \frac { - 5 } { \sqrt { 30 } }{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}\vec { a } = - 2 \hat { i} + \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k }{/tex} .Here ,a= -2, b = 1, c=-5 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Directions cosines of {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} are given as {tex}\frac { - 2 } { \sqrt { ( - 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 5 ) ^ { 2 } } } ,\frac { 1 } { \sqrt { ( - 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 5 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} and {tex}\frac { - 5 } { \sqrt { ( - 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 5 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} i.e. {tex}\frac { - 2 } { \sqrt { 30 } } , \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 30 } } , \frac { - 5 } { \sqrt { 30 } }{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\vec { a } = - 2 \hat { i} + \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k }{/tex}.Here ,a= -2, b = 1, c=-5
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Directions cosines of {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} are given as 
{tex}\frac { - 2 } { \sqrt { ( - 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 5 ) ^ { 2 } } } ,\frac { 1 } { \sqrt { ( - 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 5 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
and {tex}\frac { - 5 } { \sqrt { ( - 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 5 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
i.e.{tex}\frac { - 2 } { \sqrt { 30 } } , \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 30 } } , \frac { - 5 } { \sqrt { 30 } }{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}\vec a = \hat i + \hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = \hat j - \hat k{/tex} find a vector {tex}\vec c{/tex} such that {tex}\vec a \times \vec c = \vec b{/tex} and {tex}\vec a.\vec c = 3{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}\vec a = \hat i + \hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = \hat j - \hat k{/tex} find a vector {tex}\vec c{/tex} such that {tex}\vec a \times \vec c = \vec b{/tex} and {tex}\vec a.\vec c = 3{/tex}
explanation: Let {tex}\vec c = x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k{/tex} And {tex}\vec a = \hat i + \hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = \hat j - \hat k{/tex} For {tex}\vec a \times \vec c = \vec b,{/tex} {tex}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 1&1&1 \\ x&y&z \end{array}} \right| = \hat j - \hat k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \hat i\left( {z - y} \right) - \hat j\left( {z - x} \right) + \hat k\left( {y - x} \right) = \hat j - \hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore z - y = 0{/tex} ...(i) x - z = 1 ...(ii) x - y = 1 ...(iii) Also, {tex}\vec a.\vec c = 3{/tex} {tex}\left( {\hat i + \hat j + \hat k} \right).\left( {x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k} \right) = 3{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x + y + z = 3{/tex} ...(iv) On adding Eqs. (ii) and (iii), we get 2x - y - z = 2 ...(v) On solving Eqs. (iv) and (v), we get {tex}x = \frac{5}{3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore y = \frac{5}{3} - 1 = \frac{2}{3}{/tex} and {tex}z = \frac{2}{3}{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec c = \frac{5}{3}\hat i + \frac{2}{3}\hat j + \frac{2}{3}\hat k{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{3}\left( {5\hat i + 2\hat j + 2\hat k} \right){/tex}
solution: Let {tex}\vec c = x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k{/tex} And {tex}\vec a = \hat i + \hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = \hat j - \hat k{/tex} For {tex}\vec a \times \vec c = \vec b,{/tex} {tex}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 1&1&1 \\ x&y&z \end{array}} \right| = \hat j - \hat k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \hat i\left( {z - y} \right) - \hat j\left( {z - x} \right) + \hat k\left( {y - x} \right) = \hat j - \hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore z - y = 0{/tex} ...(i) x - z = 1 ...(ii) x - y = 1 ...(iii) Also, {tex}\vec a.\vec c = 3{/tex} {tex}\left( {\hat i + \hat j + \hat k} \right).\left( {x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k} \right) = 3{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x + y + z = 3{/tex} ...(iv) On adding Eqs. (ii) and (iii), we get 2x - y - z = 2 ...(v) On solving Eqs. (iv) and (v), we get {tex}x = \frac{5}{3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore y = \frac{5}{3} - 1 = \frac{2}{3}{/tex} and {tex}z = \frac{2}{3}{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec c = \frac{5}{3}\hat i + \frac{2}{3}\hat j + \frac{2}{3}\hat k{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{3}\left( {5\hat i + 2\hat j + 2\hat k} \right){/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\vec c = x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k{/tex}

And {tex}\vec a = \hat i + \hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = \hat j - \hat k{/tex}

For {tex}\vec a \times \vec c = \vec b,{/tex}

{tex}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 1&1&1 \\ x&y&z \end{array}} \right| = \hat j - \hat k{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \hat i\left( {z - y} \right) - \hat j\left( {z - x} \right) + \hat k\left( {y - x} \right) = \hat j - \hat k{/tex}

{tex}\therefore z - y = 0{/tex} ...(i)

x - z = 1 ...(ii)

x - y = 1 ...(iii)

Also, {tex}\vec a.\vec c = 3{/tex}

{tex}\left( {\hat i + \hat j + \hat k} \right).\left( {x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k} \right) = 3{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow x + y + z = 3{/tex} ...(iv)

On adding Eqs. (ii) and (iii), we get

2x - y - z = 2 ...(v)

On solving Eqs. (iv) and (v), we get

{tex}x = \frac{5}{3}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore y = \frac{5}{3} - 1 = \frac{2}{3}{/tex} and {tex}z = \frac{2}{3}{/tex}

Now, {tex}\vec c = \frac{5}{3}\hat i + \frac{2}{3}\hat j + \frac{2}{3}\hat k{/tex}

{tex} = \frac{1}{3}\left( {5\hat i + 2\hat j + 2\hat k} \right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If a unit vector {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} makes angle {tex}\pi / 4 \text { with } \hat{i}, \pi / 3 \text { with } \hat{j}{/tex} and an acute angle {tex}\theta\space with \space\hat{k}{/tex}, then find the components of {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and the angle {tex}\theta{/tex}.
question_text: If a unit vector {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} makes angle {tex}\pi / 4 \text { with } \hat{i}, \pi / 3 \text { with } \hat{j}{/tex} and an acute angle {tex}\theta\space with \space\hat{k}{/tex} , then find the components of {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and the angle {tex}\theta{/tex} .
explanation: We know that if a vector {tex} \vec{a}{/tex} makes angles {tex}\alpha, \beta\space and \space \gamma\space with \space \hat{i}, \hat{j}\space and \space\hat{k}\space{/tex} respectively, then {tex}\cos ^{2} \alpha+\cos ^{2} \beta+\cos ^{2} \gamma=1{/tex} It is given that: {tex}\alpha=\frac{\pi}{4}, \beta=\frac{\pi}{3} \text { and } \gamma=\theta{/tex} an acute angle. {tex}\therefore \quad \cos ^{2} \alpha+\cos ^{2} \beta+\cos ^{2} \gamma=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos ^{2} \frac{\pi}{4}+\cos ^{2} \frac{\pi}{3}+\cos ^{2} \theta=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{4}+\cos ^{2} \theta=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos ^{2} \theta=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta=\pm \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}[\because \theta \text { is an acute angle } \therefore \cos \theta>0]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \theta=\frac{\pi}{3} \Rightarrow \gamma=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec{a}=|\vec{a}|\{(\cos \alpha) \hat{i}+(\cos \beta) \hat{j}+(\cos \gamma) \hat{k}\}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec{a}=\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{4}\right) \hat{i}+\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{3}\right) \hat{j}+\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{3}\right) \hat{k} \quad\left[\because|\vec{a}|=1, \alpha=\frac{\pi}{4}, \beta=\frac{\pi}{3} \text { and } \gamma=\frac{\pi}{3}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec{a}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \hat{i}+\frac{1}{2} \hat{j}+\frac{1}{2} \hat{k}{/tex} Thus, the components of {tex} \vec{a} \text { are } \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \hat{i}, \frac{1}{2} \hat{j}, \frac{1}{2} \hat{k}{/tex} .
solution: We know that if a vector {tex} \vec{a}{/tex} makes angles {tex}\alpha, \beta\space and \space \gamma\space with \space \hat{i}, \hat{j}\space and \space\hat{k}\space{/tex} respectively, then {tex}\cos ^{2} \alpha+\cos ^{2} \beta+\cos ^{2} \gamma=1{/tex} It is given that: {tex}\alpha=\frac{\pi}{4}, \beta=\frac{\pi}{3} \text { and } \gamma=\theta{/tex} an acute angle. {tex}\therefore \quad \cos ^{2} \alpha+\cos ^{2} \beta+\cos ^{2} \gamma=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos ^{2} \frac{\pi}{4}+\cos ^{2} \frac{\pi}{3}+\cos ^{2} \theta=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{4}+\cos ^{2} \theta=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos ^{2} \theta=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta=\pm \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}[\because \theta \text { is an acute angle } \therefore \cos \theta>0]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \theta=\frac{\pi}{3} \Rightarrow \gamma=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec{a}=|\vec{a}|\{(\cos \alpha) \hat{i}+(\cos \beta) \hat{j}+(\cos \gamma) \hat{k}\}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec{a}=\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{4}\right) \hat{i}+\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{3}\right) \hat{j}+\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{3}\right) \hat{k} \quad\left[\because|\vec{a}|=1, \alpha=\frac{\pi}{4}, \beta=\frac{\pi}{3} \text { and } \gamma=\frac{\pi}{3}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec{a}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \hat{i}+\frac{1}{2} \hat{j}+\frac{1}{2} \hat{k}{/tex} Thus, the components of {tex} \vec{a} \text { are } \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \hat{i}, \frac{1}{2} \hat{j}, \frac{1}{2} \hat{k}{/tex} .
solution_html:
We know that if a vector {tex} \vec{a}{/tex} makes angles {tex}\alpha, \beta\space and \space \gamma\space with \space \hat{i}, \hat{j}\space and \space\hat{k}\space{/tex}respectively, then
{tex}\cos ^{2} \alpha+\cos ^{2} \beta+\cos ^{2} \gamma=1{/tex}
It is given that: {tex}\alpha=\frac{\pi}{4}, \beta=\frac{\pi}{3} \text { and } \gamma=\theta{/tex} an acute angle.
{tex}\therefore \quad \cos ^{2} \alpha+\cos ^{2} \beta+\cos ^{2} \gamma=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos ^{2} \frac{\pi}{4}+\cos ^{2} \frac{\pi}{3}+\cos ^{2} \theta=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{4}+\cos ^{2} \theta=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos ^{2} \theta=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta=\pm \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}   {tex}[\because \theta \text { is an acute angle } \therefore \cos \theta>0]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \theta=\frac{\pi}{3} \Rightarrow \gamma=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\vec{a}=|\vec{a}|\{(\cos \alpha) \hat{i}+(\cos \beta) \hat{j}+(\cos \gamma) \hat{k}\}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec{a}=\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{4}\right) \hat{i}+\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{3}\right) \hat{j}+\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{3}\right) \hat{k} \quad\left[\because|\vec{a}|=1, \alpha=\frac{\pi}{4}, \beta=\frac{\pi}{3} \text { and } \gamma=\frac{\pi}{3}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec{a}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \hat{i}+\frac{1}{2} \hat{j}+\frac{1}{2} \hat{k}{/tex}
Thus, the components of {tex} \vec{a} \text { are } \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \hat{i}, \frac{1}{2} \hat{j}, \frac{1}{2} \hat{k}{/tex} .

subject: Mathematics
question_html: For what value of λ are the vectors {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\lambda \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} perpendicular to each other?
question_text: For what value of λ are the vectors {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\lambda \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} perpendicular to each other?
explanation: Let {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\lambda \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} For {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} to be perpendicular to {tex}\vec{b} \ \\\\ then \ \cos \theta=0{/tex} i.e. {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=0{/tex} [vector dot product] {tex}\left(2 \hat{\imath}+\lambda \hat{\jmath}+\hat{k}{)}(\hat{\imath}-2 \hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{k})=0\right.{/tex} 2 - 2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} + 3 = 0 5 - 2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0 Hence, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\lambda \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} For {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} to be perpendicular to {tex}\vec{b} \ \\\\ then \ \cos \theta=0{/tex} i.e. {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=0{/tex} [vector dot product] {tex}\left(2 \hat{\imath}+\lambda \hat{\jmath}+\hat{k}{)}(\hat{\imath}-2 \hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{k})=0\right.{/tex} 2 - 2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} + 3 = 0 5 - 2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0 Hence, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\lambda \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
For {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} to be perpendicular to {tex}\vec{b} \ \\\\ then \ \cos \theta=0{/tex}
i.e. {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=0{/tex} [vector dot product]
{tex}\left(2 \hat{\imath}+\lambda \hat{\jmath}+\hat{k}{)}(\hat{\imath}-2 \hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{k})=0\right.{/tex}
2 - 2{tex}\lambda{/tex}+ 3 = 0
5 - 2{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0
Hence, {tex}\lambda{/tex}={tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Let {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } , \vec { b } = 3 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k }{/tex}and {tex}\vec { c } = 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }.{/tex}Find a vector {tex}\vec { p },{/tex} which is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { p } \cdot \vec { c } = 18.{/tex}
question_text: Let {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } , \vec { b } = 3 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c } = 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }.{/tex} Find a vector {tex}\vec { p },{/tex} which is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { p } \cdot \vec { c } = 18.{/tex}
explanation: According to the question vectors are {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k },{/tex} {tex}\vec { b } = 3 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\vec { c } = 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} Suppose, {tex}\vec { p } = x \hat { i } + y \hat { j } + z \hat { k }{/tex} We have, {tex}\vec { p }{/tex} is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b }.{/tex} {tex}\vec { p } \cdot \vec { a } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( x \hat { i } + y \hat { j } + z \hat { k } ) \cdot ( \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x + 4 y + 2 z = 0{/tex} ...(i) and {tex}\vec { p }. \vec { b } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( x \hat { i } + { y } \hat {j } + z \hat { k } ) \cdot ( { 3 } \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k } ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 x - 2 y + 7 z = 0{/tex} ...(ii) Also, given {tex}\vec { p } \cdot \vec { c } = 18{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left({ x } \hat { i } + { y } \hat{ j } + z \hat { k } \right) \cdot ( 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 x - y + 4 z = 18{/tex} ...(iii) Multiplying Eq. (i) by 3 and subtracting it from Eq. (ii), we get {tex}-14y + z = 0{/tex} Multiplying Eq. (i) by 2 and subtracting it from Eq. (iii), we get {tex}-9y = 18{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y = -2{/tex} On putting y = -2 and z= -28 in Eq. (i), we get {tex}x + 4 (-2) + 2 (-28) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} x - 8 - 56 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} x = 64{/tex} Hence, the required vector is {tex}\vec { p } = x \hat { i } + { y } \hat { j } + z \hat { k }{/tex} i.e. {tex}\vec { p } = 64 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } - 28 \hat { k }{/tex}
solution: According to the question vectors are {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k },{/tex} {tex}\vec { b } = 3 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\vec { c } = 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} Suppose, {tex}\vec { p } = x \hat { i } + y \hat { j } + z \hat { k }{/tex} We have, {tex}\vec { p }{/tex} is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b }.{/tex} {tex}\vec { p } \cdot \vec { a } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( x \hat { i } + y \hat { j } + z \hat { k } ) \cdot ( \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x + 4 y + 2 z = 0{/tex} ...(i) and {tex}\vec { p }. \vec { b } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( x \hat { i } + { y } \hat {j } + z \hat { k } ) \cdot ( { 3 } \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k } ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 x - 2 y + 7 z = 0{/tex} ...(ii) Also, given {tex}\vec { p } \cdot \vec { c } = 18{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left({ x } \hat { i } + { y } \hat{ j } + z \hat { k } \right) \cdot ( 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 x - y + 4 z = 18{/tex} ...(iii) Multiplying Eq. (i) by 3 and subtracting it from Eq. (ii), we get {tex}-14y + z = 0{/tex} Multiplying Eq. (i) by 2 and subtracting it from Eq. (iii), we get {tex}-9y = 18{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y = -2{/tex} On putting y = -2 and z= -28 in Eq. (i), we get {tex}x + 4 (-2) + 2 (-28) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} x - 8 - 56 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} x = 64{/tex} Hence, the required vector is {tex}\vec { p } = x \hat { i } + { y } \hat { j } + z \hat { k }{/tex} i.e. {tex}\vec { p } = 64 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } - 28 \hat { k }{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question vectors are

 {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k },{/tex}
{tex}\vec { b } = 3 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k }{/tex}
 {tex}\vec { c } = 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex}
Suppose,  {tex}\vec { p } = x \hat { i } + y \hat { j } + z \hat { k }{/tex}
We have, {tex}\vec { p }{/tex} is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b }.{/tex}
{tex}\vec { p } \cdot \vec { a } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( x \hat { i } + y \hat { j } + z \hat { k } ) \cdot ( \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } ) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x + 4 y + 2 z = 0{/tex} ...(i)
and {tex}\vec { p }. \vec { b } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( x \hat { i } + { y } \hat {j } + z \hat { k } ) \cdot ( { 3 } \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k } ) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 x - 2 y + 7 z = 0{/tex} ...(ii)
Also, given {tex}\vec { p } \cdot \vec { c } = 18{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left({ x } \hat { i } + { y } \hat{ j } + z \hat { k } \right) \cdot ( 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } ) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 x - y + 4 z = 18{/tex} ...(iii)
Multiplying Eq. (i) by 3 and subtracting it from Eq. (ii),  we get
{tex}-14y + z = 0{/tex}
Multiplying Eq. (i) by 2 and subtracting it from Eq. (iii), we get
{tex}-9y = 18{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y = -2{/tex}
On putting y = -2 and z= -28 in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}x + 4 (-2) + 2 (-28) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex} x - 8 - 56 = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex} x = 64{/tex}
Hence, the required vector is
{tex}\vec { p } = x \hat { i } + { y } \hat { j } + z \hat { k }{/tex}
i.e. {tex}\vec { p } = 64 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } - 28 \hat { k }{/tex}

question_html: Find the sum of the vectors {tex} \vec { a } = \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k } , \vec { b } = - 2 \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } + 5 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c } = \hat { i } - 6 \hat { j } - 7 \hat { k }{/tex}
question_text: Find the sum of the vectors {tex} \vec { a } = \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k } , \vec { b } = - 2 \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } + 5 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c } = \hat { i } - 6 \hat { j } - 7 \hat { k }{/tex}
explanation: Here, we have to find the sum of vectors.Given vectors are {tex} \vec { a } = \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j} + \hat { k },{/tex} {tex}\vec { b } = - 2 \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j} + 5 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c } = \hat { i } - 6 \hat { j } - 7 \hat { k }{/tex} Sum of the vectors {tex}\vec { a } , \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c }{/tex} is given by the respective sum of components of the vectors.Thus, {tex} \vec { a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } = ( \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k } ) + ( - 2 \hat { i} + 4 \hat { j} + 5 \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex} + ( \hat { i} - 6 \hat { j } - 7 \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex} = - 4 \hat { j } - \hat { k }{/tex}
solution: Here, we have to find the sum of vectors.Given vectors are {tex} \vec { a } = \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j} + \hat { k },{/tex} {tex}\vec { b } = - 2 \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j} + 5 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c } = \hat { i } - 6 \hat { j } - 7 \hat { k }{/tex} Sum of the vectors {tex}\vec { a } , \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c }{/tex} is given by the respective sum of components of the vectors.Thus, {tex} \vec { a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } = ( \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k } ) + ( - 2 \hat { i} + 4 \hat { j} + 5 \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex} + ( \hat { i} - 6 \hat { j } - 7 \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex} = - 4 \hat { j } - \hat { k }{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, we have to find the sum of vectors.Given vectors are {tex} \vec { a } = \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j} + \hat { k },{/tex}
{tex}\vec { b } = - 2 \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j} + 5 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c } = \hat { i } - 6 \hat { j } - 7 \hat { k }{/tex}
Sum of the vectors {tex}\vec { a } , \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c }{/tex} is given by the respective sum of components of the vectors.Thus,
{tex} \vec { a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } = ( \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k } ) + ( - 2 \hat { i} + 4 \hat { j} + 5 \hat { k } ){/tex}{tex} + ( \hat { i} - 6 \hat { j } - 7 \hat { k } ){/tex}
{tex} = - 4 \hat { j } - \hat { k }{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find {tex}\lambda{/tex}, when projection of {tex}\vec { a } = \lambda \hat { i } + \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} on {tex}\vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} is 4 units.
question_text: Find {tex}\lambda{/tex} , when projection of {tex}\vec { a } = \lambda \hat { i } + \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} on {tex}\vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} is 4 units.
explanation: We are given that {tex}\vec { a } = \lambda \hat { i } + \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } , \vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} Now ,projection of {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} on {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} = 4. {tex}\therefore \frac { \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } } { | \vec { b } | } = 4 ~~~\left[ \because \text { projecton of } \vec { a } \text { on } \vec { b } = \frac { \vec { a } \cdot\vec { b } } { | \vec { b } | } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { ( \lambda \hat { i } + \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } ) \cdot ( 2\hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ) } { \sqrt { ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } } } = 4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 2 \lambda + 6 + 12 } { \sqrt { 49 } } = 4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 2 \lambda + 18 } { 7 } = 4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \lambda + 18 = 28{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \lambda = 10{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda = 5{/tex}
solution: We are given that {tex}\vec { a } = \lambda \hat { i } + \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } , \vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} Now ,projection of {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} on {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} = 4. {tex}\therefore \frac { \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } } { | \vec { b } | } = 4 ~~~\left[ \because \text { projecton of } \vec { a } \text { on } \vec { b } = \frac { \vec { a } \cdot\vec { b } } { | \vec { b } | } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { ( \lambda \hat { i } + \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } ) \cdot ( 2\hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ) } { \sqrt { ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } } } = 4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 2 \lambda + 6 + 12 } { \sqrt { 49 } } = 4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 2 \lambda + 18 } { 7 } = 4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \lambda + 18 = 28{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \lambda = 10{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda = 5{/tex}
solution_html:
We are given that {tex}\vec { a } = \lambda \hat { i } + \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } , \vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex}
Now ,projection of {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} on {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} = 4.
{tex}\therefore \frac { \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } } { | \vec { b } | } = 4 ~~~\left[ \because \text { projecton of } \vec { a } \text { on } \vec { b } = \frac { \vec { a } \cdot\vec { b } } { | \vec { b } | } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { ( \lambda \hat { i } + \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } ) \cdot ( 2\hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ) } { \sqrt { ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } } } = 4{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 2 \lambda + 6 + 12 } { \sqrt { 49 } } = 4{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 2 \lambda + 18 } { 7 } = 4{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \lambda + 18 = 28{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \lambda = 10{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda = 5{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the scalar components of {tex}\vec { A B }{/tex} with initial point A (2,1) and terminal point B (- 5, 7).
question_text: Find the scalar components of {tex}\vec { A B }{/tex} with initial point A (2,1) and terminal point B (- 5, 7).
explanation: Now, given initial point is A (2, 1) and terminal point is B (- 5, 7), {tex}\begin{aligned} \overrightarrow{A B} &=\left(x_{2}-x_{1}\right) \hat{i}+\left(y_{2}-y_{1}\right) \hat{j} \\ &=(-5-2) \hat{i}+(7-1) \hat{j} \\ &=-7 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j} \end{aligned}{/tex} Hence the vector components are -7 and 6.
solution: Now, given initial point is A (2, 1) and terminal point is B (- 5, 7), {tex}\begin{aligned} \overrightarrow{A B} &=\left(x_{2}-x_{1}\right) \hat{i}+\left(y_{2}-y_{1}\right) \hat{j} \\ &=(-5-2) \hat{i}+(7-1) \hat{j} \\ &=-7 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j} \end{aligned}{/tex} Hence the vector components are -7 and 6.
solution_html:
Now, given initial point is A (2, 1) and terminal point is B (- 5, 7),
{tex}\begin{aligned} \overrightarrow{A B} &=\left(x_{2}-x_{1}\right) \hat{i}+\left(y_{2}-y_{1}\right) \hat{j} \\ &=(-5-2) \hat{i}+(7-1) \hat{j} \\ &=-7 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j} \end{aligned}{/tex}
Hence the vector components are -7 and 6.

question_html: Write the value of {tex}( \hat { k } \times \hat { j } ) \cdot \hat { i } + \hat { j } \cdot \hat { k }̣{/tex}
question_text: Write the value of {tex}( \hat { k } \times \hat { j } ) \cdot \hat { i } + \hat { j } \cdot \hat { k }̣{/tex}
explanation: We have to write the value of {tex}( \hat { k } \times \hat { j } ) \cdot \hat { i } + \hat { j } \cdot \hat { k }̣{/tex} Given, {tex}( \hat { k } \times \hat { j } ). \hat { i } + \hat { j }. \hat { k } = ( - \hat { i } ) \hat { i } + \hat { j } \cdot \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}= - ( \hat { i }. \hat { i } ) + 0 = - 1 \quad [ \because ( \hat { \imath } \cdot \hat { \imath } ) = 1 ]{/tex}
solution: We have to write the value of {tex}( \hat { k } \times \hat { j } ) \cdot \hat { i } + \hat { j } \cdot \hat { k }̣{/tex} Given, {tex}( \hat { k } \times \hat { j } ). \hat { i } + \hat { j }. \hat { k } = ( - \hat { i } ) \hat { i } + \hat { j } \cdot \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}= - ( \hat { i }. \hat { i } ) + 0 = - 1 \quad [ \because ( \hat { \imath } \cdot \hat { \imath } ) = 1 ]{/tex}
solution_html:
We have to write the value of {tex}( \hat { k } \times \hat { j } ) \cdot \hat { i } + \hat { j } \cdot \hat { k }̣{/tex}

Given, {tex}( \hat { k } \times \hat { j } ). \hat { i } + \hat { j }. \hat { k } = ( - \hat { i } ) \hat { i } + \hat { j } \cdot \hat { k }{/tex}
{tex}= - ( \hat { i }. \hat { i } ) + 0 = - 1 \quad [ \because ( \hat { \imath } \cdot \hat { \imath } ) = 1 ]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the sum of the vectors: {tex}\vec a = \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k,\vec b = - 2\hat i + 4\hat j + 5\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = \hat i - 6\hat j - 7\hat k{/tex}
question_text: Find the sum of the vectors: {tex}\vec a = \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k,\vec b = - 2\hat i + 4\hat j + 5\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = \hat i - 6\hat j - 7\hat k{/tex}
explanation: Given: {tex}\vec a = \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k,\vec b = - 2\hat i + 4\hat j + 5\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = \hat i - 6\hat j - 7\hat k{/tex} Adding, {tex}\vec a + \vec b + \vec c {/tex} {tex}= \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k - 2\hat i + 4\hat j + 5\hat k + \hat i - 6\hat j - 7\hat k{/tex} {tex} = 0\hat i - 4\hat j - \hat k = - 4\hat j - \hat k {/tex}
solution: Given: {tex}\vec a = \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k,\vec b = - 2\hat i + 4\hat j + 5\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = \hat i - 6\hat j - 7\hat k{/tex} Adding, {tex}\vec a + \vec b + \vec c {/tex} {tex}= \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k - 2\hat i + 4\hat j + 5\hat k + \hat i - 6\hat j - 7\hat k{/tex} {tex} = 0\hat i - 4\hat j - \hat k = - 4\hat j - \hat k {/tex}
solution_html:
Given: {tex}\vec a = \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k,\vec b = - 2\hat i + 4\hat j + 5\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = \hat i - 6\hat j - 7\hat k{/tex}

Adding, {tex}\vec a + \vec b + \vec c {/tex} {tex}= \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k - 2\hat i + 4\hat j + 5\hat k + \hat i - 6\hat j - 7\hat k{/tex}

{tex} = 0\hat i - 4\hat j - \hat k = - 4\hat j - \hat k {/tex}

question_html: If with reference to the right handed system of mutually {tex} \bot {/tex} unit vectors {tex}\hat i, \ \hat j, \ \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec \alpha = 3\hat i - \hat j,\vec \beta = 2\hat i + \hat j - 3\hat k{/tex} then express {tex}\vec \beta {/tex} in the form {tex}\vec \beta = {\vec \beta _1} + {\vec \beta _2}{/tex}, where {tex}\vec \beta {/tex} is || to {tex}\vec \alpha {/tex} and {tex}{\vec \beta _2}{/tex} is {tex} \bot {/tex} to {tex}\vec \alpha {/tex}
question_text: If with reference to the right handed system of mutually {tex} \bot {/tex} unit vectors {tex}\hat i, \ \hat j, \ \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec \alpha = 3\hat i - \hat j,\vec \beta = 2\hat i + \hat j - 3\hat k{/tex} then express {tex}\vec \beta {/tex} in the form {tex}\vec \beta = {\vec \beta _1} + {\vec \beta _2}{/tex} , where {tex}\vec \beta {/tex} is || to {tex}\vec \alpha {/tex} and {tex}{\vec \beta _2}{/tex} is {tex} \bot {/tex} to {tex}\vec \alpha {/tex}
explanation: Let {tex}\vec \beta = \lambda \vec \alpha {/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because {{\vec \beta }_1}\parallel to\,\vec \alpha } \right]{/tex} {tex}{\vec \beta _1} = \lambda (3\hat i - \hat j){/tex} {tex} = 3\lambda \hat i - \lambda \hat j{/tex} {tex}{\vec \beta _2} = \vec \beta - {\vec \beta _1}{/tex} {tex} = (2\hat i + \hat j - 3\hat k) - (3\lambda \hat i - \lambda \hat j){/tex} {tex} = (2 - 3\lambda )i + (1 + \lambda )\hat j - 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}\vec \alpha .{\vec \beta _2} = 0{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because {{\vec \beta }_2} \bot \vec \alpha } \right]{/tex} {tex}3(2 - 3\lambda ) - (1 + \lambda ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\lambda = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}{\vec \beta _1} = \frac{3}{2}\hat i - \frac{1}{2}\hat j{/tex} {tex}{\vec \beta _2} = \frac{1}{2}\hat i + \frac{3}{2}\hat j - 3\hat k{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}\vec \beta = \lambda \vec \alpha {/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because {{\vec \beta }_1}\parallel to\,\vec \alpha } \right]{/tex} {tex}{\vec \beta _1} = \lambda (3\hat i - \hat j){/tex} {tex} = 3\lambda \hat i - \lambda \hat j{/tex} {tex}{\vec \beta _2} = \vec \beta - {\vec \beta _1}{/tex} {tex} = (2\hat i + \hat j - 3\hat k) - (3\lambda \hat i - \lambda \hat j){/tex} {tex} = (2 - 3\lambda )i + (1 + \lambda )\hat j - 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}\vec \alpha .{\vec \beta _2} = 0{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because {{\vec \beta }_2} \bot \vec \alpha } \right]{/tex} {tex}3(2 - 3\lambda ) - (1 + \lambda ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\lambda = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}{\vec \beta _1} = \frac{3}{2}\hat i - \frac{1}{2}\hat j{/tex} {tex}{\vec \beta _2} = \frac{1}{2}\hat i + \frac{3}{2}\hat j - 3\hat k{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\vec \beta = \lambda \vec \alpha {/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because {{\vec \beta }_1}\parallel to\,\vec \alpha } \right]{/tex}
{tex}{\vec \beta _1} = \lambda (3\hat i - \hat j){/tex}
{tex} = 3\lambda \hat i - \lambda \hat j{/tex}
{tex}{\vec \beta _2} = \vec \beta - {\vec \beta _1}{/tex}
{tex} = (2\hat i + \hat j - 3\hat k) - (3\lambda \hat i - \lambda \hat j){/tex}
{tex} = (2 - 3\lambda )i + (1 + \lambda )\hat j - 3\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\vec \alpha .{\vec \beta _2} = 0{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because {{\vec \beta }_2} \bot \vec \alpha } \right]{/tex}
{tex}3(2 - 3\lambda ) - (1 + \lambda ) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\lambda = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}{\vec \beta _1} = \frac{3}{2}\hat i - \frac{1}{2}\hat j{/tex}
{tex}{\vec \beta _2} = \frac{1}{2}\hat i + \frac{3}{2}\hat j - 3\hat k{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Write the value of {tex}(\hat{i} \times \hat{j}) \cdot \hat{k}+(\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \cdot \hat{j}{/tex}.
question_text: Write the value of {tex}(\hat{i} \times \hat{j}) \cdot \hat{k}+(\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \cdot \hat{j}{/tex} .
explanation: Here, i, j & k are unit vectors along x,y & x-axis respectively. Solving , {tex}(\hat{i} \times \hat{j}) \cdot \hat{k}+(\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \cdot \hat{j}{/tex} {tex}=\hat{k} \cdot \hat{k}+\hat{j} \cdot \hat{j}+\hat{k} \cdot \hat{j}\\(\because \hat{i} \times \hat{j}=\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=|\hat{k}|^{2}+|\hat{j}|^{2}+0\\(\because \hat{k} \cdot \hat{j}=0){/tex} = 1 2 + 1 2 = 2
solution: Here, i, j & k are unit vectors along x,y & x-axis respectively. Solving , {tex}(\hat{i} \times \hat{j}) \cdot \hat{k}+(\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \cdot \hat{j}{/tex} {tex}=\hat{k} \cdot \hat{k}+\hat{j} \cdot \hat{j}+\hat{k} \cdot \hat{j}\\(\because \hat{i} \times \hat{j}=\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=|\hat{k}|^{2}+|\hat{j}|^{2}+0\\(\because \hat{k} \cdot \hat{j}=0){/tex} = 1 2 + 1 2 = 2
solution_html:
Here, i, j & k are unit vectors along x,y & x-axis respectively.

Solving ,

{tex}(\hat{i} \times \hat{j}) \cdot \hat{k}+(\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \cdot \hat{j}{/tex}
{tex}=\hat{k} \cdot \hat{k}+\hat{j} \cdot \hat{j}+\hat{k} \cdot \hat{j}\\(\because \hat{i} \times \hat{j}=\hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}=|\hat{k}|^{2}+|\hat{j}|^{2}+0\\(\because \hat{k} \cdot \hat{j}=0){/tex}
= 12 + 12
= 2

question_html: Find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} for which {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} are perpendicular, where {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}+\lambda \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex}.
question_text: Find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} for which {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} are perpendicular, where {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}+\lambda \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} .
explanation: Here,it is given {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{\imath}+\lambda \hat{\jmath}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} since these two vectors are perpendicular, their dot product is zero. {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} = {tex}|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}|\vec{a}||\vec{b}| \cos \frac{\pi}{2}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} = {tex}(2 \hat{\imath}+\lambda \hat{\jmath}+\hat{k}) \cdot(\hat{\imath}-2 \hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} = (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1)+( {tex}\lambda \times{/tex} -2)+(1 {tex}\times{/tex} 3) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} = 2 - 2 {tex} \lambda{/tex} + 3 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} 5 = 2 {tex} \lambda{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex} \lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}
solution: Here,it is given {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{\imath}+\lambda \hat{\jmath}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} since these two vectors are perpendicular, their dot product is zero. {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} = {tex}|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}|\vec{a}||\vec{b}| \cos \frac{\pi}{2}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} = {tex}(2 \hat{\imath}+\lambda \hat{\jmath}+\hat{k}) \cdot(\hat{\imath}-2 \hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} = (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1)+( {tex}\lambda \times{/tex} -2)+(1 {tex}\times{/tex} 3) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} = 2 - 2 {tex} \lambda{/tex} + 3 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} 5 = 2 {tex} \lambda{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex} \lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here,it is given{tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{\imath}+\lambda \hat{\jmath}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
since these two vectors are perpendicular, their dot product is zero.
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} = {tex}|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}|\vec{a}||\vec{b}| \cos \frac{\pi}{2}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} = {tex}(2 \hat{\imath}+\lambda \hat{\jmath}+\hat{k}) \cdot(\hat{\imath}-2 \hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} = (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1)+({tex}\lambda \times{/tex}-2)+(1 {tex}\times{/tex} 3) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} = 2 - 2{tex} \lambda{/tex} + 3 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex}5 = 2 {tex} \lambda{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex} \lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}

question_html: Write the value of {tex}(\hat{\mathrm{i}} \times \hat{\mathrm{j}}) \hat{\mathrm{k}}+\hat{\mathrm{i}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{j}}{/tex}
question_text: Write the value of {tex}(\hat{\mathrm{i}} \times \hat{\mathrm{j}}) \hat{\mathrm{k}}+\hat{\mathrm{i}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{j}}{/tex}
explanation: We need to find the value of {tex}(\hat{1} \times \hat{\jmath}) \cdot \hat{k}+\hat{1} . \hat{\jmath}{/tex} {tex}(\hat{\imath} \times \hat{\jmath}) \cdot \hat{{k}}+\hat{\imath} \cdot \hat{\jmath}=\vec{k} \vec{k}+\vec{\imath} \cdot \hat{\jmath}{/tex} { {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\hat{\mathrm{i}} \times \hat{\mathrm{j}}=\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} } {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(\hat{i} \times \hat{\jmath}) \cdot \hat{k}+\hat{i} \cdot \hat{j}=1+0=1{/tex} { {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\hat{\mathrm{i}} . \hat{\mathrm{j}}=0{/tex} } {tex} \therefore{/tex} The required value is 1.
solution: We need to find the value of {tex}(\hat{1} \times \hat{\jmath}) \cdot \hat{k}+\hat{1} . \hat{\jmath}{/tex} {tex}(\hat{\imath} \times \hat{\jmath}) \cdot \hat{{k}}+\hat{\imath} \cdot \hat{\jmath}=\vec{k} \vec{k}+\vec{\imath} \cdot \hat{\jmath}{/tex} { {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\hat{\mathrm{i}} \times \hat{\mathrm{j}}=\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} } {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(\hat{i} \times \hat{\jmath}) \cdot \hat{k}+\hat{i} \cdot \hat{j}=1+0=1{/tex} { {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\hat{\mathrm{i}} . \hat{\mathrm{j}}=0{/tex} } {tex} \therefore{/tex} The required value is 1.
solution_html:
We need to find the value of {tex}(\hat{1} \times \hat{\jmath}) \cdot \hat{k}+\hat{1} . \hat{\jmath}{/tex}
{tex}(\hat{\imath} \times \hat{\jmath}) \cdot \hat{{k}}+\hat{\imath} \cdot \hat{\jmath}=\vec{k} \vec{k}+\vec{\imath} \cdot \hat{\jmath}{/tex} {{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\hat{\mathrm{i}} \times \hat{\mathrm{j}}=\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} }
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}(\hat{i} \times \hat{\jmath}) \cdot \hat{k}+\hat{i} \cdot \hat{j}=1+0=1{/tex} {{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\hat{\mathrm{i}} . \hat{\mathrm{j}}=0{/tex} }
{tex} \therefore{/tex} The required value is 1.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}{/tex} are three vectors such that |{tex}\vec a{/tex}| = 5, |{tex}\vec b{/tex}| = 12 and |{tex}\vec{\mathrm{c}}{/tex}| = 13, and {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=\vec{o}{/tex} find the value of {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}{/tex} are three vectors such that | {tex}\vec a{/tex} | = 5, | {tex}\vec b{/tex} | = 12 and | {tex}\vec{\mathrm{c}}{/tex} | = 13, and {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=\vec{o}{/tex} find the value of {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}{/tex}
explanation: If | {tex}\vec a{/tex} | = 5, | {tex}\vec b{/tex} | = 12, | {tex}\vec c{/tex} | = 13 If ( {tex}\vec a + \vec b + \vec c{/tex} ) = 0.......(i) Find {tex}\vec a \cdot \vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec b \cdot \vec c{/tex} + {tex}\vec c \cdot \vec a{/tex} = ? Squaring the given equation (i) We get , ( {tex}\vec a + \vec b + \vec c{/tex} ) {tex}\cdot{/tex} ( {tex}\vec a + \vec b + \vec c{/tex} ) = 0 {tex}\vec a \cdot \vec a{/tex} + {tex}\vec a \cdot \vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec a \cdot \vec c{/tex} + {tex}\vec a \cdot \vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec b \cdot \vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec b \cdot \vec c{/tex} + {tex}\vec a \cdot \vec c{/tex} + {tex}\vec c \cdot \vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec c \cdot \vec c{/tex} | {tex}\vec a{/tex} | 2 + | {tex}\vec b{/tex} | 2 + | {tex}\vec c{/tex} | 2 + 2 {tex}(\vec a \cdot \vec b + \vec b \cdot \vec c + \vec c \cdot \vec a){/tex} = 0 25 + 144 + 169 + 2(x) = 0 338 + 2x = 0 2x = -338 x = -169 hence , the required term is equal to -169.
solution: If | {tex}\vec a{/tex} | = 5, | {tex}\vec b{/tex} | = 12, | {tex}\vec c{/tex} | = 13 If ( {tex}\vec a + \vec b + \vec c{/tex} ) = 0.......(i) Find {tex}\vec a \cdot \vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec b \cdot \vec c{/tex} + {tex}\vec c \cdot \vec a{/tex} = ? Squaring the given equation (i) We get , ( {tex}\vec a + \vec b + \vec c{/tex} ) {tex}\cdot{/tex} ( {tex}\vec a + \vec b + \vec c{/tex} ) = 0 {tex}\vec a \cdot \vec a{/tex} + {tex}\vec a \cdot \vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec a \cdot \vec c{/tex} + {tex}\vec a \cdot \vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec b \cdot \vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec b \cdot \vec c{/tex} + {tex}\vec a \cdot \vec c{/tex} + {tex}\vec c \cdot \vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec c \cdot \vec c{/tex} | {tex}\vec a{/tex} | 2 + | {tex}\vec b{/tex} | 2 + | {tex}\vec c{/tex} | 2 + 2 {tex}(\vec a \cdot \vec b + \vec b \cdot \vec c + \vec c \cdot \vec a){/tex} = 0 25 + 144 + 169 + 2(x) = 0 338 + 2x = 0 2x = -338 x = -169 hence , the required term is equal to -169.
solution_html:
If |{tex}\vec a{/tex}| = 5, |{tex}\vec b{/tex}| = 12, |{tex}\vec c{/tex}| = 13
If ({tex}\vec a + \vec b + \vec c{/tex}) = 0.......(i)
Find {tex}\vec a \cdot \vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec b \cdot \vec c{/tex} + {tex}\vec c \cdot \vec a{/tex} = ?
Squaring the given equation (i) We get , 
 ({tex}\vec a + \vec b + \vec c{/tex}) {tex}\cdot{/tex} ({tex}\vec a + \vec b + \vec c{/tex} ) = 0
{tex}\vec a \cdot \vec a{/tex} + {tex}\vec a \cdot \vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec a \cdot \vec c{/tex} + {tex}\vec a \cdot \vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec b \cdot \vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec b \cdot \vec c{/tex} + {tex}\vec a \cdot \vec c{/tex} + {tex}\vec c \cdot \vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec c \cdot \vec c{/tex} 
|{tex}\vec a{/tex}|2 + |{tex}\vec b{/tex}|2 + |{tex}\vec c{/tex}|2 + 2{tex}(\vec a \cdot \vec b + \vec b \cdot \vec c + \vec c \cdot \vec a){/tex} = 0
25 + 144 + 169 + 2(x) = 0
338 + 2x = 0
2x = -338
x = -169
hence , the required term is equal to -169.

question_html: Write the value of {tex}(\hat{\mathbf{i}} \times \hat{\mathbf{j}}) \cdot \hat{\mathbf{k}}+(\hat{\mathbf{j}} \times \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cdot \hat{\mathbf{i}}{/tex}
question_text: Write the value of {tex}(\hat{\mathbf{i}} \times \hat{\mathbf{j}}) \cdot \hat{\mathbf{k}}+(\hat{\mathbf{j}} \times \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cdot \hat{\mathbf{i}}{/tex}
explanation: {tex}(\hat{i} \times \hat{j}) \cdot \hat{k}+(\hat{j} \times \hat{k}) \cdot \hat{i}=\hat{k} \cdot \hat{k}+\hat{i} \cdot \hat{i}{/tex} So, 1 + 1 = 2 { {tex}|\vec{i}|=|\vec{j}|=|\vec{k}|=1{/tex} }
solution: {tex}(\hat{i} \times \hat{j}) \cdot \hat{k}+(\hat{j} \times \hat{k}) \cdot \hat{i}=\hat{k} \cdot \hat{k}+\hat{i} \cdot \hat{i}{/tex} So, 1 + 1 = 2 { {tex}|\vec{i}|=|\vec{j}|=|\vec{k}|=1{/tex} }
solution_html:
{tex}(\hat{i} \times \hat{j}) \cdot \hat{k}+(\hat{j} \times \hat{k}) \cdot \hat{i}=\hat{k} \cdot \hat{k}+\hat{i} \cdot \hat{i}{/tex}
So, 1 + 1 = 2             {{tex}|\vec{i}|=|\vec{j}|=|\vec{k}|=1{/tex}}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find {tex}\lambda{/tex} when the projection of {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=\lambda \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} on {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} is 4 units.
question_text: Find {tex}\lambda{/tex} when the projection of {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=\lambda \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} on {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} is 4 units.
explanation: Given vectors are, {tex}\vec{a}=\lambda \hat{i}+\hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} , {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} The projection of {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} along {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{b}|}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a}. \vec{b}=(\lambda \hat{i}+\hat{j}+4 \hat{k}) \cdot(2 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=2 \lambda+6+12{/tex} {tex}=2 \lambda+18{/tex} {tex}|\vec{b}|=\sqrt{2^{2}+6^{2}+3^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{49}=7{/tex} Given, the projection of vector a along vector b is 4. {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{b}|}=4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{2 \lambda+18}{7}=4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \lambda+18=28{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \lambda=10{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda=5{/tex}
solution: Given vectors are, {tex}\vec{a}=\lambda \hat{i}+\hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} , {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} The projection of {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} along {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{b}|}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a}. \vec{b}=(\lambda \hat{i}+\hat{j}+4 \hat{k}) \cdot(2 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=2 \lambda+6+12{/tex} {tex}=2 \lambda+18{/tex} {tex}|\vec{b}|=\sqrt{2^{2}+6^{2}+3^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{49}=7{/tex} Given, the projection of vector a along vector b is 4. {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{b}|}=4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{2 \lambda+18}{7}=4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \lambda+18=28{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \lambda=10{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda=5{/tex}
solution_html:
Given vectors are, {tex}\vec{a}=\lambda \hat{i}+\hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex}, {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex}
The projection of {tex}\vec{a}{/tex}  along  {tex}\vec{b}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{b}|}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{a}. \vec{b}=(\lambda \hat{i}+\hat{j}+4 \hat{k}) \cdot(2 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}=2 \lambda+6+12{/tex}
{tex}=2 \lambda+18{/tex}
{tex}|\vec{b}|=\sqrt{2^{2}+6^{2}+3^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{49}=7{/tex}

Given, the projection of vector a along vector b is 4.
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{b}|}=4{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{2 \lambda+18}{7}=4{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \lambda+18=28{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \lambda=10{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda=5{/tex}
