question_html: Let {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}, ~\vec{b}=3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}. Find a vector {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} which is perpendicular to both {tex}\bar{a}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=18{/tex}.
question_text: Let {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}, ~\vec{b}=3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} . Find a vector {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} which is perpendicular to both {tex}\bar{a}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=18{/tex} .
explanation: Let, {tex}\vec{p}=\vec{x} \vec{i}+y \vec{j}+z \vec{k}{/tex} Given, {tex}\vec{p}_{\perp} \vec{a} \ \& \ \vec{b}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow{{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathbf{a}}=0{/tex} & {tex}\vec{p} \cdot \vec{b}=0{/tex} Hence, {tex}(x \vec{i}+y \vec{j}+z \vec{k}) \cdot(\vec{i}+4 \vec{j}+2 \vec{k})=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + 4y + 2z = 0 …(1) and {tex}(\vec{x} \vec{i}+\vec{y} \vec{j}+z \vec{k}) \cdot(3 \vec{i}-2 \vec{j}+7 \vec{k})=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x – 2y + 7z = 0 …(2) Solving equation 1 and 2 using Cramer's rule, taking z as an independent variable, we have - {tex}\frac{x}{4(7)-(2)(-2)}=\frac{y}{2(3)-(1) 7}=\frac{z}{1(-2)-3(4)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{32}=\frac{y}{-1}=\frac{z}{-14}=\lambda{/tex} (Say) {tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 32 {tex}\lambda{/tex} , y = - {tex}\lambda{/tex} , z = -14 {tex}\lambda{/tex} Also, {tex}\vec{p} \cdot \vec{c}{/tex} = 18 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(\mathrm{x} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{i}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{y}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{j}}+\mathrm{z} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot(2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{i}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{j}}+4 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{k}})=18{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x – y + 4z = 18 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 (32 {tex}\lambda{/tex} ) – (- {tex}\lambda{/tex} ) + 4 (-14 {tex}\lambda{/tex} ) = 18 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9 {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 18 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 2 Hence, x = 64, y = -2 & z = -28 Or {tex}\vec{p}=64 \vec{i}-2 \vec{j}-28 \vec{k}{/tex}
solution: Let, {tex}\vec{p}=\vec{x} \vec{i}+y \vec{j}+z \vec{k}{/tex} Given, {tex}\vec{p}_{\perp} \vec{a} \ \& \ \vec{b}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow{{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathbf{a}}=0{/tex} & {tex}\vec{p} \cdot \vec{b}=0{/tex} Hence, {tex}(x \vec{i}+y \vec{j}+z \vec{k}) \cdot(\vec{i}+4 \vec{j}+2 \vec{k})=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + 4y + 2z = 0 …(1) and {tex}(\vec{x} \vec{i}+\vec{y} \vec{j}+z \vec{k}) \cdot(3 \vec{i}-2 \vec{j}+7 \vec{k})=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x – 2y + 7z = 0 …(2) Solving equation 1 and 2 using Cramer's rule, taking z as an independent variable, we have - {tex}\frac{x}{4(7)-(2)(-2)}=\frac{y}{2(3)-(1) 7}=\frac{z}{1(-2)-3(4)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{32}=\frac{y}{-1}=\frac{z}{-14}=\lambda{/tex} (Say) {tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 32 {tex}\lambda{/tex} , y = - {tex}\lambda{/tex} , z = -14 {tex}\lambda{/tex} Also, {tex}\vec{p} \cdot \vec{c}{/tex} = 18 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(\mathrm{x} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{i}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{y}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{j}}+\mathrm{z} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot(2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{i}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{j}}+4 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{k}})=18{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x – y + 4z = 18 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 (32 {tex}\lambda{/tex} ) – (- {tex}\lambda{/tex} ) + 4 (-14 {tex}\lambda{/tex} ) = 18 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9 {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 18 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 2 Hence, x = 64, y = -2 & z = -28 Or {tex}\vec{p}=64 \vec{i}-2 \vec{j}-28 \vec{k}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let, {tex}\vec{p}=\vec{x} \vec{i}+y \vec{j}+z \vec{k}{/tex}
Given, {tex}\vec{p}_{\perp} \vec{a} \ \& \ \vec{b}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \overrightarrow{{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathbf{a}}=0{/tex} & {tex}\vec{p} \cdot \vec{b}=0{/tex}
Hence,
{tex}(x \vec{i}+y \vec{j}+z \vec{k}) \cdot(\vec{i}+4 \vec{j}+2 \vec{k})=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + 4y + 2z = 0 …(1)
and {tex}(\vec{x} \vec{i}+\vec{y} \vec{j}+z \vec{k}) \cdot(3 \vec{i}-2 \vec{j}+7 \vec{k})=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x – 2y + 7z = 0 …(2)
Solving equation 1 and 2 using Cramer's rule, taking z as an independent variable, we have -
{tex}\frac{x}{4(7)-(2)(-2)}=\frac{y}{2(3)-(1) 7}=\frac{z}{1(-2)-3(4)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{32}=\frac{y}{-1}=\frac{z}{-14}=\lambda{/tex} (Say)
{tex}\therefore{/tex}x = 32{tex}\lambda{/tex}, y = -{tex}\lambda{/tex}, z = -14{tex}\lambda{/tex}
Also, {tex}\vec{p} \cdot \vec{c}{/tex} = 18
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(\mathrm{x} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{i}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{y}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{j}}+\mathrm{z} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot(2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{i}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{j}}+4 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{k}})=18{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}2x – y + 4z = 18
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 (32{tex}\lambda{/tex}) – (-{tex}\lambda{/tex}) + 4 (-14{tex}\lambda{/tex}) = 18
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 18
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 2
Hence, x = 64, y = -2 & z = -28
Or {tex}\vec{p}=64 \vec{i}-2 \vec{j}-28 \vec{k}{/tex}

question_html: Using vectors, show that the points A(-2, 3, 5), B(7, 0, -1), C(-3, -2, -5) and D( 3,4,7) are such that AB and CD intersect at the point P(1, 2, 3).
question_text: Using vectors, show that the points A(-2, 3, 5), B(7, 0, -1), C(-3, -2, -5) and D( 3,4,7) are such that AB and CD intersect at the point P(1, 2, 3).
explanation: We have to show that the points A(-2, 3, 5), B(7, 0, -1), C(-3, -2, -5) and D( 3,4,7) are such that AB and CD intersect at the point P(1, 2, 3) by using vectors. Now,the vector equation of line AB is {tex} \vec r = ( - 2\hat i + 3\hat j + 5\hat k) + \lambda [(7 + 2)\hat i + (0 - 3)\hat j + ( - 1 - 5\hat k)]{/tex} i.e. {tex} \vec { r } = ( - { 2 } \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j} + 5 \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( 9 \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } - 6 \hat { k } ){/tex} ....(i) {tex} \Rightarrow \ \vec r= ( 9 \lambda - 2 ) \hat { i } + ( - 3 \lambda + 3 ) \hat { j } + ( - 6 \lambda + 5 ) \hat { k }{/tex} and vector equation of line CD is {tex} \vec r = ( - 3\hat i - 2\hat j - 5\hat k) + \mu [(3 + 3i) + (4 + 2) \hat j + (7 + 5)\hat k]{/tex} i.e. {tex} \vec { r } = ( - \hat { 3 } i - 2 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k } ) + \mu ( 6 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 12 \hat { k } ){/tex} ....(ii) {tex} \Rightarrow \vec r = ( 6 \mu - 3 ) \hat { i } + ( 6 \mu - 2 ) \hat { j } + (1 2 \mu - 5 ) \hat { k }{/tex} Clearly, any point on the line (i) is of the form {tex} P ( 9 \lambda - 2 , -3 \lambda + 3 , - 6 \lambda + 5 ){/tex} and any point on the line (ii) is of the form {tex} Q ( 6 \mu - 3,6 \mu -2, 12 \mu - 5 ){/tex} If lines (i) and (i) intersect, then these points must coincide for some {tex} \lambda{/tex} and {tex} \mu.{/tex} Consider, {tex} 9 \lambda - 2 = 6 \mu - 3 , - 3 \lambda + 3 = 6 \mu - 2{/tex} and {tex} - 6 \lambda + 5 = 12 \mu - 5{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 9 \lambda - 6 \mu = - 1{/tex} ...............(iii) {tex} 3 \lambda + 6 \mu = 5{/tex} .....(iv) and {tex} 6 \lambda + 12 \mu = 10{/tex} .................(v) On adding Eqs. (iii) and (iv), we get {tex} 12 \lambda = 4{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex} On Substituting {tex} \lambda = \frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex} in Eq. (iv) We get {tex} 6 \mu = 5 - 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \mu = \frac { 4 } { 6 } = \frac { 2 } { 3 }{/tex} Now, on substituting {tex} \lambda = \frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex} and {tex} \mu = \frac { 2 } { 3 }{/tex} in Eq. (v) we get {tex} 6 \cdot \frac { 1 } { 3 } + 12 \cdot \frac { 2 } { 3 } = 10 \Rightarrow 2 + 8 = 10{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 10 = 10, which is true Thus, A and CD intersect and their point of intersection is given by {tex} P \left( 9 \cdot \frac { 1 } { 3 } - 2 ,- 3 \cdot \frac { 1 } { 3 } + 3 ,- 6 \cdot \frac { 1 } { 3 } + 5 \right) {/tex} i.e. P(1, 2, 3).
solution: We have to show that the points A(-2, 3, 5), B(7, 0, -1), C(-3, -2, -5) and D( 3,4,7) are such that AB and CD intersect at the point P(1, 2, 3) by using vectors. Now,the vector equation of line AB is {tex} \vec r = ( - 2\hat i + 3\hat j + 5\hat k) + \lambda [(7 + 2)\hat i + (0 - 3)\hat j + ( - 1 - 5\hat k)]{/tex} i.e. {tex} \vec { r } = ( - { 2 } \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j} + 5 \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( 9 \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } - 6 \hat { k } ){/tex} ....(i) {tex} \Rightarrow \ \vec r= ( 9 \lambda - 2 ) \hat { i } + ( - 3 \lambda + 3 ) \hat { j } + ( - 6 \lambda + 5 ) \hat { k }{/tex} and vector equation of line CD is {tex} \vec r = ( - 3\hat i - 2\hat j - 5\hat k) + \mu [(3 + 3i) + (4 + 2) \hat j + (7 + 5)\hat k]{/tex} i.e. {tex} \vec { r } = ( - \hat { 3 } i - 2 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k } ) + \mu ( 6 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 12 \hat { k } ){/tex} ....(ii) {tex} \Rightarrow \vec r = ( 6 \mu - 3 ) \hat { i } + ( 6 \mu - 2 ) \hat { j } + (1 2 \mu - 5 ) \hat { k }{/tex} Clearly, any point on the line (i) is of the form {tex} P ( 9 \lambda - 2 , -3 \lambda + 3 , - 6 \lambda + 5 ){/tex} and any point on the line (ii) is of the form {tex} Q ( 6 \mu - 3,6 \mu -2, 12 \mu - 5 ){/tex} If lines (i) and (i) intersect, then these points must coincide for some {tex} \lambda{/tex} and {tex} \mu.{/tex} Consider, {tex} 9 \lambda - 2 = 6 \mu - 3 , - 3 \lambda + 3 = 6 \mu - 2{/tex} and {tex} - 6 \lambda + 5 = 12 \mu - 5{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 9 \lambda - 6 \mu = - 1{/tex} ...............(iii) {tex} 3 \lambda + 6 \mu = 5{/tex} .....(iv) and {tex} 6 \lambda + 12 \mu = 10{/tex} .................(v) On adding Eqs. (iii) and (iv), we get {tex} 12 \lambda = 4{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex} On Substituting {tex} \lambda = \frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex} in Eq. (iv) We get {tex} 6 \mu = 5 - 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \mu = \frac { 4 } { 6 } = \frac { 2 } { 3 }{/tex} Now, on substituting {tex} \lambda = \frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex} and {tex} \mu = \frac { 2 } { 3 }{/tex} in Eq. (v) we get {tex} 6 \cdot \frac { 1 } { 3 } + 12 \cdot \frac { 2 } { 3 } = 10 \Rightarrow 2 + 8 = 10{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 10 = 10, which is true Thus, A and CD intersect and their point of intersection is given by {tex} P \left( 9 \cdot \frac { 1 } { 3 } - 2 ,- 3 \cdot \frac { 1 } { 3 } + 3 ,- 6 \cdot \frac { 1 } { 3 } + 5 \right) {/tex} i.e. P(1, 2, 3).
solution_html:
We have to show that the points A(-2, 3, 5), B(7, 0, -1), C(-3, -2, -5) and D( 3,4,7) are such that AB and CD intersect at the point P(1, 2, 3) by using vectors.

Now,the vector equation of line AB is
{tex} \vec r = ( - 2\hat i + 3\hat j + 5\hat k) + \lambda [(7 + 2)\hat i + (0 - 3)\hat j + ( - 1 - 5\hat k)]{/tex}
i.e. {tex} \vec { r } = ( - { 2 } \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j} + 5 \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( 9 \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } - 6 \hat { k } ){/tex}....(i)
{tex} \Rightarrow \ \vec r= ( 9 \lambda - 2 ) \hat { i } + ( - 3 \lambda + 3 ) \hat { j } + ( - 6 \lambda + 5 ) \hat { k }{/tex}
and vector equation of line CD is
{tex} \vec r = ( - 3\hat i - 2\hat j - 5\hat k) + \mu [(3 + 3i) + (4 + 2) \hat j + (7 + 5)\hat k]{/tex}
i.e. {tex} \vec { r } = ( - \hat { 3 } i - 2 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k } ) + \mu ( 6 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 12 \hat { k } ){/tex}....(ii)
{tex} \Rightarrow \vec r = ( 6 \mu - 3 ) \hat { i } + ( 6 \mu - 2 ) \hat { j } + (1 2 \mu - 5 ) \hat { k }{/tex}
Clearly, any point on the line (i) is of the form
{tex} P ( 9 \lambda - 2 , -3 \lambda + 3 , - 6 \lambda + 5 ){/tex}
and any point on the line (ii) is of the form
{tex} Q ( 6 \mu - 3,6 \mu -2, 12 \mu - 5 ){/tex}
If lines (i) and (i) intersect, then these points must coincide for some {tex} \lambda{/tex}and {tex} \mu.{/tex}
Consider, {tex} 9 \lambda - 2 = 6 \mu - 3 , - 3 \lambda + 3 = 6 \mu - 2{/tex}
and {tex} - 6 \lambda + 5 = 12 \mu - 5{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad 9 \lambda - 6 \mu = - 1{/tex}...............(iii)
{tex} 3 \lambda + 6 \mu = 5{/tex}.....(iv)
and {tex} 6 \lambda + 12 \mu = 10{/tex} .................(v)
On adding Eqs. (iii) and (iv), we get
{tex} 12 \lambda = 4{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex}
On Substituting {tex} \lambda = \frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex}in Eq. (iv) We get
{tex} 6 \mu = 5 - 1{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \mu = \frac { 4 } { 6 } = \frac { 2 } { 3 }{/tex}
Now, on substituting {tex} \lambda = \frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex}and {tex} \mu = \frac { 2 } { 3 }{/tex}in Eq. (v)
we get 
{tex} 6 \cdot \frac { 1 } { 3 } + 12 \cdot \frac { 2 } { 3 } = 10 \Rightarrow 2 + 8 = 10{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 10 = 10, which is true
Thus, A and CD intersect and their point of intersection  is given by
{tex} P \left( 9 \cdot \frac { 1 } { 3 } - 2 ,- 3 \cdot \frac { 1 } { 3 } + 3 ,- 6 \cdot \frac { 1 } { 3 } + 5 \right) {/tex}i.e. P(1, 2, 3).

question_html: Find the magnitude of the vector {tex}\vec { a } = 3 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex}.
question_text: Find the magnitude of the vector {tex}\vec { a } = 3 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex} .
explanation: As we know that the magnitude of a vector is given by the square root of the sum of the squares of its components. Given vector is {tex}\vec { a } = 3 \hat { i} - 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} magnitude of {tex}\vec { a } = | \vec { a } | = \sqrt { ( 3 ) ^{2} + ( - 2 )^ {2 }+ ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 9 + 4 + 36 } = \sqrt { 49 } = 7{/tex} units
solution: As we know that the magnitude of a vector is given by the square root of the sum of the squares of its components. Given vector is {tex}\vec { a } = 3 \hat { i} - 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} magnitude of {tex}\vec { a } = | \vec { a } | = \sqrt { ( 3 ) ^{2} + ( - 2 )^ {2 }+ ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 9 + 4 + 36 } = \sqrt { 49 } = 7{/tex} units
solution_html:
As we know that the magnitude of a vector is given by the square root of the sum of the squares of its components.

Given vector is {tex}\vec { a } = 3 \hat { i} - 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex}magnitude of {tex}\vec { a } = | \vec { a } | = \sqrt { ( 3 ) ^{2} + ( - 2 )^ {2 }+ ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt { 9 + 4 + 36 } = \sqrt { 49 } = 7{/tex}units

question_html: Write a unit vector in the direction of vector {tex}\vec { a } = 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k }{/tex} .
question_text: Write a unit vector in the direction of vector {tex}\vec { a } = 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k }{/tex} .
explanation: We have to find the unit vector in the direction of the given vector.We know that, unit vector in the direction of {tex} \vec { a }{/tex} is {tex}\hat { a } = \frac { \vec { a } } { | \vec { a } | }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required unit vector in the direction of vector {tex}\vec { a } = 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k }{/tex} is {tex}\hat { a } = \frac { \vec { a } } { | \vec { a } | } = \frac { 2\hat { i } + \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { 2 \hat { i} + \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 9 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 } { 3 } \hat { i } + \frac { 1 } { 3 } \hat { j } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } \hat { k }{/tex}
solution: We have to find the unit vector in the direction of the given vector.We know that, unit vector in the direction of {tex} \vec { a }{/tex} is {tex}\hat { a } = \frac { \vec { a } } { | \vec { a } | }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required unit vector in the direction of vector {tex}\vec { a } = 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k }{/tex} is {tex}\hat { a } = \frac { \vec { a } } { | \vec { a } | } = \frac { 2\hat { i } + \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { 2 \hat { i} + \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 9 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 } { 3 } \hat { i } + \frac { 1 } { 3 } \hat { j } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } \hat { k }{/tex}
solution_html:
We have to find the unit vector in the direction of the given vector.We know that, unit vector in the direction of{tex} \vec { a }{/tex} is {tex}\hat { a } = \frac { \vec { a } } { | \vec { a } | }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex}Required unit vector in the direction of  vector
{tex}\vec { a } = 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k }{/tex} is 
{tex}\hat { a } = \frac { \vec { a } } { | \vec { a } | } = \frac { 2\hat { i } + \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { 2 \hat { i} + \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 9 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 2 } { 3 } \hat { i } + \frac { 1 } { 3 } \hat { j } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } \hat { k }{/tex}

question_html:
If vectors {tex} \vec { a } = 2 \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k },{/tex}
{tex} \vec { b } = - \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { c } = 3 \hat { i } + \hat { j }{/tex} are such that {tex} \vec { a } + \lambda \vec { b }{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex} \vec { c },{/tex} then find the value of {tex} \lambda.{/tex}
question_text: If vectors {tex} \vec { a } = 2 \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k },{/tex} {tex} \vec { b } = - \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { c } = 3 \hat { i } + \hat { j }{/tex} are such that {tex} \vec { a } + \lambda \vec { b }{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex} \vec { c },{/tex} then find the value of {tex} \lambda.{/tex}
explanation: According to the question, Given, {tex} \vec { a } = 2 \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k },{/tex} {tex}\vec { b } = - \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { c } = 3 \hat {i } + \hat { j }{/tex} Also, {tex} \vec { a } + \lambda \vec { b }{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex} \vec { c }{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad ( \vec { a } + \lambda \vec { b } ) .\vec { c } = 0{/tex} ...(i) {tex} [ \because \text { when } \vec { a } \perp \vec { b } , \text { then } \vec { a } . \vec{ b } = 0 ]{/tex} Now, {tex} \vec { a } + \lambda \vec { b } = ( 2 \hat { i } + 2 \hat {j} + 3 \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( - \hat { i} + 2 \hat { j} + \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec { a } + \lambda \vec { b } = \hat {i } ( 2 - \lambda ) + \hat { j} ( 2 + 2 \lambda ) + \hat { k } ( 3 + \lambda ){/tex} Then, from Eq. (i), we get {tex} [ \hat { i } ( 2 - \lambda ) + \hat { j } ( 2 + 2 \lambda ) + \hat { k } ( 3 + \lambda ) ].[ 3\hat { i } + \hat { j } ] = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 ( 2 - \lambda ) + 1 ( 2 + 2 \lambda ) = 0 \Rightarrow 8 - \lambda = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex} \lambda = 8{/tex}
solution: According to the question, Given, {tex} \vec { a } = 2 \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k },{/tex} {tex}\vec { b } = - \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { c } = 3 \hat {i } + \hat { j }{/tex} Also, {tex} \vec { a } + \lambda \vec { b }{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex} \vec { c }{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad ( \vec { a } + \lambda \vec { b } ) .\vec { c } = 0{/tex} ...(i) {tex} [ \because \text { when } \vec { a } \perp \vec { b } , \text { then } \vec { a } . \vec{ b } = 0 ]{/tex} Now, {tex} \vec { a } + \lambda \vec { b } = ( 2 \hat { i } + 2 \hat {j} + 3 \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( - \hat { i} + 2 \hat { j} + \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec { a } + \lambda \vec { b } = \hat {i } ( 2 - \lambda ) + \hat { j} ( 2 + 2 \lambda ) + \hat { k } ( 3 + \lambda ){/tex} Then, from Eq. (i), we get {tex} [ \hat { i } ( 2 - \lambda ) + \hat { j } ( 2 + 2 \lambda ) + \hat { k } ( 3 + \lambda ) ].[ 3\hat { i } + \hat { j } ] = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 ( 2 - \lambda ) + 1 ( 2 + 2 \lambda ) = 0 \Rightarrow 8 - \lambda = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex} \lambda = 8{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question,

Given, {tex} \vec { a } = 2 \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k },{/tex}

{tex}\vec { b } = - \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} and 

{tex} \vec { c } = 3 \hat {i } + \hat { j }{/tex}
Also, {tex} \vec { a } + \lambda \vec { b }{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex} \vec { c }{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad ( \vec { a } + \lambda \vec { b } ) .\vec { c } = 0{/tex}...(i)
{tex} [ \because \text { when } \vec { a } \perp \vec { b } , \text { then } \vec { a } . \vec{ b } = 0 ]{/tex}
Now, {tex} \vec { a } + \lambda \vec { b } = ( 2 \hat { i } + 2 \hat {j} + 3 \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( - \hat { i} + 2 \hat { j} + \hat { k } ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec { a } + \lambda \vec { b } = \hat {i } ( 2 - \lambda ) + \hat { j} ( 2 + 2 \lambda ) + \hat { k } ( 3 + \lambda ){/tex}
Then, from Eq. (i), we get
{tex} [ \hat { i } ( 2 - \lambda ) + \hat { j } ( 2 + 2 \lambda ) + \hat { k } ( 3 + \lambda ) ].[ 3\hat { i } + \hat { j } ] = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 ( 2 - \lambda ) + 1 ( 2 + 2 \lambda ) = 0 \Rightarrow 8 - \lambda = 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex} \lambda = 8{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find a vector of magnitude 5 units and parallel to the resultant of the vectors {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i + 3\hat j - \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex}
question_text: Find a vector of magnitude 5 units and parallel to the resultant of the vectors {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i + 3\hat j - \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex}
explanation: Given: Vectors {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i + 3\hat j - \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex} Let vector {tex}\vec c{/tex} be the resultant vector of {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec c = \vec a + \vec b {/tex} {tex}= 2\hat i + 3\hat j - \hat k + \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex} {tex} = 3\hat i + \hat j + 0\hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required vector of magnitude 5 units and parallel (or collinear) to resultant vector {tex}\vec c = \vec a + \vec b{/tex} is {tex}5\hat c = 5\frac{{\vec c}}{{\left| {\vec c} \right|}} = 5\left( {\frac{{3\hat i + \hat j + 0\hat k}}{{\sqrt {9 + 1 + 0} }}} \right){/tex} {tex} = \frac{5}{{\sqrt {10} }}\left( {3\hat i + \hat j} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{5}{{\sqrt {10} }} \times \frac{{\sqrt {10} }}{{\sqrt {10} }}\left( {3\hat i + \hat j} \right){/tex} {tex} = \frac{{5\sqrt {10} }}{{10}}\left( {3\hat i + \hat j} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 5\hat c = \frac{{\sqrt {10} }}{2}\left( {3\vec i + \vec j} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{{3\sqrt {10} }}{2}\hat i + \frac{{\sqrt {10} }}{2}\hat j{/tex}
solution: Given: Vectors {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i + 3\hat j - \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex} Let vector {tex}\vec c{/tex} be the resultant vector of {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec c = \vec a + \vec b {/tex} {tex}= 2\hat i + 3\hat j - \hat k + \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex} {tex} = 3\hat i + \hat j + 0\hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required vector of magnitude 5 units and parallel (or collinear) to resultant vector {tex}\vec c = \vec a + \vec b{/tex} is {tex}5\hat c = 5\frac{{\vec c}}{{\left| {\vec c} \right|}} = 5\left( {\frac{{3\hat i + \hat j + 0\hat k}}{{\sqrt {9 + 1 + 0} }}} \right){/tex} {tex} = \frac{5}{{\sqrt {10} }}\left( {3\hat i + \hat j} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{5}{{\sqrt {10} }} \times \frac{{\sqrt {10} }}{{\sqrt {10} }}\left( {3\hat i + \hat j} \right){/tex} {tex} = \frac{{5\sqrt {10} }}{{10}}\left( {3\hat i + \hat j} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 5\hat c = \frac{{\sqrt {10} }}{2}\left( {3\vec i + \vec j} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{{3\sqrt {10} }}{2}\hat i + \frac{{\sqrt {10} }}{2}\hat j{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: Vectors {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i + 3\hat j - \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex}

Let vector {tex}\vec c{/tex} be the resultant vector of {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \vec c = \vec a + \vec b {/tex} {tex}= 2\hat i + 3\hat j - \hat k + \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex}

{tex} = 3\hat i + \hat j + 0\hat k{/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required vector of magnitude 5 units and parallel (or collinear) to resultant vector {tex}\vec c = \vec a + \vec b{/tex} is

{tex}5\hat c = 5\frac{{\vec c}}{{\left| {\vec c} \right|}} = 5\left( {\frac{{3\hat i + \hat j + 0\hat k}}{{\sqrt {9 + 1 + 0} }}} \right){/tex}

{tex} = \frac{5}{{\sqrt {10} }}\left( {3\hat i + \hat j} \right){/tex}

{tex}= \frac{5}{{\sqrt {10} }} \times \frac{{\sqrt {10} }}{{\sqrt {10} }}\left( {3\hat i + \hat j} \right){/tex}

{tex} = \frac{{5\sqrt {10} }}{{10}}\left( {3\hat i + \hat j} \right){/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow 5\hat c = \frac{{\sqrt {10} }}{2}\left( {3\vec i + \vec j} \right){/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{3\sqrt {10} }}{2}\hat i + \frac{{\sqrt {10} }}{2}\hat j{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Write the position vector of mid-point of the vector joining points P(2,3,4) and Q( 4,1, - 2).
question_text: Write the position vector of mid-point of the vector joining points P(2,3,4) and Q( 4,1, - 2).
explanation: Here,we have to find the position vector of mid-point of the vector joining points P(2,3,4) and Q( 4,1, - 2) Now,we get, {tex}\vec { O P } = 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j} + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { O Q } = \hat { 4 i } + \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k }{/tex} Now, position vector of mid-point of vector joining points P(2, 3, 4) and Q(4, 1, - 2) is {tex}\vec { O R } = \frac { \vec { O P } + \vec { O Q } } { 2 } = \frac { ( 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j} + 4 \hat { k } ) + ( 4 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } ) } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec { O R } = \frac { 6 \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } } { 2 } = 3 \hat { i} + 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex}
solution: Here,we have to find the position vector of mid-point of the vector joining points P(2,3,4) and Q( 4,1, - 2) Now,we get, {tex}\vec { O P } = 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j} + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { O Q } = \hat { 4 i } + \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k }{/tex} Now, position vector of mid-point of vector joining points P(2, 3, 4) and Q(4, 1, - 2) is {tex}\vec { O R } = \frac { \vec { O P } + \vec { O Q } } { 2 } = \frac { ( 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j} + 4 \hat { k } ) + ( 4 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } ) } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec { O R } = \frac { 6 \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } } { 2 } = 3 \hat { i} + 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex}
solution_html:
Here,we have to find the position vector of mid-point of the vector joining points P(2,3,4) and Q( 4,1, - 2) Now,we get, {tex}\vec { O P } = 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j} + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { O Q } = \hat { 4 i } + \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k }{/tex}
Now, position vector of mid-point of vector joining points P(2, 3, 4) and Q(4, 1, - 2) is
{tex}\vec { O R } = \frac { \vec { O P } + \vec { O Q } } { 2 } = \frac { ( 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j} + 4 \hat { k } ) + ( 4 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } ) } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec { O R } = \frac { 6 \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } } { 2 } = 3 \hat { i} + 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}\vec { a } \cdot \vec { a } = 0{/tex} and {tex}\vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = 0,{/tex} then what can be concluded about the vector {tex}\vec { b }{/tex}?
question_text: If {tex}\vec { a } \cdot \vec { a } = 0{/tex} and {tex}\vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = 0,{/tex} then what can be concluded about the vector {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} ?
explanation: Here ,we are given that {tex}\vec { a } \cdot \vec { a } = 0 \Rightarrow | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \vec { a } | = 0{/tex} ......(1) and {tex}\vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = 0{/tex} ........(2) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \vec { a } | | \vec { b } | \cos \theta = 0{/tex} From Eqs. (1) and (2), it may be concluded that {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} is either zero or non-zero perpendicular vector.
solution: Here ,we are given that {tex}\vec { a } \cdot \vec { a } = 0 \Rightarrow | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \vec { a } | = 0{/tex} ......(1) and {tex}\vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = 0{/tex} ........(2) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \vec { a } | | \vec { b } | \cos \theta = 0{/tex} From Eqs. (1) and (2), it may be concluded that {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} is either zero or non-zero perpendicular vector.
solution_html:
Here ,we are given that  {tex}\vec { a } \cdot \vec { a } = 0 \Rightarrow | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \vec { a } | = 0{/tex} ......(1)
and {tex}\vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = 0{/tex} ........(2)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \vec { a } | | \vec { b } | \cos \theta = 0{/tex}
From Eqs. (1) and (2), it may be concluded that {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} is either zero or non-zero perpendicular vector.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Write the angle between vectors a and b with magnitudes {tex}\sqrt { 3 }{/tex} and {tex}2{/tex} respectively, having {tex}\vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = \sqrt { 6 }.{/tex}
question_text: Write the angle between vectors a and b with magnitudes {tex}\sqrt { 3 }{/tex} and {tex}2{/tex} respectively, having {tex}\vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = \sqrt { 6 }.{/tex}
explanation: We are given that {tex}| \vec { a } | = \sqrt { 3 } , | \vec { b } | = 2{/tex} and {tex}\vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = \sqrt { 6 }.{/tex} Now, the angle between {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} is given by {tex}\cos \theta = \frac { \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } } { | \vec { a } | | \vec { b } | } = \frac { \sqrt { 6 } } { \sqrt { 3 } \times 2 } = \frac { \sqrt { 3 } \times \sqrt { 2 } } { \sqrt { 3 } \times 2 } = \frac { \sqrt { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex} (simplifying) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } \quad \left[ \because \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \theta = \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex}
solution: We are given that {tex}| \vec { a } | = \sqrt { 3 } , | \vec { b } | = 2{/tex} and {tex}\vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = \sqrt { 6 }.{/tex} Now, the angle between {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} is given by {tex}\cos \theta = \frac { \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } } { | \vec { a } | | \vec { b } | } = \frac { \sqrt { 6 } } { \sqrt { 3 } \times 2 } = \frac { \sqrt { 3 } \times \sqrt { 2 } } { \sqrt { 3 } \times 2 } = \frac { \sqrt { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex} (simplifying) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } \quad \left[ \because \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \theta = \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex}
solution_html:
We are given that  {tex}| \vec { a } | = \sqrt { 3 } , | \vec { b } | = 2{/tex} and {tex}\vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = \sqrt { 6 }.{/tex}
Now, the angle between {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} is given by 
{tex}\cos \theta = \frac { \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } } { | \vec { a } | | \vec { b } | } = \frac { \sqrt { 6 } } { \sqrt { 3 } \times 2 } = \frac { \sqrt { 3 } \times \sqrt { 2 } } { \sqrt { 3 } \times 2 } = \frac { \sqrt { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex}(simplifying)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } \quad \left[ \because \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \theta = \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}| \vec { a } | = 2 , | \vec { b } | = 3 \text { and } \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = 3,{/tex}then find the projection of {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} on {tex}\vec { a }.{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}| \vec { a } | = 2 , | \vec { b } | = 3 \text { and } \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = 3,{/tex} then find the projection of {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} on {tex}\vec { a }.{/tex}
explanation: If {tex}| \vec { a } | = 2 , | \vec { b } | = 3 \text { and } \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = 3,{/tex} then we have to find the projection of {tex}{/tex} {tex}\vec b\ on\ \vec a.{/tex} {tex}{/tex} Projection of {tex}\vec b\ on\ \vec a=\frac{\vec a.\vec b}{|\vec a|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
solution: If {tex}| \vec { a } | = 2 , | \vec { b } | = 3 \text { and } \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = 3,{/tex} then we have to find the projection of {tex}{/tex} {tex}\vec b\ on\ \vec a.{/tex} {tex}{/tex} Projection of {tex}\vec b\ on\ \vec a=\frac{\vec a.\vec b}{|\vec a|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
If {tex}| \vec { a } | = 2 , | \vec { b } | = 3 \text { and } \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = 3,{/tex}then we have  to find the projection of {tex}{/tex} {tex}\vec b\ on\ \vec a.{/tex} {tex}{/tex}

Projection of {tex}\vec b\ on\ \vec a=\frac{\vec a.\vec b}{|\vec a|}{/tex}={tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}

question_html: If A, B and C are the vertices of a {tex}\triangle A B C,{/tex} then what is the value of {tex}\vec { A B } + \vec { B C } + \vec { C A }{/tex}?
question_text: If A, B and C are the vertices of a {tex}\triangle A B C,{/tex} then what is the value of {tex}\vec { A B } + \vec { B C } + \vec { C A }{/tex} ?
explanation: Let ABC be the given triangle. By law of vectors, we have {tex}\vec { A B } + \vec { B C } = \vec { A C }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec { A B } + \vec { B C } + \vec { C A } = \vec { C A } + \vec { A C }{/tex} ........(1) [adding {tex}\vec { C A }{/tex} on both sides] (using (1) {tex}\Rightarrow \vec { A B } + \vec { B C } + \vec { C A } = \vec { C A } - \vec { C A } [ \because \vec { A C } = - \vec { C A } ]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec { A B } + \vec { B C } + \vec { C A } = \vec { 0 } {/tex}
solution: Let ABC be the given triangle. By law of vectors, we have {tex}\vec { A B } + \vec { B C } = \vec { A C }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec { A B } + \vec { B C } + \vec { C A } = \vec { C A } + \vec { A C }{/tex} ........(1) [adding {tex}\vec { C A }{/tex} on both sides] (using (1) {tex}\Rightarrow \vec { A B } + \vec { B C } + \vec { C A } = \vec { C A } - \vec { C A } [ \because \vec { A C } = - \vec { C A } ]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec { A B } + \vec { B C } + \vec { C A } = \vec { 0 } {/tex}
solution_html:
Let ABC be the given triangle.

By law of vectors, we have {tex}\vec { A B } + \vec { B C } = \vec { A C }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec { A B } + \vec { B C } + \vec { C A } = \vec { C A } + \vec { A C }{/tex}........(1)  [adding {tex}\vec { C A }{/tex}on both sides]
(using (1)
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec { A B } + \vec { B C } + \vec { C A } = \vec { C A } - \vec { C A } [ \because \vec { A C } = - \vec { C A } ]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec { A B } + \vec { B C } + \vec { C A } = \vec { 0 } {/tex}

question_html: Write the projection of vector {tex}\hat { i } - \hat { j }{/tex} on the vector {tex}\hat { i } + \hat { j }.{/tex}
question_text: Write the projection of vector {tex}\hat { i } - \hat { j }{/tex} on the vector {tex}\hat { i } + \hat { j }.{/tex}
explanation: Let {tex}\vec a{/tex} = {tex}\hat i{/tex} - {tex}\hat j{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} = {tex}\hat i{/tex} + {tex}\hat j{/tex} Now, projection of vector {tex}\vec a{/tex} on {tex}\vec b{/tex} is given by, {tex}\frac{1}{|\vec{b}|}(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+1}}{/tex} {1 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 1 + (-1)(1)} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} (1 - 1) = 0 Hence, the projection of vector {tex}\vec a{/tex} on {tex}\vec b{/tex} is 0
solution: Let {tex}\vec a{/tex} = {tex}\hat i{/tex} - {tex}\hat j{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} = {tex}\hat i{/tex} + {tex}\hat j{/tex} Now, projection of vector {tex}\vec a{/tex} on {tex}\vec b{/tex} is given by, {tex}\frac{1}{|\vec{b}|}(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+1}}{/tex} {1 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 1 + (-1)(1)} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} (1 - 1) = 0 Hence, the projection of vector {tex}\vec a{/tex} on {tex}\vec b{/tex} is 0
solution_html:
Let {tex}\vec a{/tex} = {tex}\hat i{/tex} - {tex}\hat j{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} = {tex}\hat i{/tex} + {tex}\hat j{/tex}
Now, projection of vector {tex}\vec a{/tex} on {tex}\vec b{/tex} is given by,
{tex}\frac{1}{|\vec{b}|}(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+1}}{/tex}{1{tex}\cdot{/tex}1 + (-1)(1)} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} (1 - 1) = 0
Hence, the projection of vector {tex}\vec a{/tex} on {tex}\vec b{/tex}  is 0

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Using vectors, find the area of triangle with vertices A (1, 1, 2), B(2, 3, 5) and C (1, 5, 5).
question_text: Using vectors, find the area of triangle with vertices A (1, 1, 2), B(2, 3, 5) and C (1, 5, 5).
explanation: We have, A(1,1,2), B(2,3,5) and C(1,5,5) Area of triangle ABC is {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} | {tex}\vec {AB}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\vec {AC}{/tex} | {tex}\vec {AB}{/tex} = (2 - 1) {tex}\hat i{/tex} + (3 - 1) {tex}\hat j{/tex} + (5 - 2) {tex}\hat k{/tex} = 1 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 2 {tex}\hat j{/tex} + 3 {tex}\hat k{/tex} {tex}\vec {AC}{/tex} = (1 - 1) {tex}\hat i{/tex} + (5 - 1) {tex}\hat j{/tex} + (5 - 2) {tex}\hat k{/tex} = 0 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 4 {tex}\hat j{/tex} + 3 {tex}\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll}{\hat{\imath}} & {\hat{\jmath}} & {\hat{k}} \\ {1} & {2} & {3} \\ {0} & {4} & {3}\end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\hat i{/tex} (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 - 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 3) - {tex}\hat j{/tex} (1 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 - 0 {tex}\times{/tex} 3) + {tex}\hat k{/tex} (1 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 - 0 {tex}\times{/tex} 2) = {tex}\hat i{/tex} (6 - 12) - {tex}\hat j{/tex} (3 - 0) + {tex}\hat k{/tex} (4 - 0) = -6 {tex}\hat i{/tex} - 3 {tex}\hat j{/tex} + 4 {tex}\hat k{/tex} Magnitude of {tex}\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{(-6)^{2}+(-3)^{2}+4^{2}}{/tex} {tex}|\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{36+9+16}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{61}{/tex} Area of triangle ABC = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}|\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{61}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{61}}{2}{/tex} Therefore, the required area is {tex}\frac{\sqrt{61}}{2}{/tex} .
solution: We have, A(1,1,2), B(2,3,5) and C(1,5,5) Area of triangle ABC is {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} | {tex}\vec {AB}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\vec {AC}{/tex} | {tex}\vec {AB}{/tex} = (2 - 1) {tex}\hat i{/tex} + (3 - 1) {tex}\hat j{/tex} + (5 - 2) {tex}\hat k{/tex} = 1 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 2 {tex}\hat j{/tex} + 3 {tex}\hat k{/tex} {tex}\vec {AC}{/tex} = (1 - 1) {tex}\hat i{/tex} + (5 - 1) {tex}\hat j{/tex} + (5 - 2) {tex}\hat k{/tex} = 0 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 4 {tex}\hat j{/tex} + 3 {tex}\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll}{\hat{\imath}} & {\hat{\jmath}} & {\hat{k}} \\ {1} & {2} & {3} \\ {0} & {4} & {3}\end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\hat i{/tex} (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 - 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 3) - {tex}\hat j{/tex} (1 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 - 0 {tex}\times{/tex} 3) + {tex}\hat k{/tex} (1 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 - 0 {tex}\times{/tex} 2) = {tex}\hat i{/tex} (6 - 12) - {tex}\hat j{/tex} (3 - 0) + {tex}\hat k{/tex} (4 - 0) = -6 {tex}\hat i{/tex} - 3 {tex}\hat j{/tex} + 4 {tex}\hat k{/tex} Magnitude of {tex}\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{(-6)^{2}+(-3)^{2}+4^{2}}{/tex} {tex}|\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{36+9+16}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{61}{/tex} Area of triangle ABC = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}|\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{61}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{61}}{2}{/tex} Therefore, the required area is {tex}\frac{\sqrt{61}}{2}{/tex} .
solution_html:
We have,
A(1,1,2), B(2,3,5) and C(1,5,5)
Area of triangle ABC is {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}|{tex}\vec {AB}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\vec {AC}{/tex}|
{tex}\vec {AB}{/tex} = (2 - 1){tex}\hat i{/tex} + (3 - 1){tex}\hat j{/tex} + (5 - 2){tex}\hat k{/tex} = 1{tex}\hat i{/tex} + 2{tex}\hat j{/tex} + 3{tex}\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\vec {AC}{/tex} = (1 - 1){tex}\hat i{/tex} + (5 - 1){tex}\hat j{/tex} + (5 - 2){tex}\hat k{/tex} = 0{tex}\hat i{/tex} + 4{tex}\hat j{/tex} + 3{tex}\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll}{\hat{\imath}} & {\hat{\jmath}} & {\hat{k}} \\ {1} & {2} & {3} \\ {0} & {4} & {3}\end{array}\right|{/tex}
= {tex}\hat i{/tex} (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 - 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 3) - {tex}\hat j{/tex}(1 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 - 0 {tex}\times{/tex} 3) + {tex}\hat k{/tex} (1 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 - 0 {tex}\times{/tex} 2)
= {tex}\hat i{/tex} (6 - 12) - {tex}\hat j{/tex}(3 - 0) + {tex}\hat k{/tex}(4 - 0)
= -6{tex}\hat i{/tex} - 3{tex}\hat j{/tex} + 4{tex}\hat k{/tex}
Magnitude of  {tex}\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{(-6)^{2}+(-3)^{2}+4^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}|\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{36+9+16}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{61}{/tex}
Area of triangle ABC = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}{tex}|\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}|{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{61}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{61}}{2}{/tex}
Therefore, the required area is {tex}\frac{\sqrt{61}}{2}{/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find a unit vector in the direction of {tex}\vec { a } = 2 \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex}
question_text: Find a unit vector in the direction of {tex}\vec { a } = 2 \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex}
explanation: Given vector {tex}\vec a{/tex} = 2 {tex}\hat i{/tex} - 3 {tex}\hat j{/tex} + 6 {tex}\hat k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec a|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{4+9+36}{/tex} = 7 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\hat a{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\vec{a}}{|a|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}}{7}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\hat a {/tex} = Unit vector in direction of {tex}\vec a{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{7}{/tex} {tex}\hat i{/tex} - {tex}\frac{3}{7}{/tex} {tex}\hat j{/tex} + {tex}\frac{6}{7}{/tex} {tex}\hat k{/tex}
solution: Given vector {tex}\vec a{/tex} = 2 {tex}\hat i{/tex} - 3 {tex}\hat j{/tex} + 6 {tex}\hat k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec a|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{4+9+36}{/tex} = 7 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\hat a{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\vec{a}}{|a|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}}{7}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\hat a {/tex} = Unit vector in direction of {tex}\vec a{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{7}{/tex} {tex}\hat i{/tex} - {tex}\frac{3}{7}{/tex} {tex}\hat j{/tex} + {tex}\frac{6}{7}{/tex} {tex}\hat k{/tex}
solution_html:
Given vector {tex}\vec a{/tex} = 2{tex}\hat i{/tex} - 3{tex}\hat j{/tex} + 6{tex}\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec a|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{4+9+36}{/tex} = 7
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\hat a{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\vec{a}}{|a|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}}{7}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\hat a {/tex} = Unit vector in direction of {tex}\vec a{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{2}{7}{/tex}{tex}\hat i{/tex} - {tex}\frac{3}{7}{/tex}{tex}\hat j{/tex} + {tex}\frac{6}{7}{/tex}{tex}\hat k{/tex}

question_html: Find a unit vector perpendicular to each of the vectors {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a}-\vec{b}{/tex}, where {tex}\vec{a}=3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex}.
question_text: Find a unit vector perpendicular to each of the vectors {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a}-\vec{b}{/tex} , where {tex}\vec{a}=3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex} .
explanation: Given: {tex}\vec{a}=3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{a}+\vec{b}=4 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+0 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a}-\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \times(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex} {tex}=\left|\begin{array}{lll} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 4 & 4 & 0 \\ 2 & 0 & 4 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=16 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore|(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \times(\vec{a}-\vec{b})|{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{256+256+64}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{576}{/tex} = 24 Unit vector perpendicular to both {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a}-\vec{b}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \times(\vec{a}-\vec{b})}{|(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \times(\vec{a}-\vec{b})|}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{16 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k}}{24}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{8(2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-\hat{k})}{24}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{3}(2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex}
solution: Given: {tex}\vec{a}=3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{a}+\vec{b}=4 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+0 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a}-\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \times(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex} {tex}=\left|\begin{array}{lll} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 4 & 4 & 0 \\ 2 & 0 & 4 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=16 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore|(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \times(\vec{a}-\vec{b})|{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{256+256+64}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{576}{/tex} = 24 Unit vector perpendicular to both {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a}-\vec{b}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \times(\vec{a}-\vec{b})}{|(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \times(\vec{a}-\vec{b})|}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{16 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k}}{24}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{8(2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-\hat{k})}{24}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{3}(2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex}
solution_html:
Given:
{tex}\vec{a}=3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{b}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{a}+\vec{b}=4 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+0 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{a}-\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \times(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex}{tex}=\left|\begin{array}{lll} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 4 & 4 & 0 \\ 2 & 0 & 4 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=16 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore|(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \times(\vec{a}-\vec{b})|{/tex}{tex}=\sqrt{256+256+64}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{576}{/tex}
= 24
Unit vector perpendicular to both  {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a}-\vec{b}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \times(\vec{a}-\vec{b})}{|(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \times(\vec{a}-\vec{b})|}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{16 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k}}{24}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{8(2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-\hat{k})}{24}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{3}(2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex}

question_html: If {tex} \vec { a }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b }{/tex} are two vectors, such that {tex} | \vec { a } | = 2 | \vec { b } | = 1{/tex} and {tex} \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = 1,{/tex} then find {tex} ( 3 \vec { a } - 5 \vec { b } ) \cdot ( 2 \vec { a } + 7 \vec { b } ).{/tex}
question_text: If {tex} \vec { a }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { b }{/tex} are two vectors, such that {tex} | \vec { a } | = 2 | \vec { b } | = 1{/tex} and {tex} \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = 1,{/tex} then find {tex} ( 3 \vec { a } - 5 \vec { b } ) \cdot ( 2 \vec { a } + 7 \vec { b } ).{/tex}
explanation: According to the question, {tex} | \vec { a } | = 2 , | \vec { b } | = 1{/tex} and {tex} \vec { a } .\vec { b } = 1{/tex} ...(i) Now, {tex} ( 3 \vec { a } - 5 \vec { b } ) \cdot ( 2 \vec { a } + 7 \vec { b } ){/tex} {tex} = 6 \vec { a } \cdot \vec { a } + 21 \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } - 10 \vec { b } \cdot \vec { a } - 35 \vec { b } \cdot \vec { b }{/tex} {tex} = 6 | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 } + 21 \vec { a }. \vec { b } - 10 \vec { a } .\vec { b } - 35 | \vec { b } | ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \vec { x } .\vec { x } = | \vec { x } | ^ { 2 } \text { and } \vec { a } .\vec { b } = \vec { b }. \vec { a } \right]{/tex} {tex} = 6 | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 } + 11 \vec { a }. \vec { b } - 35 | \vec { b } | ^ { 2 }{/tex} = 6(2) 2 + 11 (1) - 35 (1) 2 [from Eq. (i)] {tex}= 24 + 11 - 35 = 0{/tex} Hence, {tex} ( 3 \vec { a } - 5 \vec { b } ) .( 2 \vec { a } + 7 \vec { b } ) = 0{/tex}
solution: According to the question, {tex} | \vec { a } | = 2 , | \vec { b } | = 1{/tex} and {tex} \vec { a } .\vec { b } = 1{/tex} ...(i) Now, {tex} ( 3 \vec { a } - 5 \vec { b } ) \cdot ( 2 \vec { a } + 7 \vec { b } ){/tex} {tex} = 6 \vec { a } \cdot \vec { a } + 21 \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } - 10 \vec { b } \cdot \vec { a } - 35 \vec { b } \cdot \vec { b }{/tex} {tex} = 6 | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 } + 21 \vec { a }. \vec { b } - 10 \vec { a } .\vec { b } - 35 | \vec { b } | ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \vec { x } .\vec { x } = | \vec { x } | ^ { 2 } \text { and } \vec { a } .\vec { b } = \vec { b }. \vec { a } \right]{/tex} {tex} = 6 | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 } + 11 \vec { a }. \vec { b } - 35 | \vec { b } | ^ { 2 }{/tex} = 6(2) 2 + 11 (1) - 35 (1) 2 [from Eq. (i)] {tex}= 24 + 11 - 35 = 0{/tex} Hence, {tex} ( 3 \vec { a } - 5 \vec { b } ) .( 2 \vec { a } + 7 \vec { b } ) = 0{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question,

 {tex} | \vec { a } | = 2 , | \vec { b } | = 1{/tex}and {tex} \vec { a } .\vec { b } = 1{/tex}   ...(i)
Now, {tex} ( 3 \vec { a } - 5 \vec { b } ) \cdot ( 2 \vec { a } + 7 \vec { b } ){/tex}
{tex} = 6 \vec { a } \cdot \vec { a } + 21 \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } - 10 \vec { b } \cdot \vec { a } - 35 \vec { b } \cdot \vec { b }{/tex}
{tex} = 6 | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 } + 21 \vec { a }. \vec { b } - 10 \vec { a } .\vec { b } - 35 | \vec { b } | ^ { 2 }{/tex}{tex} \left[ \because \vec { x } .\vec { x } = | \vec { x } | ^ { 2 } \text { and } \vec { a } .\vec { b } = \vec { b }. \vec { a } \right]{/tex}
{tex} = 6 | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 } + 11 \vec { a }. \vec { b } - 35 | \vec { b } | ^ { 2 }{/tex}
= 6(2)2 + 11 (1) - 35 (1)2 [from Eq. (i)]
{tex}= 24 + 11 - 35 = 0{/tex}
Hence, {tex} ( 3 \vec { a } - 5 \vec { b } ) .( 2 \vec { a } + 7 \vec { b } ) = 0{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}| \vec { a } | = 2 , | \vec { b } | = 3{/tex}and {tex}\vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = 3.{/tex}then find the projection of {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} on {tex}\vec { a }.{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}| \vec { a } | = 2 , | \vec { b } | = 3{/tex} and {tex}\vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = 3.{/tex} then find the projection of {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} on {tex}\vec { a }.{/tex}
explanation: Here we are given that , {tex}| \vec { a } | = 2 , | \vec { b } | = 3{/tex} and {tex}\vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = 3{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Projection of {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} on {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { \vec { b } \cdot\vec { a } } { | \vec { a } | }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } } { | \vec { a } | }{/tex} {tex}[ \because \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = \vec { b } \cdot \vec { a } ]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 3 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}[ \because \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = 3 \text { and } | \vec { a } | = 2 ]{/tex}
solution: Here we are given that , {tex}| \vec { a } | = 2 , | \vec { b } | = 3{/tex} and {tex}\vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = 3{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Projection of {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} on {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { \vec { b } \cdot\vec { a } } { | \vec { a } | }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } } { | \vec { a } | }{/tex} {tex}[ \because \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = \vec { b } \cdot \vec { a } ]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 3 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}[ \because \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = 3 \text { and } | \vec { a } | = 2 ]{/tex}
solution_html:
Here we are given that , {tex}| \vec { a } | = 2 , | \vec { b } | = 3{/tex} and {tex}\vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = 3{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Projection of {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} on {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { \vec { b } \cdot\vec { a } } { | \vec { a } | }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } } { | \vec { a } | }{/tex} {tex}[ \because \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = \vec { b } \cdot \vec { a } ]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 3 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}[ \because \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } = 3 \text { and } | \vec { a } | = 2 ]{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} are two vectors, such that {tex}| \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } | = | \vec { a } \times \vec { b } |,{/tex}then find the angle between {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b }.{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} are two vectors, such that {tex}| \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } | = | \vec { a } \times \vec { b } |,{/tex} then find the angle between {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b }.{/tex}
explanation: Given, {tex}| \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } | = | \vec { a } \times \vec { b } |{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \vec { a } | | \vec { b } | \cos \theta = | \vec { a } \| \vec { b } | \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \sin \theta{/tex} (dividing both sides by cosθ,we have, {tex}\tan \theta = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan \theta = \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because 1 = \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right]{/tex} {tex}\theta = \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex} So, the angle between {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} is {tex}\frac { \pi } { 4 }.{/tex}
solution: Given, {tex}| \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } | = | \vec { a } \times \vec { b } |{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \vec { a } | | \vec { b } | \cos \theta = | \vec { a } \| \vec { b } | \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \sin \theta{/tex} (dividing both sides by cosθ,we have, {tex}\tan \theta = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan \theta = \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because 1 = \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right]{/tex} {tex}\theta = \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex} So, the angle between {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} is {tex}\frac { \pi } { 4 }.{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, {tex}| \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } | = | \vec { a } \times \vec { b } |{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad | \vec { a } | | \vec { b } | \cos \theta = | \vec { a } \| \vec { b } | \sin \theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \sin \theta{/tex} (dividing both sides by cosθ,we have,
{tex}\tan \theta = 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan \theta = \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because 1 = \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\theta = \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex}
So, the angle between {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} is {tex}\frac { \pi } { 4 }.{/tex}

question_html: Find the position vector of a point R, which divides the line joining two points P and Q whose position vectors are 2{tex}\vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { a } - 3 \vec { b }{/tex} respectively, externally in the ratio 1: 2. Also, show that P is the mid-point of line segment RQ.
question_text: Find the position vector of a point R, which divides the line joining two points P and Q whose position vectors are 2 {tex}\vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { a } - 3 \vec { b }{/tex} respectively, externally in the ratio 1: 2. Also, show that P is the mid-point of line segment RQ.
explanation: According to the question, {tex}\vec { O P }{/tex} = Position vector of {tex}P = 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { O Q }{/tex} = Position vector of {tex}Q = \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b }{/tex} Suppose {tex}\vec { O R }{/tex} be the position vector of point R, which divides {tex}PQ\ in\ the\ ratio\ 1: 2{/tex} externally {tex}\therefore \quad \vec { O R } = \frac { 1 ( \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b } ) - 2 ( 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) } { 1 - 2 }{/tex} [using external section formula] {tex}= \frac { \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b } - 4 \vec { a } - 2 \vec { b } } { - 1 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { - 3 \vec { a } - 5 \vec { b } } { - 1 } = 3 \vec { a } + 5 \vec { b }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec { O R } = 3 \vec { a } + 5 \vec { b }{/tex} We have to show that P is the mid-point of RQ, i.e. {tex}\vec { O P } = \frac { \vec { O R } + \vec { O Q } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\vec { O R } = 3 \vec { a } + 5 \vec { b } , \vec { O Q } = \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac { \vec { O R } + \vec { O Q } } { 2 } = \frac { ( 3 \vec { a } + 5 \vec { b } ) + \vec { ( a } - 3 \vec { b } ) } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 4 \vec { a } + 2 \vec { b } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 ( 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} {tex}= \vec { O P }{/tex} {tex}[ \because \vec { O P } = 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ]{/tex} P is the mid-point of line segment RQ.
solution: According to the question, {tex}\vec { O P }{/tex} = Position vector of {tex}P = 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { O Q }{/tex} = Position vector of {tex}Q = \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b }{/tex} Suppose {tex}\vec { O R }{/tex} be the position vector of point R, which divides {tex}PQ\ in\ the\ ratio\ 1: 2{/tex} externally {tex}\therefore \quad \vec { O R } = \frac { 1 ( \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b } ) - 2 ( 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) } { 1 - 2 }{/tex} [using external section formula] {tex}= \frac { \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b } - 4 \vec { a } - 2 \vec { b } } { - 1 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { - 3 \vec { a } - 5 \vec { b } } { - 1 } = 3 \vec { a } + 5 \vec { b }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec { O R } = 3 \vec { a } + 5 \vec { b }{/tex} We have to show that P is the mid-point of RQ, i.e. {tex}\vec { O P } = \frac { \vec { O R } + \vec { O Q } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\vec { O R } = 3 \vec { a } + 5 \vec { b } , \vec { O Q } = \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac { \vec { O R } + \vec { O Q } } { 2 } = \frac { ( 3 \vec { a } + 5 \vec { b } ) + \vec { ( a } - 3 \vec { b } ) } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 4 \vec { a } + 2 \vec { b } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 ( 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} {tex}= \vec { O P }{/tex} {tex}[ \because \vec { O P } = 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ]{/tex} P is the mid-point of line segment RQ.
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}\vec { O P }{/tex}= Position vector of {tex}P = 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex}
and {tex}\vec { O Q }{/tex}= Position vector of {tex}Q = \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b }{/tex}
Suppose  {tex}\vec { O R }{/tex} be the position vector of point R, which divides {tex}PQ\ in\ the\ ratio\ 1: 2{/tex} externally

{tex}\therefore \quad \vec { O R } = \frac { 1 ( \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b } ) - 2 ( 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) } { 1 - 2 }{/tex}
[using external section formula]
{tex}= \frac { \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b } - 4 \vec { a } - 2 \vec { b } } { - 1 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { - 3 \vec { a } - 5 \vec { b } } { - 1 } = 3 \vec { a } + 5 \vec { b }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec { O R } = 3 \vec { a } + 5 \vec { b }{/tex}
We have to show that P is the mid-point of RQ,
i.e. {tex}\vec { O P } = \frac { \vec { O R } + \vec { O Q } } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\vec { O R } = 3 \vec { a } + 5 \vec { b } , \vec { O Q } = \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac { \vec { O R } + \vec { O Q } } { 2 } = \frac { ( 3 \vec { a } + 5 \vec { b } ) + \vec { ( a } - 3 \vec { b } ) } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 4 \vec { a } + 2 \vec { b } } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 2 ( 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex}
{tex}= \vec { O P }{/tex}  {tex}[ \because \vec { O P } = 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ]{/tex}
P is the mid-point of line segment RQ.

question_html: Let {tex} \vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } , \vec { b } = 4 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { c } = \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k }.{/tex}Find a vector of magnitude 6 units, which is parallel to the vector {tex} 2 \vec { a } - \vec { b } + 3 \vec { c }.{/tex}
question_text: Let {tex} \vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } , \vec { b } = 4 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { c } = \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k }.{/tex} Find a vector of magnitude 6 units, which is parallel to the vector {tex} 2 \vec { a } - \vec { b } + 3 \vec { c }.{/tex}
explanation: According to the question , {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat {j} + \hat { k } , {/tex} {tex}\vec { b } = 4 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { c } = \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} Now {tex},2 \vec { a } - \vec { b } + \vec { 3 } \vec { c }{/tex} {tex} = 2 ( \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } ) - ( 4 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ) + 3 ( \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j}+ \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex} = 2 \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } - 4 \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } - 3 \hat { k } + 3 \hat { i } - 6 \hat { j } + 3{ \hat { k } }{/tex} {tex} = \hat { i } - 2 \hat {j } + { 2 } \hat { k }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 \vec { a } - \vec { b } + 3 \vec { c } = \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + { 2 } \hat { k }{/tex} Now, a unit vector in the direction of vector is {tex} 2 \vec { a } - \vec { b } + 3 \vec { c } = \frac { 2 \vec { a } - \vec { b } + 3 \vec { c } } { | 2 \vec { a } - \vec { b } + 3 \vec { c } | }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { ( 1) ^ { 2 } + ( - 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 9 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } } { 3 }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \hat { i } - \frac { 2 } { 3 } \hat { j } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } \hat { k }{/tex} Vector of magnitude 6 units parallel to the vector is , {tex} = 6 \left( \frac { 1 } { 3 } \hat { i } - \frac { 2 } { 3 } \hat { j } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } \hat { k } \right){/tex} {tex} = 2 \hat { i } - 4 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex}
solution: According to the question , {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat {j} + \hat { k } , {/tex} {tex}\vec { b } = 4 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { c } = \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} Now {tex},2 \vec { a } - \vec { b } + \vec { 3 } \vec { c }{/tex} {tex} = 2 ( \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } ) - ( 4 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ) + 3 ( \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j}+ \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex} = 2 \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } - 4 \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } - 3 \hat { k } + 3 \hat { i } - 6 \hat { j } + 3{ \hat { k } }{/tex} {tex} = \hat { i } - 2 \hat {j } + { 2 } \hat { k }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 \vec { a } - \vec { b } + 3 \vec { c } = \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + { 2 } \hat { k }{/tex} Now, a unit vector in the direction of vector is {tex} 2 \vec { a } - \vec { b } + 3 \vec { c } = \frac { 2 \vec { a } - \vec { b } + 3 \vec { c } } { | 2 \vec { a } - \vec { b } + 3 \vec { c } | }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { ( 1) ^ { 2 } + ( - 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 9 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } } { 3 }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \hat { i } - \frac { 2 } { 3 } \hat { j } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } \hat { k }{/tex} Vector of magnitude 6 units parallel to the vector is , {tex} = 6 \left( \frac { 1 } { 3 } \hat { i } - \frac { 2 } { 3 } \hat { j } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } \hat { k } \right){/tex} {tex} = 2 \hat { i } - 4 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question ,

 {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat {j} + \hat { k } , {/tex}

{tex}\vec { b } = 4 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} and 

{tex} \vec { c } = \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex}
Now {tex},2 \vec { a } - \vec { b } + \vec { 3 } \vec { c }{/tex}
{tex} = 2 ( \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } ) - ( 4 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ) + 3 ( \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j}+ \hat { k } ){/tex}
{tex} = 2 \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } - 4 \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } - 3 \hat { k } + 3 \hat { i } - 6 \hat { j } + 3{ \hat { k } }{/tex}

{tex} = \hat { i } - 2 \hat {j } + { 2 } \hat { k }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 \vec { a } - \vec { b } + 3 \vec { c } = \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + { 2 } \hat { k }{/tex}
Now, a unit vector in the direction of vector is {tex} 2 \vec { a } - \vec { b } + 3 \vec { c } = \frac { 2 \vec { a } - \vec { b } + 3 \vec { c } } { | 2 \vec { a } - \vec { b } + 3 \vec { c } | }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { ( 1) ^ { 2 } + ( - 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 9 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } } { 3 }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \hat { i } - \frac { 2 } { 3 } \hat { j } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } \hat { k }{/tex}
Vector of magnitude 6 units parallel to the vector is ,
{tex} = 6 \left( \frac { 1 } { 3 } \hat { i } - \frac { 2 } { 3 } \hat { j } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } \hat { k } \right){/tex}
{tex} = 2 \hat { i } - 4 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex}

question_html: Find {tex}\lambda _ { \text { } },{/tex} if {tex}( 2 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 14 \hat { k } ) \times ( \hat { i } - \lambda \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k } ) = \vec { 0 }.{/tex}
question_text: Find {tex}\lambda _ { \text { } },{/tex} if {tex}( 2 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 14 \hat { k } ) \times ( \hat { i } - \lambda \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k } ) = \vec { 0 }.{/tex}
explanation: We have given (2 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 6 {tex}\hat j{/tex} + 14 {tex}\hat k{/tex} ) {tex}\times{/tex} ( {tex}\hat i{/tex} - {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\hat j{/tex} + 7 {tex}\hat k{/tex} ) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{rrr}{\hat{i}} & {\hat{j}} & {\hat{k}} \\ {2} & {6} & {14} \\ {1} & {-\lambda} & {7}\end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\hat{i}\left|\begin{array}{rr}{6} & {14} \\ {-\lambda} & {7}\end{array}\right|-\hat{j}\left|\begin{array}{rr}{2} & {14} \\ {1} & {7}\end{array}\right|+\hat{k}\left|\begin{array}{rr}{2} & {6} \\ {1} & {-\lambda}\end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\hat i{/tex} (42 + 14 {tex}\lambda{/tex} ) - 0 {tex}\hat j{/tex} + {tex}\hat k{/tex} (-2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} - 6) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 42 + 14 {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 14 {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -42 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -3 Also, -2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} - 6 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -3 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -3
solution: We have given (2 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 6 {tex}\hat j{/tex} + 14 {tex}\hat k{/tex} ) {tex}\times{/tex} ( {tex}\hat i{/tex} - {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\hat j{/tex} + 7 {tex}\hat k{/tex} ) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{rrr}{\hat{i}} & {\hat{j}} & {\hat{k}} \\ {2} & {6} & {14} \\ {1} & {-\lambda} & {7}\end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\hat{i}\left|\begin{array}{rr}{6} & {14} \\ {-\lambda} & {7}\end{array}\right|-\hat{j}\left|\begin{array}{rr}{2} & {14} \\ {1} & {7}\end{array}\right|+\hat{k}\left|\begin{array}{rr}{2} & {6} \\ {1} & {-\lambda}\end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\hat i{/tex} (42 + 14 {tex}\lambda{/tex} ) - 0 {tex}\hat j{/tex} + {tex}\hat k{/tex} (-2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} - 6) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 42 + 14 {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 14 {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -42 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -3 Also, -2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} - 6 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -3 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -3
solution_html:
We have given
(2{tex}\hat i{/tex} + 6{tex}\hat j{/tex} + 14{tex}\hat k{/tex}) {tex}\times{/tex} ({tex}\hat i{/tex} - {tex}\lambda{/tex}{tex}\hat j{/tex} + 7{tex}\hat k{/tex}) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{rrr}{\hat{i}} & {\hat{j}} & {\hat{k}} \\ {2} & {6} & {14} \\ {1} & {-\lambda} & {7}\end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\hat{i}\left|\begin{array}{rr}{6} & {14} \\ {-\lambda} & {7}\end{array}\right|-\hat{j}\left|\begin{array}{rr}{2} & {14} \\ {1} & {7}\end{array}\right|+\hat{k}\left|\begin{array}{rr}{2} & {6} \\ {1} & {-\lambda}\end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\hat i{/tex}(42 + 14{tex}\lambda{/tex}) - 0{tex}\hat j{/tex} + {tex}\hat k{/tex}(-2{tex}\lambda{/tex} - 6) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 42 + 14{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 14{tex}\lambda{/tex} = -42 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -3
Also, -2{tex}\lambda{/tex} - 6 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -3

question_html: What is the cosine of angle which the vector{tex}\sqrt { 2 } \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} makes with Y-axis?
question_text: What is the cosine of angle which the vector {tex}\sqrt { 2 } \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} makes with Y-axis?
explanation: We will consider Vector a = {tex}\sqrt{2} \hat i{/tex} + {tex}\hat j{/tex} + {tex}\hat k{/tex} Unit vector in the direction of vector a is = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2 i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}}{\sqrt{(\sqrt{2})^{2}+(1)^{2}+(1)^{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2 i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}}{\sqrt{4}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2} \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\hat i{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\hat j{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\hat k{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}\hat i{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\hat j{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\hat k{/tex}
solution: We will consider Vector a = {tex}\sqrt{2} \hat i{/tex} + {tex}\hat j{/tex} + {tex}\hat k{/tex} Unit vector in the direction of vector a is = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2 i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}}{\sqrt{(\sqrt{2})^{2}+(1)^{2}+(1)^{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2 i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}}{\sqrt{4}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2} \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\hat i{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\hat j{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\hat k{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}\hat i{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\hat j{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\hat k{/tex}
solution_html:
We will consider
Vector a = {tex}\sqrt{2} \hat i{/tex} + {tex}\hat j{/tex} + {tex}\hat k{/tex}
Unit vector in the direction of vector a is
= {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2 i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}}{\sqrt{(\sqrt{2})^{2}+(1)^{2}+(1)^{2}}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2 i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}}{\sqrt{4}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2} \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}}{2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}{/tex}{tex}\hat i{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}{tex}\hat j{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}{tex}\hat k{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}{tex}\hat i{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}{tex}\hat j{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}{tex}\hat k{/tex}

question_html: Write a unit vector in the direction of {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex}.
question_text: Write a unit vector in the direction of {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} .
explanation: Given vector is {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}|\vec{b}|=\sqrt{2^{2}+1^{2}+2^{2}}=\sqrt{4+1+4}=\sqrt{9}=3{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Unit vector {tex}=\frac{\vec{b}}{|\vec{b}|}=\frac{1}{3}(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{2}{3} \hat{i}+\frac{1}{3} \hat{j}+\frac{2}{3} \hat{k}{/tex}
solution: Given vector is {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}|\vec{b}|=\sqrt{2^{2}+1^{2}+2^{2}}=\sqrt{4+1+4}=\sqrt{9}=3{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Unit vector {tex}=\frac{\vec{b}}{|\vec{b}|}=\frac{1}{3}(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{2}{3} \hat{i}+\frac{1}{3} \hat{j}+\frac{2}{3} \hat{k}{/tex}

explanation: Given points are A(3, 4, -2) and B(1, 2, 4) Let C is the mid point of AB {tex}\therefore{/tex} Position vector of C {tex}=\frac{3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}+\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}}{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}}{2}{/tex} {tex}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex}
solution: Given points are A(3, 4, -2) and B(1, 2, 4) Let C is the mid point of AB {tex}\therefore{/tex} Position vector of C {tex}=\frac{3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}+\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}}{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}}{2}{/tex} {tex}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex}
solution_html:

question_html: Find a vector in the direction of {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex}, which has magnitude of 6 units.
question_text: Find a vector in the direction of {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} , which has magnitude of 6 units.
explanation: Given vector is {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}|\vec{a}|=\sqrt{2^{2}+(-1)^{2}+2^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{4+1+4}=\sqrt{9}=3{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required Vector {tex}=6 \times \frac{\vec{a}}{|\vec{a}|}=6 \times \frac{(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})}{3}{/tex} {tex}=4 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex}
solution: Given vector is {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}|\vec{a}|=\sqrt{2^{2}+(-1)^{2}+2^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{4+1+4}=\sqrt{9}=3{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required Vector {tex}=6 \times \frac{\vec{a}}{|\vec{a}|}=6 \times \frac{(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})}{3}{/tex} {tex}=4 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given vector is  {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}|\vec{a}|=\sqrt{2^{2}+(-1)^{2}+2^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{4+1+4}=\sqrt{9}=3{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required Vector {tex}=6 \times \frac{\vec{a}}{|\vec{a}|}=6 \times \frac{(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})}{3}{/tex} {tex}=4 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Using vectors find the area of the triangle with vertices, A(2, 3, 5), B(3, 5, 8) and C(2,7, 8).
question_text: Using vectors find the area of the triangle with vertices, A(2, 3, 5), B(3, 5, 8) and C(2,7, 8).
explanation: Let {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}{/tex} be the position vectors of A, B and C, respectively. Then, {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}=3 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{c}=2 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}{/tex} Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}=\vec{b}-\vec{a}{/tex} {tex}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{A C}=\vec{c}-\vec{a}{/tex} {tex}=0 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}{/tex} {tex}=\left|\begin{array}{lll} \hat{i} & \tilde{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 0 & 4 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=-6 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}|=\sqrt{36+9+16}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{61}{/tex} Area of triangle ABC {tex}=\frac{1}{2}|\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}|{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\sqrt{61}}{2}{/tex} sq, units
solution: Let {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}{/tex} be the position vectors of A, B and C, respectively. Then, {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}=3 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{c}=2 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}{/tex} Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}=\vec{b}-\vec{a}{/tex} {tex}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{A C}=\vec{c}-\vec{a}{/tex} {tex}=0 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}{/tex} {tex}=\left|\begin{array}{lll} \hat{i} & \tilde{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 0 & 4 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=-6 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}|=\sqrt{36+9+16}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{61}{/tex} Area of triangle ABC {tex}=\frac{1}{2}|\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}|{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\sqrt{61}}{2}{/tex} sq, units
solution_html:
Let {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}{/tex} be the position vectors of A, B and C, respectively.
Then,
{tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{b}=3 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{c}=2 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}{/tex}
Now,
{tex}\overrightarrow{A B}=\vec{b}-\vec{a}{/tex}
{tex}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{A C}=\vec{c}-\vec{a}{/tex}
{tex}=0 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}{/tex}{tex}=\left|\begin{array}{lll} \hat{i} & \tilde{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 0 & 4 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=-6 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}|=\sqrt{36+9+16}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{61}{/tex}
Area of triangle ABC {tex}=\frac{1}{2}|\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}|{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\sqrt{61}}{2}{/tex} sq, units

question_html: Find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} if the vectors {tex}2 \hat{i}+\lambda \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}{/tex} are perpendicular to each other.
question_text: Find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} if the vectors {tex}2 \hat{i}+\lambda \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}{/tex} are perpendicular to each other.
explanation: Let {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\lambda \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}{/tex} for {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} to be perpendicular to {tex}\vec{b} {/tex} i.e. {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=0{/tex} [vector dot product] {tex}(2 \hat{\imath}+\lambda \hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{k}) \cdot(3 \hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}-4 \hat{k})=0{/tex} 6 + 2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} -12=0 2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} - 6 = 0 Therefore, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 3
solution: Let {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\lambda \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}{/tex} for {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} to be perpendicular to {tex}\vec{b} {/tex} i.e. {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=0{/tex} [vector dot product] {tex}(2 \hat{\imath}+\lambda \hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{k}) \cdot(3 \hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}-4 \hat{k})=0{/tex} 6 + 2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} -12=0 2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} - 6 = 0 Therefore, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 3
solution_html:
Let {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\lambda \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}{/tex}
for {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} to be perpendicular to {tex}\vec{b} {/tex}
i.e. {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=0{/tex} [vector dot product]
{tex}(2 \hat{\imath}+\lambda \hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{k}) \cdot(3 \hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}-4 \hat{k})=0{/tex}
6 + 2{tex}\lambda{/tex} -12=0
2{tex}\lambda{/tex} - 6 = 0 
Therefore, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 3

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Write a vector of magnitude 15 units in the direction of the vector {tex}(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex}.
question_text: Write a vector of magnitude 15 units in the direction of the vector {tex}(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\text { Let } \vec{a}=(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) .{/tex} Then,we have a unit vector in the direction of {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} is given by,so {tex}\hat{a}=\frac{\vec{a}}{|\vec{a}|}=\frac{(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{1^{2}+(-2)^{2}+2^{2}}}=\frac{1}{3}(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} a vector of magnitude 15 in the direction of {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} = {tex}15 \times \frac{1}{3}(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k})=(5 \hat{i}-10 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k}){/tex} .
solution: {tex}\text { Let } \vec{a}=(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) .{/tex} Then,we have a unit vector in the direction of {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} is given by,so {tex}\hat{a}=\frac{\vec{a}}{|\vec{a}|}=\frac{(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{1^{2}+(-2)^{2}+2^{2}}}=\frac{1}{3}(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} a vector of magnitude 15 in the direction of {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} = {tex}15 \times \frac{1}{3}(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k})=(5 \hat{i}-10 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k}){/tex} .
solution_html:
{tex}\text { Let } \vec{a}=(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) .{/tex} Then,we have
a unit vector in the direction of {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} is given by,so
{tex}\hat{a}=\frac{\vec{a}}{|\vec{a}|}=\frac{(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{1^{2}+(-2)^{2}+2^{2}}}=\frac{1}{3}(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a vector of magnitude 15 in the direction of {tex}\vec{a}{/tex}
= {tex}15 \times \frac{1}{3}(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k})=(5 \hat{i}-10 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k}){/tex}.
